title: Dressians, Tropical Grassmannians, and Their Rays
overall_summary: The paper studies the ordinary Dressian Dr(k,n) as the fan of regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), equivalently as the tropical prevariety cut out by the 3-term Plücker relations. Its main structural construction embeds the secondary fan of the product of simplices ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} piecewise-linearly into Dr(k,n), via a map τ defined from tropical maximal minors of an augmented matrix; geometrically this controls a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n) from the subdivision induced on a vertex figure. The paper develops tight-span and vertex-figure methods for constructing and recognizing rays, especially coarsest matroid subdivisions, and classifies/computes all rays and all cones of Dr(3,8). For the original problem, the most useful content is the concrete combinatorial model of Dressian cones by matroid subdivisions, the tight-span/coarsening technology for detecting rays and obstructions, and the explicit small Dressian data that can be used to test or refute proposed order-reversing symmetries.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n>k>0. For a matrix V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, form the augmented tropical matrix Vbar=(E_k|V), where E_k is the k×k tropical identity matrix with 0 on the diagonal and ∞ off the diagonal. For each k-subset σ⊆[n], define τ_V(σ)=tdet(Vbar_σ), where Vbar_σ is the k×k submatrix with column set σ and tdet(A)=min_{ω∈Sym(k)} sum_i a_{i,ω(i)}. For π∈R^{C([n],k)}, define Φ(π)∈R^{k×(n−k)} by Φ(π)_{ij}=π(([k]−{i})∪{j+k}). Then Φ(τ_V)=V for every V. Hence τ is injective, Φ is surjective onto R^{k×(n−k)}, Φ is linear, and τ is piecewise-linear.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For σ=([k]−{i})∪{j+k}, the submatrix Vbar_σ contains all columns of the tropical identity except the i-th one, together with column j of V. In this submatrix, each identity column has exactly one finite entry, equal to 0, and the only row not covered by those identity columns is row i. Thus every finite permutation contributing to the tropical determinant is forced to use the entry v_{ij} in row i and the added column, and all other chosen entries are 0. Therefore τ_V(σ)=v_{ij}, which is exactly the (i,j)-entry of Φ(τ_V). This proves Φ∘τ=id, giving injectivity of τ and surjectivity of Φ. The map Φ is just coordinate projection, hence linear. Each coordinate of τ is a minimum of finitely many linear forms, namely the terms in a tropical determinant, so τ is piecewise-linear.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (Kapranov; Speyer)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π be a lifting function on the vertices e_σ of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n)=conv{e_σ: σ∈C([n],k)}, and let Σ be the regular subdivision of ∆(k,n) induced by π. The following are equivalent: (i) π is a finite tropical Plücker vector, meaning that for every (k−2)-subset ρ⊆[n] and pairwise distinct i,j,l,m∈[n]−ρ, the minimum of π(ρij)+π(ρlm), π(ρil)+π(ρjm), and π(ρim)+π(ρjl) is attained at least twice; (ii) Σ is a matroid subdivision, meaning every cell is a matroid polytope; (iii) the 1-cells of Σ are precisely edges of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), equivalently no subdivision edge is a non-edge diagonal of ∆(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this equivalence from Kapranov and Speyer rather than reproving it. The mechanism is local on the octahedral 3-faces of the hypersimplex. Fixing ρ and four elements i,j,l,m gives the six vertices e_{ρij}, e_{ρil}, e_{ρim}, e_{ρjl}, e_{ρjm}, e_{ρlm}, which form a regular octahedron. The three possible ways in which one pair of opposite vertices is separated correspond exactly to the three terms in the 3-term Plücker relation. The condition that the minimum is attained at least twice rules out a forbidden lower edge/diagonal in this octahedron. By the Gel'fand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova characterization, a subpolytope of the hypersimplex is a matroid polytope exactly when all of its edges are parallel to hypersimplex edges, i.e. to differences e_i−e_j. Thus the tropical Plücker conditions are equivalent to every cell of the induced subdivision being a matroid polytope, and this is equivalent to the asserted 1-skeleton condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)}. Let Γ be the regular subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} induced by the height function V on its vertices, and let Σ be the regular matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n) induced by τ_V. Let e_[k] be the vertex of ∆(k,n) corresponding to [k], and let e_[k]^⊥ be the affine hyperplane containing the neighbors of e_[k]. The natural bijection ι from the vertices e_i+e_j of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, with i∈[k] and j∈[n]−[k], to the neighbors e_{[k]−{i}∪{j}} of e_[k] identifies Γ with the induced subdivision Σ∩e_[k]^⊥ of the vertex figure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The neighbors of e_[k] in ∆(k,n) lie in the affine hyperplane e_[k]^⊥ and form a vertex figure isomorphic to ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Since τ_V is a tropical Plücker vector, Proposition 3 implies that Σ is a matroid subdivision and, in particular, has no new 1-dimensional cells other than hypersimplex edges. Therefore intersecting Σ with the vertex-figure hyperplane gives a genuine subdivision of that vertex figure without introducing extra vertices. By Lemma 1, the height assigned by τ_V to the neighbor e_{[k]−{i}∪{j}} is exactly v_{ij}, the height used by V on the corresponding vertex e_i+e_j of the product of simplices. Hence the regular subdivision induced on the vertex figure is precisely ι(Γ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a polytope, P' a subpolytope of P, and Σ a subdivision of P. For a subdivision Σ, let T(Σ) denote the tight-span, i.e. the containment poset of interior cells dualized. Suppose that the tight-spans T(Σ) and T(Σ|P') are isomorphic, where Σ|P'={S∩P': S∈Σ}. Then: (i) if Σ' is any subdivision of P coarsening Σ, the tight-spans T(Σ') and T(Σ'|P') are also isomorphic; (ii) if Σ|P' is a coarsest subdivision of P', then Σ is a coarsest subdivision of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), assume for contradiction that after coarsening there is a mismatch between T(Σ') and T(Σ'|P'). Then some full-dimensional cell F' of Σ' has F'∩P' not full-dimensional. Choose a full-dimensional cell F of the finer subdivision Σ with F⊆F'. Since T(Σ) and T(Σ|P') are isomorphic, F∩P' is full-dimensional. But then F∩P'⊆F'∩P' is full-dimensional, contradicting the choice of F'. Thus coarsening preserves the tight-span isomorphism. For (ii), if Σ had a nontrivial coarsening Σ', then by (i) the induced subdivision Σ'|P' would give a coarsening of Σ|P'. Because the tight-spans remain isomorphic, this coarsening is nontrivial, contradicting coarseness of Σ|P'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a polytope and v a vertex of P. Let N be the set of vertices of P adjacent to v, and set Q=conv(N). Assume dim Q=dim P−1. For any subdivision Γ of Q, there is at most one subdivision Σ of P such that Σ∩Q=Γ and every maximal cell of Σ contains v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a cell γ of Γ, let C(γ) be the cone from v over γ and let D(γ)=C(γ)∩P. The sets D(γ) stratify P, though a priori they need not form the desired subdivision. Suppose Σ has the required properties. If σ is a maximal cell of Σ, then σ∩Q is a maximal cell γ(σ) of Γ. Since σ contains v, it contains conv(v,γ(σ)). Therefore two distinct maximal cells of Σ cannot have the same γ(σ), because their interiors would overlap along the full-dimensional cone over γ(σ). Also, σ has v as a vertex and has no other vertices on the v-side of Q, so σ⊆C(γ(σ))∩P=D(γ(σ)). Thus maximal cells of Σ inject into maximal cells of Γ with σ⊆D(γ(σ)). Since Σ covers all of P, every such inclusion must be equality and every D(γ) must appear. Hence the subdivision, if it exists, is uniquely determined by Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be any subdivision, not necessarily regular, of the product of simplices ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Then there exists a subdivision Σ of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n) with the following properties: (i) the subdivision induced by Σ on the vertex figure of e_[k] is the image ι(Γ); (ii) every maximal cell of Σ contains the vertex e_[k]; (iii) the tight-spans T(Γ) and T(Σ) are isomorphic. Moreover, if Γ is regular and induced by a lifting function V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, then Σ is regular and is induced by τ_V. If Γ is not regular, then Σ is not regular. The paper uses this to conclude that each tight-span of a subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} arises as the tight-span of a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n), and in the regular case as a regular matroid subdivision.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Translate ∆(k,n) by −e_[k], so e_[k] becomes the origin, and write T=conv{e_σ−e_[k]: σ∈C([n],k)}. Let U be the vertex figure at the origin, identified with ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. For each cell γ of Γ, define a cell pos(γ)∩T, where pos(γ) is the positive real span of γ in the translated coordinates. Set Σ={pos(γ)∩T: γ∈Γ}. This construction immediately makes every maximal cell contain the origin and gives the prescribed subdivision on U. To verify that Σ is a valid subdivision without extra vertices, the proof analyzes possible zero-dimensional faces: the vertices of U have the form e_i−e_j with j∈[k] and i∈[n]−[k]; hyperplanes spanned by such vertices have equations of the form sum_{r∈A∪B} x_r=0 for suitable nonempty A⊂[k] and B⊂[n]−[k], while faces of T are cut out by coordinate equations x_r∈{0,1,−1}. A zero-dimensional intersection of these types must be a vertex of T. Thus the conical construction gives a legitimate subdivision. For tight-spans, cells satisfy (pos C∩T)∩(pos D∩T)=(pos(C∩D))∩T, and dimensions increase by one: dim(pos C∩T)=dim C+1. Hence maximal cells and their incidence relations correspond, so T(Γ)≅T(Σ). If Γ is regular with lifting function V, the proof defines a height κ on vertices e_σ−e_[k] by a minimum-cost flow formula. Writing A=[k]−([k]∩σ) and B=([k+1,n]∩σ), κ(e_σ−e_[k]) is the minimum of sum_{i,j} w(i,j)Vbar_{ij} over nonnegative flows w from A to B with total displacement e_B−e_A. On the other hand τ_V(σ) is the minimum over bijections α:A→B of sum_{i∈A} Vbar_{i,α(i)}. Since minimum-cost flow problems with integral constraints have integral optima, the fractional flow minimum equals the bijection minimum, so κ=τ_V. Thus the regular subdivision is induced by τ_V. Finally, if Γ were nonregular but Σ regular, restricting the regular height function of Σ to the vertex figure would make Γ regular, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Theorem 7, if P is a face of Γ and G is the bipartite graph with vertex set [n] and an edge (i,j) whenever e_[k]−e_i+e_j is a vertex of P, then the corresponding face Q of Σ is the matroid polytope of the principal transversal matroid associated with G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not expanded in the paper, but it follows from the conical construction in Theorem 7. A face of the product of simplices is encoded by a bipartite incidence graph recording which replacements of elements of [k] by elements outside [k] occur. The vertices generated in the corresponding cone over the face are precisely the bases obtainable by a matching/transversal-type selection in this bipartite graph. These bases define the principal transversal matroid of G, giving the matroid polytope Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every regular subdivision Γ of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} and every vertex v of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), there exists a regular matroid subdivision Σ of ∆(k,n) such that the subdivision of the vertex figure of v induced by Σ coincides with Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 7 proves the assertion for the special vertex e_[k]. The symmetric group Sym(n) acts transitively on the vertices of ∆(k,n) by permuting coordinates, and the stabilizer of a vertex acts transitively on the corresponding product-of-simplices vertex figure by permuting rows and columns. Applying a coordinate permutation carries the construction at e_[k] to any chosen vertex v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every matrix V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, the tropical Plücker vector τ_V lies in the tropical Grassmannian Gr(k,n), not merely in the Dressian Dr(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a lift V* of the augmented tropical matrix Vbar=(E_k|V) to a k×n matrix over a Puiseux-series field K, with valuation val(V*_{ij})=Vbar_{ij}. The first k columns lift the tropical identity, so they are linearly independent, and the column span L(V*) is a k-dimensional subspace of K^n. Taking the lift generically avoids cancellation in all k×k determinants, so for every k-subset σ, the valuation of the classical Plücker coordinate p(σ) of L(V*) equals the tropical determinant tdet(Vbar_σ)=τ_V(σ). Speyer's result on tropical linear spaces then identifies the tropicalization of L(V*) with the tropical linear space defined by these valuations, proving τ_V∈Gr(k,n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A sufficient criterion for a tropical Plücker vector π∈Dr(k,n) to lie in Gr(k,n) is the following. For any vertex e_σ of ∆(k,n), form the analogue Φ^σ(π) of the matrix of Plücker coordinates adjacent to e_σ, and form τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)} using the corresponding coordinate system. If τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)}=π for some σ, then π∈Gr(k,n). The paper notes that this criterion is not necessary.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)}=π, then π is in the image of one of the τ maps after relabelling coordinates so that σ plays the role of [k]. Proposition 12 applies to every such relabelled τ image, so π lies in the tropical Grassmannian. Non-necessity is shown by examples in Gr(3,6): the generic tropical plane types EEEE and EEFF(a) are in the tropical Grassmannian but cannot be obtained by this construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n>k>1, the map τ induces a piecewise-linear embedding of the secondary fan of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} into the Dressian Dr(k,n). The image of τ is contained, as a set, in the tropical Grassmannian Gr(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 7 shows that if two lifting functions V induce different regular subdivisions of the product of simplices, their images τ_V induce different regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex, because the subdivision of the vertex figure at e_[k] recovers the original subdivision. Thus τ embeds the secondary fan piecewise-linearly into the Dressian fan. Lemma 1 supplies injectivity on the underlying vector spaces, while Theorem 7 controls the fan cones. Proposition 12 gives the inclusion of the image in Gr(k,n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)} be a tropically rigid point configuration, meaning that as a lifting function on ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} it induces a coarsest nontrivial subdivision. Let w be any column of V, and let V'=(V|w)∈R^{k×(n+1−k)} be obtained by duplicating that column. Then V' is also tropically rigid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let Γ and Γ' be the subdivisions induced by V and V' on ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} and ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k}, respectively. If w is the i-th column of V, then each maximal cell C of Γ gives a maximal cell of Γ' by taking the old vertices e_l+e_m in C and adding the new vertices e_l+e_{n+1} exactly for those e_l+e_i in C. Consequently T(Γ) and T(Γ') are isomorphic. The facet of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k} defined by x_{n+1}=0 is naturally ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, and the induced subdivision on it is Γ. Since Γ is coarsest and the tight-spans agree, Proposition 5(ii) implies Γ' is coarsest. Thus V' is tropically rigid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 21 (Feichtner-Sturmfels)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on n elements, and let c(M) be the number of connected components of M, where two elements are equivalent if they lie in a common circuit. Then the dimension of the matroid polytope of M equals n−c(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result. The underlying reason is that each connected component C of M imposes one independent affine equation sum_{i∈C} x_i=rank(M|C) on the base polytope, in addition to the decomposition of M as a direct sum over components. Within a connected component, the edge directions e_i−e_j span a codimension-one subspace. Summing over components gives dimension sum_C (|C|−1)=n−c(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ be a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n). Then the tight-span T(Σ) has dimension at most k−1. More precisely, if F is an interior cell of Σ corresponding to a loop-free rank-k matroid M on [n], then the codimension of F in ∆(k,n) is c(M)−1, where c(M) is the number of connected components of M, and c(M)≤k. If F has codimension k−1, then M has exactly k connected components C_1,...,C_k and M is the direct product of k rank-1 matroids, so its bases are exactly the sets containing one element from each C_i. If F has codimension 1, then M has two connected components C_1,C_2; F lies in the hyperplane sum_{i∈C_1} x_i=rank(M|C_1)=l, and M is the product of a rank-l matroid and a rank-(k−l) matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: An interior cell corresponds to a rank-k matroid with no loops, because being interior to the hypersimplex means no coordinate is forced to be 0. A loop-free matroid has only nontrivial connected components, and each nontrivial component has rank at least 1. Since the ranks of connected components add to k, the number c(M) of connected components is at most k. By Proposition 21, dim F=n−c(M), while dim ∆(k,n)=n−1, so codim F=c(M)−1≤k−1. Dualizing interior cells gives the dimension bound for the tight-span. If codim F=k−1, then c(M)=k and the k positive component ranks sum to k, so each component has rank 1; bases choose exactly one element from each component. If codim F=1, then c(M)=2, giving the stated decomposition into the restrictions on the two components and the displayed affine hyperplane equation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23 and hypersimplex split count
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every split of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), i.e. every regular matroid subdivision with exactly two maximal cells, gives a ray of the Dressian Dr(k,n). For n≥2k, the total number of splits of ∆(k,n) is (k−1)(2^{n−1}−(n+1)) − sum_{i=2}^{k−1} (k−i) binom(n,i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A split has precisely two maximal cells and therefore cannot be coarsened nontrivially except to the trivial subdivision. If it is a regular matroid subdivision, its secondary cone is a one-dimensional cone modulo lineality, hence a ray of Dr(k,n). The enumeration formula is cited from Herrmann-Joswig on splitting polytopes. The paper also notes that the split rays can be generated by the tropical point configurations of Example 15 together with the duplication operation of Proposition 18.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 24 and 25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)} be a tropically rigid point configuration, i.e. its columns in the tropical torus T^{k−1} induce a coarsest nontrivial regular subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Then τ_V gives a ray of Dr(k,n), also a ray of Gr(k,n), and the tight-span of the corresponding matroid subdivision equals the tropical complex of V. Consequently, for n≥2k there are at least T(n−k,k) combinatorially distinct rays of Dr(k,n) and Gr(k,n), where T(m,k) is the number of partitions of m into k positive parts.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion follows from Corollary 14 and Theorem 7: a coarsest subdivision of the product maps to a coarsest matroid subdivision of the hypersimplex with the same tight-span, so the image cone is a ray; Proposition 12 places it in Gr(k,n). For the lower bound, use the tropically rigid configuration from Example 16 whose tropical complex is a (k−1)-simplex. Distribute n−2k additional duplicate points among the k vertices of this simplex. Proposition 18 preserves tropical rigidity under each duplication. Up to the Sym(k) action on the k vertices, the resulting configurations are indexed by partitions of n−k into k positive parts, giving at least T(n−k,k) combinatorially distinct rays.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ be a coarsest matroid subdivision of ∆(3,n). Then its tight-span T(Σ) is either a line segment or is pure two-dimensional.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 22, tight-spans of matroid subdivisions of ∆(3,n) have dimension at most 2. If Σ is a split, its tight-span is a line segment. If Σ is not a split, the paper invokes Herrmann's result that for a coarsest subdivision all maximal faces of the tight-span have dimension at least 2. Combining this lower bound with the upper bound from Lemma 22 forces T(Σ) to be pure two-dimensional.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 local shape observation for two-dimensional tight-span cells
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid subdivision of ∆(3,n), let C be a codimension-2 interior cell and let S be the dual two-dimensional cell in the tight-span. The matroid corresponding to C has three connected components α,β,γ partitioning [n], and its bases are triples with one element from each component. Every codimension-1 cell containing C lies in one of the three hyperplanes H_α, H_β, H_γ associated with these components, so S has at most six sides. In the regular case, the edges of S are parallel in R^n/R(1,...,1) to the vectors sum_{i∈α} e_i, sum_{i∈β} e_i, and sum_{i∈γ} e_i, whose sum is zero; hence S is one of a triangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, pentagon, or hexagon. The paper notes that for any rank k these are the only possible two-dimensional faces in the tight-span of a tropical linear space, citing Speyer.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 22 applied to rank 3 and codimension 2, the corresponding matroid must have three connected components, each of rank 1. Hence its bases choose one element from each component α,β,γ. A neighboring codimension-1 cell is obtained by moving away from C across one of the component hyperplanes; each of the three hyperplanes can contribute at most two sides, one on each side of C, so the dual polygon S has at most six edges. In a regular subdivision, duality identifies edge directions of S with normals to the hyperplanes H_α,H_β,H_γ. In the quotient by the all-ones vector these three normals sum to zero. A convex polygon whose edges use only three directions with this relation can have only the five listed combinatorial shapes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 28 and 29
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the tight-span of a regular matroid subdivision, coarsening the subdivision contracts edges of the tight-span. For a triangular two-dimensional cell, if one edge of the triangle collapses, then all edges of that triangle collapse. For a parallelogram two-dimensional cell, if one edge collapses, then the opposite edge also collapses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records these as observations after the local shape analysis. Coarsening merges adjacent maximal cells of the subdivision, and in the dual tight-span this is represented by contracting the corresponding edge. For a triangular tight-span cell, the three edge directions come from the three component hyperplanes and are tied by the relation that their primitive direction vectors sum to zero; the local coarsening compatible with the matroid-subdivision structure cannot set just one boundary length to zero, so one collapse forces the whole triangular cell to disappear. For a parallelogram, opposite edges represent the same local direction and length parameter in the dual cell; collapsing one forces the opposite parallel edge to collapse as well. The paper uses these collapse rules to certify that certain two-dimensional tight-spans cannot be nontrivially coarsened.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Dressian Dr(3,8) has 15,470 rays, in twelve Sym(8)-orbits. The classes are: four split orbits with sizes 28, 56, 56, and 70; two 3-split orbits with sizes 420 and 560; three orbits whose tight-span is two triangles joined by an edge, with sizes 840, 1,260, and 1,680; two orbits coming from tropically rigid five-point configurations in T^2 with three or four connected triangles, each of size 5,040; and one nonplanar tight-span orbit of size 420, whose tight-span is a pure two-dimensional simplicial complex of three triangles sharing a common edge. For the last orbit, an explicit 0/1 ray of length binom(8,3)=56 takes value 0 on the following 30 triples and value 1 on the remaining 26 triples: 123, 124, 126, 127, 128, 134, 136, 137, 138, 234, 235, 236, 237, 238, 245, 247, 248, 256, 257, 258, 267, 268, 345, 347, 348, 356, 357, 358, 367, 368. These 30 triples form the bases of a matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The example combines known classifications and the paper's computations. The four split orbits are counted using the hypersimplex split classification. The two 3-split orbits come from Herrmann's classification of 3-splits. The next five orbits are obtained from the tropically rigid point configurations constructed earlier in the paper, using Corollary 24 to turn them into Dressian rays and Sym(8) orbit calculations for counts. The remaining nonplanar class is detected by the full computation of Dr(3,8); the paper gives an explicit 0/1 representative and its tight-span. The collapse rules of Propositions 28 and 29 are used in the surrounding discussion to show that such tight-span complexes can be coarsest.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 31
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Modulo the natural lineality space convention used throughout the paper, Dr(3,8) is a non-pure, non-simplicial, nine-dimensional polyhedral fan. Its f-vector is (1; 15,470; 642,677; 8,892,898; 57,394,505; 194,258,750; 353,149,650; 324,404,880; 117,594,645; 113,400). Modulo the natural Sym(8)-symmetry, the f-vector is (1; 12; 155; 1,149; 5,013; 12,737; 18,802; 14,727; 4,788; 14). There are 116,962,265 maximal cones: 113,400 of dimension 9 and 116,848,865 of dimension 8. Up to symmetry, there are 4,748 maximal cones: 14 of dimension 9 and 4,734 of dimension 8.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is computational and is implemented in polymake. A cone of Dr(k,n) is specified by choosing, for each 3-term Plücker relation, which two of the three associated linear forms are equal and dominate the third in the paper's cone convention. Each such relation corresponds to an octahedral 3-face of ∆(k,n), and the three choices correspond to the three octahedral splits. Thus a matroid subdivision can be encoded by a sequence saying, for every octahedral face, whether and how it is split. The naive backtracking algorithm iterates through octahedra, adds one of the three split inequalities/equalities, intersects the current cone, and backtracks when the dimension is too small. The improved algorithm exploits the boundary: each facet of ∆(k,n) is a hypersimplex of type ∆(k−1,n) or ∆(k,n−1), and a partial octahedral-split sequence must restrict on each ∆(k,n−1) boundary face to a known matroid subdivision. This prunes the search using the already computed Dr(k,n−1). To compute Dr(3,8), the authors additionally fix some initial Plücker choices to ensure at least one representative per Sym(8)-orbit, then compute orbit representatives and orbit sizes. The f-vector is obtained by computing rays of representative maximal cones, storing all rays by orbit, representing faces by ray-index sets, and then computing orbit representatives of faces in each dimension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 32
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There are 4,748 distinct combinatorial types of generic tropical planes in T^6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Generic tropical planes correspond to maximal cones of Dr(3,8) up to the Sym(8) action. Theorem 31 computes exactly 4,748 maximal-cone orbits, giving the stated number of combinatorial types.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 33
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: All rays of Dr(3,8) are rays of the tropical Grassmannian Gr(3,8).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 12, all ray orbits arising from tropically rigid point configurations lie in the tropical Grassmannian. This covers eleven of the twelve ray orbits described in Example 30. For the remaining nonplanar ray, the authors perform an explicit Macaulay2 computation: they compute the initial ideal of the Plücker ideal with respect to the 0/1 weight vector representing that ray and verify that the initial ideal contains no monomial. The computation is done over the polynomial ring with integer coefficients; the integral Gröbner basis of the initial ideal has only coefficients ±1, so the conclusion holds in arbitrary characteristic.

proof_techniques.name: Dressian as regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the equivalence between finite tropical Plücker vectors, regular matroid subdivisions of ∆(k,n), and subdivisions whose 1-cells are hypersimplex edges. This turns questions about tropical linear spaces into finite polyhedral/combinatorial questions about cells, coarsenings, octahedral 3-faces, and matroid polytopes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset question about tropical linear spaces, this dictionary suggests replacing inclusion/order questions by an explicit combinatorial model whenever the relevant relative Dressian has a matroid-subdivision description. It also gives computable invariants, such as cone dimension, coarsening structure, and tight-span type, that an order-reversing involution would have to preserve or exchange appropriately.

proof_techniques.name: Vertex-figure embedding from products of simplices
proof_techniques.description: A central construction fixes a vertex e_[k] of ∆(k,n), identifies its vertex figure with ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, and cones any subdivision Γ of this product into a subdivision Σ of the hypersimplex whose maximal cells all contain e_[k]. In the regular case, the construction is realized by the explicit tropical-minor map τ from a matrix V to a Plücker vector τ_V. The subdivision on the vertex figure recovers Γ, and the tight-span is preserved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to build controlled families inside a Dressian. In the original problem, one could try to construct candidate counterexamples by embedding secondary-fan pieces into a relative Dressian and checking whether any proposed order-reversing involution can act compatibly on these embedded subposets.

proof_techniques.name: Tight-span control and coarseness transfer
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats the tight-span as the dual containment poset of interior cells of a subdivision. Proposition 5 shows that if a subpolytope sees the same tight-span as the whole subdivision, then coarseness of the induced subdivision on the subpolytope forces coarseness of the whole subdivision. Theorem 7 then uses this to transfer tight-spans from product-of-simplices subdivisions to hypersimplex matroid subdivisions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Tight-spans provide small combinatorial certificates for rays and for non-coarsenability. If an order-reversing involution on a Dressian would have to send certain minimal or maximal elements to impossible tight-span types, this technique can expose the obstruction.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness from a prescribed vertex figure
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 6 proves that if every maximal cell of a subdivision contains a vertex v, then the subdivision is uniquely determined by its restriction to the vertex figure at v, provided the convex hull of the neighbors is codimension one. This uniqueness underlies the embedding theorem and prevents ambiguity in lifting local subdivisions to hypersimplex subdivisions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When studying extensions of a flat-lattice involution, one may need to know whether local data around an embedded flat determines a tropical linear space. This result gives a template: impose that all maximal cells contain a distinguished vertex/flat and then compare the induced vertex-figure subdivision.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical rigidity as a ray-construction principle
proof_techniques.description: A point configuration V in a tropical torus is called tropically rigid when its lifting subdivision of a product of simplices is coarsest. Through τ, such a configuration yields a ray of Dr(k,n), and Proposition 18 shows that duplicating points preserves tropical rigidity. This produces large explicit families of Dressian rays with controlled tight-spans.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rays are extreme elements of the fan structure and often easiest places to test whether a global order-reversing map can exist. The duplication method can produce families with asymmetric multiplicity data that may be hard for a proposed involution to match.

proof_techniques.name: Connected-component dimension bounds for matroid cells
proof_techniques.description: Using the formula dim P(M)=n−c(M), the paper bounds tight-span dimensions by k−1 for subdivisions of ∆(k,n). It also identifies the matroid structure of codimension-one and maximal-codimension interior cells in terms of direct sums over connected components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Such dimension and component-count restrictions give invariants of cells/tropical spaces. In a relative Dressian, analogous component bounds could constrain what an order-reversing involution can do to strata corresponding to contractions, restrictions, or flat-lattice elements.

proof_techniques.name: Local polygon-shape and edge-collapse rules
proof_techniques.description: For rank 3, the dual two-dimensional tight-span cells have only a few possible shapes: triangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, pentagon, or hexagon. The paper uses collapse rules for triangles and parallelograms to show that certain tight-spans cannot be nontrivially coarsened, hence define rays.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: These local rules are useful for proving extremality without full computation. Similar local collapse constraints may produce combinatorial invariants incompatible with a desired order-reversing symmetry.

proof_techniques.name: Octahedral-split encoding of Dressian cones
proof_techniques.description: Every 3-term Plücker relation corresponds to an octahedral 3-face of the hypersimplex, and choosing a cone of the Dressian amounts to choosing compatible octahedral splits. The paper encodes subdivisions as sequences of octahedral split choices and uses boundary restrictions to known smaller Dressians for efficient backtracking.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete computational route for small cases of any Dressian-like object governed by Plücker-type relations. It can be adapted to search for or rule out poset anti-automorphisms by enumerating cones/elements and checking order relations.

proof_techniques.name: Symmetry orbit reduction
proof_techniques.description: The computations exploit the Sym(n)-action on ∆(k,n), Dr(k,n), and vertex figures. Rather than enumerating all objects naively, the paper computes representatives and orbit sizes, then reconstructs f-vectors and face data modulo symmetry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matroid M has automorphisms or a flat-lattice involution, orbit reduction can make it feasible to test whether the involution extends to larger Dressian strata, and to identify invariants distinguishing possible images.

proof_techniques.name: Realizability check by monomial-free initial ideals
proof_techniques.description: To verify that a Dressian ray lies in the tropical Grassmannian, the paper computes the initial ideal of the Plücker ideal with respect to the ray weight vector and checks that it contains no monomial. Performing the computation over Z and observing only coefficients ±1 gives characteristic-independent validity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an attempted involution is expected to interact with realizable versus non-realizable tropical linear spaces, initial-ideal tests provide a way to distinguish those subsets in explicit examples.

other_useful_info: The map τ is not linear; Remark 2 gives an explicit k=2,n=4 example where τ_{V+W} differs from τ_V+τ_W. The τ construction is not surjective: Example 10 explains that for k=2 it produces caterpillar trees from tropical line segments, whereas Dr(2,n)=Gr(2,n) contains all phylogenetic tree types; for n=6 the snowflake tree is not obtained from τ. Example 20 records that Dr(2,5), as a spherical one-dimensional complex, is the Petersen graph, also the graph complement of the vertex-edge graph of ∆(2,5), and contains the embedded hexagon coming from the secondary fan of ∆_1×∆_2. Example 11 states that for Gr(3,6)=Dr(3,6) there are seven combinatorial types of finest matroid subdivisions; five arise from τ applied to configurations of three points in T^2, while the types EEEE and EEFF(a) do not. The paper's open questions include whether all coarsest matroid subdivisions of ∆(3,n) with planar tight-spans come from tropical point configurations, what the maximal spread of a ray of Dr(k,n) is, whether all rays of Dr(k,n) are always rays of Gr(k,n), and computational feasibility questions for Gr(3,8) and Dr(4,8). The authors provide computed Dr(3,8) data at http://svenherrmann.net/DR38/dr38.html. Cited background especially relevant for porting arguments includes Speyer, Tropical linear spaces; Speyer-Sturmfels, The tropical Grassmannian; Feichtner-Sturmfels, Matroid polytopes, nested sets and Bergman fans; Herrmann-Joswig, Splitting polytopes; and Herrmann, On the facets of the secondary polytope.
why_relevant: Studies Dressians as parameter spaces of tropical linear spaces and develops their secondary-fan structure. Its ray and subdivision methods are useful for constructing explicit examples or counterexamples to poset-duality claims.

title: Tropical Incidence Relations, Polytopes, and Concordant Matroids
overall_summary: The paper defines flag Dressians as moduli spaces of flags of tropical linear spaces and proves tropical incidence relations characterizing when one tropical linear space is contained in another. The central method is tropical duality: complementing Plücker indices gives an inclusion-reversing bijection on tropical linear spaces, and containment X⊂Y is converted into membership of dual tropical points in the hyperplanes defining X^⊥. For two-step flags, the paper also gives a polyhedral reformulation: tropical Plücker plus incidence relations are equivalent to the induced subdivision of ∆(p,q;n)=Conv(∆(p,n)∪∆(q,n)) having the same one-skeleton as ∆(p,q;n), with each incidence relation modeled locally by a crosspolytope. For the original problem, the most directly relevant results are the order-reversing tropical duality on ambient tropical linear spaces and the incidence equations that allow inclusion in a tropical linear space to be checked in Plücker coordinates; the later matroid-concordance discussion may help relate flags of tropical spaces to matroid quotients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (tropical duality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a tropical linear space in R^n/(1,...,1) with tropical Plücker coordinates x_I indexed by d-subsets I⊂[n]. For each (n−d)-subset J⊂[n], define x^⊥_J = x_{[n]−J}. Then the numbers x^⊥_J are tropical Plücker coordinates of a tropical linear space X^⊥, called the tropical dual linear space or orthogonal complement of X. Moreover, the map X↦X^⊥ is a bijection on tropical linear spaces, sends rank d spaces to rank n−d spaces, is involutive, and reverses inclusion: if X⊂Y then Y^⊥⊂X^⊥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the remarks preceding Speyer, Proposition 2.12, for this fact. The Plücker-coordinate part is obtained by observing that the tropical Plücker relations are invariant under replacing every index set by its complement in [n]. Applying the construction twice returns the original coordinates. The inclusion reversal is the tropical analogue of classical orthogonal-complement duality and is used in the paper by replacing a containment X⊂H of a tropical linear space in a tropical hyperplane with the dual containment H^⊥⊂X^⊥.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (tropical incidence relations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X and Y be tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) with Plücker coordinates X_I for p-subsets I⊂[n] and Y_J for q-subsets J⊂[n], with p≤q; in the paper's convention, X and Y have tropical dimensions p−1 and q−1. Then X⊂Y if and only if, for every S,T⊂[n] with |S|=p−1 and |T|=q+1, the tropical incidence relation Trop(∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}} Y_{T−{i}}) holds, equivalently the minimum of the numbers X_{S∪{i}}+Y_{T−{i}} for i∈T−S is attained at least twice. These relations are the tropicalizations of the classical incidence polynomials ∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the definition of a tropical linear space from its Plücker coordinates, Y is the intersection over all (q+1)-subsets T⊂[n] of the tropical hyperplanes H_T = Trop(∑_{j∈T} Y_{T−{j}} W_j). Hence X⊂Y is equivalent to X⊂H_T for every such T. Applying Lemma 1, this is equivalent to H_T^⊥⊂X^⊥ for every T. The dual X^⊥ is itself an intersection of tropical hyperplanes indexed by subsets I'⊂[n] of size n−p+1, with equations Trop(∑_{r} X^⊥_{I'−{i'_r}} W_{i'_r}). The dual H_T^⊥ is represented by a tropical point supported on T with coordinates Y_{T−{i}} for i∈T, so requiring this point to lie in each defining hyperplane of X^⊥ gives the tropical condition Trop(∑_{i∈T∩I'} X^⊥_{I'−{i}} Y_{T−{i}}). Now set S=[n]−I'. Then |S|=p−1, T∩I'=T−S, and X^⊥_{I'−{i}}=X_{[n]−(I'−{i})}=X_{S∪{i}}, which gives exactly Trop(∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}}). Reversing these implications proves the converse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Consequence of Theorem 1 (flag Dressian as a tropical prevariety)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed integers d1<d2<...<ds, the flag Dressian FD(d1,d2,...,ds;n) is the set of tuples (X1,...,Xs) of tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) such that Xi⊂Xi+1 for all i. Set-theoretically, membership in FD(d1,...,ds;n) is equivalent to imposing the tropical Plücker relations on the Plücker coordinates of each Xi together with the tropical incidence relations of Theorem 1 for each consecutive containment Xi⊂Xi+1. Thus the flag Dressian is an intersection of tropical hypersurfaces, called a tropical prevariety in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition of a tropical linear space supplies the tropical Plücker relations for each component Xi. Theorem 1 supplies necessary and sufficient tropical equations for each inclusion Xi⊂Xi+1. Imposing these conditions for all adjacent pairs in the flag is equivalent to the full chain of containments, so their common solution set is exactly the flag Dressian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Speyer proposition quoted in the paper (Proposition 1 in the introduction and Proposition 5 in Section 4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p_I be real weights assigned to the vertices e_I of the hypersimplex ∆(d,n), where I ranges over d-subsets of [n], and let D be the lower subdivision of ∆(d,n) induced by these weights. The paper quotes Speyer's equivalence: (1) the numbers p_I are tropical Plücker coordinates; (2) the one-skeletons of D and ∆(d,n) are the same; (3) every face or cell of D is matroidal, meaning its vertices, viewed as d-subsets of [n], are the bases of a matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No proof is given in this paper; it is cited from Speyer, Tropical linear spaces. Haque uses this equivalence as the model for the analogous incidence statement in Theorem 2 and as the source of the matroidal-cell condition for ordinary tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2 (subdivisions of the crosspolytope P_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n=Conv({±e_i : i∈[n]}). Given a function ψ on the vertices of P_n, define ψ_i^+=ψ(e_i), ψ_i^−=ψ(−e_i), and w_i=ψ_i^+ + ψ_i^−. After relabeling so that w_1≤w_2≤...≤w_n, the induced lower subdivision contains the segment between the opposite vertices e_1 and −e_1 as an edge if and only if w_1<w_2. Moreover, if w_1=w_2, then the subdivision introduces no new edges.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose the affine hyperplane in R^{n+1} through the lifted vertices (−e_i,ψ(−e_i)) for all i and (e_1,ψ(e_1)). Let L be the corresponding affine linear function on R^n and replace ψ by ψ'=ψ−L; this does not change the induced subdivision. By construction ψ'(−e_i)=0 for all i and ψ'(e_1)=0, while ψ'(e_i)≥0 for all i, with equality exactly when w_i=w_1. Thus the lower face at height zero contains all −e_i and exactly those e_j with w_j=w_1. If w_1 is strictly smaller than all other w_i, the opposite pair e_1,−e_1 appears as an edge in the subdivision. If the minimum is attained at least twice, the lower faces have no such new opposite edge, and the proposition concludes that no new edges are introduced.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (incidence polytopes are faces of ∆(p,q;n), as stated)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ∆(d,n)=Conv(e_I : I⊂[n], |I|=d), let ∆=∆(p,q;n)=Conv(∆(p,n)∪∆(q,n)), and write e_A=∑_{a∈A}e_a. For S,T⊂[n] with |S|=p−1 and |T|=q+1, define ∆_{S,T}=Conv(e_{S∪{i}}, e_{T−{i}} : i∈T−S). The paper states that ∆_{S,T} is a face of ∆.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof considers the linear functional L(x)=∑_{i∈S}x_i−∑_{j∉T}x_j. On coordinate basis vectors, L(e_j)=1 for j∈S∩T, L(e_j)=−1 for j∉S∪T, and L(e_j)=0 otherwise. Hence L(e_I)≤|S∩T| for vertices e_I of ∆, and equality occurs only when S∩T⊂I⊂S∪T. The vertices e_{S∪{i}} and e_{T−{i}} with i∈T−S all attain this value. The paper then identifies these as exactly the vertices of ∆ attaining the maximum and concludes that they form an exposed face. This identification is immediate in the common case S⊂T; the paper states the proposition for S,T of the displayed sizes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4 (incidence faces are affinely crosspolytopal)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For S,T⊂[n] with |S|=p−1 and |T|=q+1, the polytope ∆_{S,T}=Conv(e_{S∪{i}}, e_{T−{i}} : i∈T−S) is affinely equivalent to the crosspolytope P_{|T−S|}=Conv({±e_i : i∈T−S}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First treat the case S=∅ and T=[m]. Then ∆_{∅,[m]} has opposite vertices e_i and e_{[m]−{i}}. The paper gives an affine map x↦Ax+b with A(e_i)=e_i−(1/(m−2))∑_{j∈[m]}e_j and b=(1/(m−2))∑_{j∈[m]}e_j; this sends e_i to e_i and e_{[m]−{i}} to −e_i. For general S,T, first apply the affine map that kills the coordinates indexed by S and keeps the other coordinates: on vertices it sends e_{S∪{i}} to e_i and e_{T−{i}} to e_{(T−S)−{i}}. This identifies ∆_{S,T} with the S=∅ case for the ground set T−S, and composing with the previous affine map gives the desired equivalence to P_{|T−S|}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1 (one incidence relation equals no new edge on the corresponding crosspolytope)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be the subdivision of ∆_{S,T} induced by weights x_I on p-subset vertices and y_J on q-subset vertices. Then D and ∆_{S,T} have the same one-skeleton if and only if the tropical incidence relation Trop(∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}}) holds, equivalently the minimum of x_{S∪{i}}+y_{T−{i}} over i∈T−S is attained at least twice.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 4 to identify ∆_{S,T} affinely with the crosspolytope P_{|T−S|}. Under this identification, each pair of vertices e_{S∪{i}} and e_{T−{i}} becomes an opposite pair e_j and −e_j. The quantity w_j of Proposition 2 is exactly x_{S∪{i}}+y_{T−{i}}, which is the tropical weight of the corresponding term in the incidence relation. Proposition 2 says that a new opposite edge is introduced precisely when this w_j is the unique minimum. Therefore the subdivision has no new edge, equivalently the same one-skeleton, precisely when the minimum in the incidence relation is not unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 (mixed edges of ∆(p,q;n))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I,J⊂[n] with |I|=p and |J|=q, and let ∆=∆(p,q;n)=Conv(∆(p,n)∪∆(q,n)). The following are equivalent: (1) the segment e_Ie_J is an edge of ∆; (2) the monomial X_IY_J appears in no tropical incidence relation Trop(∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}}); (3) I⊂J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), the paper proves the contrapositive. If X_IY_J appears in the incidence relation for S,T, then I=S∪{i} and J=T−{i} for some i∈T−S. By Proposition 4 these two vertices are opposite vertices in the crosspolytope ∆_{S,T}, hence they are not joined by an edge of ∆_{S,T}; since ∆_{S,T} is a face of ∆ by Proposition 3, they cannot form an edge of ∆. For (2) implies (3), if I is not contained in J, choose r∈I−J, set S=I−{r} and T=J∪{r}; then X_IY_J is a term in the incidence relation indexed by S,T. For (3) implies (1), define L(x)=∑_{i∈I}x_i−∑_{j∉J}x_j. On vertices of ∆ one has L≤p, and equality occurs only for the p-vertex e_I and the q-vertex e_J when I⊂J. Thus the hyperplane L=p exposes exactly the segment e_Ie_J, making it an edge.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (polyhedral characterization of tropical incidence prevarieties)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x_I for |I|=p and y_J for |J|=q be real weights on the vertices of ∆(p,n) and ∆(q,n), and let D be the lower subdivision of ∆(p,q;n)=Conv(∆(p,n)∪∆(q,n)) induced by these weights. The following are equivalent: (1) the collections x_I and y_J satisfy the tropical Plücker relations of ranks p and q, respectively, and together satisfy all tropical incidence relations Trop(∑_{i∈T−S} X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}}); (2) the subdivision D and the polytope ∆(p,q;n) have the same one-skeleton.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The intended argument combines Speyer's hypersimplex criterion with Corollary 1 and Lemma 2. If the tropical Plücker relations hold for x and y separately, then by Speyer's proposition the restrictions of D to ∆(p,n) and ∆(q,n) introduce no new pure p-p or q-q edges. Suppose a mixed edge e_Ie_J appears in D but is not an edge of ∆(p,q;n). By Lemma 2, this means I is not contained in J, and the monomial X_IY_J occurs in some incidence relation indexed by S,T. The corresponding polytope ∆_{S,T} contains e_I and e_J as an opposite pair. Corollary 1 says that satisfaction of the incidence relation prevents such a new opposite edge in the restricted subdivision, a contradiction. Conversely, if D and ∆ have the same one-skeleton, then the restrictions to the two hypersimplices have the same one-skeleton, so Speyer's proposition gives the two sets of tropical Plücker relations. Also, for every S,T, the restriction of D to ∆_{S,T} has the same one-skeleton as ∆_{S,T}, so Corollary 1 gives the corresponding tropical incidence relation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5 / White Proposition 7.4.7(1) quoted (matroid quotient or concordance)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and M' be matroids on the same ground set E, with base sets B(M) and B(M'). The paper uses the following basis criterion from White as the definition of M' being a quotient of M, or equivalently M' and M being concordant: for every B∈B(M) and every i∈E−B, there exists B'∈B(M') with B'⊂B such that {j : (B∪{i})−{j}∈B(M)} contains {j : (B'∪{i})−{j}∈B(M')}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this criterion; it cites White, Theory of matroids, Proposition 7.4.7. The displayed containment says that whenever an element i can be exchanged into the smaller-rank basis B' of M' by removing j, the same exchange is available from the containing basis B of M. The paper uses this as the matroidal relation expected to model containment of the corresponding tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1 quoted from White (vector matroid quotients)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For vector matroids, matroid quotients correspond to quotient vector spaces. If M is represented by a set V' of vectors in a vector space V, then a quotient M' is represented by the images {v+W : v∈V'} in V/W for some subspace W. In particular, every vectorial nullity-k quotient M→M' can be realized by choosing a matrix representation of M that becomes a matrix representation of M' after deleting its first k rows.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as White, Proposition 7.4.8(2), without proof. The conceptual reason is that projecting a vector configuration to a quotient vector space can only add linear dependencies, which is exactly the strong-map or quotient behavior for matroids; deleting rows in a matrix representation realizes such a projection in coordinates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Observation in Section 4 (realizable flags have concordant cells)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of Puiseaux series in the variable t. Suppose V⊂W⊂K^n are vector spaces with dim V=p and dim W=q, and let X_I and Y_J be the tropical Plücker coordinates of Trop(V) and Trop(W). Let D be the subdivision of ∆(p,q;n) induced by these coordinates. Then every cell Q of D is a concordant polytope: if F_p and F_q are the p- and q-dimensional hypersimplex parts of Q, then the matroids whose bases are the p-subsets and q-subsets labeling the vertices of F_p and F_q are concordant in the sense of the matroid quotient relation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a cell Q of D. By the definition of a regular subdivision, there is a linear functional L such that L(e_I)=X_I for p-vertices e_I in Q and L(e_I)>X_I for p-vertices outside Q, and similarly L(e_J)=Y_J on q-vertices in Q and L(e_J)>Y_J outside Q. Let a_R=L(e_R). Because L is linear, these a_R are additive in R, so one can diagonally rescale coordinates over K by sending e_R to t^{−a_R}e_R on exterior powers. Replacing V and W by their rescaled versions shifts the tropical Plücker coordinates to X_I−L(e_I) and Y_J−L(e_J), which are zero exactly on the vertices of Q and positive outside. Reducing the rescaled vector spaces modulo t gives vector spaces over the residue field whose nonzero Plücker coordinates are precisely the vertices in Q. For a p-plane V, the Ith Plücker coordinate is nonzero exactly when the coordinate vectors indexed by [n]−I form a basis of K^n/V, and similarly for W. Since V⊂W, there is a quotient map K^n/V→K^n/W, so the matroid from the complements of the q-vertices is a quotient of the matroid from the complements of the p-vertices. Taking complements of bases gives concordance for the matroids attached to the p- and q-vertices of Q.

proof_techniques.name: Complement-index tropical duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the operation x_I↦x_{[n]−I} on Plücker coordinates to pass from a tropical linear space X to its tropical orthogonal complement X^⊥. This operation is involutive, sends rank d to rank n−d, and reverses inclusion of tropical linear spaces. It is the tropical replacement for classical orthogonal complement duality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the full ambient Dressian, this already gives an order-reversing involution on all tropical linear spaces. In the relative Dressian problem, the key test would be whether an analogous or modified duality preserves the subposet of tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M) and agrees with the prescribed flat involution.

proof_techniques.name: Deriving incidence by dualizing containment in hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: To prove the tropical incidence relations, the paper writes Y as the intersection of its defining tropical hyperplanes H_T. The containment X⊂Y is equivalent to X⊂H_T for every T; tropical duality turns this into H_T^⊥⊂X^⊥. Substituting the Plücker coordinates of the dual point H_T^⊥ into the hyperplane equations defining X^⊥ produces the explicit relations min_i(X_{S∪{i}}+Y_{T−{i}}) attained at least twice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete way to convert inclusions in a poset of tropical linear spaces into coordinate equations. A proposed order-reversing map on a relative Dressian could be checked by verifying that it transforms these incidence equations for X⊂Y into the corresponding equations for Φ(Y)⊂Φ(X).

proof_techniques.name: Flag Dressian as a Plücker-incidence prevariety
proof_techniques.description: The flag Dressian is described set-theoretically by imposing ordinary tropical Plücker relations on each component and tropical incidence relations on consecutive components. This description does not require realizability of the tropical linear spaces and is therefore genuinely Dressian-level rather than Grassmannian-level.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian Dr(M) can be approached similarly as a moduli/poset cut out by containment conditions. The paper's equations suggest studying automorphisms or anti-automorphisms of Dr(M) through their action on Plücker data and incidence relations.

proof_techniques.name: Crosspolytope local model for one incidence relation
proof_techniques.description: For each incidence relation indexed by S,T, the paper associates a polytope ∆_{S,T} whose vertices are the monomials in that relation. This polytope is affinely equivalent to a crosspolytope, with each term X_{S∪{i}}Y_{T−{i}} corresponding to a pair of opposite vertices. The tropical incidence condition is exactly the assertion that the minimum of the sums of weights on opposite pairs is not unique, which is equivalent to the induced subdivision not creating a new opposite edge.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If containment in Trop(M) or between elements of Dr(M) is hard to check globally, this technique suggests reducing it to small local crosspolytope tests. Such local tests may be useful for proving that a candidate involution preserves or reverses containment.

proof_techniques.name: One-skeleton criterion for two-step tropical flags
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2 packages the tropical Plücker relations for two spaces and all incidence relations between them into a single regular-subdivision condition: the induced subdivision of ∆(p,q;n)=Conv(∆(p,n)∪∆(q,n)) has the same one-skeleton as the original polytope. Mixed edges of ∆ correspond exactly to subset containments I⊂J between p- and q-subsets, while forbidden mixed edges correspond to monomials appearing in incidence relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution extending a flat involution might be studied combinatorially by asking whether it induces a symmetry or anti-symmetry of the relevant subdivision/edge data. The subset-containment characterization of mixed edges is particularly close to lattice-order behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction to the case p+q≤n by duality
proof_techniques.description: In the polytope section, the paper assumes p+q≤n, explaining that when p+q≥n one can dualize the incidence variety to the type (n−q,n−p), where the sum of the new ranks is at most n. The number of equations and the number of terms in the equations are preserved under this dualization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful dimension-reversal normalization. In a relative setting, it suggests that any construction of Φ should be compatible with swapping a subspace dimension with its complementary dimension.

proof_techniques.name: Initial degeneration of realizable flags to matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable flag V⊂W over Puiseaux series, each cell of the induced subdivision can be isolated by a linear functional. Diagonal rescaling by the corresponding weights and reduction modulo t turns the cell into the support of nonzero Plücker coordinates of an initial flag over the residue field. The inclusion of vector spaces then yields a quotient relation between the associated vector matroids, giving concordant matroids attached to the cell.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is matroidal, and this technique connects tropical containment data to matroid quotient data on cells. It may help analyze how the flat involution interacts with contractions, quotients, or initial matroids arising from elements of Dr(M).

other_useful_info: The paper defines FD(d1,...,ds;n) as the set of flags (X1,...,Xs) of tropical linear spaces in R^n/(1,...,1) with Xi⊂Xi+1; the two-step case FD(p,q;n) is also called a tropical incidence prevariety. It explicitly distinguishes FD from the tropicalization of the classical flag variety: in FD the individual tropical linear spaces need not be realizable, and even realizability of the two spaces need not automatically imply realizability of the containment. In Section 4 the paper introduces concordant polytopes Conv(F,G), where F and G are matroidal polytopes and their associated matroids are concordant in the matroid quotient sense. It asks whether cells of subdivisions coming from FD(p,q;n) are concordant polytopes and whether a converse holds; it proves this for realizable flags via the Observation above. The paper also records Example 1 in ∆(2,3;4): the polytope with p-side bases {12,13,24,34} and q-side bases {123,124,134} lies in the one-skeleton but the two matroids are not concordant; the example is used to motivate the stronger subdivision-level conjecture that, under the Plücker and incidence relations, a cell has no internal edges if and only if it is concordant. Main external references used by the paper are Speyer, Tropical linear spaces, for tropical Plücker vectors, duality, and matroidal subdivisions, and White, Theory of matroids, for matroid quotients/concordance.
why_relevant: Develops tropical incidence relations characterizing containment of one tropical linear space in another. This is foundational for translating inclusion in Dr(M) into valuated-matroid or polyhedral conditions.

title: Local Tropical Linear Spaces
overall_summary: The paper studies a tropical linear space L(p), associated to a tropical Plücker vector or valuated matroid p, by decomposing it into local tropical linear spaces L(p)_B indexed by bases B of the underlying matroid. The main local result is an explicit piecewise-linear homeomorphism R^m → L(p)_B, described using fundamental circuits over B, together with a criterion reducing all circuit-orthogonality checks in the local chart to fundamental circuits. Combinatorially, the paper identifies the face poset of L(p)_B with the dual of a coherent mixed subdivision of a Minkowski sum of simplices obtained from the vertex figure of the matroid polytope at e_B via the Cayley trick. It also shows that arbitrary coherent subdivisions of such vertex figures can be realized by matroid subdivisions after passing to a suitable principal transversal matroid, and studies conical tropical linear spaces whose bounded faces all lie in one local chart. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the local chart machinery: it gives concrete coordinate models, circuit tests, and mixed-subdivision duals for pieces of tropical linear spaces, which could be used to analyze how any putative order-reversing operation on a Dressian-type poset must behave locally on cells, stars, and flat-like boundary strata.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Background characterization in Section 2: tropical Plücker vectors and matroid subdivisions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T = R ∪ {∞} with tropical addition min and tropical multiplication +. For p ∈ T^(([n] choose m)), let supp(p) = {B ∈ ([n] choose m) : p_B ≠ ∞}, let Γ(supp(p)) = conv{e_B : B ∈ supp(p)} ⊆ R^n, and let D_p be the regular subdivision obtained by lifting e_B to height p_B and projecting the lower faces. The paper uses the standard characterization that p is a tropical Plücker vector, equivalently a valuated matroid of rank m, if and only if D_p is a subdivision of a matroid polytope into matroid polytopes. In this case supp(p) is the set of bases of an underlying matroid M, and for every v ∈ R^n the bases B of M maximizing w_p(v,B) = −p_B + sum_{i∈B} v_i form the bases of a matroid M_v corresponding to the face of D_p exposed by the vector (v,−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as known from Speyer and Rincón rather than reproving it. The construction is as follows. A vector p gives a height function on the vertices e_B of Γ(supp(p)); lower faces of the lifted polytope determine D_p. The tropical Plücker relations are exactly the conditions ensuring that each lower cell has edge directions of the form e_i − e_j, hence is a matroid polytope by the Gelfand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova polytope criterion. Conversely, if all lower cells are matroid polytopes, the local edge-exchange structure forces the tropical Plücker minima to be attained at least twice. The exposed face maximizing (v,−1) consists of those bases maximizing w_p(v,B), so its vertices are the bases of the matroid M_v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1: valuated circuit elimination
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be a tropical Plücker vector, and let d,e ∈ T^n be valuated circuits of p. If a,b ∈ [n] satisfy d_a < e_a and d_b = e_b ≠ ∞, then there exists a valuated circuit f ∈ T^n of p such that f_b = ∞, f_a = d_a, and f ≥ min(d,e) coordinatewise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is cited from Murota and Tamura and is not proved in the paper. Its role in the paper is as a valuated analogue of ordinary circuit elimination: starting with two circuits d and e that agree at b but one is strictly smaller at a, it eliminates b while retaining the a-coordinate of d and keeping all other coordinates no smaller than the coordinatewise minimum. In Lemma 2.4, this lets one replace a non-orthogonal circuit by another non-orthogonal circuit with fewer elements outside a chosen basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4: local membership can be tested on fundamental circuits
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p ∈ T^(([n] choose m)) be a tropical Plücker vector with underlying matroid M. Let B be a basis of M, let Σ_B be the set of vectors v ∈ R^n for which B is among the bases of M with maximal v-weight w_p(v,B) = −p_B + sum_{i∈B} v_i, and let L(p)_B = L(p) ∩ Σ_B, where L(p) is the tropical linear space C(p)^⊥ defined by tropical orthogonality to all valuated circuits of p. Then for v ∈ Σ_B, one has v ∈ L(p)_B if and only if v is tropically orthogonal to every valuated fundamental circuit of p over B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication is immediate because fundamental circuits are circuits. For the converse, assume v ∈ Σ_B is orthogonal to all fundamental circuits over B but not in L(p). Choose a valuated circuit d not orthogonal to v, with support D chosen so that D contains as few elements outside B as possible. Let a be the unique element where d_i + v_i is minimal. Since all fundamental circuits over B are orthogonal to v, D is not fundamental, so D contains at least two elements outside B. Choose b ∈ D \ B with b ≠ a, and scale d by adding a multiple of the all-ones vector so that d_b = p_B. Let e = c_{B∪b} be the valuated fundamental circuit of b over B, so e_b = p_B. Because v ∈ Σ_B, for every i in the support of e one has p_B + v_b ≤ p_{B∪b−i} + v_i = e_i + v_i; in fact the same inequality is harmless outside the support since e_i = ∞. Since d is not orthogonal and a is its unique minimum, d_a + v_a < d_b + v_b = p_B + v_b ≤ e_a + v_a, hence d_a < e_a, while d_b = e_b. Applying valuated circuit elimination gives a circuit f with f_b = ∞, f_a = d_a, and f ≥ min(d,e). The inequalities just established imply f_a + v_a < f_i + v_i for all i ≠ a, so f is also not orthogonal to v. But supp(f) is contained in D ∪ supp(e) − {b} ⊆ D ∪ B − {b}, hence has fewer elements outside B than D, contradicting the choice of d.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.5: tropical linear spaces are dual to loopless cells of the matroid subdivision
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p ∈ T^(([n] choose m)) be a tropical Plücker vector with underlying matroid M, and let D_p be its regular matroid subdivision. For v ∈ R^n, let M_v be the matroid whose bases are the bases of M of maximal v-weight. Then v ∈ L(p) if and only if M_v is loopless. Consequently L(p) ∩ R^n is a polyhedral complex dual to the faces of D_p corresponding to loopless matroids.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a maximal v-weight basis B, so v ∈ Σ_B. By Lemma 2.4, v ∈ L(p) is equivalent to v being orthogonal to all fundamental circuits over B. For a ∈ [n] \ B, the fundamental circuit is represented by c_{B∪a}; orthogonality of v to this circuit says that min_{b∈B∪a} p_{B∪a−b} + v_b is attained at least twice. Rewriting this in terms of weights shows that among the bases B∪a−b in the fundamental circuit, the maximum of w_p(v,B∪a−b) is attained not only at B but also at some B∪a−b with b ∈ B. Thus a is not a loop of M_v. This condition for every a outside B is exactly looplessness of M_v, since elements of B are already in the basis B of M_v. The duality with cells of D_p follows because M_v is the matroid labeling the face of D_p exposed by (v,−1), and the regions of constant exposed face form the normal complex dual to D_p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6: explicit piecewise-linear homeomorphism R^m → L(p)_B
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p ∈ T^(([n] choose m)) be a tropical Plücker vector, let B = {b_1,...,b_m} be a basis of its underlying matroid, and let C(i,B) denote the fundamental circuit of i over B. The local tropical linear space L(p)_B is homeomorphic to R^m. More precisely, define f_B : R^m → R^n by setting, for x = (x_1,...,x_m), (f_B(x))_i = x_j if i = b_j, and (f_B(x))_i = min_{b_j∈C(i,B)−{i}} x_j + p_{B∪i−b_j} − p_B if i ∈ [n] \ B. Then f_B is a piecewise-linear homeomorphism from R^m onto L(p)_B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v = f_B(x). First show v ∈ Σ_B. Suppose a basis A has w_p(v,A) > w_p(v,B), and choose such A minimizing |A \ B|. Pick a ∈ A \ B. Apply the tropical Plücker relation to S = A − a and T = B ∪ a. It yields some b ∈ B such that p_A + p_B ≥ p_{A−a∪b} + p_{B−b∪a}. After subtracting the sums of v-coordinates over A and B, this becomes w_p(v,A) + w_p(v,B) ≤ w_p(v,A−a∪b) + w_p(v,B−b∪a). By the defining formula for f_B, whenever B−b∪a is a basis one has w_p(v,B−b∪a) ≤ w_p(v,B). Hence w_p(v,A) ≤ w_p(v,A−a∪b), contradicting the minimality of |A \ B|. Thus B is maximal, so v ∈ Σ_B. Next, the formula for the coordinate i outside B says exactly that in the fundamental circuit c_{B∪i}, the minimum of c_{B∪i,k}+v_k is attained at i and at some basis element b_j, so v is orthogonal to every fundamental circuit over B. Lemma 2.4 then gives v ∈ L(p)_B. The map is injective because the coordinates on B recover x. For surjectivity, if v ∈ L(p)_B, then for every i ∉ B, the condition v ∈ Σ_B gives p_B + v_i ≤ p_{B∪i−b} + v_b for all b in C(i,B)−{i}, while orthogonality to the fundamental circuit forces equality with the minimum for at least one such b. Therefore v_i = min_{b∈C(i,B)−{i}} v_b + p_{B∪i−b} − p_B, so v = f_B((v_{b_1},...,v_{b_m})).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Post-Theorem 2.6 projection formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Using Theorem 2.6 together with the known description of tropical linear spaces as tropical polytopes, the paper states the following formula. If y ∈ Σ_B, then the tropical projection π(y) of y onto L(p) is given by π(y)_i = y_i for i ∈ B, and π(y)_i = min_{j∈C(i,B)−{i}} y_j + p_{B∪i−j} − p_B for i ∈ [n] \ B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not give a full proof, referring also to Corel. The formula is precisely f_B applied to the B-coordinates of y: keep y fixed on B and impose the fundamental-circuit equalities that characterize L(p)_B. Since y ∈ Σ_B, the same maximal-basis argument used in Theorem 2.6 ensures the resulting point lies in the correct local tropical linear space, and the tropical convexity interpretation identifies it with the projection of y to L(p).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1: local tropical linear spaces are dual to mixed subdivisions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be a tropical Plücker vector of rank m with underlying matroid M, let D_p be its regular matroid subdivision of Γ(M), and let B be a basis of M. Let Γ_B be the vertex figure at e_B, equivalently the convex hull of the vertices e_A adjacent to e_B, or Γ(M) ∩ {x ∈ R^n : sum_{i∈B} x_i = m−1}. For j ∈ [n] \ B, set I_j = {i ∈ B : B−i∪j is a basis of M}. Then Γ_B is naturally a Cayley embedding Cayley{Δ_{I_j} : j ∈ [n] \ B}. Let D_B be the subdivision of Γ_B induced by intersecting D_p with Γ_B, and let M_B be the mixed subdivision of sum_{j∈[n]\B} Δ_{I_j} obtained from D_B by the Cayley trick. Then the local tropical linear space L(p)_B is combinatorially dual to M_B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The local tropical linear space L(p)_B is dual to those faces of D_p that contain e_B and correspond to loopless matroids. Intersecting these faces with the vertex figure Γ_B gives faces of the local subdivision D_B. Write Γ_B as the Cayley polytope conv{e_j + Δ_{I_j} : j ∈ [n] \ B}; after identifying B with [m], the Cayley trick intersects a subdivision of this Cayley polytope with the affine slice R^m × {o}, where o is the barycenter of the outside coordinates. This gives a mixed subdivision M_B of the Minkowski sum of the simplices Δ_{I_j}. The Cayley trick gives an inclusion-preserving bijection between d-dimensional faces of M_B and faces of D_B of dimension d + (n−m−1) not contained in any coordinate hyperplane R^m × {x_j = 0}. In this matroid setting, those excluded hyperplanes correspond exactly to cells where the element j is a loop, so the remaining faces are exactly the loopless faces dual to L(p)_B. Therefore the face poset of L(p)_B is the dual of the face poset of M_B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2: realizing regular vertex-figure subdivisions by matroid subdivisions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ = Γ(M) be a matroid polytope, let B be a basis of M, and let Γ_B be the vertex figure of Γ at e_B. For any regular subdivision S of Γ_B, there exists a regular matroid subdivision D of some matroid polytope Γ′ such that Γ′ has the same vertex figure at B, namely Γ′_B = Γ_B, and D restricts to S after intersecting with Γ_B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is described at the start of Section 4. Encode the regular subdivision S of Γ_B ≅ conv{e_j + Δ_{I_j} : j ∈ [n] \ B} by a matrix V ∈ T^{B×([n]\B)}, where V_{i,j} is the height of the vertex corresponding to B−i∪j and V_{i,j}=∞ if that vertex is not present. Form an augmented m×n matrix Vbar by placing the tropical identity matrix in the columns indexed by B and V in the columns indexed by [n]\B. Define τ_V ∈ T^(([n] choose m)) by τ_V(A) = tdet(Vbar_A), the tropical determinant of the submatrix with columns A. The paper states that τ_V is a tropical Plücker vector whose underlying matroid is the principal transversal matroid of the bipartite graph with edges corresponding to finite entries V_{i,j}, equivalently i ∈ I_j. The basis B is represented by the identity matching, and the adjacent basis B−i∪j exists exactly when V_{i,j} is finite, so Γ′_B = Γ_B. Moreover τ_V(B−i∪j) = V_{i,j} for adjacent bases, so the induced lower subdivision on the vertex figure is precisely the original subdivision S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3: face-poset characterization via coherent mixed subdivisions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A poset P is isomorphic to the face poset of an m-dimensional local tropical linear space in R^n if and only if P is isomorphic to the dual face poset of a coherent mixed subdivision of a Minkowski sum of simplices sum_{j=1}^{n−m} Δ_{I_j} ⊆ R^m, where I_j ⊆ [m] for all j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, take a local tropical linear space L(p)_B. Proposition 3.1 constructs from the vertex figure Γ_B and its induced regular subdivision a coherent mixed subdivision M_B of sum_{j∈[n]\B} Δ_{I_j}, and identifies the face poset of L(p)_B with the dual face poset of M_B. For the converse, start with a coherent mixed subdivision of such a Minkowski sum. By the Cayley trick it corresponds to a regular subdivision S of the Cayley polytope conv{e_j + Δ_{I_j}}, which can be regarded as a vertex figure Γ_B. Proposition 3.2 realizes S as the local subdivision at B of a regular matroid subdivision of a suitable matroid polytope. The associated tropical Plücker vector then has a local tropical linear space whose face poset is dual to the given mixed subdivision.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4: f-vector bounds for local tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L(p)_B be an m-dimensional local tropical linear space in R^n. The number of i-dimensional faces of L(p)_B that become bounded after quotienting by the lineality space generated by (1,...,1) is at most multinomial(n−i−1; n−m−i, i−1, m−i) = binomial(n−i−1, i−1) binomial(n−2i, m−i). The total number of i-dimensional faces of L(p)_B, with no boundedness condition, is at most ((n−m)/(n−i)) multinomial(n−1; n−m, i−1, m−i) = binomial(n−i−1, m−i) binomial(n−1, i−1). For every m and n there exists a local tropical linear space achieving all these bounds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 3.1, i-dimensional faces of L(p)_B correspond to faces of codimension i−1 in the associated mixed subdivision M_B; bounded faces after quotienting by the all-ones lineality correspond to interior faces of M_B. The largest possible counts occur for a fine mixed subdivision of (n−m)·Δ_{m−1}, because the summands Δ_{I_j} are faces of Δ_{m−1}. In Lemma 3.5, the number of k-dimensional interior faces and total k-dimensional faces in a fine mixed subdivision of s·Δ_{r−1} are computed explicitly. Substituting s = n−m, r = m, and k = m−i gives the displayed formulas. The sharpness assertion follows from Proposition 3.2, because fine coherent subdivisions of the relevant vertex figure can be realized as local subdivisions of matroid subdivisions, hence as local tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5: face counts in fine mixed subdivisions of a dilated simplex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let s and r be positive integers. In any fine mixed subdivision of s·Δ_{r−1}, the number of k-dimensional interior faces is multinomial(s−1+k; s−r+k, r−1−k, k). The total number of k-dimensional faces is (s/(s+k)) multinomial(r+s−1; s, r−1−k, k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the Cayley trick, k-dimensional interior faces of a fine mixed subdivision of s·Δ_{r−1} correspond to interior faces of dimension k+s−1 in a triangulation of Δ_{r−1} × Δ_{s−1}. Products of simplices are equidecomposable, so all their triangulations have the same f-vector; since faces of products of simplices are again products of simplices, the number of interior faces in each dimension is also independent of the triangulation. The required interior-face formula is then taken from Develin-Sturmfels, Corollary 25. For the total count, sum the interior k-face counts over all faces of s·Δ_{r−1}. A face supported on l vertices is isomorphic to s·Δ_{l−1}, and there are binomial(r,l) such faces. Thus the total is sum_{l=1}^r binomial(r,l) multinomial(s−1+k; s−l+k, l−1−k, k).
