key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All x-rays form a rooted tree in the Hasse diagram, rooted at x: once two x-rays separate, Proposition 4.9 and the segment property prevent them from meeting again. Thus delta_x(e,f) is the depth of the lowest common ancestor, and it satisfies delta_x(e,f)≥min(delta_x(e,g),delta_x(g,f)), which is equivalent to the ultrametric inequality for d_x. For completeness, a d_x-Cauchy sequence of ends has increasingly long common initial segments; these compatible initial segments form an x-ray, whose end is the limit. To compare d_x and d_y, it suffices to compare adjacent basepoints z covering x. A case analysis on whether z lies on one of the two rays shows |delta_z(e,f)−delta_x(e,f)|≤1. Along chains from x and y to x∨y, this gives the stated multiplicative bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.15-4.17 (coordinate realization of a uniform semimodular lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a uniform semimodular lattice with end set E_L. Fix x in L. For y≥x define the x-coordinate y_x in Z_+^{E_L} by y_x(e)=max{l : e_x^l≤y}, where e_x is the x-ray representing end e. For general x,y define y_x=(y^{+k})_x−k 1 for k large enough that y^{+k}≥x. Then for x≤y≤z one has z_x=z_y+y_x, (y^+)_x=y_x+1, and y=join_{e in E_L} e_x^{y_x(e)}. Also y_x=−x_y for all x,y. Consequently the map y↦y_x is an order isomorphism from L onto the subset Z(L,x)={y_x : y in L} of Z^{E_L}, ordered componentwise, and the ascending operator becomes addition of the all-one vector.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For x≤y≤z, first prove the formula when z covers y. In that case the coordinate of an end increases by one exactly for those rays whose first step at y is the covering atom z; semimodularity and the ray-joining lemma rule out larger jumps. This gives z_x=z_y+y_x by induction along a chain. The formula for (y^+)_x follows by taking z=y^+, and the extension to arbitrary x,y is independent of k because ascending shifts all coordinates by 1. The join expression follows since each e_x^{y_x(e)} is below y, and if their join u were strictly below y then y_u would have some positive coordinate, contradicting equality of x-coordinates. Injectivity and order preservation are then immediate from the coordinate formula, while y_x=−x_y follows from 0=x_x=x_y+y_x.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.19 (matroid at infinity of a uniform semimodular lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a uniform semimodular lattice of uniform-rank n, with end set E_L. For x in L, call I⊆E_L x-independent if the first atoms {e_x^1 : e in I} are independent in the geometric lattice [x,x^+]. This gives a loop-free rank-n matroid M_x=(E_L,I_x). Let I_infty=union_{x in L} I_x. Then M_infty=(E_L,I_infty) is a simple matroid of rank n, called the matroid at infinity, and its base family is B_infty=union_x B_x, where B_x is the base family of M_x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The local M_x is obtained from the simple matroid associated with [x,x^+] by adding parallel elements corresponding to ends whose x-rays have the same first atom. Several monotonicity facts are used. If z≥x and z is not above e_x^1 for any e in K, then K independent at z implies K independent at x; this follows from semimodularity applied to joins of first atoms. If z lies in [x,x^+] above join_{e in K} e_x^1, the rank contribution of K at z is at least its rank at x. Also, if an independent set I at x is joined first and a larger set K is a base at the resulting point with no new ray already below the join, then K is a base at x. For exchange, take I,J in I_infty with |I|<|J| and choose x with I independent at x. If join_{e in J} e_x^1 is not below join_{e in I} e_x^1, some e in J\I can be added immediately. Otherwise define x_k=join_{e in I} e_x^k and y_k=join_{e in J} e_x^k. A lemma shows x_k evolves by repeatedly joining the first steps of the I-rays at x_{k−1}; another shows J becomes independent at some y_m. Since the rank increment of y_k eventually is |J| while that of x_k is |I|, the containment y_k≤x_k must fail at the next step for some k, producing e in J\I with I+e independent at x_k. Simplicity follows because two distinct ends have x-rays diverging at some point, where their first atoms are distinct and independent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.24 (basis extraction for bounded coordinate data)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a uniform semimodular lattice, fix x in L, and let c in Z_+^{E_L} be bounded. Let y=join_{e in E_L} e_x^{c(e)}, which exists because c is bounded. Then there is a base B of the local matroid M_y such that y_x(e)=c(e) for every e in B and y=join_{e in B} e_x^{c(e)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on max c(e). Let c'(e)=max(c(e)−1,0) and z=join_e e_x^{c'(e)}. Let Z be the set of ends e for which e_x^{c(e)} is not below z. For e in Z, c(e)>0 and z_x(e)=c(e)−1. Then y=join_{e in Z} e_z^1, and the coordinates y_z are 1 exactly on the closure of Z in the local matroid M_z. By induction choose a base B' of M_z realizing z from c'. Let I={e in B' : c(e)>0}; then I⊆Z. Extend I inside Z to an independent set J whose closure equals the closure of Z, so y=join_{e in J} e_z^1=join_{e in J} e_x^{c(e)}. If J is already a base at z, it is a base at y and works. Otherwise extend J by elements K from B' outside the closure of Z to a base B=J∪K of M_z. These added elements have c(e)=0 and y_z(e)=0, so y_x(e)=c(e) also for them, and B is a base at y realizing y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.25, 4.28, and 4.29 (Zn-skeletons and distance-to-skeleton formulas)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a uniform semimodular lattice. If B is a base of M_x, the sublattice S_x(B) generated by the x-rays for e in B is isomorphic to Z_+^n, and S(B)=union_{k in Z} S_x(B)^{+k} is a Zn-skeleton. Lemma 4.25 says S(B)={y in L : B is a base of M_y}, so S(B) is independent of the initial x. For x in L and B in B_infty, let x_B be the maximum element of S(B) below x. If y≤x, then y=x_B if and only if B is a base at y and x_y(e)=0 for all e in B; moreover x_B≤y if and only if x_y(e)=0 for all e in B. Finally, for a height function r, r(x_B)+sum_{e in B} y_x(e)=r(y) when y lies in S(B), while r(x_B)+sum_{e in B} y_x(e)<r(y) when y does not lie in S(B).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.25, the inclusion S(B)⊆{y : B in B_y} follows from Proposition 4.9. Conversely, if y≥x and B is a base at y, set y'=join_{e in B} e_x^{y_x(e)} in S(B). If y'<y, choose an atom a of [y',y'^+] below y not among the B-directions. Since B is a base at y', adding a makes B dependent at a, and the monotonicity lemma then makes B dependent at y, contradiction. Thus y=y'. For the x_B characterizations, if y in S(B), then the largest skeleton point below x is obtained by joining e_y^{x_y(e)} over e in B; hence y is exactly x_B precisely when all these coordinates vanish. If B is not a base at y, iteratively replacing y by (join_{e in B} e_y^1)^− moves downward until B becomes a base, while preserving the zero-coordinate condition, yielding x_B≤y. For the rank formula, if y in S(B), Proposition 4.9 gives r[y,x_B]=sum_{e in B} x_y(e), equivalent to the displayed equality using y_x=−x_y. If y is not in S(B), compare y with y'=join_{e in B} e_y^{x_y(e)}; the inequalities r[y,y']≤sum x_y(e) and r(x_B)≤r(y') cannot both be equal, because equality would let one descend and eventually force B to be a base at y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.26 (from a uniform semimodular lattice to a complete valuated matroid)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a uniform semimodular lattice of uniform-rank n with end set E_L and matroid at infinity M_infty=(E_L,B_infty). Fix x in L. For B in B_infty, let S(B) be its Zn-skeleton and let x_B be the maximum element of S(B) below x. Define omega_{L,x}(B)=−r[x_B,x], where r[u,v] is the length of a maximal chain in [u,v]. Then omega_{L,x}:B_infty→Z is a complete valuated matroid of rank n. Moreover, T(omega_{L,x})∩Z^{E_L} is isomorphic to L, and T(omega_{L,x}) is a geometric realization of the simplicial complex C(L) consisting of chains x0<x1<...<xm with xm≤x0^+.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The valuation axiom is verified using Murota's Lemma 3.3. For a coordinate vector c=y_x with y≤x, Lemma 4.29 shows that omega_{L,x}(B)+sum_{e in B} c(e) is maximized exactly by those B with y in S(B), i.e. by the base family B_y of the local matroid M_y. For general bounded integer c, shift by a multiple of 1 so c≥0, set y=join_e e_x^{c(e)}, and let c_tilde=y_x. Lemma 4.24 gives a base B at y with c_tilde(e)=c(e) on B, which shows the maxima of omega+c and omega+c_tilde are equal and B_{omega+c} is the maximizer family of a linear functional over the matroid B_{omega+c_tilde}=B_y; hence it is a matroid. Therefore omega is a valuated matroid. To identify integer points, if c=y_x then B_{omega+c}=B_y is loop-free, so c lies in T(omega). Conversely, if c is not in Z(L,x), the construction above gives c_tilde>c in some coordinate e, and no maximizing base for omega+c can contain e, so c is not in T(omega). Thus T(omega)∩Z^{E_L}=Z(L,x). The geometric realization statement follows by applying Lemma 3.7: each real point is obtained from an integer point by a fractional chain inside the geometric interval [x,x^+], exactly the simplices of C(L). Completeness follows later from identifying the end ultrametric of L with the Dress-Terhalle ultrametric of omega.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.31 (local flat lattice inside L(omega))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let omega be an integer-valued valuated matroid and let L(omega)=T(omega)∩Z^E. For x in L(omega), the interval [x,x+1] in L(omega), ordered componentwise, is isomorphic to the lattice of flats of the local matroid M_{omega+x}; the isomorphism sends x+1_F to the flat F. In particular, y covers x in L(omega) if and only if y=x+1_F for a parallel class F of M_{omega+x}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After replacing omega by omega+x, assume x=0. If F is a flat of M_omega, Lemma 3.9 shows that bases of M_omega with maximal intersection with F remain maximizing for omega+1_F; because F is flat and M_omega has no loop, every element is contained in some such maximizing base, so 1_F lies in T(omega). If F is not flat, choose e in cl(F)\F. Then the maximum possible |B∩(F+e)| over B in B_omega equals the maximum possible |B∩F|; hence adding 1_F makes no maximizing base contain e, so e is a loop and 1_F is not in T(omega). This proves the interval-flat correspondence. For covers, write y−x as a sum of incidence vectors of nested level sets. The top nonzero level set must be a flat; if it is not a minimal nonempty flat, an intermediate flat gives an element strictly between x and y. Minimal nonempty flats are precisely parallel classes in the local matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.30 (from a valuated matroid to a uniform semimodular lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (E,omega) be an integer-valued valuated matroid of rank n, and let L(omega)=T(omega)∩Z^E with the componentwise order. Then L(omega) is a uniform semimodular lattice of uniform-rank n. Its ascending operator is x↦x+1. A height function is r(x)=max_{B} (omega+x)(B). The meet and join are x∧y=min(x,y) and x∨y=meet{z in L(omega) : x≤z and y≤z}. Finally, for x in L(omega), the valuated matroid constructed from the uniform semimodular lattice L(omega) at x is the Dress-Terhalle completion of a valuated matroid projectively equivalent to (E,omega).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The height formula follows from Lemma 4.31: if y covers x, then y=x+1_F for a parallel class F of M_{omega+x}; the maximizing bases containing F become exactly one unit larger, so r(y)=r(x)+1. The meet formula follows from tropical convexity, Lemma 3.10, because min(x,y) remains in T(omega) and is plainly the greatest lower bound. The existence of joins follows because, for x,y in L(omega), a large translate x+k1 is an upper bound and the height function plus the finiteness of intervals gives a minimal upper bound; it is the meet of all upper bounds. Semimodularity follows from Lemma 4.31 and the fact that intervals [x,x+1] are geometric lattices: if a,b cover a∧b, their join covers both. The ascending operator is addition of 1, because [x,x+1] is the interval generated by all covers of x, and translation by 1 is an order automorphism. The rank of [x,x+1] is the rank n of the local matroid M_{omega+x}. For the completion statement, the paper identifies ends of L(omega) with Dress-Terhalle Cauchy classes via normal rays. It then proves, for bases B from the original ground set, the identity (omega+x)(B)=r(x_B)=omega_{L(omega),x}(B)+r(x), showing projective equivalence on E, and density/completeness identifies the constructed ground set with the Dress-Terhalle completion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.32-4.36 (normal rays and the completion of L(omega))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In L(omega)=T(omega)∩Z^E, every ray a0<a1<... has the form a_{l+1}=a_l+1_{F_l}, where F0⊇F1⊇... is a decreasing sequence of nonempty subsets and F_l is a parallel class in the local matroid M_{omega+a_l}. If intersection_l F_l is nonempty, it is a parallel class of the underlying matroid; such a ray is called normal and has that intersection as its infinity-direction. Two normal rays are parallel if and only if they have the same infinity-direction, and for every x in L(omega) and every parallel class F of the underlying matroid there is a normal x-ray with infinity-direction F. If omega is simple, E embeds into the end set of L(omega) as the normal rays with singleton directions; for x in L(omega), B_{omega+x}={B in B_{L,x} : B⊆E}. If r(x)=0, then −x lies in the tight span TS(omega) and the Dress-Terhalle metric D_{−x} on E equals the end metric d_x. The normal rays are dense in the full end space E_{L(omega)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The form a_{l+1}=a_l+1_{F_l} and the fact that F_l is a local parallel class follow from Lemma 4.31. The sets decrease because if e lies in F_l∩F_{l+1} and f lies in F_{l+1}\F_l, then one can choose a base of M_{omega+a_l} containing e and f that remains maximizing after adding 1_{F_l}, contradicting e and f becoming parallel in the next local matroid. If two nonparallel underlying elements e,f survived in every F_l, a base containing both would gain two units along the ray while the height only rises by one per step, impossible. For existence of a normal ray with direction F, repeatedly let F_l be the closure of F in M_{omega+a_l} and set a_{l+1}=a_l+1_{F_l}. Parallelism of normal rays is checked by joining rays to common upper bounds; joining preserves the limiting direction, and two rays at the same point with the same direction coincide. The equality B_{omega+x}={B in B_{L,x}:B⊆E} follows because the first step of the normal x-ray for e is x+1_{F_e}, where F_e is the local parallel class of e. For the metric identity, define x_i by x_{i+1}=e_{x_i}^1∨f_{x_i}^1. The first i at which e and f become independent locally is both the splitting depth delta_x(e,f) and the negative of max{(omega+x)(B): B contains e,f}, giving D_{−x}=d_x. Density follows by taking an arbitrary ray with decreasing F_l and choosing e_l in F_l; the corresponding normal ends converge to it.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 example (Bergman fan of an ordinary matroid)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(E,B) be a matroid, viewed as a {0,−infinity}-valued valuated matroid omega by omega(B)=0 exactly for bases B of M. For a simple matroid M, the tropical linear space T(omega) is the Bergman fan and has the explicit cone decomposition T(omega)=R 1 + union over chains of nontrivial flats F1⊂...⊂Fk of the conical hull of 1_{F1},...,1_{Fk}. Its integer points are L(omega)=Z 1 + union over such chains of the integer conical hull of 1_{F1},...,1_{Fk}. For x=mu 1+sum_i lambda_i 1_{F_i} in this fan, with F0=empty and F_{k+1}=E, the local matroid at x is the direct product of the minors (M|F_{i+1})/F_i for i=0,...,k. The matroid at the origin is M, the end set is naturally identified with E via the rays k 1_e, and the matroid at infinity is also M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an ordinary matroid valuation, x lies in T(omega) exactly when the bases of M maximizing the linear functional B↦sum_{e in B} x(e) have no loop. If x is in T(omega), then for every real alpha the upper level set {e : x(e)≥alpha} must be a flat; otherwise an element in its closure but outside it would become a loop among maximizers. Thus the distinct nontrivial upper level sets of x form a chain of flats, giving the displayed expression. Conversely, a vector built from a chain of flats maximizes precisely on bases that are lexicographically maximal with respect to the flag, and every element remains present in the appropriate minor, so it lies in the Bergman fan. The local matroid at a flag point splits because maximization along the flag first fixes the ranks in each interval F_i⊂F_{i+1}; the independent choices are exactly bases of the contractions/restrictions (M|F_{i+1})/F_i. The 0-rays are k 1_e, so ends are the original elements and the infinity matroid recovers M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1 (representable valuated matroids as module lattices)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a field, K(t) the rational function field with degree valuation deg, and E⊆K(t)^n contain a K(t)-basis. Let B be the family of K(t)-bases B⊆E and define omega_E(B)=deg det(B). Let K(t)_− be the ring of rational functions of degree at most 0. For z in Z^E write E^z={t^{z(u)}u : u in E}, and let L(E)={⟨E^z⟩ : z in Z^E}, where ⟨·⟩ denotes the K(t)_−-submodule generated. For L in L(E), set z_L(p)=max{alpha in Z : t^alpha p in L}. Then L(E) is a uniform semimodular lattice isomorphic to L(omega_E)=T(omega_E)∩Z^E via L↦z_L and z↦⟨E^z⟩. Under this isomorphism, the ascending operator is L↦tL, the Zn-skeleton S(B) equals L(B)={⟨B^z⟩ : z in Z^B}, a height function is r(L)=deg det(Q) for any K(t)_−-basis Q of L, and for x in L(omega_E) one has ⟨E^x⟩_B=⟨B^x⟩ and omega_{L(omega_E),x}(B)=(omega_E+x)(B)−r(⟨E^x⟩).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key algebraic lemma is that if B maximizes omega_E, then B is a K(t)_−-basis of ⟨E⟩. Indeed, every u in E can be written u=B lambda, and Cramer's rule gives deg lambda_i=deg det(B_i)−deg det(B)=omega_E(B_i)−omega_E(B)≤0. Hence lambda_i lies in K(t)_−. To show z_L in T(omega_E), suppose p were a loop in the maximizer matroid for omega_E+z_L. Taking a maximizing basis B, the same Cramer-rule computation for t^{z_L(p)}p in the basis B^{z_L} gives all coefficients degree at most −1, implying t^{z_L(p)+1}p still lies in L, contradicting maximality of z_L(p). This also proves that the maps L↦z_L and z↦⟨E^z⟩ are inverse. The ascending operator statement follows from z_{tL}=z_L+1. The skeleton identity follows because B in B_{omega+z_L} exactly when B^z is a module basis, so L(B) consists of points whose local matroid contains B; Lemma 4.25 identifies this with S(B). The height formula is the determinant-degree change under covers. Finally, ⟨E^x⟩_B=⟨B^x⟩ because any larger skeleton point below ⟨E^x⟩ would force z_{⟨E^x⟩}>x in some coordinate; the displayed formula for omega_{L,x} is then just deg det(B^x)−r(⟨E^x⟩).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3 (modular valuated matroids and Euclidean buildings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A modular valuated matroid is an integer-valued valuated matroid (E,omega) such that L(omega)=T(omega)∩Z^E is a uniform modular lattice. For any modular valuated matroid, the projectivized tropical linear space T(omega)/R1 is a geometric realization of the Euclidean building associated with the uniform modular lattice L(omega).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the companion result that uniform modular lattices are cryptomorphically equivalent to Euclidean buildings of type A: for a uniform modular lattice L, the simplicial complex C(L) modulo the equivalence x~x^{+k} is a Euclidean building, and every such building arises this way. Theorem 4.26 identifies T(omega) as the geometric realization of C(L(omega)). Quotienting T(omega) by translations R1 corresponds exactly to quotienting L(omega) by powers of the ascending operator, so T(omega)/R1 realizes the associated Euclidean building.

proof_techniques.name: Localizing a tropical linear space to a flat lattice
proof_techniques.description: The central local observation is Lemma 4.31: for an integer point x of T(omega), the unit interval [x,x+1] in L(omega)=T(omega)∩Z^E is precisely the lattice of flats of the local matroid M_{omega+x}. Covers of x correspond to parallel classes of this local matroid. This converts local polyhedral or tropical data into classical matroid flat-lattice data, and it is used to prove semimodularity and determine the ascending operator.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem starts with an order-reversing symmetry of a flat lattice. This technique suggests first understanding any proposed extension on Dr(M) through how it acts on local intervals [x,x+1], where the objects are again flat lattices of local matroids/minors.

proof_techniques.name: Bergman fan as an affine uniform semimodular thickening of the flat lattice
proof_techniques.description: For an ordinary matroid M, the integer Bergman fan L(M) is described as Z1 plus nonnegative integer combinations of incidence vectors of chains of flats. Thus the flat lattice is not merely the face poset of a fan; it generates a canonical affine lattice whose short intervals are flat lattices of matroid minors along flags. The local matroid at a flag F1⊂...⊂Fk is the direct product of the minors (M|F_{i+1})/F_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F↦F^perp reverses the flat lattice, one concrete strategy is to try to extend it first to flags and integer combinations in this affine Bergman lattice, e.g. by reversing/complementing flags, before attempting an action on all tropical subspaces in the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Murota maximizer criterion for constructing valuations from lattice data
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs a valuated matroid from a uniform semimodular lattice by defining omega(B) as a negative distance from a basepoint x to a Zn-skeleton S(B). Instead of verifying the valuated exchange axiom directly, it uses Murota's criterion: it proves that every bounded integer perturbation has a matroidal maximizer family. The proof reduces arbitrary perturbations to genuine lattice coordinates and then uses local matroids M_y.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: To build a Dressian involution, one may define the image of a valuated matroid or tropical subspace through transformed lattice/skeleton data and then verify it is again valuated by checking perturbed maximizers, rather than by checking tropical Plucker relations directly.

proof_techniques.name: Ends, rays, and matroid at infinity
proof_techniques.description: Uniform semimodular lattices have rays, parallel classes of rays called ends, and a canonical matroid at infinity on the end set. Local matroids M_x come from first steps of rays in [x,x^+], while M_infty is the union of all local independence systems and is proved to be a simple matroid. Zn-skeletons are indexed by bases at infinity and consist exactly of points y for which that basis is a local basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A tropical linear subspace inside a Bergman fan can be studied through its associated lattice and its directions at infinity. This may help compare subspaces under a flat-lattice anti-involution: the involution may need to act on directions, bases at infinity, and skeletons, not just on individual flats.

proof_techniques.name: Rounding plus fractional flat-chain decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Every real point x of T(omega) has floor(x) again in T(omega), and the fractional part x−floor(x) is a nonnegative combination, with total coefficient less than one, of incidence vectors of a chain of flats in the local matroid M_{omega+floor(x)}. This is the bridge between integer lattice structure and the full real tropical linear space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an involution or anti-involution can be defined on integer points and on local flat chains, this lemma gives a canonical way to extend it piecewise-linearly to the full tropical projective space or to cells of tropical linear spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Short-chain realization of tropical linear spaces
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.26 identifies T(omega) with the geometric realization of the simplicial complex C(L) of short chains x0<x1<...<xm with xm≤x0^+ in the uniform semimodular lattice L=T(omega)∩Z^E. This packages all cells of the tropical linear space as chains inside consecutive local flat lattices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing lattice map, if it can be upgraded to an appropriate automorphism or anti-automorphism of the uniform semimodular lattice, should induce a piecewise-linear transformation on the associated tropical linear space via its action on short chains.

proof_techniques.name: Skeleton projection and distance-to-skeleton formulas
proof_techniques.description: For each basis B at infinity, the paper defines a Zn-skeleton S(B) and the projection x_B, the largest point of S(B) below x. The formula r(x_B)+sum_{e in B} y_x(e)=r(y), with strict inequality off S(B), is the key inequality selecting local bases as maximizers of the constructed valuation. It converts incidence with skeletons into linear optimization identities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressian elements are valuated data modulo projective shifts. These formulas suggest a way to encode or transform valuated data by how a point projects to basis-indexed skeletons, potentially making symmetries of the flat lattice act on basis valuations.

proof_techniques.name: Ultrametric completion to remove simplification and parallel-class ambiguities
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies the end space of L(omega) with the Dress-Terhalle completion of a valuated matroid. For simple omega, original elements are dense normal ends, and the end ultrametric equals the Dress-Terhalle ultrametric. Parallel classes in nonsimple matroids are handled by simplification, with tropical linear spaces related by an order-preserving coordinate bijection.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The flat-to-Dressian embedding in the original problem involves contractions and loops, where parallelism and simplification issues can occur. The completion/simplification framework gives a controlled way to ignore or restore such data while preserving tropical linear spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Representable case via K(t)_−-module lattices
proof_techniques.description: For K(t)-representable valuated matroids, the uniform semimodular lattice L(omega) is realized as a lattice of K(t)_−-submodules generated by scaled columns. Cramer's rule identifies local bases and proves the tropical condition. Zn-skeletons become module apartments L(B), and the constructed valuation is a determinant-degree formula relative to a module.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the flat-lattice involution of M comes from a linear polarity or orthogonal complement in a representable matroid, this module model suggests trying to construct the Dressian involution by taking dual or orthogonal K(t)_−-modules.

proof_techniques.name: Modular/building dictionary
proof_techniques.description: When L(omega) is a uniform modular lattice, the associated tropical linear space modulo R1 is a Euclidean building of type A. The paper relies on a companion equivalence between uniform modular lattices and Euclidean buildings, with C(L) modulo ascending shifts giving the building.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For modular matroids, such as projective geometries where order-reversing flat maps are often polarities, known opposition or duality maps on buildings may provide a model for the desired order-reversing operation on tropical subspaces.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the convention T(omega)={x : the maximizer matroid M_{omega+x} has no loop}; this is sign-compatible with the tropical Wick condition max_f omega(C−f)−x(f) attained at least twice, and the tight span is TS(omega)=−{x in T(omega) : max_B (omega+x)(B)=0}. Example 4.2 gives an explicit model for the uniform matroid U_{n,E}: for finite |E|=m, the integer tropical linear space is Z^{E,n}={x in Z^E : the minimum of x is attained by at least m−n+1 elements}, with ascending operator x↦x+1 and uniform-rank n. The paper cites Ardila-Klivans for the Bergman complex, Speyer and Murota-Tamura for tropical linear spaces/covectors, Dress-Terhalle for completion, Keel-Tevelev for membranes, and Hirai's companion paper arXiv:1801.00240 for uniform modular lattices and Euclidean buildings. The paper does not itself study order-reversing involutions or relative Dressians, but it supplies a lattice-theoretic language in which tropical linear spaces, Bergman fans, local flats, and valuated matroid data can be translated back and forth.
why_relevant: Gives a lattice-theoretic characterization of integer-valued valuated matroids extending the classical flat-lattice characterization of matroids. This is relevant to lifting an order-reversing flat-lattice symmetry to valuated matroid data.

title: On local Dressians of matroids
overall_summary: The paper studies local Dressians Dr(M), where M is a fixed matroid and Dr(M) parametrizes valuated matroids with underlying support M, equivalently height functions on the vertices of the matroid polytope P_M satisfying the local three-term tropical Plücker relations. Its main theorem is that the Plücker fan structure on Dr(M) coincides with the secondary-fan structure coming from regular matroid subdivisions of P_M; the proof shows that matroid subdivisions are determined by their behavior on the 3-dimensional skeleton, especially on octahedral faces. The paper also proves structural results: local Dressians are invariant under combinatorial isomorphism of matroid polytopes, factor as products under direct sums of matroids, simplify under deletion of parallel elements modulo lineality, and are linear for binary matroids. For the original problem, the most useful material is not a direct answer, because the paper's Dr(M) is the local Dressian rather than the relative Dressian, but its subdivision technology, duality of matroid polytopes, direct-sum factorization, and 3-skeleton rigidity provide concrete tools for constructing or obstructing involutions on tropical-linear-space parameter spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Matroid polytope facts from Section 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank d matroid on ground set [n] with set of bases B(M). Its matroid polytope is P_M = conv{e_B : B in B(M)} inside R^n, where e_B = sum_{i in B} e_i. A collection of d-subsets of [n] is the set of bases of a matroid if and only if every edge of the corresponding polytope is parallel to an edge e_i - e_j of the hypersimplex Δ(d,n). If i is an element of [n], then, up to the natural coordinate identification, P_{M\i} = P_M ∩ {x_i = 0} and P_{M/i} = P_M ∩ {x_i = 1}. If M* is the dual matroid, then P_{M*} = f(P_M), where f is the affine coordinate reflection f(x_1,...,x_n) = (1 - x_1,...,1 - x_n). In particular P_M and P_{M*} are affinely isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The deletion and contraction identities follow directly from bases: bases of M\i are exactly bases of M not containing i, hence their vertices are those of P_M with x_i = 0; bases of M/i are bases B minus i where B is a basis of M containing i, hence they correspond to vertices of P_M with x_i = 1. For duality, bases of M* are complements [n] \ B of bases B of M, and e_{[n]\B} = 1 - e_B, giving the coordinate reflection. The edge characterization is cited as Edmonds/Gelfand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova: the basis exchange axiom is equivalent to all polytope edges having hypersimplex directions e_i - e_j.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9, cited from Speyer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers 0 <= d <= n, let Dr(d,n) be the Dressian defined by the tropical three-term Plücker relations on coordinates indexed by d-subsets of [n]. For a vector w in R^{binom(n,d)}, lift each vertex e_B of the hypersimplex Δ(d,n) to height w_B and project the lower faces to obtain a regular subdivision of Δ(d,n). Then w lies in Dr(d,n) if and only if this regular subdivision is a matroid subdivision, meaning that every cell is a matroid polytope.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Proposition 2.2 of Speyer and does not reprove it. The conceptual content used throughout the paper is that the tropical three-term Plücker relations are exactly the local conditions on octahedral 3-faces of the hypersimplex that force every lower cell of the induced subdivision to have only matroid-edge directions, hence to be a matroid polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 10, cited from Speyer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let v be a valuated matroid, i.e. a point of Dr(d,n). The associated tropical linear space L_v is the intersection of the tropical hyperplanes defined by the tropical linear forms f_S(v) = min_{i in S}(v_{S\i} + x_i), for all (d+1)-subsets S of [n]. For x in R^n, define M_x to be the matroid whose bases are the d-subsets B of [n] minimizing v_B - sum_{i in B} x_i. Then L_v consists exactly of those points x for which M_x has no loops.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Proposition 2.3 of Speyer. The mechanism is that, for each (d+1)-set S, the tropical hyperplane condition says the minimum among v_{S\i} + x_i is attained at least twice. Since v_{S\i} + x_i differs from v_{S\i} - sum_{j in S\i} x_j by the constant sum_{j in S} x_j, this is exactly the condition that no element is uniquely excluded from all minimizing bases in the initial matroid M_x. Equivalently, every ground-set element appears in some minimizing basis, so M_x is loopless.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary-stratum description of local Dressians and Corollary 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M = ([n],B) be a rank d matroid with b = |B| bases. The local Dressian Dr(M) is the tropical prevariety in R^b/R1 obtained from the three-term Plücker relations by setting p_A = 0 algebraically, equivalently tropical coordinate infinity, for every d-subset A that is not a basis of M. Equivalently, Dr(M) is the intersection of the closure of Dr(d,n) in tropical projective space with the boundary stratum where the coordinates indexed by nonbases of M are infinity. A vector w in R^b/R1 lies in Dr(M) if and only if the regular subdivision of the matroid polytope P_M induced by the heights w_B on its basis vertices e_B is a matroid subdivision.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The boundary-stratum statement is obtained by viewing the global Dressian in compactified tropical projective space and restricting to the stratum with finite coordinates exactly on the bases of M. For Corollary 13, one applies Speyer's subdivision characterization to a height vector on the full hypersimplex whose finite part is w on bases of M and whose nonbasis coordinates are at infinity. The induced finite subdivision is precisely the regular subdivision of P_M. Thus the local tropical Plücker conditions are equivalent to all cells in that subdivision being matroid polytopes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 14 and Corollary 15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank d on n elements. The Plücker fan structure on the local Dressian Dr(M), defined by the cones cut out by the local tropical three-term Plücker relations, coincides with the secondary fan structure on Dr(M), defined by regular matroid subdivisions of the matroid polytope P_M. In particular, taking M to be the uniform matroid U_{d,n}, the Plücker fan structure on the global Dressian Dr(d,n) as a fan in R^{binom(n,d)}/R1 coincides with its secondary fan structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First suppose two height vectors v and w lie in the same cone of the secondary fan. They induce the same regular subdivision of P_M, hence the same subdivision on every 3-dimensional face. On each octahedral 3-face, the induced subdivision records exactly which of the three quantities in the corresponding tropical three-term Plücker relation are minimal and whether all three agree. Therefore v and w satisfy the same Plücker equalities and inequalities, so they lie in the same Plücker cone. Conversely, suppose v and w lie in the same Plücker cone. By Corollary 13 they induce matroid subdivisions Σ_v and Σ_w of P_M. Since they have identical Plücker data, the two subdivisions agree on the 3-dimensional skeleton: on octahedra the common Plücker pattern determines one of the three possible subdivisions into two quadrilateral pyramids or the trivial subdivision, while the other relevant low-dimensional faces have no nontrivial matroid subdivision. To prove global equality, take a maximal cell σ_v of Σ_v and suppose it is not a cell of Σ_w. Choose vertices q_1 and q_k of σ_v that are not contained together in a maximal cell of Σ_w, and choose a path q_1,q_2,...,q_k in the vertex-edge graph of σ_v. Pick a cell σ_w of Σ_w containing q_1,...,q_i but such that no cell contains q_1,...,q_{i+1}. Then q_{i-1} and q_{i+1} are at distance at most two in the basis graph. Using the basis exchange axiom, the minimal face F of σ_v spanned by q_{i-1} and q_{i+1} has at most six relevant vertices and is one of the small matroid-polytope faces considered in the proof. If F is an octahedron, then Σ_w would subdivide F differently from Σ_v, contradicting agreement on the 3-skeleton. If F is a pyramid, square, or triangle, it cannot be subdivided matroidally in the required way, so q_{i+1} would also have to lie in σ_w, contradicting the choice of i. Hence every maximal cell of Σ_v is also a cell of Σ_w, and by symmetry the subdivisions agree. Therefore the secondary and Plücker cones coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d >= 2, and let Σ and Σ' be two matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex Δ(d,n). If Σ and Σ' induce the same subdivision on the 3-dimensional skeleton of Δ(d,n), equivalently if they agree on all octahedral 3-faces of Δ(d,n), then Σ = Σ'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The subdivision induced on each octahedral 3-face is exactly the combinatorial datum recorded by the corresponding tropical three-term Plücker relation. If two matroid subdivisions agree on all such faces, their height vectors belong to the same Plücker cone. By Theorem 14, Plücker cones and secondary cones are identical, so the two height vectors induce the same secondary cone and hence the same subdivision.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on n elements with b bases and c connected components. Then the lineality space of the local Dressian Dr(M), considered in R^b/R1, has dimension dim P_M = n - c. More explicitly, the lineality space is the image, modulo constants, of the linear map R^n -> R^b sending the coordinate vector e_i to sum_{B basis of M, i in B} e_B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Adding an affine-linear function on the ambient space R^n to a height function on the vertices of P_M does not change the induced regular subdivision, so such functions give lineality of the secondary fan and hence of Dr(M). The image formula records the effect of adding the coordinate function x_i to all vertices e_B: it adds 1 exactly to those basis coordinates B containing i. The lineality dimension of the secondary fan of a vertex configuration equals the affine dimension of that configuration. By the standard dimension formula for matroid polytopes, dim P_M = n - c, where c is the number of connected components of M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 24 and Remark 25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and M' be matroids such that their matroid polytopes P_M and P_{M'} are combinatorially isomorphic. Then their local Dressians are isomorphic as fans: Dr(M) ≅ Dr(M'). The paper also remarks that matroid polytopes P_M and P_{M'} are combinatorially isomorphic if and only if the matroids are isomorphic up to adding or deleting loops, adding or deleting coloops, and dualizing connected components; this characterization is attributed to work in preparation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A matroid subdivision of P_M introduces no new edge directions: every cell is a matroid polytope and its edges are inherited matroid edges. A combinatorial isomorphism P_M -> P_{M'} sends cells to cells and preserves the relevant graph structure. Using the asserted explicit description of such isomorphisms as compositions of coordinate permutations, embeddings accounting for loops or coloops, and coordinate reflections accounting for dual connected components, the image of each matroid cell is again a matroid polytope and regularity of the subdivision is preserved. Thus regular matroid subdivisions of P_M and P_{M'} correspond, yielding an isomorphism of the secondary fans, and by Theorem 14 this is an isomorphism of local Dressians.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 26
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1 = (E_1,B_1) and M_2 = (E_2,B_2) be matroids on disjoint ground sets. Their direct sum M_1 ⊕ M_2 has bases B_1 ∪ B_2 with B_1 in B_1 and B_2 in B_2. Then the local Dressian of the direct sum is the product of the local Dressians: Dr(M_1 ⊕ M_2) = Dr(M_1) × Dr(M_2), as fans.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a map ⊗ from Dr(M_1) × Dr(M_2) to Dr(M_1 ⊕ M_2) by (w ⊗ v)_{B_1 ∪ B_2} = w_{B_1} + v_{B_2}. Since P_{M_1 ⊕ M_2} = P_{M_1} × P_{M_2}, any octahedral face of P_{M_1 ⊕ M_2} is of the form {e_{B_1}} × O_2 with O_2 an octahedral face of P_{M_2}, or O_1 × {e_{B_2}} with O_1 an octahedral face of P_{M_1}. Thus the local Plücker relations for w ⊗ v reduce to those for w and v, and cones map to cones. For the inverse, fix one basis B_1 ∪ B_2 of M_1 ⊕ M_2 and define φ_1(W)_{A_1} = W_{A_1 ∪ B_2} and φ_2(W)_{A_2} = W_{B_1 ∪ A_2}. These satisfy the local Plücker relations in the factors. The key point is that φ is independent, up to additive constants, of the fixed basis. If B_2 and B_2' are adjacent bases of M_2 and A,A' are adjacent bases of M_1, then the four vertices e_{A∪B_2}, e_{A∪B_2'}, e_{A'∪B_2'}, e_{A'∪B_2} form a square face of P_{M_1 ⊕ M_2}. This square cannot be subdivided matroidally, so any W in Dr(M_1 ⊕ M_2) satisfies W_{A∪B_2} - W_{A'∪B_2} = W_{A∪B_2'} - W_{A'∪B_2'}. Connectivity of the basis graph of M_1 propagates this equality, proving independence; similarly for the other factor. Then φ(w ⊗ v) = (w,v) in the tropical torus because the fixed-basis terms add constants, and W = φ_1(W) ⊗ φ_2(W) by comparing differences along adjacent bases of M_1 ⊕ M_2 and using basis-graph connectivity. Hence the two linear cone-preserving maps are inverse fan isomorphisms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let e and e' be parallel elements of M, meaning rk({e,e'}) = 1. Then Dr(M) modulo its lineality space is isomorphic to Dr(M\e') modulo its lineality space: Dr(M)/lin Dr(M) ≅ Dr(M\e')/lin Dr(M\e'). Moreover dim lin Dr(M) = dim lin Dr(M\e') + 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because {e,e'} is a circuit, deleting e' does not change the number of connected components, while it reduces the ground-set size by one. Corollary 18 therefore gives dim lin Dr(M) = dim lin Dr(M\e') + 1. The projection that forgets coordinates corresponding to bases containing e' maps Dr(M) onto Dr(M\e'). To prove injectivity modulo lineality, take W in Dr(M). Using the lineality vector sum_{B containing e'} e_B, normalize so that W_{B_e} = W_{B_{e'}} for one pair of corresponding bases B_e containing e and B_{e'} = B_e - e + e'. Now take an adjacent corresponding pair B'_e and B'_{e'} with B'_e = B'_{e'} - e' + e and B'_e at basis-graph distance one from B_e. The four vertices e_{B_e}, e_{B_{e'}}, e_{B'_{e'}}, e_{B'_e} form a square face of P_M. The set B_e ∩ B'_{e'} ∪ {e,e'} is a nonbasis adjacent to these four bases, so this square is not subdivided by the matroid subdivision induced by W. Therefore W_{B_e} + W_{B'_{e'}} = W_{B_{e'}} + W_{B'_e}. Since W_{B_e} = W_{B_{e'}}, it follows that W_{B'_e} = W_{B'_{e'}}. Iterating over the connected basis-exchange graph gives W_B = W_{B - e + e'} for every basis B containing e. Hence all forgotten coordinates are determined modulo lineality, proving the quotient isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 29, Tutte's binary-matroid criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A matroid is binary, i.e. representable over the field with two elements, if and only if it has no minor isomorphic to the uniform matroid U_{2,4}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this classical theorem of Tutte and uses it as a black box. Its role in the paper is to identify octahedral 3-faces of matroid polytopes with U_{2,4} minors: excluding U_{2,4} excludes octahedral faces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 31
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a binary matroid. Then its local Dressian Dr(M) is a linear space, namely only its lineality space. Equivalently, the matroid polytope P_M has no nontrivial matroid subdivision; in the paper's terminology, M is indecomposable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Tutte's criterion, a binary matroid has no U_{2,4} minor. An octahedral 3-face of a matroid polytope corresponds to such a U_{2,4} minor, so the 3-skeleton of P_M contains no octahedral face. Thus there is no 3-dimensional face on which a nontrivial local Plücker subdivision can occur. By Corollary 16 and the 3-skeleton rigidity behind Theorem 14, any matroid subdivision must agree with the trivial subdivision on the 3-skeleton and hence must be globally trivial. Therefore Dr(M) consists only of height functions inducing the trivial subdivision, i.e. its lineality space.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 32
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be the simple rank-3 matroid on 13 elements given by the ternary projective plane. Then Dr(P) is a 12-dimensional linear space. In particular P is indecomposable, even though P is non-binary and its matroid polytope has 117 octahedral faces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since P is connected on 13 elements, Corollary 18 gives a 12-dimensional lineality space. It remains to show there is no nontrivial matroid subdivision. The paper proves the stronger statement that P contains no proper connected submatroid. Suppose for contradiction that M is a proper connected submatroid of P, meaning every basis of M is a basis of P. Using closure operators cl_P and cl_M, one has cl_P(S) subset cl_M(S) whenever S has the same rank in P and M. The proof proceeds by relabeling after each step. First, because M is proper, some P-basis 123 is dependent in M. If it contains no parallel pair in M, then cl_M(123) has rank 2; choosing 4 outside this flat and using the projective-plane intersection cl_P(23) ∩ cl_P(14) gives an element 5 lying in cl_M(14) ∩ cl_M(123), forcing 5 parallel to 1 in M. Thus M has parallel elements. Second, if 1 and 2 are parallel in M, choose 3 outside cl_M(12) ∪ cl_P(12). Then the distinct P-lines cl_P(13) and cl_P(23) are both contained in the same M-line cl_M(13) = cl_M(23), so two P-lines collapse to one M-line. Third, suppose two P-lines, labeled 1234 and 1567, collapse to an M-line. Since M is connected, there are at least two elements 8 and 9 outside that line. Lines through 8 meeting 234 meet 567 in three different points and induce a bijection between 234 and 567 whose paired elements are parallel in M; doing the same with 9 gives a different bijection. Hence at least four parallel elements of M form a quadrilateral in P. Fourth, suppose a quadrilateral 1234 in P collapses to one M-point. Let 5,6,7 be the three diagonal intersection points cl_P(12)∩cl_P(34), cl_P(13)∩cl_P(24), and cl_P(14)∩cl_P(23). Connectedness forces an element 8 outside cl_M(1234) in addition to these; then three P-lines through 8 and through points of the quadrilateral collapse to a single M-line. Fifth, if three concurrent P-lines through 1 collapse to an M-line, let S be the nine other points on these lines. Connectedness gives at least two elements outside cl_M(S). Each such outside point partitions S into three triples via lines not passing through 1, and each triple lies in a parallelism class of M. The two partitions are transversal, forcing all of S into one parallelism class. Since the complement of S is a P-line, this makes M disconnected, contradiction. Therefore no proper connected submatroid exists, no nontrivial matroid subdivision exists, and Dr(P) is exactly its 12-dimensional lineality space.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 34
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every cell of a finest matroid subdivision of the hypersimplex Δ(2,n) is the matroid polytope of a binary matroid. Consequently every such cell is indecomposable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses known results that every matroid subdivision of Δ(2,n) is regular and arises as a sequence of compatible splits, and that Dr(2,n) equals the characteristic-zero tropical Grassmannian TGr_0(2,n). A finest subdivision of Δ(2,n) has n - 2 maximal cells. Speyer's K-theoretic result says that for a subdivision of Δ(d,n) induced by a point of TGr_0(d,n) with binom(n-2,d-1) maximal cells, all cells are graphical matroids. For d = 2 this applies to finest subdivisions, so their cells are graphical. Graphical matroids are binary, and Corollary 31 then implies that these cells are indecomposable.

proof_techniques.name: Replace tropical Plücker data by regular matroid subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically treats a valuated matroid w with fixed support M as a height function on the basis vertices of the matroid polytope P_M. The condition w in Dr(M) is converted into the condition that the lower subdivision of P_M is a matroid subdivision. This makes cones of the Dressian equal to collections of height functions inducing the same subdivision, once Theorem 14 identifies the Plücker and secondary structures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a class of tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M) can be encoded by valuated matroids or subdivisions of matroid polytopes, then an involution can be sought as a map on the corresponding subdivisions or height functions. This gives a concrete polyhedral substitute for reasoning directly with inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Three-skeleton and octahedral-face rigidity
proof_techniques.description: The key proof idea is that the three-term Plücker relations control exactly the possible subdivisions of octahedral 3-faces: an octahedron is either unsubdivided or split in one of three ways. The authors show that if two matroid subdivisions agree on the 3-skeleton, then a basis-exchange path argument forces agreement on every maximal cell. The proof propagates local agreement using small faces spanned by bases at distance at most two.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an order-reversing map on a Dressian-like poset, it may be enough to define and verify it on local rank-2 or U_{2,4}-type configurations and then use 3-skeleton rigidity to force global consistency. This is especially relevant if the flat-lattice involution acts predictably on minors corresponding to octahedral faces.

proof_techniques.name: Duality through coordinate reflection of matroid polytopes
proof_techniques.description: The paper records that P_{M*} is the image of P_M under the affine reflection x_i -> 1 - x_i, and Proposition 24 shows that combinatorial isomorphisms of matroid polytopes induce isomorphisms of local Dressians. More generally, dualizing connected components is one of the operations that preserves the combinatorial type of a matroid polytope.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing flat-lattice involution resembles a matroid duality or polarity. If the desired Phi can be related to dualizing valuated matroids or reflecting matroid polytopes, Proposition 24 supplies a tested mechanism for transporting local Dressian structure under such dualities.

proof_techniques.name: Direct-sum product decomposition
proof_techniques.description: For disjoint matroids M_1 and M_2, the paper proves Dr(M_1 ⊕ M_2) = Dr(M_1) × Dr(M_2). The construction sends two valuated matroids w and v to the sum height function w_{B_1} + v_{B_2} on product bases. The inverse is obtained by slicing along a fixed basis in the other factor, and independence of the slice is proved using square faces and connectedness of basis graphs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The flat-lattice embedding in the original problem uses matroids of the form M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}. Product decompositions and behavior under loop summands may help analyze how any extension Phi must act on these embedded flat-associated tropical linear spaces.

proof_techniques.name: Square-face rigidity plus basis-graph propagation
proof_techniques.description: Several proofs use the fact that certain square faces of matroid polytopes cannot be subdivided matroidally. This yields affine equalities among heights on the four vertices of the square. Because the basis exchange graph of a matroid is connected, equalities verified for adjacent bases propagate to all bases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful uniqueness tool: if an extension of a flat-level operation is known on some adjacent bases or local pieces, square relations may force its values elsewhere. It may also help prove that a candidate map is independent of arbitrary choices, as in the direct-sum theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Lineality quotient and simplification by parallel elements
proof_techniques.description: The lineality of Dr(M) is exactly the space of adding linear functions on the ambient coordinates to the height function on P_M. Theorem 28 shows that deleting a parallel element changes only the lineality, not the essential quotient Dr(M)/lin Dr(M). Thus the nontrivial subdivision information is invariant under simplifying parallel classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When testing an involution or comparing parameter spaces, one can often quotient out coordinate scalings or lineality first. Parallel-element deletion suggests reducing to simplified matroids before addressing the essential order-reversing structure.

proof_techniques.name: Indecomposability from absence or rigidity of octahedra
proof_techniques.description: Binary matroids have no U_{2,4} minor, hence their matroid polytopes have no octahedral 3-faces; by 3-skeleton rigidity their local Dressians are linear. The ternary projective plane shows a stronger phenomenon: even with many octahedral faces, global incidence constraints may still prevent any nontrivial matroid subdivision.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In cases where the relevant Dressian-like space has no nontrivial subdivision parameters, the original question may reduce to the flat-lattice involution itself. Conversely, non-binary examples with rigid local Dressians can serve as test cases for whether an involution exists for reasons unrelated to local subdivision freedom.

proof_techniques.name: Loopless initial matroid criterion for tropical linear spaces
proof_techniques.description: For a valuated matroid v, the tropical linear space L_v is described pointwise by initial matroids M_x: a point x lies in L_v exactly when M_x has no loops. The paper also recalls that the cells of L_v correspond to loopless matroids appearing in the subdivision induced by v, and bounded cells correspond to those with no coloops.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Inclusion of tropical linear spaces inside a Bergman fan or another tropical linear space can potentially be checked through initial matroids and looplessness. This criterion may help translate order-reversal by flats into statements about contractions, loops, and initial matroids.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-stratum viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The local Dressian Dr(M) is realized as a boundary stratum of the compactified global Dressian Dr(d,n), namely the stratum where exactly the nonbases of M have coordinate infinity. This connects fixed-support valuated matroids with degenerations of arbitrary valuated matroids.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the relative Dressian in the original problem can be stratified by supports or underlying matroids, this boundary-stratum picture suggests analyzing each support stratum separately and then checking compatibility of a candidate involution across strata.

other_useful_info: The paper's notation Dr(M) denotes the local Dressian of a fixed-support matroid M, not the relative Dressian of tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M). It recalls that when a valuated matroid v has only 0 and infinity coordinates, L_v is the Bergman fan of the support matroid; for general v, the recession fan of L_v is the Bergman fan of the underlying matroid, and locally near x the tropical linear space is the Bergman fan of the initial matroid M_x. The bounded subcomplex of L_v is the tight span, with bounded cells corresponding to matroids with no coloops. Remark 12 notes that defining local Dressians using only three-term Plücker relations agrees with the definition using all quadratic Plücker relations, citing Baker-Bowler's language of weak and strong matroids over the tropical hyperfield. Examples computed in the paper include Dr(U_{2,4}) = Dr(2,4), a 5-dimensional pure balanced fan in R^6/R1 with three maximal cones and 3-dimensional lineality, and Dr(U_{1,2} ⊕ U_{1,2}), a 2-dimensional linear space in R^4/R1. The final section conjectures that all matroids arising from projective spaces over finite fields are indecomposable; it also asks whether there exist connected matroids M and M' with P_{M'} strictly contained in P_M but no matroid subdivision of P_M has P_{M'} as a cell, and asks whether all cells in finest matroid subdivisions of hypersimplices are indecomposable.
why_relevant: Analyzes the Dressian of valuated matroids with fixed underlying matroid and its fan structure via matroid subdivisions. Although local Dressians differ from relative Dressians, their subdivision techniques are important for understanding the ambient valuated-matroid geometry.

title: Matroidal Representations of Groups
overall_summary: The paper develops a framework for representation theory over an idempotent semifield S by studying group actions on tropical linear spaces, equivalently on S-valued valuated matroids. Its main technical language is the tropical exterior algebra: tropical linear spaces are encoded by projective tropical Plücker vectors, and monomial linear maps act on the Dressian by reindexing and rescaling Plücker coordinates. The part most relevant to the original problem is the paper's use of tropical Hodge star and valuated matroid duality: when S is totally ordered, the Hodge star sends a tropical linear space T in V to its orthogonal dual T^⊥ in V^∨, and this operation is compatible with group actions up to projective scalar. The paper also proves a tropical Maschke-type construction: for a finite group, averaging the standard inner product gives a G-equivariant nondegenerate pairing V ≅ V^∨, so orthogonal duals can be transported back into the original ambient space. These results provide a citable mechanism for constructing order-reversing duality operations on tropical linear spaces, though the paper works with ordinary Dressians in free modules rather than relative Dressians inside Trop(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: §2.1 definitions and recalled facts: tropical Plücker vectors and tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be an idempotent semifield, let V ≅ S^n have basis e_1,...,e_n, and let x_1,...,x_n be the dual basis of V^∨. The tropical exterior algebra ∧V is the quotient of the symmetric algebra by the congruence generated by e_i^2 ∼ 0; for I = {i_1,...,i_d}, the elements e_I = e_{i_1} ∧ ... ∧ e_{i_d} form a basis of ∧^d V. A rank d tropical Plücker vector is a nonzero vector v = Σ_{|I|=d} v_I e_I in ∧^d V satisfying the bend-form tropical Plücker relations: for every A in binomial([n],d+1), every B in binomial([n],d-1), and every j in A \ B, one has Σ_{i in A\B} v_{A\i} v_{B∪i} = Σ_{i in (A\B)\j} v_{A\i} v_{B∪i}. To such v the paper associates a tropical linear space T_v ⊆ V, defined as the tropical kernel of the wedge multiplication map -∧v: V → ∧^{d+1}V; concretely, T_v is the intersection of tropical hyperplanes defined by the valuated circuit vectors Σ_{i in A} v_{A\i} x_i for A in binomial([n],d+1). If S is totally ordered, T_v can equivalently be described as the S-submodule spanned by the valuated cocircuit vectors Σ_{i in [n]\B} v_{B∪i} e_i for B in binomial([n],d-1). Tropical linear spaces depend only on the projective class of v, and the projective tropical Plücker vector can be recovered from T_v by an appropriate top wedge power, as cited from GG18, Proposition 6.2.1. When S is the Boolean semifield B = {0,1}, a tropical Plücker vector is exactly the basis-indicator vector of a matroid, and T_v is the B-module spanned by the indicator vectors of the cocircuits.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This portion is primarily definitional and is recalled from earlier work, especially GG18 and CGM17. The exterior algebra basis e_I survives because the relations kill repeated indices. The bend relations are the idempotent-semiring replacement for the condition that the maximum in each three-term or multi-term tropical Plücker relation is attained at least twice; over B these relations are exactly strong basis exchange for the support set of bases. The circuit vectors are obtained by the coefficients of the wedge map V → ∧^{d+1}V: for A of size d+1, the coefficient pattern Σ_{i in A} v_{A\i} x_i defines a tropical hyperplane, and intersecting all such hyperplanes gives the tropical kernel. In the totally ordered case, the cited theory identifies this kernel with the span of the cocircuit vectors, which are the dual coefficient patterns indexed by B of size d−1. The recovery of v from T_v by top wedge power is also cited rather than reproved in this paper; it is used later to pass between invariant tropical linear spaces and projective fixed points in the Dressian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be an idempotent semifield and V ≅ S^n a finite free S-module. A symmetric bilinear form ⟨−,−⟩: V × V → S is called nondegenerate if the induced S-linear map φ: V → V^∨, v ↦ ⟨v,−⟩, is an isomorphism. If φ is injective, then φ is automatically an isomorphism; hence any symmetric bilinear form inducing an injective map V → V^∨ is nondegenerate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let φ: V → V^∨ be the injective map induced by the form. The proof uses Frenk's result, cited as Fre13, Lemma 3.2.1(ii) and the subsequent remark, that for an injective map of finite free modules over an idempotent semifield, the dual map φ^∨ is surjective after identifying V with V^∨∨. Given any f in V^∨, choose v in V such that φ^∨(ev_v) = f, where ev_v: V^∨ → S evaluates a linear form at v. Unpacking the definition, f is the functional w ↦ ev_v(φ(w)) = ⟨w,v⟩. By symmetry of the bilinear form, ⟨w,v⟩ = ⟨v,w⟩, so f = φ(v). Thus every f lies in the image of φ, proving surjectivity and hence nondegeneracy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Background fact cited before Definition 3.0.1: automorphisms of free modules are monomial
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite free module V ≅ S^n over an idempotent semifield S, every basis is obtained from any fixed basis by permuting and rescaling the basis vectors by elements of S×. Equivalently, the group of S-linear automorphisms is GL(V) ≅ Σ_n ⋉ (S×)^n, i.e. every automorphism is represented in a fixed basis by a monomial matrix.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites GG18, Proposition 2.2.2. The underlying idea is that finite free modules over an idempotent semifield have a rigid basis theory: an automorphism must send a basis to a basis, and all bases are related by a permutation and nonzero scalar rescalings. Therefore each column and each row of an invertible matrix has exactly one nonzero entry, so the matrix is monomial. This fact is used throughout the paper to reduce all equivariance checks to permutations of coordinates and multiplication by invertible scalars.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.0.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a group, let S be an idempotent semifield, let V ≅ S^n be a finite free S-module, and let G → GL(V) be a linear representation. For each d, the induced linear action of G on ∧^d V preserves the tropical Plücker locus: if v in ∧^d V satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, then g·v also satisfies the tropical Plücker relations for every g in G. Consequently the G-action on ∧^d V descends to an action on the Dressian Dr(d,n) ⊆ P(∧^d V).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix g in G. Because every automorphism is monomial, write g as a permutation σ of [n] followed by diagonal rescaling with entries λ_1,...,λ_n in S×. For J ⊆ [n], write λ_J = product_{j in J} λ_j. If v = Σ v_I e_I, then g·v = Σ λ_I v_I e_{σ(I)} = Σ λ_{σ^{-1}(I)} v_{σ^{-1}(I)} e_I. To verify a Plücker relation for g·v at C in binomial([n],d+1), D in binomial([n],d−1), compare the sum over i in C\D. After setting A = σ^{-1}(C), B = σ^{-1}(D), and j = σ^{-1}(i), each term contains the scalar factor λ_{A\j}λ_{B∪j} = λ_A λ_B, independent of j. Thus the whole Plücker sum for g·v is λ_A λ_B times the corresponding Plücker sum for v. Since the bend relation for v says that deleting any one term does not change the sum, the same is true after multiplication by this common factor. Hence g·v is again tropical Plücker.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.0.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G → GL(V) be a linear representation over an idempotent semifield S. If v in ∧^d V is a tropical Plücker vector and T_v ⊆ V is its associated tropical linear space, then for every g in G the tropical linear space associated to the tropical Plücker vector g·v is exactly the image of T_v under g: T_{g·v} = g·T_v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write g as a permutation σ followed by diagonal rescaling λ_1,...,λ_n, and again set λ_J = product_{j in J} λ_j. For p = Σ p_i e_i in T_v, one has g·p = Σ λ_{σ^{-1}(i)}p_{σ^{-1}(i)}e_i. The valuated circuit vector of g·v associated to A in binomial([n],d+1) is Σ_{i in A} λ_{σ^{-1}(A\i)} v_{σ^{-1}(A\i)} x_i. Evaluating this circuit on g·p gives λ_{σ^{-1}(A)} times the corresponding valuated circuit expression for v evaluated at p, after reindexing by j = σ^{-1}(i). Since p lies in T_v, the tropical hyperplane condition for that circuit is satisfied; multiplying by the nonzero scalar λ_{σ^{-1}(A)} preserves it. Thus g·T_v is contained in T_{g·v}. Both are tropical linear spaces of the same rank d, and the paper invokes the standard fact that containment of tropical linear spaces of the same rank forces equality, citing CGM17 and Oxley.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.0.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a fixed linear representation G → GL(V) over an idempotent semifield S, d-dimensional tropical subrepresentations T ⊆ V are exactly fixed points of the induced G-action on the Dressian Dr(d,n) ⊆ P(∧^d V). Here a tropical subrepresentation means a G-invariant tropical linear space T of rank d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If T = T_v is represented by the projective tropical Plücker vector [v], Proposition 3.0.3 gives T_{g·v} = g·T_v. Thus T is G-invariant exactly when T_{g·v} = T_v for all g. Since the tropical linear space determines its projective Plücker vector, as recalled from GG18, this is equivalent to [g·v] = [v] in the projective Dressian for all g. Conversely, if [v] is fixed projectively, then T_{g·v} = T_v, so Proposition 3.0.3 implies g·T_v = T_v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.1 and Lemma 3.1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S = B be the Boolean semifield, let V ≅ B^n, and let G → GL(V) be a linear representation. A tropical G-representation T ⊆ V is equivalent to a group homomorphism G → Aut(M(T)), where M(T) is the matroid corresponding to the Boolean tropical linear space T. The matroid-theoretic fact used is: a permutation of the ground set is an automorphism of a matroid M if and only if it sends cocircuits of M to cocircuits of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Over B there are no nontrivial units, so GL(B^n) is just the symmetric group on the coordinate basis; a linear G-action is a permutation action on the ground set [n]. A Boolean tropical linear space T is generated by the indicator vectors of the cocircuits of its matroid M(T). Hence T is G-invariant exactly when the G-action permutes these cocircuit vectors. Lemma 3.1.3 proves that this condition is the same as preserving the matroid. One direction: if σ is a matroid automorphism, it sends independent sets to independent sets, hence dependent sets to dependent sets, hence minimal dependent sets, i.e. circuits, to circuits. Since cocircuits of M are circuits of the dual matroid M*, and a permutation is an automorphism of M iff it is an automorphism of M*, σ sends cocircuits to cocircuits. The converse is obtained by reversing this argument through duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2.2, Proposition 3.2.4, and Corollary 3.2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a field with valuation ν: k → S to an idempotent semifield, fix W ≅ k^n with basis, and let V ≅ S^n be the corresponding tropical free module. Entrywise valuation sends the group Σ_n ⋉ (k×)^n of monomial matrices over k to GL(V), and this map is a group homomorphism. If G acts monomially on W and the corresponding tropicalized representation acts on V, then for every linear subspace L ⊆ W and every g in G one has trop(g·L) = g·trop(L), where trop(L) is the S-module generated by valuations of vectors in L. In particular, if L is G-invariant, then trop(L) is a tropical subrepresentation of V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For monomial matrices, every nonzero entry of a product is a single product of nonzero entries, not a sum of several terms. Therefore multiplicativity of the valuation ν immediately implies that entrywise valuation respects matrix multiplication on the monomial subgroup. For the compatibility with tropicalization, a vector w in W is transformed by a monomial matrix by permuting coordinates and multiplying each coordinate by a scalar. Applying ν gives the same coordinate permutation and scalar multiplication in S, because ν(ab) = ν(a)ν(b). Therefore the set of valuations of vectors in g·L is exactly the image under the tropicalized monomial matrix of the set of valuations of vectors in L, and taking the generated S-submodules gives trop(g·L) = g·trop(L). If g·L = L for all g, this equality gives G-invariance of trop(L).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: §3.3 recalled facts from GG18: stable sum, Hodge star, orthogonal dual, and stable intersection
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V ≅ S^n be a finite free module with basis e_1,...,e_n and fix an identification ∧^n V ≅ S by choosing e_[n] as generator. The tropical Hodge star is the S-linear isomorphism ⋆: ∧^d V → ∧^{n−d} V^∨ sending e_I to x_[n]\I. If v in ∧^d V and v′ in ∧^{d′} V are tropical Plücker vectors and v∧v′ is nonzero, then v∧v′ is a tropical Plücker vector and T_{v∧v′} is the stable sum of T_v and T_{v′}. If S is totally ordered, the Hodge star sends a valuated matroid to its dual valuated matroid and sends the tropical linear space T_v to its orthogonal dual T_v^⊥ ⊆ V^∨, meaning T_{⋆v} = T_v^⊥; here the orthogonal dual of a submodule M ⊆ V is the intersection of all tropical hyperplanes in V^∨ defined by elements of M, viewing V ≅ V^∨∨. The stable intersection of T_v and T_{v′} is T_{⋆(⋆v ∧ ⋆v′)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls these facts from GG18, especially Propositions 5.1.1 and 5.1.2 and §4.4, rather than reproving them. The wedge product of Plücker vectors implements the tropical stable sum operation; algebraically, its coefficients are the tropical analogues of the Plücker coordinates of a sum, and GG18 proves they again satisfy tropical Plücker relations when the wedge is nonzero. The Hodge star complements index sets I ↦ [n]\I, so on Boolean matroids it turns bases into complementary bases, i.e. matroid duality; in the valuated setting this is valuated matroid duality. The equality T_{⋆v} = T_v^⊥ identifies this coordinate-level duality with the tropical hyperplane definition of orthogonality. Stable intersection is then obtained by dualizing, taking stable sum in the dual, and dualizing back.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G → GL(V) be a linear representation over an idempotent semifield S, let ⋆: ∧^d V → ∧^{n−d}V^∨ be the tropical Hodge star determined by the chosen basis and volume form, and let g in G. With the induced actions on ∧^d V and ∧^{n−d}V^∨, one has g·⋆v = (det g)^{-1} ⋆(g·v) for every v in ∧^d V. If g is represented by a monomial matrix equal to a permutation σ followed by diagonal scalars λ_1,...,λ_n, then det(g) in this formula is λ_1...λ_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By linearity it suffices to check basis elements e_I. Write λ_J = product_{j in J} λ_j. Then g·e_I = λ_I e_{σ(I)}, so ⋆(g·e_I) = λ_I x_[n]\σ(I). On the other hand, ⋆e_I = x_[n]\I, and the dual action sends this to (1/λ_[n]\I) x_{σ([n]\I)}. Since σ([n]\I) = [n]\σ(I), these two expressions have the same basis vector. Their scalar factors differ by λ_I λ_[n]\I = λ_[n] = det(g), giving g·⋆e_I = (det g)^{-1}⋆(g·e_I).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a linear representation G → GL(V) over an idempotent semifield S. The stable sum and stable intersection of tropical subrepresentations of V are again tropical subrepresentations. If S is totally ordered, then the orthogonal dual T^⊥ ⊆ V^∨ of a tropical subrepresentation T ⊆ V is a tropical subrepresentation of the dual representation G → GL(V^∨).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the orthogonal dual statement, let T = T_v be G-invariant. By Corollary 3.0.4, the projective Plücker vector [v] is G-fixed, so for each g there is a nonzero scalar λ with g·v = λv. Lemma 3.3.1 gives g·⋆v = (λ/det g)⋆v, so [⋆v] is fixed projectively. When S is totally ordered, T_{⋆v} = T_v^⊥ by the recalled Hodge-star duality, hence T^⊥ is invariant under the dual representation. For stable sums, if T_v and T_{v′} are invariant and g·v = λv, g·v′ = λ′v′, then g·(v∧v′) = (g·v)∧(g·v′) = λλ′(v∧v′), so the projective Plücker vector of the stable sum is fixed. For stable intersections, use the formula T_v ∩_st T_{v′} = T_{⋆(⋆v∧⋆v′)} and Lemma 3.3.1 repeatedly; the paper computes g·⋆(⋆v∧⋆v′) = (λλ′)/(det g)^3 ⋆(⋆v∧⋆v′), which is again projective fixedness. Corollary 3.0.4 then converts these fixed Plücker vectors back into G-invariant tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite group acting linearly on V ≅ S^n over an idempotent semifield S, and let ⟨−,−⟩ be the standard inner product on V with respect to the chosen basis. Define the averaged form ⟨u,w⟩_G = Σ_{g in G} ⟨g·u,g·w⟩. Then ⟨−,−⟩_G is a nondegenerate symmetric bilinear form on V. If S is totally ordered and T ⊆ V is a tropical subrepresentation, then the isomorphism V^∨ ≅ V induced by this averaged form sends T^⊥ ⊆ V^∨ to a tropical subrepresentation of V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Symmetry and bilinearity are immediate from the definition. To prove nondegeneracy, Proposition 2.2.1 reduces the task to proving that the induced map V → V^∨ is injective. Write each g as a permutation σ_g followed by diagonal scalars λ_{g,1},...,λ_{g,n}. For v = Σ v_i e_i, the jth coordinate of the associated functional is ⟨v,e_j⟩_G = Σ_{g in G} ⟨Σ_i v_i λ_{g,i}e_{σ_g(i)}, λ_{g,j}e_{σ_g(j)}⟩ = v_j Σ_{g in G} λ_{g,j}^2. The scalar c_j = Σ_g λ_{g,j}^2 is nonzero, hence invertible in the semifield, so v_j is recovered from ⟨v,e_j⟩_G. Thus the map is injective and the form is nondegenerate. The averaged form is G-invariant because left multiplication permutes the finite index set G in the sum. Therefore the induced isomorphism V → V^∨, equivalently V^∨ → V, is G-equivariant: ⟨g·v,−⟩_G = ⟨v,g^{-1}·−⟩_G, matching the dual action. By Theorem 3.3.2, T^⊥ is a tropical subrepresentation of V^∨. Applying a G-equivariant isomorphism sends it to a G-invariant tropical linear space in V, hence to a tropical subrepresentation of V.

proof_techniques.name: Plücker-vector-first approach to tropical linear spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper consistently represents a tropical linear space T by a projective tropical Plücker vector v in ∧^d V. Geometric assertions about T are then checked algebraically on Plücker coordinates using the tropical Plücker bend relations. Because T_v determines [v], invariance or equality of tropical linear spaces can be tested projectively on the Dressian.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a relative Dressian element, one can try to work with its Plücker vector plus the additional conditions expressing containment in Trop(M). A proposed involution Φ can then be checked by showing that a coordinate operation sends the relevant Plücker vectors to Plücker vectors satisfying the same relative containment constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Hodge star as valuated matroid duality
proof_techniques.description: After choosing a volume form, the tropical Hodge star sends e_I to x_[n]\I. In the totally ordered setting, the paper recalls that this operation sends a tropical Plücker vector to the dual valuated matroid and sends the corresponding tropical linear space to its orthogonal dual in the dual free module. Stable intersection is also expressed by dualizing, wedging, and dualizing back.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the closest mechanism in the paper to an order-reversing involution: duality reverses containment by turning a subspace into an orthogonal object in the dual ambient module. A plausible strategy for the original problem is to combine this Hodge-star duality with an identification of the dual ambient tropical space with the original one induced by the flat-lattice polarity.

proof_techniques.name: Equivariance only up to scalar on projective Dressians
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses that tropical linear spaces depend only on projective Plücker classes. Thus an operation need not commute strictly with an action; it suffices to commute up to a nonzero scalar. Lemma 3.3.1 is the model calculation: g·⋆v equals (det g)^{-1}⋆(g·v), and the determinant factor disappears in projective space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When attempting to extend F ↦ F^⊥ to all of Dr(M), scalar ambiguities in Plücker coordinates should be harmless. One can aim to define Φ on representatives and prove compatibility only in tropical projective space.

proof_techniques.name: Monomial reindexing and common-factor verification of Plücker relations
proof_techniques.description: Because all free-module automorphisms over an idempotent semifield are monomial, preservation of tropical Plücker relations reduces to a reindexing calculation.
