In Proposition 3.0.2, every term in a transformed Plücker relation receives the same scalar factor λ_A λ_B after reindexing, so the bend relation is preserved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the given flat-lattice involution is induced, even partly, by a permutation or monomial transformation on the ground set after dualizing, the paper gives the exact calculation needed to prove that the induced coordinate operation preserves the Dressian equations.

proof_techniques.name: Boolean cocircuit-span dictionary
proof_techniques.description: In the Boolean case, tropical linear spaces are matroids and are generated as B-modules by cocircuit indicator vectors. The paper uses this to translate G-invariance of a tropical linear space into preservation of cocircuits, and then into ordinary matroid automorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural embedded flats F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) are matroidal objects. Cocircuit or flat descriptions may be the most efficient way to verify that a candidate duality operation agrees with F ↦ F^⊥ on the lattice part of the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Averaging a tropical inner product to identify V and V^∨
proof_techniques.description: For a finite group action, the paper averages the standard inner product over the group. The averaged form is nondegenerate because each coordinate pairing is multiplied by a nonzero scalar c_j = Σ_g λ_{g,j}^2, so the coordinates of a vector can still be recovered. The form is G-invariant, hence gives a G-equivariant isomorphism V ≅ V^∨.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The Hodge-star dual naturally lands in a dual ambient module. If the matroid polarity in the original problem can be represented by, or constructed from, a finite symmetry or a suitable bilinear pairing, this averaging argument suggests a way to transport orthogonal duals back into the original ambient tropical projective space.

proof_techniques.name: Stable operations via wedge and duality
proof_techniques.description: Stable sum is represented by wedging tropical Plücker vectors, while stable intersection is represented by applying Hodge star, wedging, and applying Hodge star again. The paper proves these operations preserve tropical subrepresentations by checking projective fixedness of the corresponding Plücker vectors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The poset structure on Dr(M) is by inclusion, and order-reversing maps often interchange sum-like and intersection-like operations. These wedge/Hodge formulas give algebraic tests for whether a proposed Φ has the expected behavior with respect to tropical stable sum or stable intersection.

proof_techniques.name: Transport of tropical linear spaces under free-module isomorphisms
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the fact that any isomorphism of finite free S-modules sends tropical linear spaces to tropical linear spaces. In the monomial setting this follows concretely from the compatibility of the action with tropical Plücker vectors and valuated circuits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one constructs an identification between the dual of the ambient module and the original module from the flat-lattice involution, this fact is needed to ensure that the image of an orthogonal dual is still a tropical linear space.

other_useful_info: The orthogonal-dual statements in the paper require S to be totally ordered; this includes the Boolean and standard max-plus tropical settings, but the paper is careful that arbitrary partially ordered idempotent semifields are subtler. The paper's orthogonal dual T^⊥ first lives in V^∨; returning to V requires an additional nondegenerate pairing or isomorphism V^∨ ≅ V, as in Theorem 3.3.3. Example 3.3.4 shows that the tropical Maschke complement need not behave like a classical direct-sum complement: for the defining representation of Σ_3, the two-dimensional tropical complement to the trivial representation contains the trivial tropical line. The key external reference for the Hodge star, stable sum/intersection, orthogonal duality, and recovery of Plücker vectors from tropical linear spaces is GG18, A Grassmann algebra for matroids; CGM17 is cited for the module-theoretic matroid viewpoint and same-rank containment facts.
why_relevant: Develops tropical linear spaces over idempotent semifields and includes an orthogonal-complement operation via valuated matroid duality. This is relevant to any attempt to build a polarity-like involution on tropical subspaces.

title: Lorentzian Polynomials
overall_summary: The paper develops the theory of Lorentzian homogeneous polynomials with nonnegative coefficients and proves that this analytic class is exactly controlled by M-convex supports and one-positive-eigenvalue Hessian conditions. Its most relevant contribution for matroidal/tropical questions is the equivalence between M-convex functions, valuated matroids, and tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over Puiseux series; in particular, the Dressian of a matroid with basis set B is identified, up to sign and closure, with tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials whose support is B. The paper also proves robust closure properties under nonnegative linear substitutions, directional derivatives, polarization/projection, and certain linear operators, giving a toolbox for constructing and transporting valuated matroids. Topologically, strata of Lorentzian polynomials with fixed M-convex support, including fixed matroid-basis support, are nonempty and contractible via coefficient-power homotopies. These results could be useful for the original problem whenever elements of a relative Dressian are encoded by valuated matroids or M-convex functions and one wants to construct maps, lifts, or deformations compatible with matroid minors or flats.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L^d_n denote the paper's closed Lorentzian class of degree d homogeneous polynomials in variables w_1,...,w_n with nonnegative coefficients: for d=0,1 it is the stable class, for d=2 it is the class of quadratic stable polynomials with nonnegative coefficients, and for d>2 it consists of polynomials with M-convex support whose first partial derivatives all lie in L^{d-1}_n. If f is in L^d_n, if i,j are indices in {1,...,n}, and if θ is a nonnegative real number, then (1+θ w_i ∂_j)f is again in L^d_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first checks the support. Lemma 2.8 shows that if f has M-convex support, then applying 1+θ w_i ∂_j preserves M-convexity; the proof of that lemma realizes the support operation by elementary splitting of a variable, restriction to an interval, and elementary aggregation, using the fact from discrete convex analysis that splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity. It remains to prove that every quadratic derivative is stable. For a multi-index α of weight d-2, write g=(1+θ w_i ∂_j)f. If α_i=0, then ∂^α g=∂^α f+θ w_i ∂^{α+e_j}f, and stability follows from the standard facts that derivatives of stable polynomials are stable and interlace the original polynomial. If α_i>0, then ∂^α g=∂^α f+θ α_i ∂^{α-e_i+e_j}f+θ w_i ∂^{α+e_j}f; the first two derivative terms are controlled by interlacing relations coming from Lemma 2.9, and positive linear combinations preserve stability in the required interlacing cone. The only delicate case is when ∂^{α+e_j}f vanishes; the argument reduces to a cubic, perturbs f by h_s=(1+s w_i ∂_{i_1})f to create the missing mixed derivative, applies the nondegenerate case, and then lets s tend to 0, using stability as a closed condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f(w_1,...,w_n) be in L^d_n, and let A be an n by m matrix with nonnegative real entries. If v=(v_1,...,v_m), then the homogeneous polynomial f(Av) in the variables v_1,...,v_m lies in L^d_m. In words, Lorentzian polynomials are closed under arbitrary nonnegative linear changes of variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces an arbitrary nonnegative matrix to three elementary operations: elementary splitting of a variable, dilation of a variable by a scalar θ≥0, and diagonalization/identification of two variables. Splitting is obtained by the limit lim_{k→∞}(1+w_{n+1}∂_n/k)^k f=f(w_1,...,w_{n-1},w_n+w_{n+1}); Proposition 2.7 ensures each finite stage is Lorentzian, while a separate M-convexity check is made before taking the limit. Dilation is immediate for θ>0 and follows at θ=0 because setting a variable to zero preserves M-convexity and the derivative conditions. Diagonalization is treated similarly to splitting by applying lim_{k→∞}(1+w_{n-1}∂_n/k)^k and then setting w_n=0. Since every nonnegative linear substitution is a composition of these operations, the theorem follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If f is in L^d_n and a_1,...,a_n are nonnegative real numbers, then the nonnegative directional derivative Σ_i a_i ∂_i f lies in L^{d-1}_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 2.10 to the polynomial f after adjoining a new variable w_{n+1} via the nonnegative substitution w_i ↦ w_i+a_i w_{n+1}. The resulting polynomial g(w,w_{n+1})=f(w_1+a_1w_{n+1},...,w_n+a_nw_{n+1}) lies in L^d_{n+1}. Its derivative ∂_{n+1}g lies in L^{d-1}_{n+1} by definition of L^d. Finally set w_{n+1}=0 using another nonnegative linear substitution, obtaining Σ_i a_i ∂_i f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a nonzero homogeneous polynomial of degree d≥2 in real variables w_1,...,w_n. If f is strictly Lorentzian, then for every w in the positive orthant R^n_{>0}, the Hessian matrix H_f(w) is nonsingular. If f is Lorentzian, then for every w in R^n_{>0}, H_f(w) has exactly one positive eigenvalue.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof proceeds by induction on d. For d=2 the assertion is part of the definition and the quadratic characterization in Lemma 2.5. For d≥3, Lemma 2.15 gives a kernel identity: if the Hessians of all first derivatives ∂_i f each have exactly one positive eigenvalue at w, then ker H_f(w) is the intersection of the kernels of the Hessians H_{∂_i f}(w). This identity uses Euler's formula for homogeneous functions, the relation (d-2)H_f=Σ_i w_i H_{∂_i f}, and the fact that a matrix with exactly one positive eigenvalue is negative semidefinite on the hyperplane orthogonal to a positive direction. Induction gives nonsingularity for the derivative Hessians in the strict case, so H_f is nonsingular. For the one-positive-eigenvalue assertion, the theorem first handles stable polynomials by showing the quadratic part of f(z+w) is stable and hence has one positive eigenvalue; since the strictly Lorentzian cone is connected by the Nuij-type homotopy and nonsingularity prevents eigenvalues from crossing zero, the signature persists. General Lorentzian polynomials follow by approximation by strictly Lorentzian polynomials.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.17 and Proposition 2.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a homogeneous polynomial of degree d≥2 with nonnegative coefficients. If H_f(w) has exactly one positive eigenvalue for every w in R^n_{>0}, then for all i,j and all w in R^n_{≥0}, one has f(w) ∂_i∂_j f(w) ≤ 2(1-1/d) ∂_i f(w) ∂_j f(w). Consequently every polynomial in L^d_n is c-Rayleigh with c=2(1-1/d), meaning that for every multi-index α, every i,j, and every w in R^n_{≥0}, ∂^α f(w) ∂^{α+e_i+e_j}f(w) ≤ c ∂^{α+e_i}f(w) ∂^{α+e_j}f(w), with the same c valid because 2(1-1/e) increases with the degree e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix w in the positive orthant and write H=H_f(w). Euler's formula gives w^T H w=d(d-1)f(w) and w^T H e_i=(d-1)∂_i f(w). For a real parameter t, restrict H to the plane spanned by w and v_t=e_i+t e_j. Since H has exactly one positive eigenvalue, Cauchy interlacing implies that the determinant of this two-dimensional restriction is nonpositive. Expanding the determinant yields a quadratic inequality in t. A weaker quadratic inequality, retaining only the mixed second derivative term, must hold for all t; requiring its discriminant to be nonpositive gives f ∂_i∂_j f ≤ 2(1-1/d)∂_i f ∂_j f. The Lorentzian c-Rayleigh statement applies this estimate to all derivatives of f, using Theorem 2.16 for the Hessian signature of each nonzero Lorentzian derivative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative coefficients. If f is c-Rayleigh for some positive real number c, in the sense that all inequalities ∂^α f(w) ∂^{α+e_i+e_j}f(w) ≤ c ∂^{α+e_i}f(w) ∂^{α+e_j}f(w) hold for all multi-indices α, indices i,j, and w in R^n_{≥0}, then the support of f is an M-convex subset of N^n. Here M-convex means that for any α,β in the support and any i with α_i>β_i, there exists j with α_j<β_j such that α-e_i+e_j and β-e_j+e_i are both in the support.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first establishes two structural lemmas for supports of c-Rayleigh polynomials. Lemma 2.22(1) proves interval convexity: if α and β are in the support and α≤γ≤β coordinatewise, then γ is in the support. This is proved by choosing a minimal counterexample, reducing by contractions and deletions, and evaluating the Rayleigh inequality near the origin to force a contradiction between the lowest-order terms. Lemma 2.22(2) proves that if f(0)≠0, then supp(f) is M^natural-convex; this uses interval convexity plus the augmentation criterion of Lemma 2.21 and again argues by minimal counterexample, reducing to a violation of a Rayleigh inequality after differentiating by α-e_i. For homogeneous f, translate by the positive vector 1: g(w)=f(w+1) is c-Rayleigh by Lemma 2.20 and has nonzero constant term. Thus supp(g) is M^natural-convex, so the support of its homogenization is M-convex. The original support of f is obtained by intersecting this homogenized M-convex set with a coordinate hyperplane, and such intersections preserve M-convexity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n and d, the closure in the coefficient topology of the strictly Lorentzian cone °L^d_n is exactly L^d_n. Equivalently, a homogeneous degree d polynomial with nonnegative coefficients is Lorentzian if and only if its support is M-convex and every derivative of order d-2 is a Lorentzian quadratic, i.e. a quadratic form with nonnegative coefficients whose Hessian has at most one positive eigenvalue.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One inclusion is Theorem 2.13: every polynomial in L^d_n is a limit of strictly Lorentzian polynomials. The proof of Theorem 2.13 uses a Nuij-type homotopy: first apply a positive averaging substitution S(θ,f) that sends f toward (w_1+...+w_n)^d while keeping the polynomial in L^d_n by Theorem 2.10; then apply the operator T_n(θ,−)=∏_{i<n}(1+θ w_i∂_n)^d. Lemma 2.12 shows that for θ>0 this produces a strictly Lorentzian polynomial, and as θ→0 one recovers f. For the reverse inclusion, limits of strictly Lorentzian polynomials are limits of c-Rayleigh polynomials by Proposition 2.19, hence are c-Rayleigh; Theorem 2.23 forces their supports to be M-convex. The derivative quadratic conditions are closed under limits, so the limiting polynomial belongs to L^d_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P H^d_n be the projective space of degree d homogeneous polynomials in n variables, and let P L^d_n be the image in P H^d_n of the nonzero Lorentzian polynomials L^d_n. Then P L^d_n is compact and contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Compactness follows because L^d_n is a closed cone by Theorem 2.25, and projectivizing may be identified with intersecting the cone with the Euclidean unit sphere in coefficient space. Contractibility is obtained from the explicit deformation constructed in the proof of Theorem 2.13: nonnegative linear substitutions move any Lorentzian polynomial toward the symmetric polynomial (w_1+...+w_n)^d, and the Nuij operator T_n preserves the Lorentzian cone. After projectivization this gives a deformation retraction to a point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a homogeneous polynomial f with nonnegative coefficients, the following are equivalent: (1) f is completely log-concave, meaning that for every finite product of nonnegative directional derivatives D_1...D_m f, the result is either zero or has log concave on the positive orthant; (2) f is strongly log-concave, meaning that every ordinary partial derivative ∂^α f is either zero or log-concave on the positive orthant; (3) f is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication complete log-concave implies strong log-concave is immediate. For strong log-concavity, Proposition 2.33 identifies log-concavity of a nonzero homogeneous polynomial with the property that its Hessian has exactly one positive eigenvalue on the positive orthant. Applying Proposition 2.17 to all derivatives makes f c-Rayleigh, and then Theorem 2.23 gives M-convexity of the support; Theorem 2.25 then yields Lorentzianity. Conversely, if f is Lorentzian, Theorem 2.16 and Proposition 2.33 give log-concavity of f and of all Lorentzian derivatives. Complete log-concavity follows because Corollary 2.11 shows that every nonnegative directional derivative of a Lorentzian polynomial is again Lorentzian, so arbitrary products of such derivatives retain the same Hessian/log-concavity property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix κ=(κ_1,...,κ_n) in N^n. Let R_κ[w] be the space of polynomials of degree at most κ_i in each variable w_i, and let R^a_κ[w_{ij}] be the multi-affine polynomial ring in variables w_{ij} for 1≤i≤n and 1≤j≤κ_i. The projection Π^↓_κ substitutes every w_{ij} by w_i. The polarization Π^↑_κ sends w^α to the normalized product over i of the elementary symmetric polynomial of degree α_i in w_{i1},...,w_{iκ_i}, divided by ∏_i binom(κ_i,α_i). Both Π^↓_κ and Π^↑_κ preserve the Lorentzian property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Projection is just a nonnegative linear substitution, so Theorem 2.10 applies. For polarization, it suffices by density to treat strictly Lorentzian f. The support of Π^↑_κ f is the full Boolean slice of the correct degree, hence M-convex. For any derivative of order d-2 of the polarized polynomial, after renaming variables it is a positive scalar times the polarization of the corresponding quadratic derivative ∂^α f. Quadratic derivatives of f are stable by strict Lorentzianity, and polarization preserves stability by the Borcea-Brändén polarization theorem. Thus all required quadratic derivatives are stable, so Theorem 2.25 gives Lorentzianity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let κ in N^n and γ in N^m. Let T:R_κ[w_1,...,w_n]→R_γ[w_1,...,w_m] be a homogeneous linear operator of degree ℓ, meaning T(w^α) is either zero or homogeneous of degree |α|+ℓ. Define its symbol by sym_T(w,u)=Σ_{0≤α≤κ} binom(κ,α) T(w^α) u^{κ-α}, a homogeneous polynomial in the output variables w and auxiliary input variables u. If sym_T is Lorentzian of degree |κ|+ℓ and f is Lorentzian of degree d with f in R_κ[w], then T(f) is Lorentzian of degree d+ℓ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first polarizes both the operator and the input. The symbol of the polarized operator is the polarization of sym_T, hence is Lorentzian by Proposition 3.1. Thus one reduces to the multi-affine case κ=(1,...,1) and γ=(1,...,1). In the multi-affine case, write sym_T(w,u)f(v)=Σ_{S⊂[n]}T(w^S)u^{[n]\S}f(v), which is Lorentzian because products of Lorentzian polynomials are Lorentzian. Apply, for each i, a two-variable operator T_{u_i,v_i} from Lemma 3.3 that merges the auxiliary variables u_i and v_i in a way that preserves Lorentzianity. After all n such operators, one obtains Σ_S T(w^S)(∂_S f)(v), still Lorentzian. Setting all v variables to zero is a nonnegative linear specialization and yields exactly T(f), so T(f) is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4 and Corollaries 3.5, 3.7, 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If T is a homogeneous linear operator that preserves stable polynomials and also preserves polynomials with nonnegative coefficients, then T preserves Lorentzian polynomials. Consequences include: the multi-affine part of a Lorentzian polynomial is Lorentzian; the normalization operator N sending w^α to w^α/α! preserves Lorentzian polynomials; and if N(f) and N(g) are Lorentzian, then N(fg) is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 3.4 uses the Borcea-Brändén classification of stability-preserving linear operators. Such an operator either has rank at most two with stable image, or its symbol sym_T(w,u) or sym_T(w,−u) is stable. In the rank-at-most-two case, the output is stable with nonnegative coefficients and hence Lorentzian by Proposition 2.2. In the symbol cases, nonnegativity of coefficients and stability imply that the relevant symbol is Lorentzian, and Theorem 3.2 applies. The multi-affine-part operator is known to preserve stability and nonnegative coefficients, so it preserves Lorentzianity. The normalization operator is treated by Theorem 3.2 directly: its symbol factors into bivariate polynomials whose coefficient sequences 1/k! are log-concave in the required normalized sense, so the symbol is Lorentzian. The convolution statement N(fg) is proved by writing multiplication by g after normalization as a linear operator and verifying, via a second symbol computation and Theorem 3.2, that its symbol is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10 and Lemma 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a nonempty subset J of N^n, let f_J=Σ_{α in J} w^α/α! be its exponential generating function. The following are equivalent: J is M-convex; f_J is Lorentzian; f_J is homogeneous c-Rayleigh for some c>0; and there exists some Lorentzian, or homogeneous c-Rayleigh, polynomial whose support is J. If J is contained in the Boolean cube {0,1}^n, these conditions are also equivalent to J being the set of bases of a matroid on [n]. The key quadratic input, Lemma 3.11, says that for a symmetric {0,1}-matrix A, the quadratic polynomial w^T A w is Lorentzian if and only if its support is M-convex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications from Lorentzian to Rayleigh to M-convex use Proposition 2.19 and Theorem 2.23. To prove that an M-convex J gives a Lorentzian f_J, one uses Theorem 2.25 and checks all degree-two derivatives. Any derivative ∂^α f_J has support obtained from J by translation and contraction, hence still M-convex; thus the problem reduces to Lemma 3.11 for quadratic {0,1}-coefficient forms. For Lemma 3.11, after deleting zero columns, let S be the set of indices i for which 2e_i is in the support. The exchange axiom forces e_i+e_j to be in the support for all i in S and all j. On the remaining indices, the support is the basis set of a rank-two matroid without loops, so it decomposes into parallel classes S_1,...,S_k. The quadratic form can then be written as (Σ_j w_j)^2−Σ_{r=1}^k(Σ_{j in S_r}w_j)^2, which visibly has at most one positive eigenvalue. The Boolean-cube assertion is the standard equivalence between M-convex subsets of a fixed Boolean slice and matroid basis sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.25 and Remark 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f=Σ_{α} c_α w^α/α! be a Lorentzian homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative normalized coefficients. For 0≤p≤1, define R_p(f)=Σ_{α in supp(f)} c_α^p w^α/α!, with the convention that R_0(f)=f_{supp(f)}. Then R_p(f) is Lorentzian for every p in [0,1]. Consequently, for every nonempty M-convex set J, the projectivized stratum of Lorentzian polynomials with support exactly J is contractible. In the multi-affine case, for every rank d matroid with basis set B⊂[n choose d], the projective stratum P L_B of Lorentzian multi-affine polynomials with support B is nonempty and contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of Proposition 3.25 reduces by Theorem 2.25 and Theorem 2.10 to the case of a positive-coefficient quadratic form. Lemma 3.24 is the key linear algebra statement: if a positive symmetric matrix (a_{ij}) has exactly one positive eigenvalue, then its entrywise p-th power (a_{ij}^p) also has exactly one positive eigenvalue for 0≤p≤1. This uses Perron-Frobenius: after scaling by the positive Perron eigenvector, the matrix is conditionally negative definite, and a theorem on powers of conditionally negative definite kernels preserves this property. Applying the lemma to every quadratic derivative gives Lorentzianity of R_p(f). The contractibility statement follows because p↦R_p(f) continuously deforms every polynomial in the fixed-support stratum to the canonical generating function f_J without changing support for p>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a function d on two-element subsets of [n], define the symmetric matrix H_q(d) by diagonal entries 0 and off-diagonal entries q^{d(i,j)}. The following are equivalent: H_q(d) has exactly one positive eigenvalue for every q≥1; and d is a tree distance, meaning d(i,j) is the sum of edge weights along the unique path between leaves i and j in some phylogenetic tree with n labelled leaves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the implication from the spectral condition to tree distance, one first reduces to rational d and observes that w^T H_q(d)w is stable for every q≥1. By quantifier elimination over real closed fields, the Puiseux polynomial Σ_{i<j} t^{-d(i,j)}w_iw_j is stable. Brändén's tropicalization theorem for stable polynomials implies that −d is M-convex on the rank-two Boolean slice, equivalently d lies in the rank-two Dressian Dr(2,n), which equals the space T_n of phylogenetic tree distances. Conversely, if d is an ultrametric, then q^d is again an ultrametric for q≥1; by Timan-Vestfrid and Schoenberg, the associated squared distance matrix is conditionally negative semidefinite, hence H_q(d) has at most one positive eigenvalue, and Perron-Frobenius gives exactly one when appropriate. A general tree distance differs from an ultrametric by an element of the linearity space of T_n, which corresponds to congruence by a positive diagonal matrix and preserves inertia.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞} be a function on the degree d discrete simplex, with effective domain dom(ν)={α:ν(α)<∞}. Define f^ν_q(w)=Σ_{α in dom(ν)} q^{ν(α)} w^α/α! and g^ν_q(w)=Σ_{α in dom(ν)} binom(δ,α) q^{ν(α)} w^α, where δ=(d,...,d) and binom(δ,α)=∏_i binom(d,α_i). Then the following are equivalent: ν is M-convex in the discrete convex analysis sense, i.e. it satisfies the symmetric exchange inequality ν(α)+ν(β)≥ν(α−e_i+e_j)+ν(β−e_j+e_i) for suitable j whenever α_i>β_i; f^ν_q is Lorentzian for every 0<q≤1; and g^ν_q is Lorentzian for every 0<q≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from M-convexity to Lorentzianity of g^ν_q is proved in stages. First assume dom(ν) is the full Boolean slice [n choose d]. Then every quadratic derivative corresponds to a rank-two valuated matroid, and Lemma 3.21 supplies the one-positive-eigenvalue condition for all required quadratic forms. Second, when dom(ν)=∆^d_n, polarize ν to a function Π^↑ν on a Boolean slice; Lemma 3.26 preserves M-convexity, the Boolean case applies, and Proposition 3.1 projects Lorentzianity back. Third, for arbitrary M-convex effective domain and q=1, Theorem 3.10 handles the support. For arbitrary domain and q<1, Lemma 3.27 approximates ν by finite-valued M-convex functions ν_k with full domain and ν_k→ν; closedness of the Lorentzian cone gives the result. To pass from g^ν_q to f^ν_q, apply the already proved result to ν/p and then use Proposition 3.25: R_p(g^{ν/p}_q) is Lorentzian and tends to f^ν_q as p→0. The reverse implications use quantifier elimination: if f^ν_q or g^ν_q is Lorentzian for all small q, then the corresponding Puiseux polynomial is Lorentzian over K and has tropicalization ν; Theorem 3.20 then forces ν to be M-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f=Σ_{α in ∆^d_n} c_α w^α/α! be a homogeneous polynomial with nonnegative coefficients. Define ν_f(α)=log(c_α), with ν_f(α)=−∞ when c_α=0. If ν_f is M-concave, equivalently −ν_f is M-convex, then f is Lorentzian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3.14 to the M-convex function −ν_f. Taking q=e^{-1} gives coefficients q^{−ν_f(α)}=e^{ν_f(α)}=c_α in the normalized form, so f is exactly the corresponding polynomial f^{−ν_f}_{e^{-1}} and is Lorentzian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.26 and Lemma 3.27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞}. Its polarization Π^↑ν is the function on a Boolean slice in variables e_{ij} whose value at a Boolean vector β is ν(φ(β)), where φ(e_{ij})=e_i; its projection Π^↓µ of a function µ on the polarized simplex is the function α↦min{µ(β):φ(β)=α}. Lemma 3.26 states that polarization and projection preserve M-convexity. Lemma 3.27 states that every M-convex ν:∆^d_n→R∪{∞} is the pointwise limit of finite-valued M-convex functions ν_k:∆^d_n→R; moreover ν_k can be chosen equal to ν on dom(ν) and strictly increasing outside dom(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.26, the preservation of the symmetric exchange axiom is checked directly, or equivalently interpreted using operations from discrete convex analysis: polarization is obtained by repeated splitting of variables and restricting to a Boolean slice, and projection is obtained by aggregation of variables; splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity. For Lemma 3.27, introduce variables e_{ij} in R^{n^2} and maps φ(e_{ij})=e_i and ψ(e_{ij})=e_j. For a function ν, form φ^*ν and then push forward along ψ after adding a penalty ℓ_k with ℓ_k(e_{ij})=0 if i=j and k if i≠j. Explicitly ν_k=ψ_*(ℓ_k+φ^*ν). Splitting and aggregation preserve M-convexity, and the transportation/existence theorem for nonnegative matrices with prescribed row and column sums ensures ν_k is finite. As k grows, the penalty forces mass to remain on the diagonal exactly where possible, giving pointwise convergence to ν and the stated monotonicity outside dom(ν).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the real closed field of real convergent Puiseux series and let f_t be a homogeneous polynomial with coefficients in K_{≥0}. The following are equivalent over K: every finite product of nonnegative directional derivatives of f_t is either zero or log-concave on K^n_{>0}; every ordinary partial derivative ∂^α f_t is either zero or log-concave on K^n_{>0}; and f_t is Lorentzian in the Puiseux-series sense, namely it has M-convex support and all order d−2 derivatives are quadratic forms whose Hessians have at most one positive eigenvalue in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence is the transfer of Theorem 2.30 to the real closed field K. Proposition 2.33 expresses log-concavity of a homogeneous polynomial in terms of the inertia of its Hessian, and all relevant statements about signs of principal minors, nonnegativity of coefficients, and directional derivatives are first-order in the language of ordered fields once the coefficients are fixed. Since K is real closed and satisfies Tarski's principle, the real proof applies verbatim over K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.20 and Corollary 3.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of real convergent Puiseux series with valuation val, and let trop(f_t)(α)=val(s_α(t)) for f_t=Σ_{α in ∆^d_n}s_α(t)w^α with coefficients in K_{≥0}. For a function ν:∆^d_n→Q∪{∞}, ν is M-convex if and only if there exists a Lorentzian polynomial f_t in K[w_1,...,w_n] whose tropicalization is ν. More generally, tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over K are M-convex, and every real-valued M-convex function is a limit of tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over K. In particular, if M is a matroid with basis set B, the ordinary Dressian Dr(M) of valuated matroids with underlying matroid M satisfies Dr(M)=closure{−trop(f_t): f_t is Lorentzian over K and supp(f_t)=B}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the direction from Lorentzian tropicalization to M-convexity, it suffices to verify the local exchange axiom. Given α,β in dom(ν) with |α−β|_1=4, write them as γ+e_p+e_q and γ+e_r+e_s. The quadratic derivative ∂^γ f_t is Lorentzian, hence stable in the quadratic sense over K; Brändén's theorem that tropicalizations of homogeneous stable polynomials are M-convex gives the required local exchange inequality for ν. For the converse with rational ν, Theorem 3.14 says f^ν_q is Lorentzian for all sufficiently small positive real q. By quantifier elimination over real closed fields, the Puiseux polynomial f^ν_t=Σ t^{ν(α)}w^α/α! is Lorentzian over K, and its tropicalization is ν. Corollary 3.28 handles real-valued ν by approximating it by rational M-convex functions: after reducing to full domain using Lemma 3.27, polarize to a valuated matroid on a uniform matroid; the Dressian is a rational polyhedral fan, so rational points approximate; projecting back by Lemma 3.26 preserves M-convexity. The Dressian statement uses the convention that valuated matroids are M-concave, so a minus sign converts M-convex tropicalizations into valuated matroids.

proof_techniques.name: Encode M-convex functions as tropicalized Lorentzian polynomials
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that M-convex functions on ∆^d_n are precisely tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials over Puiseux series, at least for rational-valued functions, and all real-valued M-convex functions are limits of such tropicalizations. The construction is explicit: for ν, use f^ν_t=Σ t^{ν(α)}w^α/α!, and Lorentzianity over the Puiseux field is certified by Theorem 3.14 plus Tarski transfer. Conversely, local quadratic derivatives and Brändén's tropicalization theorem for stable polynomials force the tropicalization to satisfy the local exchange axiom.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If elements of the relative Dressian can be represented by valuated matroids or M-convex functions satisfying extra incidence constraints with Trop(M), this technique suggests lifting them to Lorentzian polynomials, applying algebraic or linear operations there, and tropicalizing back.

proof_techniques.name: Nuij-type homotopy inside the Lorentzian cone
proof_techniques.description: The proof that L^d_n is the closure of strictly Lorentzian polynomials constructs an explicit homotopy using nonnegative averaging substitutions and repeated operators of the form 1+θ w_i∂_j. This homotopy deforms arbitrary Lorentzian polynomials toward the symmetric polynomial (w_1+...+w_n)^d while staying in the Lorentzian class after projectivization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proposed involution or extension map on a Dressian-like parameter space might be tested or constructed on dense/strict loci and then extended by closure. The homotopy also gives a canonical contraction of fixed support strata, useful if one needs to compare a desired map to its values on simpler matroidal boundary points such as flats.

proof_techniques.name: Support extraction from analytic inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly turns Hessian or Rayleigh inequalities into combinatorial exchange axioms. The key route is: Lorentzian Hessian signature implies c-Rayleigh inequalities; c-Rayleigh inequalities force interval convexity and then M-convexity of support; M-convex support is exactly the support condition for matroids or valuated matroids.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If containment of tropical linear spaces can be recast as inequalities or degenerations of coefficients, this technique may help show that the support of an image under a candidate duality operation remains a matroid or a permitted relative-Dressian object.

proof_techniques.name: Closure under nonnegative linear substitutions and derivatives
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.10 and Corollary 2.11 show that Lorentzian polynomials are stable under arbitrary nonnegative linear changes of variables and nonnegative directional derivatives. The proof decomposes substitutions into splitting, dilation, and diagonalization, all controlled by the fundamental operator 1+θ w_i∂_j.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Matroid operations such as deletion, contraction, restriction to flats, direct sums with loops, and certain tropical projections often correspond to specialization or differentiation at the level of generating polynomials. These closure properties are a ready-made mechanism for checking that such operations stay within the valuated-matroid/Lorentzian framework.

proof_techniques.name: Polarization and projection to move between polymatroids and matroids
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses polarization to replace general monomial exponents by multi-affine variables, and projection to return to the original variables. Both operations preserve Lorentzian polynomials, and their tropical/discrete analogues preserve M-convex functions. This allows many statements to be reduced to the Boolean slice, where matroid language applies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a relative Dressian construction involves non-multi-affine polymatroidal data or needs a reduction to ordinary matroids, polarization can transform the problem to a Boolean setting while preserving the relevant convexity/tropical structure.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-operator symbol criterion
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.2 gives a Lorentzian analogue of the Borcea-Brändén symbol method: a homogeneous linear operator preserves Lorentzian polynomials if its symbol is Lorentzian. The proof polarizes to the multi-affine case, multiplies by the input polynomial, applies pairwise Lorentzian-preserving merge operators, and specializes auxiliary variables.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A candidate order-reversing map on valuated objects might arise from a linear transformation on coefficient vectors, such as complementing bases, applying a Hodge-star-like operator, or summing over fibers. The symbol criterion provides a concrete way to verify that such an operator preserves the ambient Dressian/Lorentzian class.

proof_techniques.name: Coefficient-power deformation to canonical support polynomial
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.25 shows that raising all normalized coefficients of a Lorentzian polynomial to a power p∈[0,1] preserves Lorentzianity. Letting p go to 0 deforms any polynomial with fixed support J to the canonical exponential generating function f_J. This proves contractibility of each fixed M-convex support stratum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the desired involution must extend a known map on flat-associated support objects, the deformation suggests reducing questions on a stratum to its support or unvaluated matroid. It may also help identify obstructions or canonical choices in fibers over supports.

proof_techniques.name: Local rank-two verification via tree metrics
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 3.14 reduces Lorentzianity of valuated objects to checking quadratic derivatives. In the Boolean full-support case, those quadratic derivatives are governed by rank-two valuated matroids, i.e. tree distances in the rank-two Dressian, and Lemma 3.21 translates tree-distance structure into a one-positive-eigenvalue condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many tropical linear-space incidence conditions are local on rank-two contractions or links. This method suggests proving global extension properties by checking compatibility on rank-two minors, where the geometry is governed by trees.

proof_techniques.name: Real-closed-field transfer and tropical limiting
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically uses the real closed field of convergent Puiseux series and Tarski's quantifier elimination. Properties such as Lorentzianity, Hessian inertia, and log-concavity can be expressed first-order over ordered fields, so real q-deformation statements transfer to Puiseux polynomials whose valuations encode the desired tropical data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a candidate map on a tropical or Dressian object, one can try to lift to Puiseux coefficients, prove an algebraic statement over K, and then tropicalize. This is particularly relevant when an involution is easier to define algebraically than purely piecewise-linearly.

proof_techniques.name: Contractibility of matroid-basis Lorentzian strata
proof_techniques.description: Combining Theorem 3.10 and Proposition 3.25, the paper shows that for every matroid basis set B, the projectivized space of multi-affine Lorentzian polynomials with support B is nonempty and contractible. This contrasts with stable polynomials, where some matroids, such as the Fano plane, have no stable polynomial with that support.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If relative Dressian strata are organized by underlying matroids or supports, contractibility of analogous Lorentzian strata may provide deformation-theoretic flexibility for extending maps from combinatorial boundary pieces to valuated interiors.

other_useful_info: The paper's notation Dr(M) refers to the ordinary Dressian of valuated matroids with fixed underlying matroid M, not to the relative Dressian of tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M) from the original problem. The sign convention matters: tropicalizations of Lorentzian polynomials are M-convex, while valuated matroids are M-concave, so the paper identifies Dr(M) with the closure of negative tropicalizations. The paper cites Maclagan-Sturmfels for Dressians and Brändén 2010 for tropicalizations of stable polynomials as M-convex functions. It also records that every matroid basis support occurs for Lorentzian polynomials, unlike stable polynomials; for example, the Fano matroid has no stable polynomial with its basis support but does have a Lorentzian generating polynomial. Conjecture 2.29 says the full projectivized Lorentzian space P L^d_n should be a closed Euclidean ball; this is not proved but may be relevant if one looks for global topological obstructions to involutions.
why_relevant: Establishes the Lorentzian/M-convex framework underlying many recent results on valuated matroids and tropical incidence. Its convexity and tropicalization techniques feed directly into modern work on relative Dressians.

title: Moduli Spaces of Codimension-One Subspaces in a Linear Variety and Their Tropicalization
overall_summary: The paper studies, for a fixed linear subspace X of projective or affine space, the moduli space L(X) of codimension-one linear subspaces contained in X, embedded in the appropriate Grassmannian by Plücker coordinates. Its main algebraic result is that L(X) is itself a linear subspace of the Plücker ambient projective space, with two explicit descriptions: as the rowspace of a cofactor-type matrix V and as the kernel of an incidence matrix U. The paper then identifies the matroid of L(X) in several equivalent ways, most importantly as the matroid of lines of the coordinate hyperplane arrangement restricted to X, and in the generic case as a relabeled Dilworth truncation of the free matroid. After tropicalization, in the constant-coefficient case, trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of this matroid; in nonconstant coefficients there is a valuated analogue. For the original problem, the most relevant contribution is a precise combinatorial model for the codimension-one part of the space of tropicalized linear subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space, together with examples showing that tropical point-hyperplane self-duality is subtle and not automatic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an algebraically closed field. Let X ⊂ K^n be a (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace, let W ∈ K^{(d+1)×n} be a matrix whose rowspace is X, and let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the set of d-dimensional linear subspaces Y ⊂ X, embedded by their Plücker coordinates indexed by d-subsets J ⊂ [n]. For i ∈ {1,...,d+1} and J ∈ binom([n],d), let W^J_{−i} be the d×d submatrix of W using columns J and all rows except row i, and define the (d+1)×binom(n,d) matrix V by V_{i,J} = (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}). Then L(X) is equal to the projectivization of the rowspace of V. In particular L(X) is a d-dimensional projective linear subspace of P^{binom(n,d)-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every d-dimensional subspace Y of X can be written as rowspace(TW), where T is a d×(d+1) matrix of rank d. For a d-subset J ⊂ [n], the Plücker coordinate det((TW)^J) is computed by Cauchy-Binet as the sum over i=1,...,d+1 of det(T^{[d+1]∖i}) det(W^J_{−i}), where T^{[d+1]∖i} is T with column i removed. Setting t_i = (−1)^{i+1} det(T^{[d+1]∖i}), the whole Plücker vector of Y is tV. Conversely, as T ranges over full-rank d×(d+1) matrices, its maximal minors range over Gr(d,d+1) ≅ P^d, so the possible vectors t fill the projective rowspace of V. To prove that this rowspace has projective dimension d, choose a (d+1)-subset I ⊂ [n] such that W^I is invertible. The square submatrix of V with columns I∖{j}, j ∈ I, is, up to alternating signs, the cofactor matrix of W^I; since W^I is invertible, this cofactor matrix is invertible. Hence rank(V)=d+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With K, X, W and L(X) as in Theorem 1, write q_I = det(W^I) for each (d+1)-subset I ⊂ [n]. Let U be the matrix whose rows are indexed by pairs (A,B) with A ∈ binom([n],d−1) and B ∈ binom([n],d+2), whose columns are indexed by J ∈ binom([n],d), and whose entry is U_{(A,B),J} = (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} q_{B∖i} if J=A∪{i} for some i ∈ B∖A, and 0 otherwise. Equivalently, the row (A,B) records the coefficients of the incidence relation I_{A,B} = sum_{i∈B∖A} (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} q_{B∖i} P_{A∪i}. Then L(X), as a projective subspace of P^{binom(n,d)-1}, is equal to the projectivized kernel of U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The incidence relations I_{A,B}=0 are the classical linear equations expressing that a d-dimensional subspace with Plücker coordinates P_J is contained in the fixed (d+1)-dimensional space X with Plücker coordinates q_I. Therefore ker(U) ∩ Gr(d,n) is exactly L(X). Theorem 1 already gives L(X) as a projective d-plane, so it remains to see that ker(U) has projective dimension at most d. Choose C ∈ binom([n],d+1) with q_C ≠ 0. For each d-subset I not contained in C, choose i ∈ I∖C and consider the row indexed by A=I∖i and B=C∪i. In column I, this row has coefficient ±q_C. The possible other nonzero entries in this row occur in columns whose intersection size with C is ordered strictly on one side of |I∩C|; ordering the d-subsets not contained in C accordingly gives a triangular submatrix with diagonal entries ±q_C. Thus U has at least binom(n,d)−(d+1) independent rows, so ker(U) has dimension at most d+1 as a vector space, i.e. projective dimension at most d. Since it contains L(X), equality follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be the polynomial ring with variables q_I indexed by (d+1)-subsets I ⊂ [n] and variables P_J indexed by d-subsets J ⊂ [n]. For D ∈ binom([n],d−1) and E ∈ binom([n],d+2), define the codimension-one incidence relation I_{D,E} = sum_{i∈E∖D} (−1)^{|[i]∩D|+|[i]∩E|} q_{E∖i} P_{D∪i}, and let I be the ideal generated by all such I_{D,E}. For A ∈ binom([n],d−1) and B ∈ binom([n],d+1), let R_{A,B} = sum_{i∈B∖A} (−1)^{|[i]∩A|+|[i]∩B|} P_{A∪i} P_{B∖i} be the Plücker relation for Gr(d,n). Then for every fixed C ∈ binom([n],d+1), every Plücker relation R_{A,B} lies in the saturation (I : <q_C>^∞). Equivalently, for each R_{A,B} there exists N ≥ 0 such that q_C^N R_{A,B} ∈ I.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is an algebraic double induction built from three explicit sign-sensitive identities. First, Lemma 30 gives, for suitable A,B,C and an element a of A used to replace entries by elements of C, an identity of the form sum_i ± P_{B∖i} I_{A∖a∪i, C∪a} = R_{A,B} q_C + sum_j ± R_{A∖a∪j,B} q_{C∖j∪a}. The signs are the standard Plücker/incidence signs, and the key sign check is that the exponent attached to the second sum is independent of the summation index i; this is verified by a modulo-2 comparison of the order positions of a,i,j. Second, Lemma 31 proves the special case R_{A,C} ∈ (I:<q_C>^∞) by induction on |A∖C|: if A⊂C the relation is zero, and otherwise Lemma 30 with B=C expresses R_{A,C} q_C modulo I in terms of relations R_{A∖a∪j,C} with smaller |(A∖a∪j)∖C|. Third, Lemma 32 handles the case A⊂C while B varies: for b ∈ B∖C it gives an identity R_{A,B} q_C + sum_{j∈C∖B} ± R_{A,B∖b∪j} q_{C∪b∖j} = ± P_{B∖b} I_{A,C∪b} + sum_{i∈B∖A, i≠b} ± P_{A∪i} I_{B∖i∖b,C∪b}. The right-hand side is in I, and the relations in the left-hand sum have larger intersection with C. The theorem first proves the base case A⊂C by induction on |B∩C| using Lemma 31 and Lemma 32. It then proves the general case by induction on |A∩C|, using Lemma 30 to replace an element outside C by an element of C; the lower-order Plücker relations are already in the saturation, and the incidence terms are in I. Saturation by q_C allows the repeated appearances of the factor q_C to be cleared.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊂ K^n be a (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace over an algebraically closed field K, let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the projective space of d-dimensional subspaces contained in X, and let W be a (d+1)×n matrix whose rowspace is X. Let V be the matrix V_{i,J} = (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}) from Theorem 1, and let U be the incidence matrix from Corollary 2. Let A(X) be the hyperplane arrangement in K^{d+1} with hyperplanes H_k = {x ∈ K^{d+1} : w_k · x = 0}, where w_k is the k-th column of W. For each d-subset J ⊂ [n], define ℓ_J = ∩_{k∈J} H_k if this intersection is one-dimensional and ℓ_J=0 otherwise. Then the rank-(d+1) matroid of the projective linear subspace L(X) has the following equivalent descriptions: (i) the matroid of dependencies among the columns of V; (ii) the dual matroid to the matroid of dependencies among the columns of U; and (iii) the matroid of lines of A(X), namely the matroid on ground set binom([n],d) whose independent sets are collections of the lines ℓ_J spanning a subspace of the corresponding dimension, with zero ℓ_J treated as loops.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Description (i) is immediate from Theorem 1: a projective linear subspace represented as the rowspace of V has associated matroid equal to the column matroid of V. Description (ii) follows from Corollary 2 and linear matroid duality: if a subspace is the kernel of U, then its representing matroid is dual to the column matroid of U. For (iii), note that ℓ_J is the left kernel of the (d+1)×d submatrix W^J. This intersection is a line exactly when W^J has rank d, equivalently when at least one maximal minor of W^J is nonzero. The maximal minors of W^J are exactly the entries of the column V_J, up to the signs in the definition of V. When W^J has rank d, Cramer's rule says that V_J spans the left kernel ℓ_J. Thus linear dependencies among the columns V_J are exactly linear dependencies among the corresponding lines ℓ_J, and zero columns correspond to the zero ℓ_J.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 7 and Remark 20 (valuated analogue)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume K carries a non-Archimedean valuation and X is a linear subspace not necessarily defined over a trivially valued subfield. The paper states that a valuated analogue of Theorem 6 holds with the same proof: the valuated matroid of the linear subspace L(X) is computed from the valuations of the maximal minors of the matrix V, equivalently as the dual valuated matroid obtained from U. Consequently trop(L(X)) is the tropical linear space encoded by this valuated matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: No separate proof is given in the paper. The point is that the linear-algebraic descriptions L(X)=rowspace(V) and L(X)=ker(U) remain valid over the valued field. A linear subspace over a valued field determines a valuated matroid by taking valuations of the maximal minors of a representing matrix, and passing from a kernel description to a rowspace description corresponds to valuated matroid duality. Thus the same matrices V and U that represent the ordinary matroid in constant coefficients represent the valuated matroid in nonconstant coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For fixed d and n, there is a Zariski open dense subset of the Grassmannian Gr(d+1,n) such that for every (d+1)-dimensional linear subspace X in this subset, the matroid of L(X) is the same. The paper calls such subspaces X generic. It also warns that this generic subset can be strictly smaller than the set of X whose own matroid is the uniform matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work on the affine chart of Gr(d+1,n) where q_{[d+1]} ≠ 0 and normalize q_{[d+1]}=1. Row-reduce W so that its first d+1 columns form the identity matrix. In this gauge, the entries of V can be written directly in terms of the Plücker coordinates q_I of X: V_{i,J} = (−1)^{|[i]∩J|} q_{J∪{i}} if i∉J, and V_{i,J}=0 if i∈J. Therefore every maximal minor of V is a polynomial in the Plücker coordinates q_I, restricted to the Grassmannian. Some of these polynomials may vanish identically on Gr(d+1,n), while the others cut out proper closed subsets because the Grassmannian is irreducible. Requiring all non-identically-zero maximal minors of V to be nonzero defines a Zariski open dense subset on which exactly the same subsets of columns of V are bases. Hence the matroid of L(X) is constant on this subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 13 (Brylawski, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank-e matroid on n elements realized by a configuration P of n points in P^{e−1}. For 1 ≤ k ≤ e, let D_k(M) denote the k-th Dilworth truncation: its ground set is the set of rank-k flats of M, and a set {J_1,...,J_m} of such flats is independent if for every nonempty subset S ⊂ {1,...,m}, the rank in M of the union of the corresponding flats is at least |S|+k−1. Let H ⊂ P^{e−1} be a generic projective subspace of codimension k−1. Each rank-k flat F spanned by P, of projective dimension k−1, intersects H in a point h_F. Then the point configuration {h_F} in H realizes the Dilworth truncation D_k(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Brylawski and does not prove it. It is used as a geometric coordinatization of Dilworth truncation: intersect all k-dimensional spans of the original point configuration with one generic complementary subspace, and the resulting points realize precisely the combinatorial truncation D_k(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ∈ Gr(d+1,n) be generic in the sense of Proposition 9. Let U_{n,n} be the free matroid on [n]. For k=n−d, let eD_k(U_{n,n}) be the relabeling of the Dilworth truncation D_k(U_{n,n}) by taking complements: its ground set is binom([n],d), and a collection of d-subsets J_1,...,J_m is independent exactly when, for every nonempty subcollection of size s, the intersection of the corresponding J_l has size at most d+1−s. Then the matroid of L(X) is eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: View X as a generic projective d-plane in P^{n−1}, hence of codimension n−d−1. The n coordinate points e_i in P^{n−1} realize the free matroid U_{n,n}. For each subset J ⊂ [n] of size n−d, let F_J be the projective span of {e_i : i∈J}; equivalently F_J is the intersection of the coordinate hyperplanes H_i={x_i=0} for i∉J. Since X is generic, X∩F_J is a point h_J. By Brylawski's Theorem 13 with k=n−d, the points h_J realize D_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). On the other hand, h_J = X ∩ (∩_{i∉J} H_i) corresponds exactly to the line ℓ_{J^c} in the restricted coordinate hyperplane arrangement A(X) appearing in Theorem 6. Therefore the matroid of L(X), which is the matroid of these lines, is obtained from D_{n−d}(U_{n,n}) by relabeling J by its complement J^c, i.e. it is eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 18 (Ardila-Klivans and Feichtner-Sturmfels, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a linear subspace over a valued field but defined over a subfield on which the valuation is trivial, and let A be a matrix whose rowspace is X. Let M be the matroid of the columns of A. For u ∈ R^n, let F(u) be the flag of subsets of [n] obtained by grouping the coordinates of u by equal values and ordering the groups by increasing coordinate value. A flag is a flag of flats of M if every subset appearing in the flag is a flat of M. Then the tropicalization trop(X) is the Bergman fan of M, namely the union of the weight cones of all flags of flats of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this standard theorem and uses it as the bridge from linear spaces to matroids. The defining tropical equations of a constant-coefficient linear space require that, on every circuit of the matroid, the maximum of the corresponding coordinates be attained at least twice. Ardila-Klivans and Feichtner-Sturmfels identify exactly the weight vectors satisfying these circuit conditions with those whose coordinate flag is a flag of flats. Thus the tropicalization depends only on the underlying matroid and equals its Bergman fan.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊂ P^{n−1} be a d-dimensional projective linear subspace defined over a trivially valued subfield, and let L(X) ⊂ Gr(d,n) ⊂ P^{binom(n,d)-1} be the locus of projective (d−1)-planes contained in X. Equivalently, the affine cone over X has vector dimension d+1. Then trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of each of the following equivalent matroids: (i) the column matroid of the matrix V with entries V_{i,J}= (−1)^{i+1} det(W^J_{−i}) for a matrix W whose rowspace is the affine cone over X; (ii) the dual of the column matroid of the incidence matrix U; and (iii) the matroid of lines of the coordinate hyperplane arrangement induced on X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 6 identifies the ordinary matroid of the constant-coefficient linear subspace L(X) in three equivalent ways. Since L(X) is itself a constant-coefficient linear subspace by Theorem 1, Theorem 18 applies directly: its tropicalization is the Bergman fan of its associated matroid. Substituting the three descriptions from Theorem 6 gives the three listed descriptions of trop(L(X)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Over the complex numbers C, there is a Zariski open dense subset of Gr_C(d+1,n) such that for all constant-coefficient subspaces X in this subset, the Bergman fan trop(L(X)) is the same. This subset is exactly the generic subset of Proposition 9.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 9, the matroid of L(X) is constant on the generic open dense subset of Gr(d+1,n). In constant coefficients, Corollary 19 says trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of this matroid. Since a Bergman fan depends only on the matroid, trop(L(X)) is constant throughout that same generic subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ∈ Gr(d+1,n) be generic in the sense of Proposition 9, and let trop(L(X)) be the tropicalization of the moduli space of d-dimensional linear subspaces contained in X. Then trop(L(X)), denoted in the paper by L_trop(trop(X)) in this generic situation, is the Bergman fan of the relabeled Dilworth truncation eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 16 identifies the matroid of L(X) for generic X as eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). Applying Corollary 19 to the constant-coefficient linear subspace L(X) then identifies trop(L(X)) with the Bergman fan of that matroid.

proof_techniques.name: Cauchy-Binet cofactor parametrization of codimension-one subspaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the nonlinear condition Y ⊂ X, with Y a codimension-one subspace of X, by an explicit linear parametrization in Plücker space. If X is the rowspace of a (d+1)×n matrix W and Y is the rowspace of TW for a d×(d+1) matrix T, Cauchy-Binet expresses every d×d minor of TW as a linear combination of the d×d minors obtained from W by deleting one row. These minors are assembled into a cofactor-type matrix V, and the Plücker vector of Y is tV, where t is the vector of maximal minors of T. Since Gr(d,d+1) is projective space, t ranges over all of P^d, making L(X) a linear space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete model for the realizable codimension-one tropical linear subspaces inside a fixed tropicalized linear space. In attempts to construct or obstruct an order-reversing involution on a relative Dressian, the codimension-one layer can be studied through the explicit matrix V rather than through abstract containment alone.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence equations plus dimension forcing
proof_techniques.description: The containment Y ⊂ X is encoded by classical linear incidence relations in Plücker coordinates. The paper forms a matrix U of these relations and proves that its projective kernel equals L(X), not merely that its intersection with the Grassmannian equals L(X). The key extra step is a rank lower bound on U: after choosing a nonzero Plücker coordinate q_C of X, selected incidence rows form a triangular submatrix with diagonal entries ±q_C.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressians are defined by tropical containment conditions. This incidence-matrix viewpoint suggests a way to isolate tropical hyperplanes inside a fixed tropical linear space by tropicalizing explicit linear incidence relations, and it gives rank or dimension constraints that may obstruct a proposed global duality.

proof_techniques.name: Saturation argument showing incidence implies Plücker in codimension one
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 proves that, in the universal polynomial ring with both the Plücker coordinates q_I of X and the Plücker coordinates P_J of Y as variables, the codimension-one incidence ideal implies the Plücker ideal of Y after saturating by any chosen nonzero q_C. The proof uses explicit identities that express q_C times a Plücker relation as a combination of incidence relations plus Plücker relations closer to a chosen base case, then runs a double induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if one wants to tropicalize relative incidence conditions without separately imposing all Plücker conditions in codimension one. It suggests that codimension-one containment may have a simpler algebraic or valuated structure than arbitrary containment in the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Matroid of lines of a restricted coordinate arrangement
proof_techniques.description: The paper identifies the matroid of L(X) with the matroid of the lines obtained by intersecting d hyperplanes in the coordinate hyperplane arrangement restricted to X. The bridge is Cramer's rule: the column V_J is a spanning vector for the line ℓ_J = ∩_{j∈J} H_j whenever that intersection is one-dimensional, and it is zero otherwise. Thus dependencies among Plücker-coordinate columns are dependencies among arrangement lines.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on the lattice of flats resembles a polarity or duality on incidence data. This theorem supplies a precise arrangement-theoretic object controlling hyperplanes inside a tropical linear space, so one can test whether the flat involution is compatible with the matroid-of-lines construction.

proof_techniques.name: Generic constancy via Plücker-minor nonvanishing
proof_techniques.description: After choosing a Plücker chart q_[d+1]=1 and row-reducing W, the entries of V become either zero or signed Plücker coordinates q_{J∪i} of X. Therefore the bases of the matroid of L(X) are controlled by maximal minors of V, which are regular functions on Gr(d+1,n). Requiring every non-identically-zero such minor to be nonzero gives a single open dense locus where the matroid of L(X) is constant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If studying a relative Dressian by strata of matroids or valuated matroids, this technique identifies the generic codimension-one behavior and separates it from special degenerations. A proposed involution can be checked first on this generic model before addressing nongeneric strata.

proof_techniques.name: Dilworth truncation as the generic hyperplane-moduli matroid
proof_techniques.description: For generic X, the matroid of L(X) is described without coordinates: it is the relabeled Dilworth truncation eD_{n−d}(U_{n,n}). The proof uses Brylawski's realization of Dilworth truncation by intersecting all k-flats spanned by a point configuration with a generic complementary subspace. In the coordinate setting, these intersection points correspond, after complement relabeling, to the lines of the restricted coordinate hyperplane arrangement.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Dilworth truncations provide explicit combinatorial invariants of the codimension-one part of the relative tropical geometry. These invariants may be compared with the flat lattice involution in the original problem, especially for Boolean or uniform examples where the flat involution is explicit.

proof_techniques.name: Tropicalization through Bergman fans and valuated matroids
proof_techniques.description: Once L(X) is known to be a linear space, its tropicalization is controlled entirely by its matroid in constant coefficients, or by its valuated matroid in nonconstant coefficients. Thus the paper converts a moduli problem about tropicalized subspaces into the computation of a matroid or valuated matroid from V or U. In constant coefficients, trop(L(X)) is the Bergman fan of the matroid of lines of A(X).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. Bergman fan descriptions translate parts of this order into matroidal data, which can be used to test whether an order-reversing map could preserve dimensions, ranks, flats, or incidence relations.

proof_techniques.name: Using examples to detect failure of naive tropical self-duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper analyzes explicit tropical planes and their spaces of tropicalized lines. For the standard tropical plane, the generic space of tropicalized lines is the Bergman fan of eD_2(U_{4,4}), whose coarse link is the Petersen graph, rather than an evident copy of the original plane. For nongeneric planes, the matroid of lines may fail to be simple and may fail to be a Dilworth truncation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: These examples are useful test cases for any proposed extension of a flat-lattice involution to a relative Dressian. They show that classical point-hyperplane duality does not automatically tropicalize to a self-duality of the whole moduli space of subspaces.

other_useful_info: The paper distinguishes carefully between the space of tropicalized codimension-one subspaces contained in trop(X) and the larger space of all tropical linear subspaces contained in trop(X); the former is a realizability-sensitive tropicalized Fano scheme, while the original problem's relative Dressian may include non-realizable tropical subspaces. The introduction explicitly notes that self-duality of tropical planes is not automatic: for example, the space of tropical lines in the standard tropical plane in R^3 cannot naturally be identified with the plane itself. Example 15 describes eD_{n−2}(U_{n,n}) concretely: its ground set is the set of 2-subsets of [n], its 3-circuits are exactly triples {a,a_1},{a,a_2},{a,a_3} sharing a common element, its 4-circuits are the 4-element sets containing no such 3-circuit, and it has no larger circuits; it is represented by pairwise intersections of n generic lines in P^2. Example 22, following Lamboglia, shows that two different algebraic lifts X_1 and X_2 with the same tropicalization can have different trop(L(X_i)); in the Gr(3,6) example one lift is generic while another satisfies the extra equation q124 q356 − q123 q456 = 0. Example 25 computes the generic space of tropicalized lines in the Bergman fan of U_{3,4}: it is the Bergman fan of eD_2(U_{4,4}), with fine link the Petersen graph plus three extra vertices and coarse link the Petersen graph. Example 26 shows that for a nongeneric plane, the matroid of L(X) is still the matroid of lines of A(X) but need not be simple and need not be a Dilworth truncation. Related papers cited by the authors that may be relevant are Brylawski [Bry85] on Dilworth truncations, Ardila-Billey [AB07] on generic flag arrangements, Lamboglia [Lam18] on tropical Fano schemes, and Brandt-Eur-Zhang [BEZ21] on tropical flag varieties and flag Dressians.
why_relevant: Studies the moduli space of codimension-one linear subspaces inside a fixed linear variety and its tropicalization. Its matroid-of-lines/adjoint constructions are directly useful for understanding hyperplanes inside Trop(M) and potential duality operations.

title: Tropical Flag Varieties
overall_summary: The paper introduces flag Dressians as tropical analogues of partial flag varieties and proves that their points are equivalently valuated flag matroids, coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions, and flags of projective tropical linear spaces. A central technical innovation is the use of projective tropical linear spaces, which include matroids with loops and are characterized by circuits, cocircuit spans, coloopless cells in dual complexes, and projective closures. The most relevant consequence for studying ordered collections of tropical linear spaces is Theorem 4.3.1: for valuated matroids µ and ν, the inclusion trop(µ) ⊆ trop(ν) is equivalent to the valuated matroid quotient relation µ ↞ ν. Thus chains of tropical linear spaces can be encoded algebraically by valuated flag matroids and polyhedrally by flag-matroidal subdivisions, giving a framework for any attempted order-reversing operation on a poset of tropical subspaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix integers 0 ≤ r ≤ n, write [n] = {1,...,n}, and let T = R ∪ {∞}. For µ ∈ T^{C([n],r)}, where C([n],r) denotes the r-subsets of [n], view µ as a function on r-subsets and as a weight on the point configuration {e_I ∈ R^[n] : µ(I) ≠ ∞}. The following are equivalent: (a) the projective class of µ in P(T^{C([n],r)}) lies in the Dressian Dr(r;n), i.e. satisfies all tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations; (b) the finite support of µ is the set of bases of a rank-r matroid and the finite values define a valuated matroid, meaning that for every two bases B,B′ and every i ∈ B−B′ there exists j ∈ B′−B such that µ(B)+µ(B′) ≥ µ(B−i+j)+µ(B′−j+i); (c) the coherent subdivision ∆_µ induced by the weight µ on Conv(e_I : µ(I) ≠ ∞) has every face equal to the base polytope of a matroid. Consequently, for a fixed matroid M, the stratum Dr(M) = Dr(r;n) ∩ T_{B(M)} parametrizes valuated matroids with underlying matroid M, equivalently weights on Q(M) inducing matroidal subdivisions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Extend µ to a function Z^[n] → T by putting µ(e_I)=µ(I) on r-subset indicator vectors and ∞ elsewhere. The tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations are exactly the M-convex exchange inequalities for this function, and these are exactly the valuated basis exchange axioms. M-convex functions have tight coherent subdivisions whose cells are generalized permutohedra, and conversely such subdivisions give M-convex functions. Since the domain lies in the hypersimplex, the Gelfand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova characterization says that generalized permutohedra contained in the hypersimplex are precisely matroid base polytopes. This gives the equivalence between tropical Plücker points, valuated matroids, and matroidal subdivisions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ : Z^[n] → T be an M-convex function with finite effective domain Q = dom(µ), viewed as a weighted lattice point configuration in R^[n]. For a nonempty subset S ⊆ [n], let Q_{e_{[n]−S}} be the face of Q minimizing the linear functional e_{[n]−S}; let Q|S be its projection to R^S and Q/S its projection to R^{[n]−S}. Then there exist weights µ|S on Q|S and µ/S on Q/S, each unique up to adding a global constant, such that the restriction of µ to the face Q_{e_{[n]−S}} is the product weight µ|S × µ/S, i.e. µ(p,q)=µ|S(p)+µ/S(q) under Q_{e_{[n]−S}} = Q|S × Q/S. Moreover µ restricted to this face, µ|S, and µ/S are all M-convex functions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The effective domain of an M-convex function is a generalized permutohedron. The Hopf-monoid face factorization for generalized permutohedra gives Q_{e_{[n]−S}} = Q|S × Q/S. To prove that the weight splits additively, it suffices to show that for p,p′ ∈ Q|S and q,q′ ∈ Q/S one has µ(p,q)−µ(p′,q)=µ(p,q′)−µ(p′,q′). Because generalized permutohedra have edges parallel to e_i−e_j, one reduces to p−p′=e_i−e_i′ with i,i′ ∈ S and q−q′=e_j−e_j′ with j,j′ ∈ [n]−S. Applying the M-convex exchange axiom twice, once to ((p,q),(p′,q′)) and once to ((p,q′),(p′,q)), forces the desired equality. M-convexity of the face and factors follows from the characterization of M-convex functions by subdivisions into generalized permutohedra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3.8 and Corollary 2.3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be an M-convex function on Z^[n], viewed as a weighted point configuration, and let Σ_µ be the dual complex of its coherent subdivision in R^[n]/R1. For each cell σ of Σ_µ, let closure(σ) denote its closure inside tropical projective space P(T^[n]). If ∅ ≠ S ⊆ [n] and T_S is the stratum of P(T^[n]) consisting of points with finite support exactly S, identified with R^S/R1, then {closure(σ) ∩ T_S : σ ∈ Σ_µ} is exactly the dual complex Σ_{µ|S}. Equivalently, for u ∈ T_S written as u′ × ∞^{[n]−S}, define ∆^u_µ := ∆^{u′}_{µ|S}; then P(T^[n]) is partitioned by the relative interiors {u : ∆^u_µ = F}, as F ranges over all faces of all subdivisions ∆_{µ|S} for nonempty S ⊆ [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A general boundary lemma for weighted point configurations says that near a boundary stratum T_S, the lower face selected by a nearby point in the dense torus depends first on minimizing e_{[n]−S}; thus locally the dual complex is the dual complex of the restriction of the weight to the face A_{e_{[n]−S}}. For M-convex functions, Lemma 2.3.7 splits that restricted weighted face as µ|S × µ/S. When one intersects the corresponding local product dual complex with the stratum T_S, only the R^S/R1 factor remains, producing Σ_{µ|S}. This gives both the description of closures of cells and the projective extension of the face-cell correspondence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a valuated matroid of rank r on [n], and let S ⊆ [n] be nonempty. Valuated circuits are the non-all-∞ vectors C_µ(A) ∈ T^[n] for A ∈ C([n],r+1), with coordinate C_µ(A)_i = µ(A−i) if i ∈ A and ∞ otherwise. Then the valuated circuits of the restriction µ|S are exactly those valuated circuits C of µ whose support is contained in S. The valuated circuits of the contraction µ/S are the minimal-support elements among the projected vectors (C_i)_{i∈[n]−S} as C ranges over valuated circuits of µ. Moreover restriction and contraction are dual: (µ/S)^* = µ^*|([n]−S).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Baker-Bowler results on matroids over partial hyperstructures for this statement. The restriction formula keeps precisely those circuit vectors already supported in S. The contraction formula projects all circuits away from S and discards projected vectors whose support properly contains another projected circuit support, matching the usual minimal-support characterization of contracted circuits. Duality follows from the standard dual relation between deletion/restriction and contraction, applied to valuated matroids.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem B
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a valuated matroid on ground set [n], let M be its underlying matroid, and let ℓ ⊆ [n] be the set of loops of M. In tropical projective space P(T^[n]), the following five subsets coincide. (i) The projective tropical linear space trop(µ) defined by trop(µ)=⋃_{∅⊆S⊊[n]} (trop(µ/S) × {∞}^S), where µ/S is the contraction to [n]−S and the usual tropical linear space is used on the dense torus. (ii) The projective tropical prevariety cut out by all valuated circuits C of µ: the set of u ∈ P(T^[n]) such that the minimum among {C_i+u_i : i∈[n]} is attained at least twice, with the paper's convention for all-∞ values. (iii) The union of coloopless cells in the projective closure of the dual complex of the dual valuated matroid µ^*: the set of u ∈ P(T^[n]) such that ∆^u_{µ^*} is the base polytope of a coloopless matroid. (iv) The tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of µ, i.e. projective classes of finite tropical linear combinations (a_1⊙C_1^*)⊕...⊕(a_m⊙C_m^*) with a_i ∈ R and C_i^* valuated cocircuits of µ. (v) The closure in P(T^[n]) of trop(µ/ℓ) × {∞}^ℓ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality between the circuit-prevariety description and the cocircuit-span description is cited from Murota-Tamura. The equality of (i) and (iii) follows by applying the usual dense-torus loopless-cell description to each contraction µ/S, using (µ/S)^* = µ^*|([n]−S), and then using Theorem 2.3.8 to identify boundary strata of the projective closure of the dual complex. For (iii) ⊆ (v), after reducing to the loopless case, take a boundary point u × ∞^{[n]−S} in a coloopless cell. Choose u′ in trop(µ|([n]−S)) with positive coordinates. For large c, the nearby dense-torus points u × c u′ have selected dual faces that split as ∆^u_{µ^*|S} × ∆^{cu′}_{µ^*/S}; both factors are coloopless, so these nearby points lie in trop(µ), and the boundary point is in the closure. For (v) ⊆ (ii), circuit prevarieties are closed and agree with the usual tropical linear space on the dense torus; if loops are present, singleton loop circuits force the corresponding coordinates to be ∞. For (ii) ⊆ (i), restrict the circuit equations to a stratum T_{[n]−S}; Theorem 3.1.6 identifies the resulting equations with the circuit equations for the contraction µ/S, so Proposition 3.2.1 puts the point in trop(µ/S) × {∞}^S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.1.1 with Brylawski quotient criteria
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be matroids on the same finite ground set [n]. The paper writes M ↞ N and says that M is a matroid quotient of N if any, equivalently all, of the following conditions hold: (1) for all A ⊆ B ⊆ [n], rk_M(B)−rk_M(A) ≤ rk_N(B)−rk_N(A); (2) every circuit of N is a union of circuits of M; (3) there exists a matroid M̃ on [n] disjoint union [n′] such that M = M̃/[n′] and N = M̃ delete [n′]; (4) N^* is a quotient of M^*. A sequence M = (M_1,...,M_k) of matroids on [n] is a flag matroid if M_i ↞ M_j for all i < j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of the four quotient criteria is cited from Brylawski.
