The paper uses the rank-function criterion to check minor stability of flag matroids, the circuit criterion to compare with tropical circuit equations, the extension criterion for rank-difference-one constructions, and the dual criterion to reverse quotient order under matroid duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r_1,...,r_k be nonnegative integers and let Q ⊂ R^[n] be a lattice polytope. First, Q is the base polytope of a flag matroid of rank sequence (r_1,...,r_k) if and only if Q is a generalized permutohedron and every vertex of Q is in the S_n-orbit of e_{1,...,r_1}+...+e_{1,...,r_k}. Second, if M=(M_1,...,M_k) is a flag matroid on [n] and S ⊆ [n], then M|S=(M_1|S,...,M_k|S) and M/S=(M_1/S,...,M_k/S) are flag matroids, and the face Q(M)_{e_{[n]−S}} is the product Q(M|S) × Q(M/S).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The polytope characterization is cited from Borovik-Gelfand-White. For the minor statement, the fact that restrictions and contractions preserve the flag quotient relations follows directly from the rank-function characterization of matroid quotients. The product decomposition of the face is obtained by applying the generalized-permutohedron face factorization to the Minkowski-sum base polytope Q(M)=Q(M_1)+...+Q(M_k), together with the fact that faces of Minkowski sums are sums of the corresponding faces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0 ≤ r ≤ s ≤ n. Let µ ∈ T^{C([n],r)} and ν ∈ T^{C([n],s)}. The image of µ × ν in P(T^{C([n],r)}) × P(T^{C([n],s)}) lies in the flag Dressian FlDr(r,s;n), defined by the tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations for ranks r and s together with the tropical incidence-Plücker relations, if and only if µ and ν are valuated matroids and µ is a valuated matroid quotient of ν. Here µ ↞ ν means that, for every basis I of the underlying matroid of µ, every basis J of the underlying matroid of ν, and every i ∈ I−J, there exists j ∈ J−I such that µ(I)+ν(J) ≥ µ(I−i+j)+ν(J−j+i). More generally, points of FlDr(r_1,...,r_k;n) correspond to valuated flag matroids of rank sequence (r_1,...,r_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The tropical Grassmann-Plücker equations separately force µ and ν to be valuated matroids by Theorem 3.1.3. For the incidence equations, take I′ ∈ C([n],r−1) and J′ ∈ C([n],s+1). Choosing i ∈ J′−I′ and setting I=I′∪i and J=J′−i rewrites the corresponding tropical incidence polynomial as P_I⊙P_J ⊕ ⊕_{j∈J−I} P_{I−i+j}⊙P_{J−j+i}. The condition that its minimum be attained at least twice is precisely the assertion that the term µ(I)+ν(J) is not the unique minimum, equivalently that some j gives the valuated quotient inequality. Running through all I′,J′ is the same as running through all I,J,i in the quotient axiom.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ and ν be valuated matroids of ranks r and s on a common ground set [n]. Then µ ↞ ν as valuated matroids if and only if the associated projective tropical linear spaces satisfy trop(µ) ⊆ trop(ν) inside P(T^[n]). Consequently, a sequence µ=(µ_1,...,µ_k) of valuated matroids is a valuated flag matroid if and only if trop(µ_1) ⊆ ... ⊆ trop(µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem B(iv), trop(µ) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits C^*(µ). Hence trop(µ) ⊆ trop(ν) is equivalent to requiring every valuated cocircuit of µ to lie in trop(ν). By Theorem B(ii), trop(ν) is cut out by the tropical hyperplanes associated to valuated circuits of ν. A valuated cocircuit of µ indexed by I′ ∈ C([n],r−1) and a valuated circuit of ν indexed by J′ ∈ C([n],s+1) give the condition that the minimum of {ν(J′−j′)+µ(I′∪j′) : j′∈J′−I′} is attained at least twice. These are exactly the tropical incidence-Plücker relations. Proposition 4.2.3 identifies those relations with the valuated quotient condition µ ↞ ν.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ, ν, and ξ be valuated matroids on [n]. The following hold. (1) Valuated matroid quotients compose: if µ ↞ ν and ν ↞ ξ, then µ ↞ ξ. (2) Quotients are preserved by minors: if µ ↞ ν and S ⊆ [n], then µ/S ↞ ν/S and the deletions of S from µ and ν also form a quotient. (3) For every S ⊂ [n], a valuated matroid satisfies µ/S ↞ deletion_S(µ). (4) If µ ↞ ν, then the underlying ordinary matroids M and N satisfy M ↞ N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), translate quotients into inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces using Theorem 4.3.1 and compose inclusions. For (2), the contraction statement follows from the stratum-by-stratum definition of projective tropical linear spaces in Theorem B(i); the deletion statement follows by duality because µ ↞ ν is equivalent to ν^* ↞ µ^*. For (3), Theorem 3.1.6 gives an inclusion between the relevant cocircuit sets after deletion and contraction, and Theorem B(iv) plus Theorem 4.3.1 converts this into a quotient. For (4), Theorem B(iv) implies that every cocircuit support of M is a union of cocircuit supports of N; equivalently N^* ↞ M^*, hence M ↞ N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem A
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ=(µ_1,...,µ_k) be a sequence of valuated matroids on [n], with underlying matroids M=(M_1,...,M_k), and assume M is a flag matroid. Let B(M)=B(M_1)+...+B(M_k) be the Minkowski-sum base configuration and Q(M) its convex hull. Then the following are equivalent: (a) µ is a point of the flag Dressian FlDr(M), i.e. it satisfies the tropical incidence-Plücker relations in addition to the individual tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations; (b) µ is a valuated flag matroid with underlying flag matroid M, meaning µ_i ↞ µ_j for all i<j; (c) the Minkowski-sum weight µ_1+...+µ_k induces a coherent subdivision of Q(M) into base polytopes of flag matroids; (d) the projective tropical linear spaces form a flag trop(µ_1) ⊆ ... ⊆ trop(µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence (a) ⇔ (b) is Proposition 4.2.3 applied to all pairs of ranks. The equivalence (b) ⇔ (d) is Theorem 4.3.1 applied pairwise, using transitivity of inclusion and of valuated quotients. The implication (b) ⇒ (c) is Theorem 4.4.2: faces of the Minkowski-sum subdivision are sums of initial matroids, and initial valuated quotients have underlying ordinary quotient matroids. The implication (c) ⇒ (d) is Theorem 4.4.3: from a flag-matroidal subdivision one recovers valuated matroids and uses the coloopless-cell characterization of projective tropical linear spaces to prove the inclusions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ=(µ_1,...,µ_k) be a valuated flag matroid with underlying flag matroid M=(M_1,...,M_k) on [n]. Regard each µ_i as a weight on the base configuration B(M_i). Then the Minkowski-sum weight µ_1+...+µ_k on B(M)=B(M_1)+...+B(M_k) induces a flag-matroidal subdivision of B(M), hence a subdivision of Q(M) whose cells are base polytopes of flag matroids.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any u ∈ R^[n]/R1, the face selected by u in the subdivision of the Minkowski sum is ∆^u_{µ_1}+...+∆^u_{µ_k}. Adding the same linear functional to each µ_i does not change the valuated quotient relations and lets one reduce to u=0. The minimum face of a valuated matroid is the underlying matroid of its initial valuated matroid. If µ_i ↞ µ_j, then taking minima preserves the valuated quotient relation, and Corollary 4.3.2(4) says the underlying initial matroids form an ordinary matroid quotient. Thus every face is the base configuration of a flag matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4.4 and Theorem 4.4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 4.4.4: If M=(M_1,...,M_k) is a flag matroid and M_i is loopless for some i, then M_j is loopless for every j>i; equivalently, if M_i^* is coloopless, then M_j^* is coloopless for every j>i. Theorem 4.4.3: Let M=(M_1,...,M_k) be a flag matroid on [n], let w be a weight on the base configuration B(M), and suppose the coherent subdivision ∆_w is flag-matroidal. If µ_1,...,µ_k are weights on B(M_1),...,B(M_k) such that ∆_w = ∆_{µ_1+...+µ_k}, then each µ_i is a valuated matroid and trop(µ_1) ⊆ ... ⊆ trop(µ_k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.4.4, the rank-function criterion for matroid quotients implies that if an element has rank zero in M_j, then it has rank zero in all earlier quotient matroids M_i; dualizing gives the coloop statement in the direction needed later. For Theorem 4.4.3, since every face of ∆_w is a Minkowski sum of matroid base polytopes, each factor face ∆^u_{µ_i} is a matroid base polytope; Theorem 3.1.3 therefore makes each µ_i a valuated matroid. Passing to dual valuated matroids sends Q(M_i) to −Q(M_i)+1, so the dual weights induce a flag-matroidal subdivision for the reversed dual flag. For a boundary point u ∈ P(T^[n]) in stratum T_S, Lemmas 2.3.4 and 2.3.7 identify the selected face ∆^u_{µ_i^*} with the appropriate restriction factor of a face in that dual flag subdivision. Hence the sequence of selected dual faces is a dual flag matroid. Lemma 4.4.4 then implies that colooplessness propagates from i to all j>i. By Theorem B(iii), colooplessness of ∆^u_{µ_i^*} is exactly the condition u ∈ trop(µ_i), so u ∈ trop(µ_i) implies u ∈ trop(µ_j) for j>i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2.9 and Corollary 4.4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 2.2.9: Let A=A_1+...+A_k be a Minkowski sum of integral homogeneous point configurations, with the simplifying assumption that if dim Conv(A_i)=1 then |A_i|=2. If a weight w on A induces a coherent subdivision ∆_w that is mixed, then there exist weights w_i on A_i such that ∆_{w_1+...+w_k}=∆_w. Corollary 4.4.5: Every coherent flag-matroidal subdivision of the base polytope of a flag matroid arises from a valuated flag matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Theorem 2.2.9, write each mixed cell over u as F_{1,u}+...+F_{k,u}. For each factor i, partition R^E/R1 by the condition F_{i,u}=F_{i,u′}; the closures form a polyhedral complex Σ_i coarsening the dual complex of ∆_w. At any nonmaximal cell, the star of Σ_i is the normal fan of the lattice polytope Conv(F_{i,u}), so the union of codimension-one cells is rational, pure, and balanced. A standard tropical convexity result then realizes Σ_i as the dual complex of some weighted point configuration. Using connectedness of maximal cells through codimension one and the one-dimensional assumption, the necessary dilation between this auxiliary configuration and the actual vertices of A_i is global; this assigns weights w_i with the prescribed factor faces. Lemma 2.2.6 then gives ∆_{Σw_i}=∆_w. For Corollary 4.4.5, a flag-matroidal subdivision is mixed; Theorem 2.2.9 supplies Minkowski-summand weights, and Theorem 4.4.3 shows these weights form a valuated flag matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1.2 and Corollary 5.1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [n~]={0,1,...,n}. Consider the partially defined coordinate-splitting map P(T^{C([n~],r+1)}) ⇢ P(T^{C([n],r)}) × P(T^{C([n],r+1)}) sending a valuated rank-(r+1) object on [n~] to the coordinates containing 0, with 0 removed, and the coordinates not containing 0. Let Ω(r+1;[n~]) be the locus where both coordinate blocks are not all ∞. Then the induced map Ω(r+1;[n~]) ∩ Dr(r+1;n+1) → FlDr(r,r+1;n) is surjective. The fiber over a point (µ′,µ), where µ′ has rank r and µ has rank r+1 on [n], is {a⊙µ′ ⊕ b⊙µ : a,b ∈ R} ≅ R^2/R(1,1), where µ′ is embedded on bases containing 0 and µ on bases not containing 0. Consequently, under the identification T^{C([n+1],r+1)} ≅ T^{C([n],r)} × T^{C([n],r+1)}, the affine cones over Dr(r+1;n+1) and FlDr(r,r+1;n) are identical.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Well-definedness follows from Corollary 4.3.2(3): deleting and contracting an element of a valuated matroid give a valuated quotient. For surjectivity, begin with a valuated quotient µ′ ↞ µ of adjacent ranks and let M′ ↞ M be its underlying quotient. The classical adjacent-rank quotient construction gives a unique matroid M̃ on {0,...,n} with bases {I′∪0 : I′∈B(M′)} ∪ B(M), satisfying M′=M̃/0 and M=M̃ delete 0. Define a weight µ̃ by a+µ′ on bases containing 0 and b+µ on bases not containing 0. The base polytope Q(M̃) is the Cayley polytope of Q(M′) and Q(M); by the Cayley trick, cells of the subdivision induced by µ̃ correspond to cells of the subdivision induced by (a+µ′)+(b+µ) on B(M′)+B(M). Theorem 4.4.2 makes those cells flag-matroidal, and the adjacent-rank matroid construction turns them into matroid base polytopes, so Theorem 3.1.3 shows µ̃ is a valuated matroid. The fiber description records the only freedom: independent additive constants on the two coordinate blocks, modulo global projective scaling. The affine-cone statement also checks the cases where one block is all ∞, corresponding to 0 being a loop or coloop.

proof_techniques.name: Encode inclusion by valuated matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper turns the geometric condition trop(µ) ⊆ trop(ν) into the algebraic condition µ ↞ ν. The proof uses two dual descriptions of a projective tropical linear space: it is the tropical span of valuated cocircuits, and it is cut out by valuated circuit tropical hyperplanes. Testing each cocircuit of µ against each circuit of ν produces exactly the tropical incidence-Plücker relations, which are equivalent to the valuated quotient exchange inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A poset of tropical linear subspaces can be studied via quotient relations among valuated matroids. To construct or rule out an order-reversing map, one can try to define it on the corresponding valuated quotient data and check that it reverses the quotient relation.

proof_techniques.name: Use projective tropical linear spaces to handle loops and boundary strata
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces ordinary tropical linear spaces in R^[n]/R1 by projective tropical linear spaces in P(T^[n]). This is essential because restrictions, contractions, and flags naturally create loops; ordinary tropical linear spaces of matroids with loops are empty, while projective tropical linear spaces retain the correct boundary pieces. The projective space is decomposed into strata T_S according to finite support, and the intersection with T_{[n]−S} is the tropical linear space of the contraction µ/S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The flat-indexed subspaces in the prompt involve contractions together with added loops. The projective formalism is the right language for comparing such objects without losing information at the boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Coloopless-cell criterion in the dual complex
proof_techniques.description: Theorem B identifies trop(µ) with the set of projective points u for which the selected face ∆^u_{µ^*} in the dual valuated matroid subdivision is a coloopless matroid base polytope. This converts inclusion questions for tropical linear spaces into statements about propagation of colooplessness through families of matroidal faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution might be easier to study on dual subdivisions or face posets: one can attempt to show that a candidate map sends the coloopless-cell condition for one valuated matroid to the corresponding condition for the reversed object.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary control via M-convex face factorization
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex functions, faces toward a boundary stratum split as products µ|S × µ/S, and closures of dual-complex cells meet the stratum T_S in the dual complex of µ|S. This gives precise control of how tropical linear spaces and their subdivisions degenerate along projective boundary strata.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of the lattice of flats into a Dressian-like poset is boundary/minor flavored. The factorization technique can be used to check whether a proposed involution is compatible with all flats by reducing to restrictions and contractions on strata.

proof_techniques.name: Flags as coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a valuated flag matroid µ_1,...,µ_k by the Minkowski-sum weight µ_1+...+µ_k on the base configuration B(M_1)+...+B(M_k). The cells of the induced subdivision are base polytopes of flag matroids, and conversely coherent flag-matroidal subdivisions arise from valuated flag matroids under the mixed-subdivision criterion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Chains in an inclusion poset of tropical linear spaces can be modeled as points of a flag Dressian or as flag-matroidal subdivisions. An anti-involution on the poset might be sought as a transformation of these coherent subdivisions that reverses the order of the Minkowski summands.

proof_techniques.name: Propagation of looplessness or colooplessness through a flag
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.4.4 shows that in a flag matroid M_1 ↞ ... ↞ M_k, looplessness propagates forward in rank, and dually colooplessness propagates forward for the corresponding dual sequence. This simple rank-function observation is the key local step in proving that flag-matroidal subdivisions yield inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Order reversal often exchanges loop-like and coloop-like behavior. This propagation lemma gives a concrete criterion to verify monotonicity or antimonotonicity of a candidate operation on chains.

proof_techniques.name: Adjacent-rank quotients via one-element extensions
proof_techniques.description: For a quotient of matroids or valuated matroids whose ranks differ by one, the paper encodes the pair as deletion and contraction of a single valuated matroid on one larger ground set. Tropically, this gives a surjective map from a subset of an ordinary Dressian Dr(r+1;n+1) to the adjacent-rank flag Dressian FlDr(r,r+1;n), with one-dimensional fibers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the poset problem can be reduced to covering relations or adjacent-dimensional inclusions, this construction lets one replace a two-level inclusion by a single valuated matroid with a distinguished element, where duality or a flat involution may be easier to express.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit-cocircuit testing of candidate maps
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 4.3.1 gives a practical test for inclusion: every valuated cocircuit of the smaller space must satisfy every valuated circuit equation of the larger space. This test is equivalent to all incidence-Plücker relations and avoids direct comparison of polyhedral supports.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a proposed order-reversing involution Φ, one can attempt to prove L ⊆ L′ implies Φ(L′) ⊆ Φ(L) by transforming circuits of Φ(L) and cocircuits of Φ(L′) and checking the resulting tropical minima.

other_useful_info: Notation note: in this paper Dr(M) denotes the usual Dressian stratum parametrizing valuated matroids with fixed underlying matroid M, whereas the original problem uses relative Dressian for tropical linear subspaces inside a fixed tropical linear space. The bridge supplied by the paper is not the notation but Theorem 4.3.1 and Theorem A, which turn inclusions and chains of projective tropical linear spaces into valuated quotient and flag-Dressian data. Remark 4.1.6 emphasizes that loops cannot be discarded in flag settings, motivating the projective definitions; this is directly relevant whenever contractions plus added loops appear. Remark 4.3.3 extends the quotient-inclusion dictionary to valuated matroid morphisms for a map of finite sets φ: [n]→[m], where ν ↠ φ^{-1}µ is equivalent to a commutative diagram trop(µ) → trop(ν) inside P(T^[m]) → P(T^[n]). The paper also proves small-ground-set realizability of all valuated flag matroids for n≤5 and gives examples of nonrealizable valuated flag matroids on 6 elements, which may be useful for testing conjectural constructions but is not central to the order-reversal question.
why_relevant: Proves equivalences among valuated flag matroids, flags of projective tropical linear spaces, and coherent flag matroidal subdivisions. Since chains in Dr(M) are flags of tropical subspaces, this supplies the main flag-Dressian framework.

title: Generalized Permutahedra and Positive Flag Dressians
overall_summary: The paper studies regular subdivisions of regular permutahedra into generalized permutahedra, interpreting them through full flags of valuated matroids and tropical incidence relations. Its central non-positive results are: tropical incidence relations between two consecutive ranks already force the two functions to be valuated matroids; a full flag of valuated uniform matroids compresses to exactly a valuated permutahedron; and a height function on the vertices of the regular permutahedron induces a permutahedral subdivision if and only if explicit square and hexagon conditions hold on the 2-skeleton. For the original problem, the most useful material is the valuated-matroid quotient/incidence formalism, because the paper recalls that quotients are equivalent to inclusions of tropical linear spaces, and because Theorem 10 lets one verify valuatedness from incidence alone. The truncation/elongation construction and the embedding of an ordinary Dressian into a flag Dressian may also be useful for building canonical incident flags around a given tropical linear space. The paper does not address relative Dressians or involutions directly, but it provides tools for translating inclusion questions into valuated matroid incidence equations and for reducing global flag-subdivision conditions to local rank-two or 2-skeleton checks.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Π_n be the regular permutahedron in R^n, defined as the convex hull of the points (σ(1),…,σ(n)) for σ in Sym(n). For a flag F=(F_1,…,F_k) of nonempty subsets of [n], meaning F_1 ⊂ ··· ⊂ F_k, choose real numbers λ_1 ≫ ··· ≫ λ_k > 0 and set λ_F = λ_1 e_{F_1} + λ_2 e_{F_2\F_1} + ··· + λ_k e_{F_k\F_{k-1}}, where e_A = sum_{i in A} e_i. The map sending F to the nonempty proper face of Π_n maximizing the linear functional λ_F is a bijection between such flags and nonempty proper faces of Π_n. In particular: a flag with k constituents corresponds to a codimension-k face affinely equivalent to Π_|F_1| × Π_|F_2\F_1| × ··· × Π_|F_k\F_{k-1}| × Π_|[n]\F_k|; facets correspond to nonempty proper subsets of [n]; vertices correspond to full flags; edges correspond to pairs of full flags differing in exactly one constituent; every 2-face is either a hexagon, when one block F_{i+1}\F_i has size 3, or a square, when two distinct blocks have size 2; and, if A,B ⊂ [n] have |A|=|B| and |A △ B|=2, then all edges defined by full flags extending the pair (A∩B, A∪B) span a face isomorphic to Π_|A∩B| × Π_2 × Π_{n-|A∪B|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites standard descriptions of the face lattice of the permutahedron. The normal fan of Π_n is the braid fan, and choosing λ_1 ≫ ··· ≫ λ_k makes the maximized face depend exactly on the ordered partition F_1, F_2\F_1, …, F_k\F_{k-1}, [n]\F_k. The product decomposition of the face follows because permutations maximizing λ_F may permute freely inside each block and must respect the block order. The descriptions of facets, vertices, edges, and 2-faces are the corresponding special cases of the block sizes. The final assertion follows by fixing the two adjacent ranks A∩B and A∪B in a full flag; the variable middle block is Π_2 and the lower and upper parts vary independently as smaller permutahedra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 4, the group of combinatorial automorphisms Aut(Π_n) of the regular permutahedron is isomorphic to Sym(n) × Z_2 and has order 2·n!. For n=3, Aut(Π_3) is the dihedral group of order 12, and for n=2 it is the cyclic group of order 2. In all these cases every combinatorial automorphism is induced by a linear automorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Sym(n) acts freely on the vertices of Π_n by right multiplication and preserves the facet structure. The paper then constructs an additional reflection. With λ(x)=x_1−x_2−x_{n−1}+x_n, define s(x)=x−((x_1−x_2−x_{n−1}+x_n)/2)(1,−1,0,…,0,−1,1). Direct inspection shows that s permutes the vertices and fixes o=(1,2,…,n). For n≥4 this reflection commutes with the Sym(n)-action. To prove there are no further automorphisms, it suffices to identify the stabilizer of o. The facets through o correspond to the subsets [1],[2],…,[n−1]. The facet corresponding to [k] is isomorphic to Π_k × Π_{n−k}, and s exchanges [k] with [n−k]. These product types force any automorphism fixing o to act as either the identity or this reversal on the chain of incident facets; after fixing one incident facet, the stabilizer is trivial. The n=2 and n=3 cases are checked separately: Π_2 is a segment and Π_3 is a regular hexagon.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quotient and incidence equivalences recalled in Section 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be matroids on [n] of ranks d and d+1, respectively. The paper writes M ↞ N and says that M and N form a quotient if conv(M×{1} ∪ N×{0}) is a matroid, identifying matroids with their base polytopes. If μ and ν are valuated matroids with underlying matroids M and N, respectively, then μ ↞ ν means M ↞ N and the 3-term tropical incidence relations hold: for every S in C([n],d−1) and every distinct i,j,k in [n]\S, the minimum of μ(S∪{i})+ν(S∪{j,k}), μ(S∪{j})+ν(S∪{i,k}), and μ(S∪{k})+ν(S∪{i,j}) is attained at least twice. The paper recalls that these quotient notions are equivalent to inclusion of the corresponding ordinary or tropical linear spaces, with the smaller-rank object incident to or contained in the larger-rank object. For consecutive ranks, the paper also recalls the equivalent single-element-extension formulation: there exists a valuated matroid ξ on [n+1] such that μ is obtained from ξ by contracting n+1 and ν is obtained from ξ by deleting n+1; the same equivalence holds in the unvaluated matroid case.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as background from the tropical flag varieties literature, especially Brandt–Eur–Zhang. The incidence relations are the tropicalizations of the classical incidence equations for consecutive linear subspaces. The deletion/contraction formulation packages an incident consecutive pair into one valuated matroid on a ground set with one extra element; deleting the new element gives the higher-rank member and contracting it gives the lower-rank member. Conversely, any such single-element valuated matroid extension yields the 3-term incidence relations for the deletion/contraction pair, and these relations encode containment of the associated tropical linear spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (Borovik–Gelfand–White, reformulated)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A polytope is the base polytope of a full flag matroid if and only if it is a subpermutahedron of the regular permutahedron Π_n. Here a full flag matroid is a sequence M=(M_1,…,M_n) of matroids on [n] such that rank(M_i)=i and M_i ↞ M_j for every 1≤i<j≤n; its base polytope is the Minkowski sum M_1+···+M_n, equivalently the convex hull of sums e_{B_1}+···+e_{B_n} over compatible choices of bases B_i in M_i. A subpermutahedron of Π_n means a subpolytope of Π_n that is itself a generalized permutahedron, i.e. all of its edges are parallel to e_i−e_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes the result from Borovik, Gelfand, and White, using a reformulation from Brandt–Eur–Zhang. One direction is that a flag matroid base polytope is a generalized permutahedron whose vertices are among the vertices of Π_n, hence a subpermutahedron. Conversely, a subpolytope of Π_n whose edges are all in root directions e_i−e_j determines a compatible sequence of matroid base polytopes in each rank; the corresponding sequence satisfies the flag-matroid quotient conditions and has the given polytope as its base polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6 (Brandt–Eur–Zhang)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A height function w: Sym(n) → R on the vertices of the regular permutahedron Π_n is a valuated permutahedron if and only if it is the restriction to Sym(n) of the compression of a full flag of valuated uniform matroids. In this statement, w is a valuated permutahedron when the regular subdivision of Π_n induced by w has only subpermutahedra as cells, equivalently when the piecewise-linear extension of w to Π_n∩Z^n is M-convex. If (μ_1,…,μ_n) is a full flag of valuated uniform matroids, with μ_i a valuation on bases of U_{i,n}, its compression is the function u on the Minkowski sum of the corresponding matroid polytopes defined by u(x)=min{sum_i μ_i(Y_i) : x=sum_i e_{Y_i}, Y_i is a basis of U_{i,n} for all i}. The theorem says exactly that w=u restricted to the vertices Sym(n) for some such valuated flag if and only if w induces a permutahedral subdivision.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper derives this from Brandt–Eur–Zhang. If (μ_1,…,μ_n) is a valuated flag matroid, then the subdivision of Π_n∩Z^n induced by its compression has cells that are base polytopes of flag matroids. By Theorem 3, those cells are subpermutahedra of Π_n, so the restricted height function is a valuated permutahedron. Conversely, Brandt–Eur–Zhang, Corollary 4.4.5, says that any regular subdivision of Π_n into subpermutahedra arises from the compression of a valuated flag matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every subpermutahedron P of Π_n appears as a cell of some regular permutahedral subdivision of Π_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 3, P is the base polytope of a full flag matroid (M_1,…,M_n). For each constituent M_i, take the corank valuation μ_i, i.e. μ_i(T)=corank_{M_i}(T) on subsets T of the relevant rank. The paper checks that (μ_1,…,μ_n) is a valuated flag matroid. For consecutive constituents M_d and M_{d+1}, the quotient condition gives a matroid N on [n+1] such that N/(n+1)=M_d and N\(n+1)=M_{d+1}. The corank valuation of N is a valuated matroid; deleting and contracting the extra element gives the corank valuations of M_{d+1} and M_d, so the tropical incidence relations hold for μ_d and μ_{d+1}. Thus the whole sequence is a valuated flag. Its compression is zero on the vertices of P, because all constituents are bases of their M_i, and positive on all other vertices of Π_n, because some constituent has positive corank. Hence P is a lower cell of the induced regular subdivision, and the subdivision is permutahedral by Theorem 6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ: C([n],d) → R be a valuated matroid of rank d. Its truncation is the valuated matroid tr(μ)=μ^(1) of rank d−1 defined by μ^(1)(S)=min{μ(T) : S⊂T, |T|=d} for S in C([n],d−1). Its elongation is the valuated matroid el(μ)=μ^(−1) of rank d+1 defined dually by μ^(−1)(S)=min{μ(T) : T⊂S, |T|=d} for S in C([n],d+1). Then the truncation and μ form a valuated matroid quotient μ^(1) ↞ μ, and μ and its elongation form a valuated matroid quotient μ ↞ μ^(−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the exchange-inequality characterization of tropical incidence from Brandt–Eur–Zhang. For the truncation relation, fix exchange data in that quotient criterion and choose an element k realizing the truncation minimum, so that μ^(1)(I)=μ(I∪{k}) for the appropriate set I in the exchange axiom. Apply the valuated matroid exchange inequality for μ to the relevant two sets, one of which is I∪{k}, and to the prescribed exchanged element i. This gives an inequality of the form μ(I∪{k})+μ(J) ≥ min_j μ((I∪{k})−{i}∪{j})+μ(J−{j}∪{i}). By definition of truncation, μ((I∪{k})−{i}∪{j}) ≥ μ^(1)(I−{i}∪{j}), so the right-hand side is at least the corresponding minimum with μ^(1). This is exactly the quotient exchange inequality for μ^(1) ↞ μ. The elongation statement follows by duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a valuated matroid μ of rank d on [n], define iterated truncations and elongations by μ^(0)=μ, μ^(k+1)=(μ^(k))^(1), and μ^(−k−1)=(μ^(−k))^(−1), whenever the ranks make sense. Then the map ι from the Dressian Dr(d,n) to the full flag Dressian FlDr(n) given by ι(μ) = (μ^(d−1), …, μ^(1), μ, μ^(−1), …, μ^(d−n)) is a piecewise-linear embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 8 shows that each adjacent pair in the displayed sequence is a valuated quotient, so the sequence is a valuated full flag. The construction is piecewise-linear because each truncation or elongation coordinate is obtained as the minimum of finitely many coordinates of the previous valuated matroid, hence is linear on the cones where the minimizing choices are fixed. The map is injective because μ itself appears as one constituent of the flag, so the original valuated matroid can be read off from ι(μ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ: C([n],d) → R and ν: C([n],d+1) → R be arbitrary functions. Assume that they satisfy the 3-term tropical incidence relations: for every S in C([n],d−1) and every distinct i,j,k in [n]\S, the minimum of μ(S∪{i})+ν(S∪{j,k}), μ(S∪{j})+ν(S∪{i,k}), and μ(S∪{k})+ν(S∪{i,j}) is attained at least twice. Then μ and ν are valuated matroids, i.e. they satisfy all 3-term tropical Plücker relations in ranks d and d+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first shows that ν satisfies the 3-term Plücker relations. Suppose not. Then there exist S in C([n],d−1) and distinct i,j,k,l outside S such that, writing for example Sij for S∪{i,j}, one has ν(Sij)+ν(Skl) < ν(Sik)+ν(Sjl) and ν(Sij)+ν(Skl) < ν(Sil)+ν(Sjk). Subtract a suitable linear form from both μ and ν: take ξ=μ(Si)e_i+μ(Sj)e_j+μ(Sk)e_k+μ(Sl)e_l, replace ν(T) by ν(T)−〈ξ,e_T〉 and μ analogously. This preserves the incidence relations and normalizes μ(Si)=μ(Sj)=μ(Sk)=μ(Sl)=0. With this normalization, the incidence relations for the triples ijk, ikl, and ijl say that each of min(ν(Sij),ν(Sik),ν(Sjk)), min(ν(Sik),ν(Sil),ν(Skl)), and min(ν(Sij),ν(Sil),ν(Sjl)) is attained at least twice. By relabeling within the assumed Plücker failure, the paper assumes ν(Sij)<ν(Sik). The first of the three incidence minima then forces ν(Sij)=ν(Sjk). The second strict Plücker inequality gives ν(Skl)<ν(Sil), and the second incidence minimum then forces ν(Skl)=ν(Sik). The first strict Plücker inequality gives ν(Sij)<ν(Sjl), and the third incidence minimum forces ν(Sij)=ν(Sil). Combining these equalities yields ν(Sij)=ν(Sil)>ν(Skl)=ν(Sik), contradicting ν(Sij)<ν(Sik). Therefore ν satisfies all 3-term Plücker relations. The same conclusion for μ follows by duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a polytope with vertex-edge graph Γ=(V,E), and replace each edge by the two opposite directed edges to obtain Γ^±=(V,E^±). Suppose g:E^±→R is an antisymmetric edge function, so g(w,v)=−g(v,w), whose sum around the cyclically oriented boundary of every 2-face of P is zero. Then, for any fixed vertex s in V and any f_0 in R, there is a unique function f:V→R such that f(s)=f_0 and f(v)−f(w)=g(v,w) for every directed edge (v,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a directed spanning tree of Γ^± rooted at s. Define f inductively along the tree by f(s)=f_0 and f(v)=g(v,w)+f(w) along each directed tree edge. To prove this does not depend on the chosen tree path and works for every edge, it is enough to show that the sum of g around every directed cycle in Γ is zero. The paper uses the fact that the boundary complex of a polytope has its edge-cycle homotopy generated by 2-face boundaries: in dimension at least 3 this follows from simple connectivity of the boundary sphere, and in the polygonal 2-dimensional case it is immediate from the single face boundary. Thus any graph cycle can be reduced, through symmetric differences with 2-face cycles, to the trivial cycle. Since g sums to zero around each 2-face, it sums to zero around every cycle. This gives existence and path-independence; uniqueness follows because any two such vertex functions differ by a constant and the value at s is fixed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G^±_{Π(n)} be the directed vertex-edge graph of the regular permutahedron Π_n, and let G^±_{Δ(d,n)} be the directed vertex-edge graph of the hypersimplex Δ(d,n). Suppose g is a function on the directed edges of G^±_{Π(n)} such that, on each square 2-face of Π_n, parallel directed edges have the same g-value. Then g determines a well-defined function g′ on the directed edges of the disjoint union over d=0,…,n of G^±_{Δ(d,n)} as follows: if A and B are equicardinal subsets of [n] with |A△B|=2, and e is any edge of Π_n corresponding to the pair (A,B), set g′(A,B)=g(e). This value is independent of the choice of e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix equicardinal A,B with |A△B|=2. By Proposition 1, the full flags extending the pair (A∩B, A∪B) define a face of Π_n isomorphic to Π_|A∩B| × Π_2 × Π_{n−|A∪B|}. The edges corresponding to the exchange A↔B are exactly the Π_2-direction edges in this product face. Any two such edges can be connected by a sequence of square 2-faces within the product, where consecutive exchange edges occur as parallel sides of one square. The square hypothesis says parallel directed exchange edges have equal g-values, so all permutahedron edges corresponding to (A,B) have the same value. This defines g′ on the hypersimplex edge (A,B).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let w: Sym(n) → R be a height function on the vertices of the regular permutahedron Π_n. Then w induces a regular subdivision of Π_n into subpermutahedra if and only if the induced subdivision on the 2-skeleton of Π_n is permutahedral. Equivalently, the following local conditions hold. For every hexagonal 2-face with cyclically labeled vertices a,b,c,d,e,f: HXE: w(a)+w(c)+w(e)=w(b)+w(d)+w(f); HXM: the maximum of w(a)+w(d), w(b)+w(e), and w(c)+w(f) is attained at least twice. For every square 2-face with cyclically labeled vertices a,b,c,d: SQR: w(a)+w(c)=w(b)+w(d).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Necessity is obtained by restricting the regular subdivision to each 2-face. A square 2-face cannot be subdivided further into smaller subpermutahedra, so its four lifted vertices must be coplanar, giving SQR. On a hexagonal 2-face, a permutahedral subdivision has at least four cyclically consecutive vertices on one lower hyperplane and the other two on or above it; after relabeling and subtracting an affine-linear function, the heights can be taken as w(a)=w(b)=w(c)=w(d)=0 and w(e)=w(f)≥0, which gives HXE and HXM. For sufficiency, assume HXE, HXM, and SQR. Define an edge-difference h′ on directed permutahedron edges by h′(a,b)=w(a)−w(b). By SQR and Lemma 13, h′ descends to edge functions h_d on the directed graphs of all hypersimplices Δ(d,n). The square conditions also give zero sums on square 2-faces of the hypersimplices, while HXE on hexagons of Π_n gives zero sums on triangular 2-faces: a hexagon corresponding to flags extending (S,S∪{i,j,k}) yields h(Si,Sj)+h(Sj,Sk)+h(Sk,Si)=0, and similarly in the adjacent rank. Proposition 12 therefore integrates each h_d to a vertex function g_d on Δ(d,n), with additive constants chosen so that for one fixed full flag G=(G_1,…,G_n) corresponding to a vertex u of Π_n, sum_d g_d(G_d)=w(u). For any other vertex v corresponding to a full flag F=(F_1,…,F_n), a path from u to v in the permutahedron decomposes into paths from G_d to F_d in the hypersimplices; hence w(v)=sum_d g_d(F_d). Now apply HXM to a hexagon corresponding to intermediate sets S⊂S∪{i,j,k}. All ranks except |S|+1 and |S|+2 cancel in the expression w(v)=sum_d g_d(F_d). After subtracting the sum of the six relevant g-values and multiplying by −1, HXM becomes the 3-term tropical incidence relation min(g_{r+1}(S∪{j})+g_{r+2}(S∪{i,k}), g_{r+1}(S∪{k})+g_{r+2}(S∪{i,j}), g_{r+1}(S∪{i})+g_{r+2}(S∪{j,k})) attained at least twice, where r=|S|. Thus adjacent g_d satisfy tropical incidence. By Theorem 10, the g_d are valuated matroids, and together they form a valuated flag. The function w is the restriction to vertices of the compression of this valuated flag, so Theorem 6 implies that w induces a permutahedral subdivision.

proof_techniques.name: Translate containment into valuated matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper consistently treats incidence or containment of consecutive tropical linear spaces via valuated matroid quotients. For ranks d and d+1, the quotient relation μ ↞ ν is encoded by the 3-term tropical incidence relations, and the paper recalls that this is equivalent to inclusion of the associated tropical linear spaces. For consecutive ranks, the same data can be packaged as deletion and contraction of a single valuated matroid on a ground set with one extra element.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian Dr(M) is defined by tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M), so quotient/incidence data are a natural coordinate system for its elements. An attempted order-reversing involution could be studied by how it transforms these quotient pairs or the corresponding single-element extensions.

proof_techniques.name: Use incidence relations to force Plücker relations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 10 shows that if two arbitrary finite functions in consecutive ranks satisfy only the tropical incidence relations, then each function automatically satisfies the tropical Plücker relations and hence is a valuated matroid. The proof normalizes selected μ-values by subtracting a tropical linear form, applies incidence to three triples, and derives a contradiction to any failed Plücker relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When constructing or testing a map on a relative Dressian, it may be enough to verify preservation of incidence with an ambient object rather than separately checking all Plücker relations for the image. This can reduce extension problems to local incidence checks.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical flag completion by truncation and elongation
proof_techniques.description: Given one valuated matroid μ of rank d, the paper forms lower-rank truncations by minimizing over one-element supersets and higher-rank elongations by minimizing over one-element subsets. Lemma 8 proves that each adjacent pair is a valuated quotient, and Proposition 9 packages all iterated truncations and elongations into a full valuated flag, giving a piecewise-linear embedding of an ordinary Dressian into the flag Dressian.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This supplies canonical incident objects above and below a tropical linear space. For a proposed involution extending an order-reversing flat involution, truncation/elongation flags may provide test objects or a way to compare behavior across ranks.

proof_techniques.name: Compression converts valuated flags into permutahedral subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The compression construction takes a flag of valuated matroids and produces a single height function on a Minkowski sum of matroid polytopes, in the uniform full-flag case on Π_n. Theorem 6 says that height functions inducing regular subdivisions of Π_n into subpermutahedra are exactly those arising as such compressions. This turns flag-valuated-matroid data into polyhedral subdivision data.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a relative Dressian problem can be embedded into a flag Dressian or represented by subdivisions of a matroid/generalized permutahedron, compression may turn inclusion-order questions into questions about cells, subdivisions, or symmetries of polytopes.

proof_techniques.name: Recover global vertex potentials from local edge differences
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 12 is a discrete integration lemma: an antisymmetric edge function on a polytope graph comes from vertex heights if and only if it sums to zero around 2-face boundaries. Lemma 13 applies this to the permutahedron, showing that square-face consistency lets edge differences on Π_n descend to edge differences on hypersimplices. Theorem 14 then integrates those descended edge functions into valuated matroid constituents of a flag.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: To define a global involution on a Dressian-like object from local rank-one or codimension-one data, one may try to define edge-level transformations first and then prove 2-face cycle conditions. This is a possible route for extending an involution from the flat lattice to larger valuated data.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce global flag conditions to square and hexagon tests
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 14 gives a strong local-to-global principle for regular permutahedral subdivisions: it suffices to inspect the 2-skeleton, with square affine-dependence equations and hexagon tropical Plücker-type maximum conditions. In the proof, square equations ensure well-defined descent to hypersimplex graphs, hexagon equalities ensure integrability, and hexagon maximum conditions become tropical incidence relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For relative Dressians of special matroids where cells or strata are controlled by generalized permutahedra, analogous low-dimensional tests could make it feasible to check whether a candidate order-reversing operation preserves the entire relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Normalize valuations by subtracting linear forms
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 10, the authors subtract the same linear form from μ and ν to set four chosen μ-coordinates equal to zero. This operation preserves incidence and Plücker-type tropical relations because it corresponds to adding lineality from the tropical torus action.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any proof involving a candidate involution on valuated matroids can quotient out or normalize by tropical linear forms to simplify local checks, especially around circuits or flats where only relative valuations matter.

other_useful_info: The positive part of the paper may be useful only under additional positivity hypotheses: Proposition 4 identifies Bruhat interval polytopes with flag matroid base polytopes realizable in the totally nonnegative flag variety, and Theorem 20 characterizes compressions of totally positive flags by subdivisions into Bruhat interval polytopes and by a strengthened hexagon condition HXM+. Section 5 computes the fan Φ_4 of height functions on Π_4 satisfying the square and hexagon conditions: its link is a pure 2-dimensional spherical polytopal complex with f-vector (20,76,75), simply connected and with top integral homology Z^18; moreover, the fan from the 2-skeleton conditions is coarser than the corresponding subfan of the secondary fan, so distinct permutahedral subdivisions can induce the same 2-skeleton pattern. The paper asks in Question 24 how Theorem 14 should generalize from regular permutahedra to arbitrary generalized permutahedra, which is directly relevant if one tries to move from uniform flag settings to arbitrary matroid polytopes. The paper also points to Brandt–Eur–Zhang, Tropical flag varieties, for the foundational equivalence between valuated flag matroids, incidence, and tropical flags, and to Olarte–Panizzut–Schröter on local Dressians of matroids for nearby Dressian subdivision structures.
why_relevant: Studies valuated matroids, tropical incidence relations, flag matroids, and subdivisions into generalized permutahedra. It contains structural results on flag Dressians and incidence that specialize to codimension-one relative Dressian questions.

title: Flag matroids with coefficients
overall_summary: The paper generalizes flag matroids to matroids over an arbitrary tract F, including valuated and tropical matroids, and proves cryptomorphic characterizations in terms of Grassmann-Plücker flag relations, circuits, cocircuits, vectors, and covectors. Its central technical result is that a quotient N ։ M of F-matroids is equivalent to the containment C*(M) ⊆ V*(N), and over perfect tracts, including the tropical hyperfield T, flag F-matroids are exactly chains of covector sets. This identifies flag T-matroids with flags of tropical linear spaces and with points of the flag Dressian, and gives functorial duality and minors for such flags. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the framework relating inclusion of tropical linear spaces to quotient/flag structures, together with the theorem that duality reverses quotients and induces an isomorphism on flag varieties; these are natural tools for trying to construct an order-reversing operation on a relative Dressian. The moduli-space results also suggest viewing a relative Dressian as a locus inside an absolute flag Dressian and testing whether the universal duality morphism restricts to it.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.7, Baker-Bowler dual-pair cryptomorphism recalled in the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract, let E be a finite ground set, and let N be a classical matroid on E. An F-signature of N is a subset C of F^E whose supports are exactly the circuits of N and which satisfies the usual nonzero, scaling, and support-minimality axioms. If D is an F-signature of the dual classical matroid N*, and if C ⊥ D under the tract orthogonality relation, then (C,D) is called a dual pair of F-signatures of N. The theorem states that F-matroids M with underlying classical matroid N are in bijection with dual pairs of F-signatures of N, via M ↦ (C(M), C*(M)), where C(M) is the set of F-circuits and C*(M)=C(M*) is the set of F-cocircuits.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Baker-Bowler rather than reproving it. The construction from a Grassmann-Plücker function ϕ representing M produces F-circuit representatives by evaluating ϕ on fundamental circuits, and dual representatives by applying the Baker-Bowler dual Grassmann-Plücker function ϕ*. Orthogonality of circuits and cocircuits gives the dual-pair condition. Conversely, the Baker-Bowler theorem reconstructs the F-matroid from a compatible pair of F-signatures, and the circuit set determines the F-matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.8, Baker-Bowler minors recalled in the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract, let ϕ:E^r → F be a Grassmann-Plücker function representing an F-matroid M on a finite set E, and let A ⊆ E. For contraction, let ℓ be the rank of A in M and choose a maximal M-independent subset {a1,...,aℓ} of A; define (ϕ/A)(x1,...,x_{r-ℓ}) = ϕ(x1,...,x_{r-ℓ},a1,...,aℓ) on E−A. For deletion, let k be the rank of E−A in M and choose a1,...,a_{r-k} in A forming a basis of M/(E−A); define (ϕ minus A)(x1,...,x_k) = ϕ(x1,...,x_k,a1,...,a_{r-k}). Then ϕ/A and ϕ minus A are Grassmann-Plücker functions, are independent of all choices up to multiplication by an element of F×, have underlying classical matroids equal to the usual contraction and deletion, and satisfy the duality identities (ϕ minus A)* = ϕ*/A and (ϕ/A)* = ϕ* minus A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as a Baker-Bowler lemma. The key point is that substituting a basis of the part to be contracted or deleted into the Grassmann-Plücker relations gives the Grassmann-Plücker relations for the minor. Different choices of maximal independent sets or complementary bases are related by Plücker exchange, producing only a global scalar change. The duality identities follow by comparing the complementary coordinate formula defining the dual Grassmann-Plücker function with the coordinate definitions of deletion and contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3, push-forwards preserve quotients and flags
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:F → F′ be a morphism of tracts. If M and N are F-matroids and N ։ M is a quotient in the sense of the Plücker flag relations, then f_*N ։ f_*M is a quotient of F′-matroids. Consequently, if M=(M1,...,Ms) is a flag F-matroid, then f_*M=(f_*M1,...,f_*Ms) is a flag F′-matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose Grassmann-Plücker functions μ and ν representing M and N. The Plücker flag relation for N ։ M is a finite formal sum of products of values of ν and μ lying in the nullset N_F. Applying f to every coefficient gives exactly the corresponding Plücker flag relation for f_*N and f_*M. Since a tract morphism sends null relations in F to null relations in F′, the quotient relation is preserved. The flag assertion follows by applying this pairwise to every quotient relation in the flag.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4, cryptomorphism for flag F-matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract, let M and N be F-matroids on the same finite ground set E, let r=rk(M), w=rk(N), and let C*(M) and V*(N) denote the cocircuit set of M and the covector set of N. Write N ։ M if, for every choice of Grassmann-Plücker functions μ:E^r → F and ν:E^w → F representing M and N, the Plücker flag relations ∑_{k=1}^{w+1} ε^k ν(y1,...,omit y_k,...,y_{w+1}) μ(y_k,x1,...,x_{r-1}) ∈ N_F hold for all y1,...,y_{w+1},x1,...,x_{r-1} in E. Then N ։ M if and only if C*(M) ⊆ V*(N). Consequently, a sequence (M1,...,Ms) of F-matroids is a flag F-matroid if and only if C*(Mi) ⊆ V*(Mj) for every 1 ≤ i < j ≤ s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the implication from Plücker flag relations to C*(M) ⊆ V*(N), fix Z in C*(M)=C(M*) and Y in C(N). Represent Z using a basis {z1,...,z_{n-r}} of M* containing the support of Z except for one element z0, so that Z(zi) is a scalar times ε^i μ*(z0,...,omit zi,...,z_{n-r}); choose x1,...,x_{r-1} to be the complement of {z0,...,z_{n-r}}. Represent Y similarly using a basis {y1,...,yw} of N containing the support of Y except y0, so that Y(yj) is a scalar times ε^j ν(y0,...,omit yj,...,yw). Let S be the intersection of the two supports and match common elements yj=zb(j). A direct sign computation using the formula for μ* rewrites the inner product Y·Z, up to multiplication by a unit and a global sign, as the Plücker flag sum ∑ ε^j ν(y0,...,omit yj,...,yw) μ(yj,x1,...,x_{r-1}); terms outside the intersection vanish. Since N ։ M, this sum lies in N_F, and the nullset is stable under multiplication by units, so Y ⊥ Z. As this holds for every circuit Y of N, Z is a covector of N. For the converse, assume C*(M) ⊆ V*(N) and fix arbitrary y0,...,yw and x1,...,x_{r-1}. If no index ℓ makes {yℓ,x1,...,x_{r-1}} a basis of M and {y0,...,omit yℓ,...,yw} a basis of N, then every product in the flag relation is zero. Otherwise choose such an ℓ, set z0=yℓ, let {z1,...,z_{n-r}} be the complement of {z0,x1,...,x_{r-1}}, and build H in C*(M) from μ* and G in C(N) from ν using the standard fundamental-circuit formulas. Since H ∈ V*(N), G·H ∈ N_F. The same sign computation as before shows that the required Plücker flag sum is a unit multiple of G·H, hence lies in N_F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 2.6 and 2.10, equal-rank quotients and rank monotonicity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract. If M and N are F-matroids with N ։ M and rk(N)=rk(M), then N=M as F-matroids. Consequently, if M=(M1,...,Ms) is a flag F-matroid, then the rank sequence rk(M1),...,rk(Ms) is nondecreasing.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For equal-rank quotients, Theorem 2.4 gives C*(M) ⊆ V*(N). Passing to underlying classical matroids via the terminal morphism F → K gives a classical quotient of equal rank, hence the underlying classical matroids are equal. The cocircuits of M and N therefore have the same supports. Since cocircuits are exactly the nonzero covectors of minimal support, every element of C*(M) lies in C*(N), and conversely support equality plus the scaling axiom gives C*(N) ⊆ C*(M). The cocircuit set determines the F-matroid, so M=N. For rank monotonicity, push forward along F → K and use that classical matroid quotients have nondecreasing rank.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8, classical flag matroids are flag K-matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the Krasner hyperfield, so that K-matroids are identified with ordinary matroids. If M and N are K-matroids on E, then M is a quotient of N in the paper's Plücker-flag sense if and only if the underlying ordinary matroid M is a quotient of the ordinary matroid N, equivalently every flat of M is a flat of N, equivalently every cocircuit of M is a union of cocircuits of N. Hence a sequence (M1,...,Ms) of K-matroids is a flag K-matroid if and only if the underlying ordinary matroids form a classical flag matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Baker-Bowler theory gives a bijection between K-cocircuits and ordinary cocircuits by taking supports. Anderson's result used in the proof says that K-covectors correspond to unions of ordinary cocircuits. Thus C*(M) ⊆ V*(N) is exactly the statement that every ordinary cocircuit of M is a union of ordinary cocircuits of N. Theorem 2.4 identifies this condition with N ։ M. Applying the same equivalence to every pair in a sequence gives the flag statement.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.11 and Remark 2.12, duality reverses quotients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract with a fixed involution used to define dual F-matroids. For F-matroids M and N on the same ground set E, N ։ M if and only if M* ։ N*. Equivalently, the following conditions are all equivalent: N ։ M; M* ։ N*; C(N) ⊆ V(M); C*(M) ⊆ V*(N); C(N) ⊥ C*(M); and C*(M) ⊥ C(N).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume N ։ M. By Theorem 2.4, C*(M)=C(M*) is contained in V*(N). This means every circuit of M* is orthogonal to every circuit of N. Taking orthogonal complements gives C*(N*)=C(N) contained in V*(M*), which by Theorem 2.4 is exactly M* ։ N*. The reverse implication follows by applying the same argument after dualizing again. The equivalences with the circuit-vector and orthogonality conditions are just the definitions V(M)=C*(M)^⊥ and V*(N)=C(N)^⊥ together with the preceding theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.13, duality for flag F-matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract with a fixed involution, let E have n elements, and let 0 ≤ r1 ≤ ... ≤ rs ≤ n. The map M=(M1,...,Ms) ↦ M*=(M_s*,...,M_1*) is a bijection from flag F-matroids of rank (r1,...,rs) on E to flag F-matroids of rank (n−rs,...,n−r1) on E. It is involutive: (M*)*=M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M is a flag F-matroid, then Mj ։ Mi for all i<j. Proposition 2.11 gives Mi* ։ Mj* for all i<j, so after reversing the order, (M_s*,...,M_1*) again satisfies all flag quotient relations. The rank of Mi* is n−rk(Mi), giving the stated rank sequence. Baker-Bowler duality gives M**=M for every F-matroid, so reversing twice recovers the original flag.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.14 and Theorem 2.15, minors preserve quotients and flag F-matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract, let M and N be F-matroids on E with N ։ M, and let e ∈ E. Then N/e ։ M/e and N minus e ։ M minus e. More generally, if M=(M1,...,Ms) is a flag F-matroid and I,J are disjoint subsets of E, then (M1 with I deleted and J contracted,...,Ms with I deleted and J contracted) is a flag F-matroid on E−(I∪J). The paper also notes the compatibility (M with I deleted and J contracted)* = M* with J deleted and I contracted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For contraction by one element e, choose Grassmann-Plücker functions μ and ν for M and N. The proof checks the Plücker flag relations for ν/e and μ/e in three cases: e is not a loop of M, e is a loop of both M and N, and e is a loop of M but not of N. In each case the desired flag relation for the contractions is either exactly a flag relation for ν and μ, or becomes one after adding a zero term involving μ(...,e,...,e) or μ(e,...) when e is a loop. Deletion is then deduced from contraction using Proposition 2.11 and the Baker-Bowler identities that duality swaps deletion and contraction. For arbitrary disjoint I and J, repeat the one-element deletion and contraction argument for each element. Applying this pairwise to all quotient relations in the flag proves the flag-minor theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.17, flag F-matroids over perfect tracts are chains of covectors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract, and let M1,...,Ms be F-matroids on the same finite set E. Consider the following properties: (1) the covector sets form a chain V*(M1) ⊆ ... ⊆ V*(Ms); (2) (M1,...,Ms) is a flag F-matroid, meaning Mj ։ Mi for all i<j; (3) Mi+1 ։ Mi for every i=1,...,s−1. In every tract, (1) implies (2) and (2) implies (3). If F is perfect, meaning V(M) ⊥ V*(M) for every F-matroid M, then (3) also implies (1), so all three properties are equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If V*(M1) ⊆ ... ⊆ V*(Ms), then for i<j one has C*(Mi) ⊆ V*(Mi) ⊆ V*(Mj), so Theorem 2.4 gives Mj ։ Mi. The implication from all pairwise quotient relations to adjacent quotient relations is immediate. For the converse over a perfect tract, assume Mi+1 ։ Mi. By Proposition 2.11, Mi* ։ M_{i+1}*. Theorem 2.4 gives C*(M_{i+1}*) ⊆ V*(Mi*). Taking orthogonal complements and using perfection yields V*(Mi)=V*(Mi*)^⊥ ⊆ C*(M_{i+1}*)^⊥ = V(M_{i+1}*) = V*(Mi+1). Iterating over i gives the chain.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.20, flag matroids over fields are ordinary flags of linear subspaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a field, regarded as a tract, and let M=(M1,...,Ms) be a flag K-matroid of rank r=(r1,...,rs) on E. Then V*(M1) ⊂ ... ⊂ V*(Ms), and each V*(Mi) is a K-linear subspace of K^E of dimension ri. This gives a bijection between flag K-matroids of rank r on E and flags V1 ⊂ ... ⊂ Vs of K-linear subspaces of K^E with dim Vi=ri.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Anderson's vector theorem identifies K-matroids of rank r with r-dimensional K-linear subspaces of K^E via M ↦ V*(M), and fields are perfect tracts. Theorem 2.17 then says that a sequence of K-matroids is a flag K-matroid exactly when their covector spaces form an inclusion chain. This is precisely the usual notion of a flag of linear subspaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.21 and Remark 2.22, valuated flag matroids are flag T-matroids and flags of tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the tropical hyperfield in the Berkovich convention, with underlying multiplicative monoid R_{≥0}, unit group R_{>0}, ε=1, and nullset consisting of finite sums of positive real terms whose maximum is attained at least twice. A Dress-Wenzel valuated matroid μ:E^r → R_{≥0}, modulo global scaling, corresponds to a T-matroid by composing μ with the identity R_{≥0} → T. A sequence of valuated matroids is a valuated flag matroid in the sense of Brandt-Eur-Zhang if and only if the associated sequence of T-matroids is a flag T-matroid. Since T is perfect and the covector set of a valuated matroid is a tropical linear space, flag T-matroids are exactly chains V*(M1) ⊂ ... ⊂ V*(Ms) of tropical linear spaces; equivalently they are points of the flag Dressian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a pair of valuated matroids represented by μ of rank r and ν of rank w, the Brandt-Eur-Zhang quotient condition says that for every tuple x1,...,x_{r-1}, y1,...,y_{w+1}, one of the products ν(y1,...,omit yi,...,y_{w+1}) μ(yi,x1,...,x_{r-1}) is at least as large as a chosen product, equivalently the maximum among all these products is attained at least twice after allowing reorderings. In T, a sum lies in the nullset exactly when its maximum term occurs at least twice, and ε=1. Thus the Brandt-Eur-Zhang quotient inequalities are precisely the Plücker flag relations for the associated T-matroids. The identification with chains of tropical linear spaces then follows from Theorem 2.17 because T is perfect, together with the standard fact recalled in the paper that tropical linear spaces are covector sets of valuated matroids.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.23, flags of minors of a single F-matroid
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a tract. Let E={1,...,n}, let p be a nonnegative integer, let E′=E ⊔ {n+1,...,n+p}, let M′ be an F-matroid on E′, and choose integers 0 ≤ n_s ≤ ... ≤ n_1 ≤ p. For i=1,...,s set J_i={n+1,...,n+n_i} and I_i={n+n_i+1,...,n+p}. Then the sequence M_i = M′ with I_i deleted and J_i contracted is a flag F-matroid on E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to prove the quotient relation for two indices b<a, i.e. M′ with I_b deleted and J_b contracted is a quotient of M′ with I_a deleted and J_a contracted, with the paper's convention that larger rank appears later in the flag. Choose a Grassmann-Plücker function ϕ for M′. The proof chooses bases Y, Y+, V, and Z in the relevant restrictions and contractions so that the two minor Grassmann-Plücker functions can both be written as evaluations of ϕ with fixed appended lists: one appends (Y,Z,V), the other appends (Y,Y+,V,Z). Given a desired Plücker flag relation for the two minors, extend the y-list and x-list by these appended elements to form an ordinary Plücker relation for ϕ. The extra summands in the ordinary Plücker relation vanish because the appended y-elements also appear among the appended x-elements, creating repeated arguments in the alternating Grassmann-Plücker function. The remaining summands are exactly the flag relation for the two minors, hence lie in N_F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.25, adjacent-rank flags over perfect tracts come from one-element minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a perfect tract, let E be a finite set, and let M=(M,N) be a flag F-matroid of rank (r,r+1) on E. Let Ē=E ⊔ {e}. Then there exists an F-matroid ĤM of rank r+1 on Ē such that M = (ĤM/e, ĤM minus e). More precisely, the set of F-matroids ĤM with this property is in bijection with F×.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose Grassmann-Plücker functions μ:E^r → F for M and ν:E^{r+1} → F for N. Define a function ϕ:Ē^{r+1} → F by ϕ(x1,...,x_{r+1})=ν(x1,...,x_{r+1}) if e does not occur, by ϕ(x1,...,x_{r+1})=ε^{r+1−ℓ} μ(x1,...,omit x_ℓ,...,x_{r+1}) if e occurs exactly once at position ℓ, and by 0 if e occurs at least twice. Nontriviality and alternation are immediate. Since F is perfect, the paper uses Baker-Bowler's reduced criterion for Grassmann-Plücker functions: it is enough to check the 3-term relations with |I−J|=3. There are four cases. If e is in neither list, the relation is the Plücker relation for ν. If e lies in both I and J, it is the Plücker relation for μ. If e is the unique element of J−I, the relation is a Plücker flag relation expressing N ։ M. If e lies in I−J, after relabeling it is again a Plücker flag relation for N ։ M. Thus ϕ represents an F-matroid ĤM with ϕ/e=μ and ϕ minus e=ν. For uniqueness, any Grassmann-Plücker function λ giving the same minors restricts to αμ and βν for some α,β in F×, and conversely each pair (α,β) gives such a λ by the same construction; quotienting by global scalar leaves the parameter β/α in F×.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.8 and Corollary 3.9, the flag variety represents flag matroid bundles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r=(r1,...,rs), let E be finite, and let Fl(r,E) be the ordered blue F±_1-scheme defined as the closed subscheme of the product of projective spaces with coordinates T_{i,I} by the alternating relations, zero-on-repeated-indices relations, and all Plücker flag relations 0 ≤ ∑ ε^k T_{i,(y_k,x1,...,x_{ri-1})} T_{j,(y1,...,omit y_k,...,y_{rj+1})}. For every ordered blue F±_1-scheme X, morphisms X → Fl(r,E) are functorially in bijection with flag matroid bundles of rank r on E over X. In particular, for every idyll F, the F-rational points Fl(r,E)(F) are canonically in bijection with flag F-matroids of rank r on E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The universal flag matroid bundle on Fl(r,E) is obtained by pulling back O(1) from each projective factor and using the homogeneous coordinates as universal Grassmann-Plücker functions. Given a flag matroid bundle over X, represented by line bundles L_i and generating sections ϕ_i:E^{ri} → Γ(X,L_i), the universal property of projective space over ordered blue schemes gives unique maps ψ_i:X → P^{N_i} and isomorphisms ψ_i^*O(1) ≅ L_i carrying the homogeneous coordinates to the sections ϕ_i. Taking the product gives ψ:X → product of projective spaces. The ordinary Grassmann-Plücker relations force ψ to factor through the product of Grassmannians, and the Plücker flag relations force it to factor through Fl(r,E). This constructs the inverse to pulling back the universal bundle. Checking that the two constructions are inverse is done coordinatewise: pullback of the universal coordinates recovers the original sections, and a morphism to Fl(r,E) is recovered from its coordinate sections. Functoriality follows from functoriality of pullback.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11, projection onto subflags
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let i=(i1,...,it) be a strictly increasing sequence with 1 ≤ i1 < ... < it ≤ s, and let r′=(r_{i1},...,r_{it}). The coordinate projection from the product of Grassmannians ∏_{a=1}^s Gr(r_a,E) to ∏_{k=1}^t Gr(r_{ik},E) restricts to a morphism π_i:Fl(r,E) → Fl(r′,E). If F is an idyll and M=(M1,...,Ms) is a flag F-matroid with characteristic morphism χ_M:Spec F → Fl(r,E), then π_i ∘ χ_M is the characteristic morphism of the subflag (M_{i1},...,M_{it}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A coordinate projection simply discards some Grassmann-Plücker functions from a flag. Since any subsequence of a flag still satisfies all Plücker flag relations among its remaining components, the projection lands in the smaller flag variety. The statement about characteristic morphisms follows directly from the construction in the proof of Theorem 3.8: the coordinates of χ_M are the Grassmann-Plücker functions of the individual M_i, so retaining only the selected coordinate blocks gives the characteristic morphism of the selected subflag.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.12, geometric duality of flag varieties
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E have n elements, let r=(r1,...,rs), and let r*=(n−rs,...,n−r1). The standard Grassmannian duality isomorphisms δ_{r_i,E}:Gr(r_i,E) → Gr(n−r_i,E), defined on homogeneous coordinates by δ_{r,E}^*(T_{x1,...,x_{n-r}})=sign(x1,...,x_{n-r},x′1,...,x′r) T_{x′1,...,x′r} whenever the two lists partition E, combine to an isomorphism δ_{r,E}:Fl(r,E) → Fl(r*,E). If F is an idyll with involution τ, τ* is the induced automorphism of Spec F, and M is a flag F-matroid with characteristic morphism χ_M:Spec F → Fl(r,E), then the characteristic morphism of the dual flag M* is δ_{r,E} ∘ χ_M ∘ τ*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The product of the Grassmannian duality maps gives an isomorphism between the ambient products of Grassmannians. To see that it restricts to flag varieties, apply the coordinate pullback to a Plücker flag relation for indices i<j. After replacing each tuple by its complementary tuple in E, and tracking the standard signs from the Grassmannian duality formula, the pulled-back relation becomes, up to one common sign, precisely a Plücker flag relation for the reversed complementary rank sequence r*. Symmetry gives the inverse restriction. For the characteristic morphism formula, write the flag M by Grassmann-Plücker functions ϕ_i whose values are the pullbacks of universal coordinates along χ_M. The dual Grassmann-Plücker functions are obtained by applying the involution τ to complementary coordinates and multiplying by the same signs used in δ. Therefore their coordinate pullbacks are exactly those of δ_{r,E} ∘ χ_M ∘ τ*, and the moduli interpretation gives equality of characteristic morphisms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.13 and Remark 3.14, geometric minors of flag varieties and compatibility with duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let e ∈ E, put E′=E−{e}, and let r=(r1,...,rs). For contraction, the paper defines locally closed subschemes W_{r,k,/e} of Fl(r,E), for k=0,...,s, according to whether e is nonloop in the first k components and a loop in the remaining components, and morphisms Ψ_{r,k,/e}:W_{r,k,/e} → Fl(r^k,E′), where r^k=(r1−1,...,rk−1,r_{k+1},...,rs). Analogous locally closed subschemes W_{r,k,minus e} and morphisms Ψ_{r,k,minus e} encode deletion. For every idyll F, the disjoint unions of F-points ⨿_k W_{r,k,/e}(F) → Fl(r,E)(F) and ⨿_k W_{r,k,minus e}(F) → Fl(r,E)(F) are bijective. If M is a flag F-matroid with characteristic morphism χ_M and k is the unique stratum containing χ_M, then Ψ_{r,k,/e} ∘ χ_M is the characteristic morphism of M/e, and Ψ_{r,k,minus e} ∘ χ_M is the characteristic morphism of M minus e. Remark 3.14 adds that duality identifies the contraction stratum W_{r,k,/e} with the corresponding deletion stratum for r* and gives a commutative square expressing (M/e)*=(M*) minus e and (M minus e)*=M*/e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction starts from the corresponding open and closed loci in each Grassmannian Gr(r_i,E): for contraction, whether e appears in some basis determines whether contraction lowers rank or leaves rank unchanged, and these alternatives are represented by complementary open and closed subschemes. Intersecting the product of the appropriate choices with Fl(r,E) gives W_{r,k,/e}. For an F-point corresponding to M=(M1,...,Ms), χ_M factors through W_{r,k,/e} exactly when e is nonloop for M1,...,Mk and loop for M_{k+1},...,Ms. The quotient property plus a cited matroid fact implies that if e is a loop in an earlier component, it is a loop in every later component, so exactly one k occurs; this proves the bijection on F-points. The morphism Ψ is induced by the Grassmannian minor morphisms of Baker-Lorscheid. Composing with each coordinate projection to a Grassmannian gives the characteristic morphism of M_i/e by the Grassmannian minor theorem, so the whole map gives the flag minor M/e. Deletion is analogous or follows by duality. The compatibility in Remark 3.14 is inherited coordinatewise from the Grassmannian compatibility of duality with deletion and contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.15, the morphism from a Grassmannian to a flag variety sending one matroid to a flag of minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r=(r1,...,rs), let E={1,...,n}, let n′=n+r_s−r_1, and let E′={1,...,n′}. There is an open locus W_{r,E} in Gr(r_s,E′), consisting of matroids for which the auxiliary set {n+1,...,n′} is both independent and co-independent, and a canonical morphism μ_{r,E}:W_{r,E} → Fl(r,E). In homogeneous coordinates, μ_{r,E} sends the coordinate T_{e1,...,e_{ri}} of the i-th flag component to T_{e1,...,e_{ri},n+1,...,n+r_s−r_i} on Gr(r_s,E′). If F is a tract and M′ is an F-matroid of rank r_s on E′ whose characteristic morphism lands in W_{r,E}, then μ_{r,E} sends M′ to the flag F-matroid whose i-th component is the corresponding deletion-contraction minor of M′ on E, equivalently the flag of minors encoded by the displayed coordinate formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives this result without a detailed proof. The coordinate formula is the geometric version of Theorem 2.23 with p=r_s−r_1 and with the auxiliary elements split so that the i-th component is obtained from M′ by contracting the first r_s−r_i auxiliary elements and deleting the remaining auxiliary elements. The open condition that the auxiliary set is independent and co-independent ensures that these minors have the desired ranks and that the relevant Plücker coordinates are not all zero. Theorem 2.23 supplies the flag relations, so the rational coordinate rule lands in Fl(r,E) on the stated domain. Evaluating the coordinate pullbacks on the characteristic morphism of M′ gives exactly the Grassmann-Plücker functions of the stated minors, hence the characteristic morphism of the resulting flag.

proof_techniques.name: Detect quotient relations through cocircuit-covector containment
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the Plücker flag equations defining a quotient N ։ M by the condition C*(M) ⊆ V*(N). The proof shows how to convert an inner product of a circuit of N with a cocircuit of M into a Plücker flag relation by choosing fundamental circuit and cocircuit representatives and matching their common support; the converse builds such a circuit and cocircuit from a potentially nonzero term in a Plücker flag relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset of tropical linear subspaces ordered by inclusion, this gives a way to translate inclusions into quotient data of valuated matroids, because over T covector sets are tropical linear spaces. It suggests proving order-reversal properties by manipulating circuits, cocircuits, and quotients rather than directly manipulating all points of a tropical linear space.

proof_techniques.name: Use perfectness to identify flags with chains of covector sets
proof_techniques.description: Over a perfect tract F, adjacent quotient relations Mi+1 ։ Mi are equivalent to the covector-set chain V*(Mi) ⊆ V*(Mi+1). The key extra input is the perfectness identity V(M) ⊥ V*(M), which lets one take orthogonal complements in the quotient relation after dualizing.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The tropical hyperfield T is perfect, so any chain of tropical linear spaces can be treated as a flag T-matroid. This is the main bridge between the relative Dressian as a collection of contained tropical linear spaces and the algebraic flag-matroid formalism.

proof_techniques.name: Dualize flags by reversing both order and ranks
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that N ։ M is equivalent to M* ։ N*, and hence that a flag (M1,...,Ms) dualizes to (M_s*,...,M_1*). This is not merely a set-theoretic operation: it is also realized by an isomorphism δ_{r,E}:Fl(r,E) → Fl(r*,E) on the representing flag varieties, with an explicit complementary-coordinate formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on a Dressian-like poset is likely to come from duality, possibly composed with a ground-set symmetry or a self-duality of the ambient matroid. The paper supplies the exact quotient-reversal theorem needed to check that such a construction reverses inclusion.

proof_techniques.name: Represent tropical flags as points of a moduli space
proof_techniques.description: Flag matroid bundles are represented by the ordered-blue flag variety Fl(r,E), and F-rational points are flag F-matroids. For F=T this recovers the flag Dressian as the space of flags of tropical linear spaces, with coordinates satisfying tropical Plücker and Plücker flag relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: One can try to realize a relative Dressian as a sublocus or fiber of an absolute flag Dressian or flag variety. Then the question of whether an order-reversing involution exists becomes the question of whether the geometric duality morphism δ preserves that sublocus.

proof_techniques.name: Control flat-like constructions using minors
proof_techniques.description: Deletion and contraction preserve quotient relations and therefore preserve flag F-matroids. The paper also realizes these operations by locally closed strata and morphisms on flag varieties, and duality interchanges deletion and contraction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of flats into the relative Dressian in the original problem is expressed using contractions and loops. The paper's functorial minor and duality formalism is directly suited to checking how such flat-associated tropical linear spaces transform under candidate involutions.
