proof_techniques.name: Build flags from a single ambient matroid via deletion-contraction sequences
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.23 shows that nested deletion-contraction minors of one F-matroid always form a flag F-matroid. Geometrically, Theorem 3.15 packages this as a morphism from an open locus in a Grassmannian to a flag variety, with an explicit coordinate formula obtained by appending auxiliary elements to Plücker coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If elements of a relative Dressian can be modeled as minors or quotients of a fixed ambient object, this construction gives a systematic way to produce the required flags and to compare them under duality. It may be useful for understanding the embedded lattice of flats inside the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce tropical Plücker flag relations to max-attained-twice checks
proof_techniques.description: In the tropical hyperfield T, the nullset condition for a formal sum is exactly that its maximum term occurs at least twice. The paper uses this to identify Brandt-Eur-Zhang valuated flag inequalities with tract-theoretic Plücker flag relations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For explicit examples or counterexamples in a relative Dressian, this gives a concrete numerical criterion for whether a proposed pair of tropical linear spaces forms a quotient or flag. It can be used to test whether a candidate involution preserves the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Use characteristic morphisms to prove compatibility statements coordinatewise
proof_techniques.description: The paper often proves that an operation on flag matroids matches a morphism of flag varieties by checking after projection to each Grassmannian factor. Because the universal bundle records the Grassmann-Plücker coordinates, equality of characteristic morphisms follows from equality of the pulled-back coordinate functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate order-reversing map on a relative Dressian is defined coordinatewise on valuated matroids, this technique suggests verifying it by comparing Plücker coordinates and checking compatibility with the defining relative constraints.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not define the relative Dressian Dr(M); its results concern absolute flag Dressians and flag varieties, so a solver must impose separately the condition that a tropical linear space lies inside a fixed Trop(M). Section 3.11.2 states that Fl(r,E)(T) is canonically homeomorphic to the flag Dressian of Brandt-Eur-Zhang and that its points correspond to rank-r flags of tropical linear spaces in T^E. The tropical hyperfield T is explicitly listed as perfect, so the strongest covector-chain characterization applies in the tropical setting. Proposition 2.8 relates the tract quotient notion to ordinary matroid quotients and strong maps, which is relevant for comparing the tropical theory with the lattice of flats. Remark 3.14 records that the geometric minor maps commute with duality by swapping deletion and contraction, a likely useful identity for flat-associated subspaces. The paper cites Brandt-Eur-Zhang, Tropical flag varieties, as the source for flag Dressians and flags of tropical linear spaces; Baker-Bowler for matroids over tracts; Anderson for vectors and covectors of matroids over tracts; and Baker-Lorscheid for the moduli space of matroids.
why_relevant: Extends flag matroids to tract/hyperfield coefficients and treats duality and minors. It gives an algebraic framework in which flags of tropical linear spaces, quotients, and duality can be studied uniformly.

title: Self-dual matroids from canonical curves
overall_summary: The paper studies self-dual configurations of 2n points in P^{n−1}, the self-dual matroids they define, and their valuated and tropical analogues. Its central constructions are the classical Hodge star on Gr(n,2n), self-duality modulo the torus action by column scalings, and the tropical Hodge star on valuated matroids, where self-duality means being fixed modulo the tropical lineality space. The paper proves structural results such as a birational parametrization of the self-dual configuration space by SO(n), toric-binomial equations for self-dual realization spaces of self-dual matroids, and a cell-duality property for self-dual valuated matroids. The tropical section is most relevant to the original problem: it shows how a duality involution acts on valuated matroids and matroid subdivisions, identifies the full self-dual Dressian/Grassmannian in the first nontrivial case n=3, and shows in rank 4 that the self-dual Dressian can be strictly larger than the realizable tropical fixed locus. The paper does not address relative Dressians directly, but it provides concrete models and obstructions for extending combinatorial dualities to valuated or tropical data.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2, Hodge star construction on Gr(n,2n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a field and let Gr(n,2n) be the Grassmannian of n-dimensional subspaces of K^{2n}, with Plücker coordinates p_I indexed by n-element subsets I of [2n]={1,...,2n}. If I is an ordered n-subset and I^c is its ordered complement, the Hodge star p ↦ p* is given by p*_{I^c}=sign(I,I^c) p_I, where sign(I,I^c) is the sign of the permutation sending (1,...,2n) to the concatenation (I,I^c). The torus (K*)^{2n} acts by column scaling, namely p_I ↦ (∏_{i∈I} λ_i) p_I. A point p in Gr(n,2n) is called self-dual if p*=λ p for some λ in (K*)^{2n}. The self-dual Grassmannian SGr(n,2n) is the locus of self-dual points, SGr(n,2n)^o is its intersection with the open Grassmannian where all Plücker coordinates are nonzero, and S(n)=SGr(n,2n)^o/(K*)^{2n} is the self-dual configuration space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Hodge star formula is the standard coordinate expression for passing from an n-plane in K^{2n} to its orthogonal complement, written in Plücker coordinates. The torus action is induced by scaling columns of an n by 2n matrix representing the point of the Grassmannian. Thus p*=λp says that the orthogonal complement has the same Plücker vector as the original subspace after rescaling the 2n coordinate directions. In the case n=3, the paper demonstrates how this condition can be converted into equations: write λ_i λ_j λ_k=p*_{ijk}/p_{ijk}, substitute these quantities into toric relations among the squarefree monomials λ_i λ_j λ_k, and clear denominators. This gives, for six points in P^2, the quartic binomial p_{123}p_{145}p_{356}p_{246}−p_{124}p_{135}p_{456}p_{236}=0, the usual condition that the six points lie on a conic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4 and Corollary 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume K is algebraically closed. Let S(n)=SGr(n,2n)^o/(K*)^{2n} be the moduli space of self-dual configurations of 2n points in general linear position in P^{n−1}, modulo projective transformations. Then S(n) has a birational parametrization by the rotation group SO(n). In particular S(n) is rational of dimension C(n,2). Consequently the self-dual Grassmannian SGr(n,2n) is rational of dimension C(n,2)+2n−1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent a point p in SGr(n,2n)^o by an n by 2n matrix X=(X_1 | X_2), where X_1 and X_2 are invertible n by n blocks. The self-duality condition XΛX^T=0 can be written as X_1Λ_1X_1^T=X_2Λ_2X_2^T for invertible diagonal matrices Λ_1 and Λ_2. Since K is algebraically closed, choose square roots of the diagonal entries. Rearranging gives Λ_1^{−1/2}(X_1^{−1}X_2)Λ_2^{1/2} · Λ_2^{1/2}(X_1^{−1}X_2)^TΛ_1^{−1/2}=Id_n, so R=Λ_1^{−1/2}(X_1^{−1}X_2)Λ_2^{1/2} is, up to sign, in SO(n). The row span of X is the same as the row span of (Id_n | R) after a diagonal column scaling, so the quotient point in S(n) is represented by R. Conversely, any R in the open subset of SO(n) for which all maximal minors of (Id_n | R) are nonzero gives a point of S(n). Since SO(n) is rational of dimension C(n,2), this proves the birational parametrization and the dimension of S(n). Adding back the torus orbit of dimension 2n−1 gives the dimension of SGr(n,2n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a self-dual matroid of rank n on [2n], meaning that an n-subset I is a basis of M if and only if its complement I^c is a basis. Let R(M)=Gr(M)/(K*)^{2n} be the realization space of M, where Gr(M) is the matroid stratum in Gr(n,2n), and let S(M)⊆R(M) be the locus of self-dual realizations. Let I_M be the toric ideal of the matroid polytope of M, with one variable y_I for each basis I of M. Fix quadratic binomials that generate I_M in the Laurent polynomial ring. For each basis I, replace y_I by p*_I/p_I, where p_I is the Plücker coordinate and p*_I is the Hodge-star Plücker coordinate. After clearing denominators, one obtains quartic binomials in the Plücker coordinates indexed by bases of M. These quartics define S(M) as a subvariety of R(M), with the proof giving the set-theoretic elimination of the diagonal scaling variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The self-duality condition p*=λp means that for every basis I={i_1,...,i_n}, the product λ_{i_1}...λ_{i_n} equals p*_I/p_I. The products λ_I=∏_{i∈I}λ_i are not independent; their algebraic relations are exactly the toric relations defining the matroid polytope, i.e. the toric ideal I_M. Substituting λ_I=p*_I/p_I into generators of I_M eliminates the unknown diagonal scaling λ. Clearing denominators yields quartic binomials in the Plücker coordinates. A point of R(M) satisfies these quartics exactly when the ratios p*_I/p_I are compatible with some diagonal λ, which is precisely the condition for the realization to lie in S(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a self-dual matroid of rank n on [2n], and let P_M be its matroid polytope, the convex hull of the basis indicator vectors e_I in R^{2n}. The following four conditions are equivalent: (1) M is connected; (2) P_M has maximal possible dimension 2n−1 inside the affine hyperplane ∑_{i=1}^{2n} x_i=n; (3) the point (1/2)∑_{i=1}^{2n} e_i lies in the relative interior of P_M; (4) every proper subset A⊊[2n] satisfies rank_M(A)>|A|/2. A self-dual matroid satisfying these equivalent conditions is called stable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For arbitrary matroids, connectedness is equivalent to the matroid polytope having dimension |E|−1; here |E|=2n, giving equivalence of (1) and (2). Condition (3) plainly implies full dimension. Conversely, if P_M is full-dimensional, then because M is self-dual the vertex set of P_M is invariant under complementing bases, so the average of all vertices is exactly (1/2)∑ e_i; the average of the vertices of a full-dimensional polytope lies in its relative interior. Finally, use the standard inequality description P_M={x∈R^{2n}: ∑ x_i=n and ∑_{i∈A}x_i≤rank_M(A) for all A⊆[2n]}. At the point (1/2)∑ e_i, the left side for A is |A|/2. This point is in the relative interior precisely when all proper defining inequalities are strict, which is equivalent to rank_M(A)>|A|/2 for every proper A, and it is enough to check flats.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Up to isomorphism, there are 1042 simple self-dual matroids M of rank 5 on a 10-element ground set. Of these, 346 are not realizable over C. For the remaining 696 realizable matroids, the dimensions of the complex realization spaces R(M) have distribution 0^16, 1^113, 2^220, 3^175, 4^83, 5^27, 6^34, 7^4, 8^13, 9^1, 10^6, 12^2, 14^1, 16^1, where the exponent denotes the number of matroids with that realization-space dimension. At least three rank-5 self-dual matroids are realizable, i.e. have R(M) nonempty, but have empty self-dual realization space S(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The authors construct the database computationally. Starting from the complete list of rank-5 matroids on 9 elements in polyDB, they close each set of bases under complements in [10], test whether the result is a matroid, and keep one representative from each isomorphism class. This yields 1042 self-dual matroids. Realization spaces are computed by choosing a basis, writing a 5 by 10 normal-form matrix X_M=(Id_5 | x_{ij}), imposing vanishing of minors indexed by nonbases, normalizing selected nonzero entries, and saturating by all basis minors. Gröbner basis computations in Magma determine whether the resulting very affine realization space is empty and compute its dimension in all but a few cases; a random-linear-section heuristic is used for the remaining difficult cases. To compute S(M), the equations XΛX^T=0 for a diagonal matrix Λ=diag(λ_1,...,λ_10) are added, the ideal is saturated by λ_1...λ_10, and the λ variables are eliminated. This detects examples for which realizations exist but no self-dual realization exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists an explicit rank-5 self-dual matroid M on [10] with R(M) nonempty and S(M)=empty. The paper specifies M by listing the nonbases not containing 10 as 12347, 12369, 12468, 12789, 13568, 14569, 23459, 23567, and 36789, with the remaining nonbases recovered by self-duality. Its realization space contains configurations represented in normal form by the 5 by 10 matrix X=[[1,0,0,0,0,1,1,1,0,1],[0,1,0,0,0,1,a,b,1,1],[0,0,1,0,0,1,c,d,1,0],[0,0,0,1,0,1,1,b,e,f],[0,0,0,0,1,1,0,d,e,g]], where all basis minors are nonzero and the nonbasis equations are ce+g=0, ae−e+f−1=0, ad−bc+c−d=0, bg+de−dg−d=0, and be+bf+de−dg−2b−d−e+g+1=0. No such realization admits a diagonal Λ=diag(λ_1,...,λ_10) with all λ_i nonzero and XΛX^T=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The displayed normal form and equations show that the realization space is a nonempty very affine threefold. To test self-duality, introduce variables λ_1,...,λ_10 and form the ideal generated by the entries of the symmetric 5 by 5 matrix XΛX^T in the polynomial ring in a,b,c,d,e,f,g and the λ_i. A direct computation shows that the monomial λ_2λ_5 lies in this ideal. Since a self-dual realization requires all diagonal entries λ_i to be nonzero, the containment of λ_2λ_5 in the ideal forces a contradiction after saturating by λ_1...λ_10. Therefore S(M)=empty even though R(M) is nonempty.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2 and Theorem 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a trivalent 3-connected graph with 2g−2 vertices and 3g−3 edges. Let Cyc_G be any g by (3g−3) matrix whose rows span the cycle space of G. The columns of Cyc_G realize the bond matroid of G in P^{g−1}; for each vertex of G, the three incident edges give a dependent triple of columns, and these triples span 2g−2 lines whose union is a graph curve C_G in P^{g−1}. The curve C_G is unique up to projective transformations. If H≅P^{g−2} is a generic hyperplane and X_G=H∩C_G is the resulting configuration of 2g−2 points, one on each line, then the rank g−1 matroid M_G defined by X_G is self-dual and depends only on the graph G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The matrix Cyc_G realizes the graphic matroid of G, and its dual realizes the bond matroid. Since graphic and bond matroids are regular, their realizations are projectively unique. Because G is trivalent and 3-connected, the three edges incident to each vertex form a 3-cycle in the bond matroid, hence the corresponding three columns span a line; these lines meet according to the graph G and form C_G. This proves uniqueness of C_G up to projective transformations. For a generic hyperplane H, the matroid of the point configuration H∩C_G is independent of H and depends only on C_G, hence only on G. The self-duality of M_G is identified with Riemann-Roch for the stable canonical curve C_G: for a divisor D consisting of g−1 points of the canonical divisor X_G, D lies in a hyperplane if and only if the complementary divisor X_G−D lies in a hyperplane. This is exactly the condition that bases of M_G are closed under complement.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6, tropical Hodge star and self-dual valuated matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Work over the Puiseux series field K=C{{t}} with its t-adic valuation. The tropical Grassmannian trop(Gr(n,2n)) lies in R^{C(2n,n)}/R1 and has lineality space L={ (∑_{i∈I} μ_i)_I : μ_1,...,μ_{2n}∈R }. The Dressian Dr(n,2n) is the tropical prevariety of Plücker relations and parametrizes valuated matroids of rank n on [2n]. For q=(q_I) in R^{C(2n,n)}, define the tropical Hodge star by q*_I=q_{I^c}. A vector q is self-dual if there exist μ_1,...,μ_{2n} in R such that q_I+∑_{i∈I} μ_i=q_{I^c} for every n-subset I of [2n]; equivalently q−q* lies in L. The corresponding self-dual linear subspace L_sd(n,2n) has codimension 2n−1 and is spanned by L together with the vectors e_I+e_{I^c}. The paper defines trop(Gr(n,2n))^sd, Dr(n,2n)^sd, and trop(R(n,2n))^sd by intersecting trop(Gr(n,2n)), Dr(n,2n), and the tropical configuration space with this self-dual linear condition. It also notes the inclusions trop(SGr(n,2n))⊆trop(Gr(n,2n))^sd and trop(S(n))⊆trop(R(n,2n))^sd.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The classical equation p*=λp tropicalizes as q*_I=q_I+∑_{i∈I} μ_i, because signs disappear under valuation and column scaling tropicalizes to addition by a vector in the lineality space L. Therefore self-duality is exactly fixedness under q↦q* after quotienting by L. Eliminating the μ_i gives a linear subspace L_sd; the paper describes it explicitly as the span of L and the symmetric complement-pair vectors e_I+e_{I^c}. The inclusions involving trop(SGr) and trop(S(n)) follow from the general fact that tropicalizing a subvariety cut out by equations lies inside the tropical prevariety of those equations; here the self-duality equations define the relevant fixed locus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let q be a self-dual valuated matroid in Dr(n,2n)^sd. Let M(q) be the set of rank-n matroids whose matroid polytopes occur as cells in the regular matroid subdivision of the hypersimplex Δ(n,2n) induced by q. Then M(q) is closed under matroid duality: if M∈M(q), then the dual matroid M* also lies in M(q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a cell corresponding to M in the regular subdivision induced by q. By adding a vector from the lineality space L to q, which does not change the subdivision, one may assume q is nonnegative and q_I=0 exactly for bases I of M. The tropical Hodge star q* is then nonnegative and has q*_J=0 exactly when J is a basis of M*, because J is a basis of M* precisely when J^c is a basis of M. Since q is self-dual, q and q* differ by a lineality vector and therefore induce the same regular subdivision of the hypersimplex. Thus the cell for M* appears in the same subdivision, proving closure under duality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The tropical self-dual Grassmannian trop(SGr(n,2n)) is a balanced fan of pure dimension C(n,2)+2n−1 in R^{C(2n,n)}/R1. Its lineality space has dimension 2n−1. The quotient by this lineality space is trop(S(n)), a pointed fan of pure dimension C(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The classical self-dual Grassmannian SGr(n,2n) is rational of dimension C(n,2)+2n−1 by Corollary 2.5. The general theorem that tropicalizations of irreducible very affine varieties are balanced fans of pure dimension equal to the dimension of the variety gives the dimension and balancing statement. The torus action by column scalings contributes the lineality space of dimension 2n−1 after projectivization, and quotienting by it gives the tropicalization of the self-dual configuration space S(n), whose dimension is C(n,2) by Theorem 2.4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n=3, the tropical self-dual Grassmannian, the self-dual part of the tropical Grassmannian, and the self-dual part of the Dressian all coincide: trop(SGr(3,6))=trop(Gr(3,6))^sd=Dr(3,6)^sd as balanced fans of pure dimension 8 in R^{20}/R1. This fan is linearly isomorphic to the tropical Grassmannian trop(Gr(2,6)), the tree space of phylogenetic trees with six leaves. The isomorphism is the tropical Pachter-Speyer map sending a vector (q_{ij}) indexed by pairs to the vector (q_{ij}+q_{ik}+q_{jk}) indexed by triples ijk. After quotienting by lineality spaces, one obtains trop(R(2,6))=trop(S(3))=trop(R(3,6))^sd, a 3-dimensional pointed fan over a 2-dimensional simplicial complex with f-vector (25,105,105).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality Dr(3,6)=trop(Gr(3,6)) is known and is cited from the standard tropical Grassmannian theory. The self-dual Grassmannian SGr(3,6) is cut out inside Gr(3,6) by the 35 Plücker quadrics together with the quartic self-duality binomial for six points on a conic. Using Singular, the authors compute the tropical variety of these equations and find an 8-dimensional fan with 5-dimensional lineality space and quotient f-vector (25,105,105). Independently, using polymake, they intersect the Dressian Dr(3,6) with the self-dual linear space L_sd(3,6) and compare rays and cones up to S_6 symmetry, obtaining the same fan. The Pachter-Speyer monomial map Gr(2,6)⇢Gr(3,6), p↦(p_{ij}p_{ik}p_{jk})_{ijk}, parametrizes six points on a conic and becomes the stated linear map after tropicalization; by tropical functoriality for monomial maps, it identifies trop(Gr(2,6)) with the common self-dual fan. Modding out the respective lineality spaces gives the pointed 3-dimensional fan.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the rank-4 case, the paper states that the tropical self-dual Grassmannian trop(γ(Gr(4,7)))=trop(SGr(4,8)) is a balanced fan of pure dimension 16 in R^{70}/R1 with 7-dimensional lineality space, where γ is the Cayley-octad rational map discussed earlier in the paper. This fan is contained in trop(Gr(4,8))^sd. Moreover, the containment trop(Gr(4,8))^sd⊊Dr(4,8)^sd is strict.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The balanced fan assertion is attributed to the Fundamental Theorem of Tropical Geometry applied to the image defining SGr(4,8). The containment trop(SGr(4,8))⊆trop(Gr(4,8))^sd follows because SGr(4,8) is contained in the self-dual locus of Gr(4,8), and tropicalization lies in the corresponding tropical prevariety. To prove strictness of trop(Gr(4,8))^sd⊊Dr(4,8)^sd, the authors use the self-dual rank-4 matroid labeled 4.14.a in Table 1, which is not realizable over C. Define q_I=0 if I is a basis of this matroid and q_I=1 otherwise. This q is a self-dual valuated matroid, hence lies in Dr(4,8)^sd, and its matroid subdivision has a cell equal to the nonrealizable matroid 4.14.a. If q lay in trop(Gr(4,8)), then every matroid appearing as a cell in its subdivision would be realizable over C, by the cited Dressian realizability criterion. This contradiction shows q is not in trop(Gr(4,8))^sd, proving strictness.

proof_techniques.name: Hodge star modulo torus scaling
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats self-duality not as literal equality p=p* in Plücker coordinates, but as equality after the natural torus action coming from rescaling the 2n columns of a representing matrix. This is crucial because point configurations are considered modulo projective transformations and independent scalings of labeled points. In coordinates, the classical Hodge star sends a Plücker coordinate indexed by I to the complementary coordinate indexed by I^c, up to a sign, and the torus scaling contributes the multiplicative factor ∏_{i∈I}λ_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an attempted involution on a Dressian-like parameter space, this suggests looking for duality only after quotienting by the natural lineality or rescaling action, not on raw Plücker or valuated coordinates. It is a concrete model for how an order-reversing flat-lattice involution might lift to a map on tropical linear data.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical Hodge star as fixedness modulo lineality
proof_techniques.description: In the tropical setting, signs disappear and torus scaling becomes addition of a lineality vector. The dual of a valuated matroid q is q*_I=q_{I^c}; q is self-dual precisely when q−q* lies in the lineality space L={ (∑_{i∈I} μ_i)_I }. Thus self-dual valuated matroids form the intersection of the Dressian or tropical Grassmannian with an explicitly described linear subspace L_sd.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a precise template for defining a candidate map on tropical linear spaces: send the valuated object to its dual/complementary valuated object and then quotient by lineality. Any extension of a flat involution on a relative Dressian would likely need an analogue of this fixedness or dualization condition.

proof_techniques.name: Eliminating diagonal scalings via the matroid-polytope toric ideal
proof_techniques.description: For a self-dual matroid M, the unknown scalars λ_i in p*=λp are eliminated by using the toric ideal of the matroid polytope P_M. The variables in this toric ideal correspond to basis monomials ∏_{i∈I}λ_i. Substituting p*_I/p_I for these variables translates compatibility of all basis products into explicit quartic binomials in Plücker coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed involution on a relative Dressian must be compatible with basis-indexed valuated coordinates, toric relations among basis monomials give a systematic way to identify the necessary compatibility constraints. Tropically, the same construction becomes a linear or piecewise-linear consistency test.

proof_techniques.name: Cellwise duality in matroid subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Proposition 6.2 uses a standard maneuver for valuated matroids: add a lineality vector so that a chosen cell is the zero set of a nonnegative valuation. Applying the tropical Hodge star changes that zero set to the bases of the dual matroid. If the valuation is self-dual modulo lineality, the original and dual valuations induce the same regular subdivision, so the subdivision is closed under matroid duality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressian elements are often studied through subdivisions or matroidal decompositions of a matroid polytope. This argument may be portable: an involution on the underlying combinatorial data can sometimes be proved to act on the set of cells of every subdivision, even if individual cells are not fixed.

proof_techniques.name: Using nonrealizable cells to separate Dressian from tropical Grassmannian
proof_techniques.description: To prove that Dr(4,8)^sd strictly contains trop(Gr(4,8))^sd, the paper constructs a self-dual 0/1 valuated matroid whose subdivision contains a known nonrealizable self-dual matroid as a cell. A theorem cited by the paper says that if a valuated matroid lies in the tropical Grassmannian over C, then all matroids appearing in its subdivision are realizable over C. This gives a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful obstruction strategy: if a proposed involution or extension is first constructed in a realizable or geometric subspace, nonrealizable cells may show that the construction cannot automatically extend to the full Dressian without a purely combinatorial definition.

proof_techniques.name: Low-dimensional fixed-locus identification via a monomial parametrization
proof_techniques.description: For n=3, the paper identifies the self-dual tropical Grassmannian/Dressian with tree space trop(Gr(2,6)) by tropicalizing the Pachter-Speyer monomial map p_{ij} ↦ p_{ij}p_{ik}p_{jk}. The monomial map becomes a linear map on tropical coordinates, making it possible to compare fan structures, rays, and cones explicitly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Small uniform or rank-3 cases of a relative Dressian may admit similar monomial or tree-space models. Such models can provide test cases for whether a desired inclusion-reversing involution exists and how it behaves on strata.

proof_techniques.name: Riemann-Roch as a source of self-dual matroids
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that a canonical divisor X of degree 2g−2 on a genus-g curve yields a self-dual configuration: for a divisor D of degree g−1 supported on X, Riemann-Roch gives ℓ(D)=ℓ(X−D), so D satisfies a hyperplane dependence exactly when the complementary divisor does. For graph curves, this produces self-dual matroids determined purely by the trivalent 3-connected graph.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Geometric self-duality from Riemann-Roch may supply structured families of matroids with duality or polarity on flats. These families can be used as examples for testing candidate involutions on relative Dressians.

proof_techniques.name: Computational realization and self-dual realization tests by saturation
proof_techniques.description: The paper computes realization spaces by choosing a matrix normal form, imposing vanishing of nonbasis minors, normalizing by projective scalings, and saturating by the product of basis minors. It computes self-dual realization spaces by adding the matrix equations XΛX^T=0 for an invertible diagonal Λ, saturating by the product of the λ_i, and eliminating λ. This detects examples where combinatorial self-duality does not lift to self-dual realizability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Analogous saturation or elimination computations could be used to search for counterexamples or supporting examples in small relative Dressians, especially where one wants to know whether a combinatorial flat duality can be realized by valuated or tropical data.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Kapranov, Chow quotients of Grassmannians, for the fact that Gale duality induces a morphism of Chow quotients, and Keel-Tevelev for interpreting this Chow quotient as a tropical compactification of the point-configuration space. The paper works with the ordinary Dressian Dr(n,2n) of the uniform matroid, not with relative Dressians of an arbitrary matroid M. It explicitly notes that for n≥4 it is not known whether the inclusions trop(SGr(n,2n))⊆trop(Gr(n,2n))^sd and trop(S(n))⊆trop(R(n,2n))^sd are equalities. Example 6.3 shows that a self-dual valuated matroid can have a subdivision whose maximal cells are exchanged by duality rather than fixed individually: for q=(1,0,0,0,0,1) in Dr(2,4)^sd, the two maximal cells are dual to each other. The dimension stated in Proposition 6.8 for trop(SGr(4,8)) is 16 as printed in the paper, while Corollary 6.4 gives the general formula C(n,2)+2n−1 for trop(SGr(n,2n)); this should be checked against the original context if the precise dimension is needed.
why_relevant: Studies self-dual matroids and self-dual valuated matroids, including the tropical Hodge star map. It is relevant for understanding when matroid self-duality or polarity lifts to valuated/tropical data.

title: Adjoints of Matroids
overall_summary: The paper studies adjoints of finite matroids, where an adjoint of M is a matroid adM whose flat lattice contains an order-reversed copy of the flat lattice of M, sending hyperplanes of M to points of adM. Its most relevant results are that adjoints preserve and reflect connectivity, that modular matroids have a unique adjoint up to isomorphism and this adjoint is exactly the matroid determined by the opposite lattice L(M)^op, and that adjoint sequences either terminate, cycle through finite projective geometries, or converge to infinite projective geometries. The core techniques are to relabel an adjoint by the hyperplanes of M, encode elements of M as hyperplanes H[e] of the adjoint, compare adjoints with the extension lattice of M via linear subclasses of hyperplanes, and use modular-geometric lattice intersection properties. For the original problem, the key takeaway is that an order-reversing self-duality of the flat lattice is an adjoint-like polarity phenomenon; in the simple matroid setting, the paper’s results make such phenomena highly rigid and closely tied to modular/projective-geometric structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.2 and formula (2.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite matroid with flat lattice L(M), hyperplane set H(M), and rank r(M). A matroid adM is called an adjoint of M if r(adM)=r(M) and there is an injective order-reversing map φ:L(M)→L(adM) that sends the coatoms of L(M), equivalently the hyperplanes of M, bijectively onto the atoms of L(adM), equivalently the elements of the simple matroid adM. For every flat X of M, the adjoint map satisfies φ(X)=∨{φ(H): H∈H(M), X⊆H}=∪{φ(H): H∈H(M), X⊆H}, where the join and union are taken in L(adM).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the paper’s defining setup for adjoints. The displayed formula is recorded as a basic consequence of the definition: the flat X is below exactly the hyperplanes H containing X, and under the order-reversing embedding those hyperplanes become atoms of adM whose join is the flat φ(X). The equality with union uses the fact that in a simple matroid, the join of a set of atoms is the closure of their union, and in the adjoint-map image this is denoted by the union of those atoms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid, let adM be an adjoint of M, and let φ:L(M)→L(adM) be an adjoint map. For flats X,Y∈L(M): (i) if X covers Y in L(M), then φ(Y) covers φ(X) in L(adM); (ii) r_adM(φ(X))=r(M)-r_M(X); (iii) φ(X)∧φ(Y)=φ(X∨Y), where ∧ and ∨ are the meet and join in the indicated flat lattices; (iv) r_adM(φ(X))+r_adM(φ(Y))=r_adM(φ(X)∧φ(Y))+r_adM(φ(X)∨φ(Y)); and (v) if H1,...,Hm are hyperplanes of M such that H1∩...∩Hm ⫋ H1∩...∩H_{m-1} ⫋ ... ⫋ H1∩H2 ⫋ H1, then φ(H1),...,φ(Hm) is an independent set of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites these properties from Bixby-Coullard and Hochstättler-Kromberg rather than reproving them. The properties are used as structural rules for adjoint maps: cover reversal gives the rank formula by comparing maximal chains; order reversal turns joins in L(M) into meets of images; the stated rank equality is the modular rank equality inside the image sublattice; and the strict chain of intersections of hyperplanes gives successive rank increases for the corresponding atoms in adM, hence independence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fact 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let adM be an adjoint of M. If e is either a loop of M or an element parallel to another element of M, then adM is also an adjoint of the deletion M\e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as an immediate fact. The point is that loops and parallel elements do not affect the simple geometric-lattice data that the adjoint sees: adjoints are simple, and the relevant incidence of flats and hyperplanes is unchanged after deleting a loop or one element from a parallel class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and adM be matroids of the same rank r. Then adM is an adjoint of M if and only if, after relabeling the ground set of adM as E(adM)=H(M), the set H[e]:={H∈H(M): e∈H} is a hyperplane of adM for every nonloop element e∈E(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Fact 2.4 the proof reduces to the case where M is simple. If adM is an adjoint, relabel each atom φ(H) of adM by the hyperplane H of M. For an element e of M, formula (2.1) gives φ(e)=∪_{H∋e}φ(H), which becomes the subset H[e] of H(M). By Proposition 2.3(ii), r_adM(φ(e))=r(M)-1, so H[e] is a hyperplane of adM. Conversely, assume E(adM)=H(M) and all H[e] are hyperplanes of adM. Define ψ:L(M)→L(adM) by ψ(X)=∩_{e∈X}H[e]={H∈H(M): X⊆H}. This is a flat of adM because it is an intersection of hyperplanes. The map is order-reversing: if X⊆Y, then ψ(Y)⊆ψ(X). It sends a hyperplane H0 of M to the singleton {H0}, so it bijects coatoms of L(M) with atoms of L(adM). Finally, ψ is injective because distinct flats of M have distinct collections of containing hyperplanes. Thus ψ is an adjoint map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r and let adM be an adjoint whose ground set is relabeled as H(M), as in Proposition 3.1. If B is a basis of M and H(e;B) denotes the fundamental hyperplane of e∈B with respect to B, namely the unique hyperplane containing B−{e}, then {H(e;B): e∈B} is a basis of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write B={e1,...,er}. For each k, the fundamental hyperplane H(ei;B) contains ek for all i≠k and does not contain ek when i=k. Therefore the intersections H(e1;B)∩...∩H(ei;B) form a strictly decreasing chain as i increases. Proposition 2.3(v) applied to these hyperplanes implies that the corresponding r elements of adM are independent. Since r(adM)=r, this independent set is a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a loopless matroid and let adM be an adjoint of M. If adM decomposes as a direct sum adM=N1⊕N2, then there are submatroids M1 and M2 of M such that M=M1⊕M2 and Ni is an adjoint of Mi for i=1,2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Fact 2.4, assume M is simple and use the hyperplane labeling E(adM)=H(M) from Proposition 3.1. Since adM=N1⊕N2, every cocircuit of adM lies in one summand, and hyperplanes of adM are partitioned according to which summand ground set they contain. Let H_E(Ni) be the hyperplanes of adM containing E(Ni). Define Ei={e∈E(M): H[e]∈H_E(Ni)}. Then E(M)=E1⊔E2, and neither Ei is empty: if, for instance, E1 were empty, then every H[e] would contain E(N2), forcing every hyperplane of M corresponding to an element of E(N2) to contain all elements of M, impossible. Choose a basis B of M, write B∩E1={e1,...,ek} and B∩E2={e_{k+1},...,er}. By the definition of Ei, every hyperplane of M belonging to E(N1) contains B∩E1, and every hyperplane belonging to E(N2) contains B∩E2. Thus the fundamental hyperplanes H(ei;B) for ei∈E1 lie in E(N2), while those for ei∈E2 lie in E(N1). Lemma 3.2 says all these fundamental hyperplanes form a basis of adM, so r_adM(E(N1))=|B∩E2| and r_adM(E(N2))=|B∩E1|. Repeating this argument with a basis adapted to E1 or E2 shows r_M(E1)=|B∩E1| and r_M(E2)=|B∩E2|; hence r_M(E1)+r_M(E2)=r(M), so M is the direct sum of its restrictions to E1 and E2. Finally, intervals in L(M) corresponding to the two direct-sum factors are carried by the adjoint map onto the flat lattices of N1 and N2, giving Ni=adMi.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 and Corollaries 3.5-3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a loopless matroid and adM an adjoint of M. If M=M1⊕M2, then adM=N1⊕N2 for submatroids N1,N2 of adM, where Ni is an adjoint of Mi for i=1,2. Consequently, for a loopless matroid M with an adjoint adM, M is connected if and only if adM is connected. Also, if M is loopless and decomposes as the direct sum of its connected components M1,...,Mn, then M has an adjoint if and only if every component Mi has an adjoint.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The direct-sum assertion is cited from Bixby-Coullard. Combined with Theorem 3.3, it gives the equivalence of connectivity for M and any adjoint adM. For the componentwise existence statement, one direction follows by repeatedly applying Theorem 3.3 to decompose an adjoint along connected components. Conversely, if each Mi has an adjoint adMi with adjoint map φi, then adM:=adM1⊕...⊕adMn has rank sum equal to r(M). Every flat X of M is a disjoint union X=⊔Xi with Xi∈L(Mi), and the map φ(X)=⊔φi(Xi) is injective, order-reversing, and sends hyperplanes of M bijectively to atoms of adM. Thus adM is an adjoint of M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular, meaning every pair of flats X,Y satisfies r_M(X∨Y)+r_M(X∧Y)=r_M(X)+r_M(Y), if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that L(adM) is isomorphic to the opposite lattice L(M)^op.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M is modular, then the opposite lattice L(M)^op is again a geometric lattice; hence it is the flat lattice of a simple matroid, and the natural order-reversing identification L(M)→L(M)^op gives an adjoint of M. Conversely, if M has an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op, then L(M)^op is a geometric lattice. The paper uses the standard lattice fact that a finite geometric lattice has geometric opposite exactly in the modular case. Therefore M is modular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple modular matroid of rank r. Let E(M) denote the extension lattice of M, whose elements are linear subclasses ℋ⊆H(M), ordered by inclusion, where ℋ is linear if whenever H1,H2∈ℋ with r_M(H1∩H2)=r−2, every hyperplane H3 containing H1∩H2 also lies in ℋ. Then the map λ:L(M)^op→E(M) defined by λ(X)=H_X(M):={H∈H(M): X⊆H} is an isomorphism of posets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First λ is well defined. If r_M(X)=r or r−1 this is immediate. If r_M(X)≤r−2 and H1,H2 are distinct hyperplanes containing X, modularity gives r_M(H1∩H2)=r−2; any hyperplane containing H1∩H2 also contains X, so H_X(M) is linear. The map is order-preserving as a map out of L(M)^op: X≤_{op}Y means Y⊆X, hence H_X(M)⊆H_Y(M). It is injective because distinct flats are separated by their sets of containing hyperplanes. For surjectivity, let ℋ be a linear subclass and put X_ℋ=∩_{H∈ℋ}H. Clearly ℋ⊆H_{X_ℋ}(M). To prove the reverse inclusion, suppose H0∈H_{X_ℋ}(M)−ℋ. Linearity implies that no intersection H∩H' with H,H'∈ℋ can be contained in H0, otherwise H0 would be forced into ℋ. Choose H1,...,H_{r−k} in ℋ, where k=r_M(X_ℋ), with a strictly descending chain X_ℋ=∩_{j=1}^{r−k}Hj ⋖ ... ⋖ H1∩H2 ⋖ H1. Let i0 be minimal such that ∩_{j=1}^{i0−1}Hj is not contained in H0 but ∩_{j=1}^{i0}Hj is contained in H0; then i0≥3. The opposite lattice of a modular geometric lattice has the intersection property: for distinct atoms and an element satisfying the appropriate incidence condition, there is another atom below the relevant meet. Applying this in L(M)^op produces a hyperplane H_{0i0} with H_{i0}∩H0⊆H_{0i0} and ∩_{j=1}^{i0−1}Hj⊆H_{0i0}. If H_{0i0} belonged to ℋ, then linearity applied to H_{i0} and H_{0i0} would force H0∈ℋ, contradiction. Thus H_{0i0} is another member of H_{X_ℋ}(M)−ℋ, distinct from H0, and the same construction yields a smaller index. Iterating gives an infinite descent in a finite interval of possible indices, impossible. Hence H_{X_ℋ}(M)=ℋ, proving surjectivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple modular matroid. Then M has exactly one adjoint up to isomorphism, and for every adjoint adM of M one has L(adM)≅L(M)^op.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 4.1 gives existence of an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op. For uniqueness, the paper uses a known characterization due to Cheung: if adM is an adjoint of M, then L(adM) can be viewed as a sublattice of the extension lattice E(M), and L(M)^op embeds as a sublattice of L(adM). Lemma 4.2 identifies L(M)^op with E(M) when M is modular. Thus L(M)^op⊆L(adM)⊆E(M) collapses to equality up to isomorphism. Therefore every adjoint has flat lattice L(M)^op.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular if and only if every connected component of M is either the one-element free matroid U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this classical characterization from Birkhoff and Oxley. It is used to translate modularity of a simple matroid into projective-geometric connected components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5 and Theorem 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If (P,L,ι) is a finite projective geometry, its dual geometry is obtained by taking the coatoms of the subspace lattice L(P) as points and declaring two such points incident with a line when their intersection has rank r−2. The lattice of the dual geometry is isomorphic to L(P)^op, and the dual geometry is again a finite projective geometry. Moreover, by the Coordinatization Theorem, every projective geometry of rank r≥4 is isomorphic to PG(r−1,F) for some field F; in particular, every finite projective geometry of rank r≥4 is isomorphic to PG(r−1,q) for a finite field GF(q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 4.5, the paper treats this as a direct consequence of the standard point-hyperplane duality of projective geometries and Proposition 4.1: the subspaces of the dual geometry are the order-reversed subspaces of the original geometry. The Coordinatization Theorem is cited as the Veblen-Young theorem; no proof is given in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid with no connected component of rank 3. Then M is modular if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that adM≅M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose first that M has an adjoint adM isomorphic to M. Since adM and M have the same number of flats and the adjoint map embeds L(M)^op into L(adM), the embedding is onto, so L(adM)≅L(M)^op. Hence L(M)^op is geometric and M is modular by Proposition 4.1. Conversely, assume M is modular. By the direct-sum results of Section 3 it suffices to treat connected components. A connected simple modular component of rank 1 is U_{1,1}, and of rank 2 is U_{2,m}; both are self-adjoint by direct inspection. Since rank 3 components are excluded, any remaining connected component has rank r≥4 and, by Proposition 4.4 and the Coordinatization Theorem, is PG(r−1,q). The unique adjoint is the type I adjoint, and elementary linear algebra gives a bijection between hyperplanes of GF(q)^r and their one-dimensional normal complements. This identifies the type I adjoint of PG(r−1,q) with PG(r−1,q) itself. Combining components gives adM≅M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. Then M is modular if and only if M has an adjoint adM such that |E(M)|=|E(adM)|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Greene’s theorem that a simple matroid M is modular if and only if the number of hyperplanes of M equals the number of elements of M. In any adjoint, atoms of adM correspond bijectively to hyperplanes of M, so |E(adM)|=|H(M)|. Thus the equality |E(M)|=|E(adM)| is equivalent to |E(M)|=|H(M)|, and hence to modularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≤2 and size m. For every integer k≥0, the kth adjoint ad^k M exists. If r=1 then ad^k M≅U_{1,1} for all k, and if r=2 then ad^k M≅U_{2,m} for all k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this follows easily from the fact that matroids of rank at most three always admit adjoints and from direct inspection of rank-one and rank-two flat lattices. A simple connected rank-one matroid is U_{1,1}; a simple connected rank-two matroid of size m is U_{2,m}; their adjoints have the same flat-lattice form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. If M has a second adjoint ad^2 M, then M is isomorphic to a submatroid of ad^2 M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let φ1:L(M)→L(adM) and φ2:L(adM)→L(ad^2 M) be adjoint maps. Their composition is order-preserving on flat lattices and sends each element e of M first to the hyperplane φ1(e) of adM and then to an element φ2(φ1(e)) of ad^2 M. If {e1,...,ek} is independent in M, then Proposition 2.3(ii) and (iii) imply that the intersections ∩_{j=1}^i φ1(ej) have ranks r(M)−i, hence form a strictly decreasing chain. Proposition 2.3(v), applied to the adjoint map φ2, then implies that {φ2φ1(e1),...,φ2φ1(ek)} is independent in ad^2 M. Thus the element map induced by φ2φ1 embeds M as a submatroid of ad^2 M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple matroid. If M has a kth adjoint ad^k M for some k≥3 and M≅ad^k M, then M≅adM or M≅ad^2 M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If k is even, repeated use of Lemma 5.2 shows that ad^2 M is a submatroid of ad^k M, and the same embeddings give |E(M)|≤|E(ad^2 M)|≤|E(ad^k M)|. Since ad^k M≅M, all inequalities are equalities, forcing M≅ad^2 M. If k is odd, the same argument applied to the adjoint sequence starting at adM gives M≅adM.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≥3. Suppose M has an adjoint sequence ad^0 M=M, adM, ad^2 M, ... and that ad^i M≅ad^j M for some nonnegative integers i<j. If r=3, then all later adjoints ad^k M exist for k≥i; if j−i is odd, all ad^k M for k≥i are isomorphic to one fixed finite projective plane, while if j−i is even, the subsequence ad^{i+2k} M is constant up to isomorphism and the subsequence ad^{i+2k+1} M is constant up to isomorphism, each being a finite projective plane. If r≥4, then all later adjoints ad^k M for k≥i are isomorphic to one fixed finite projective geometry PG(r−1,q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 5.3, the repetition ad^i M≅ad^j M reduces to either ad^i M≅ad^{i+1} M when j−i is odd or ad^i M≅ad^{i+2} M when j−i is even. In either case the adjoint relation identifies L(ad^{i+1} M) with L(ad^i M)^op, so ad^i M is modular. Theorem 4.3 then gives uniqueness of adjoints from that stage onward. In rank 3, Proposition 4.4 identifies connected modular simple matroids with finite projective planes, and uniqueness yields the stated constant or two-periodic behavior. In rank at least 4, Theorem 4.8 and Remark 4.7 identify the modular connected matroid and all its adjoints with the same finite projective geometry PG(r−1,q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.6 and Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r with an infinite adjoint sequence ad^0 M, adM, ad^2 M, ... . For i≤j, Lemma 5.2 gives natural embeddings ad^{2i} M→ad^{2j} M, forming a directed system. Let E∞ be the union of the ground sets of all even adjoints, identify elements that become equal under later embeddings, and call the quotient ground set Ebar. A finite subset of Ebar is independent if its representatives become independent in some common even adjoint. Then the resulting matroid Mbar=(Ebar,Ibar) is simple, connected, has rank r, and is the direct limit lim_i ad^{2i} M in the category of matroids with embeddings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The independence axioms for Mbar are checked by moving any finite collection of representatives to a sufficiently late even adjoint, where the ordinary matroid axioms hold. Rank is r because all even adjoints have rank r, and a basis of M maps to an independent r-set. Simplicity follows from simplicity of all adjoints. For connectivity, any two elements of Mbar have representatives in some common even adjoint; that adjoint is connected by Corollary 3.5, so it contains a circuit through them, whose image remains a circuit in Mbar. For the universal property, the canonical maps ad^{2i} M→Mbar are embeddings compatible with the directed system. Any compatible family of embeddings into another matroid N defines a unique map on equivalence classes; compatibility makes it well defined, and independence is preserved by checking in a common finite stage.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.8 and Theorem 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank r≥3 with an infinite adjoint sequence. Then the direct limit lim_i ad^{2i} M of the even adjoints is a projective geometry; the analogous direct limit of the odd adjoints is also a projective geometry. If, in addition, ad^i M is not isomorphic to ad^j M for all 0≤i<j, then for r=3 these two direct limits are infinite projective planes, and for r≥4 both direct limits are isomorphic to the same infinite projective geometry PG(r−1,F) over some infinite field F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 5.8 is stated as a straightforward consequence of the directed-limit construction and is said to be implicit in an exercise from Oriented Matroids; the paper omits the details. For Theorem 5.9, when r≥4, Proposition 5.8 and the Coordinatization Theorem identify the even direct limit with PG(r−1,F) for some field F. If F were finite, PG(r−1,F) would have only finitely many submatroids up to isomorphism, while all even adjoints embed into it; this would force repetitions, contrary to hypothesis. Hence F is infinite. The even adjoints are representable over F, and a cited result of Bixby-Coullard says adjoints of representable matroids are type I adjoints over the same field up to isomorphism; this gives representability of the odd adjoints over F and identifies the odd direct limit with the same PG(r−1,F). For r=3, Proposition 5.8 gives projective planes; finiteness would again force repetitions, so they are infinite.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a simple connected matroid of rank greater than three. Then M is representable over some field if and only if M has an infinite adjoint sequence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If M is representable, the type I adjoint construction can be iterated indefinitely, giving an infinite adjoint sequence. Conversely, if M has an infinite adjoint sequence, Proposition 5.8 embeds M into the projective-geometry direct limit of the even adjoints. Since the rank is greater than three, the Coordinatization Theorem realizes that projective geometry as PG(r−1,F) over a field F, so M is representable over F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,A) be an F-represented matroid of rank r on ground set E={e1,...,em}, with A∈F^{r×m}. Let A'∈F^{(m−r)×m} be a matrix whose rows form a basis of the solution space of Ax=0, so A' represents the dual matroid M*. For each circuit C of M let c_C∈F^m be a circuit vector supported exactly on C, and define the Oxley-Wang derived matroid δ_OW M=M[c_C: C∈C(M)]. For each hyperplane H of M*, let h_H be a normal vector to the span of the columns of A' indexed by H, and define the type I adjoint σM*=M[h_H: H∈H(M*)]. Then δ_OW M≅σM*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A circuit C of M corresponds to the hyperplane C*=E−C of M*. Since c_C∈ker A, it can be written uniquely as c_C=hA' for some row vector h. The entries of c_C vanish exactly on C*, so h annihilates precisely the columns of A' indexed by the hyperplane C*; hence h may be chosen as the normal vector h_{C*}. For any circuits C1,...,Ck, the matrix with rows c_{Ci} equals the matrix with rows h_{C_i*} multiplied by A'. Since A' has full row rank, multiplication by A' preserves the row rank of these h-vectors. Thus every set of circuit vectors has the same rank as the corresponding set of normal vectors, proving the two represented matroids are isomorphic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and adM be matroids of the same rank r, and let M* be the dual matroid of M. Then adM is an adjoint of M if and only if its ground set can be relabeled as E(adM)=C(M*), the set of circuits of the dual matroid, equivalently cocircuits of M, in such a way that for every nonloop e∈E(M), the set Cstar[e]:={C*∈C(M*): e∉C*} is a hyperplane of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a dual reformulation of Proposition 3.1. Circuits C* of M* are cocircuits of M, and each corresponds to the hyperplane H=E(M)−C* of M. Under this correspondence, the condition e∉C* is exactly e∈H. Therefore the set Cstar[e] corresponds to H[e]={H∈H(M): e∈H}. Replacing each cocircuit label C* by its complementary hyperplane H turns the stated condition into the hyperplane-incidence condition of Proposition 3.1, and the result follows in both directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r and let adM be an adjoint of M whose ground set is relabeled as C(M*), the cocircuits of M. If B is a basis of M and C*(e;B) denotes the fundamental cocircuit of e∈B with respect to B, then {C*(e;B): e∈B} is a basis of adM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The set has r elements, so it is enough to prove independence. Suppose it contained a circuit of adM, and choose an element C*(er;B) in that circuit. By Proposition 7.3, Cstar[er]={C*∈C(M*): er∉C*} is a hyperplane of adM. This hyperplane contains every fundamental cocircuit C*(ei;B) with i≠r, because er is not in those cocircuits, but it does not contain C*(er;B), because er∈C*(er;B). In a matroid, if all but one element of a circuit lie in a flat, then the remaining element also lies in that flat. This contradiction proves independence, hence the set is a basis.

proof_techniques.name: Relabel an adjoint by hyperplanes and encode elements as incidence hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: A central move is Proposition 3.1: instead of treating the adjoint abstractly, relabel E(adM) by the hyperplanes H(M) of M. Under this labeling, every element e of M determines the subset H[e] of hyperplanes containing e, and the adjoint condition is exactly that each H[e] be a hyperplane of adM. This turns the existence of an order-reversing flat-lattice embedding into a concrete incidence condition between elements and hyperplanes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already has an inclusion of L(M)^op into a larger poset Dr(M). This technique suggests looking for an incidence-theoretic description of the flat stratum F↦Trop(M/F⊕U_{0,F}) and asking whether any candidate involution on Dr(M) is forced by how it acts on the analogues of the sets H[e].

proof_techniques.name: Extension-lattice sandwich for modular matroids
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the extension lattice E(M), whose elements are linear subclasses of hyperplanes, as an ambient lattice for all adjoints of M. Cheung’s characterization gives a sandwich L(M)^op⊆L(adM)⊆E(M). For modular simple M, Lemma 4.2 proves that the natural map X↦{hyperplanes containing X} identifies L(M)^op with E(M), so the sandwich collapses and the adjoint is unique.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If Dr(M) plays a role analogous to an extension or enlargement poset of L(M)^op, the modular case may be rigid: the flat part could already be maximal among adjoint-like structures. This is a possible route to either constructing a unique extension or proving that extra Dressian elements obstruct it.

proof_techniques.name: Use opposite-lattice geometricity to force modularity
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.1 exploits the classical fact that a finite geometric lattice has a geometric opposite exactly in the modular case. Thus an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op exists precisely for modular M. Theorem 4.8 strengthens this in many cases: if M has an adjoint isomorphic to itself and no rank-3 connected components, then M is modular, and conversely modularity gives a self-adjoint matroid.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution of L(M) is a self-duality of the flat lattice, so in the simple finite setting this strongly suggests first reducing to modular/projective-geometric matroids. This can sharply narrow the cases in which an extension to Dr(M) could exist.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce adjoint questions componentwise
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that adjoints preserve and reflect direct-sum decompositions: if M decomposes, its adjoint decomposes into adjoints of the components, and if an adjoint decomposes, then M decomposes accordingly. Hence connectedness is equivalent for M and adM, and adjoint existence is determined by connected components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matroid M in the original problem is disconnected, the flat-lattice involution may permute or reverse components. This technique suggests reducing any extension problem to connected factors and then reassembling, while tracking whether the proposed involution swaps factors.

proof_techniques.name: Fundamental hyperplane and cocircuit bases as tests for adjoint structure
proof_techniques.description: Given a basis B of M, Lemma 3.2 shows that the fundamental hyperplanes H(e;B), for e∈B, form a basis of any hyperplane-labeled adjoint. Dually, Lemma 7.5 shows that the fundamental cocircuits C*(e;B) form a basis in the cocircuit-labeled model. These basis criteria convert abstract adjoint structure into checkable behavior on the fundamental objects associated to a basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For tropical linear spaces inside Trop(M), bases and fundamental circuits/cocircuits control local cones of the Bergman fan. These lemmas suggest checking a candidate Dr(M)-involution on local stars around basis cones or on subspaces associated with fundamental flats.

proof_techniques.name: Projective-geometric duality via normal complements
proof_techniques.description: For connected modular matroids of rank at least four, the paper identifies them with finite projective geometries PG(r−1,q). In that setting, the adjoint can be realized by sending each hyperplane to its one-dimensional normal complement; this gives an isomorphism between PG(r−1,q) and its type I adjoint. Proposition 4.5 similarly identifies the lattice of the dual projective geometry with the opposite lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If M is projective-geometric or realizable with a nondegenerate polarity, this is the closest matroidal analogue of extending F↦F^perp. It suggests trying a tropical orthogonal-complement or polar-dual operation on tropical linear subspaces as the candidate Φ.

proof_techniques.name: Adjoint iteration as a rigidity detector
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.2 embeds M into ad^2 M, Lemma 5.3 reduces any finite adjoint-cycle to period one or two, and Theorem 5.4 shows that cyclic adjoint sequences stabilize at finite projective geometries. Infinite nonrepeating sequences have direct limits that are projective geometries. Thus repeated adjoint-like dualization tends to force projective geometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on Dr(M) would impose a strict period-two symmetry on a much larger poset. The adjoint-sequence results suggest using periodicity as a rigidity or obstruction principle: if the flat part induces adjoint-like cycles, projective geometry may be forced.

proof_techniques.name: Dual cocircuit formulation
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 7.3 rewrites adjoints using cocircuits rather than hyperplanes: relabel the adjoint ground set by C(M*) and require the sets of cocircuits avoiding a fixed element to be hyperplanes. This formulation aligns adjoints with derived-matroid constructions and with the standard circuit-cocircuit duality of matroid theory.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The notation F↦F^perp in the original problem evokes duality. The cocircuit formulation may be more natural than the hyperplane formulation when comparing tropical orthogonality, valuated matroid duality, or tropical linear spaces defined by circuits.

other_useful_info: The paper relies on Cheung [9] and Alfter-Kern-Wanka [2] for the characterization of adjoints via the extension lattice. It uses Greene [15, Theorem 2], which says a simple matroid M is modular if and only if |H(M)|=|E(M)|. It cites Bixby-Coullard [7] for type I adjoints of representable matroids and for the fact used in Theorem 5.9 that adjoints of representable matroids are type I adjoints up to isomorphism. The authors explicitly note that rank 3 is exceptional for self-adjointness because some projective planes are not self-dual, which is why Theorem 4.8 excludes rank-3 connected components. Section 7 connects adjoints to combinatorial derived matroids and records Conjecture 7.2 from Freij-Hollanti-Jurrius-Kuznetsova: if M* has an adjoint, then the combinatorial derived matroid δM should be isomorphic to one of the adjoints of M*. The paper ends with Question 7.8 asking how to construct a combinatorial adjoint for an arbitrary matroid.
why_relevant: Modern treatment of matroid adjoints, opposite lattices, modular matroids, and adjoint sequences. Since an order-reversing involution on flats is a strong adjoint/polarity phenomenon, these results are central.

title: Quotients of M-convex sets and M-convex functions
overall_summary: The paper unifies quotient notions for matroids, polymatroids, valuated matroids, submodular functions, M-convex sets, and M-convex functions using discrete convex analysis. Its main finite theorem gives ten equivalent characterizations of a quotient P ↠ Q of M-convex sets, including submodular inequalities, top and bottom layers of an M♮-convex set, deletion-contraction from a larger M-convex set, asymmetric exchange, induction through linking sets, Green preorder in a linking-set monoid, compatible matroid lifts, and flag polytope compression. It then develops a partial hierarchy for quotients of M-convex functions, with special consequences for valuated matroids via minimizers and flag-matroid subdivisions. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the translation of containment/quotient data for tropical linear spaces into valuated matroid quotient criteria, especially the minimizer criterion, induction/linking-set formalism, and top-bottom generalized polymatroid realization, any of which could be used either to construct a candidate order-reversing operation or to produce obstructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be a finite set and write x(A)=sum_{i∈A} x_i for x∈ℤ^E. A nonempty bounded set P⊆ℤ^E is M-convex if for all x,y∈P and all i with x_i>y_i there is j with x_j<y_j such that x-e_i+e_j and y+e_i-e_j both lie in P. A normalized integral submodular function is a map p:2^E→ℤ with p(∅)=0 and p(A∪B)+p(A∩B)≤p(A)+p(B) for all A,B⊆E. Its base polyhedron is B(p)={x∈ℝ^E: x(A)≤p(A) for all A⊆E and x(E)=p(E)}. The theorem gives a one-to-one correspondence among bounded M-convex sets P⊆ℤ^E, integral submodular functions p:2^E→ℤ, and integral generalized permutohedra: if p is submodular then B(p)∩ℤ^E is M-convex; if P is M-convex then p(A)=max{x(A):x∈P} is submodular and P=B(p)∩ℤ^E; and P is M-convex iff P=conv(P)∩ℤ^E and conv(P) is an integral generalized permutohedron, i.e. a polytope whose edge directions are among e_i-e_j and whose vertices are integral.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Murota for this standard equivalence. The direction from submodular functions uses Edmonds base polyhedra: normalized integral submodular inequalities define an integral polyhedron lying in the hyperplane x(E)=p(E), and its edge directions are e_i-e_j; the integer points satisfy the M-convex exchange axiom. Conversely, for an M-convex set P, defining p(A) by maximizing x(A) over P records all supporting inequalities of conv(P); the exchange axiom forces p to be submodular, and the resulting base polyhedron has exactly P as its lattice points. The generalized permutohedron formulation follows because base polyhedra of submodular functions are precisely generalized permutohedra, and integrality of the vertices is equivalent to recovering the lattice points as an M-convex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℤ^E be bounded M-convex sets with associated normalized integral submodular functions p,q. Say p and q are compliant, or P ↠ Q, if q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for every pair X⊆Y⊆E. For each permutation σ of E, let x_σ be the greedy vertex of B(p) and y_σ the greedy vertex of B(q), where for S_k={σ(1),...,σ(k)} the coordinate of x_σ at σ(k) is p(S_k)-p(S_{k-1}), and similarly for y_σ. Then P ↠ Q if and only if x_σ≥y_σ coordinatewise for every σ∈Sym(E).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed permutation σ, the greedy-coordinate formula expresses each coordinate of x_σ and y_σ as a rank increment along the chain ∅=S_0⊂S_1⊂...⊂S_n=E. If p and q are compliant, applying q(S_k)-q(S_{k-1})≤p(S_k)-p(S_{k-1}) gives y_σ(σ(k))≤x_σ(σ(k)) for every k. Conversely, given X⊆Y, choose a permutation σ whose first |X| entries are X and whose first |Y| entries are Y. Summing the coordinate inequalities x_σ≥y_σ over the block Y∖X gives q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.18 and Lemma 2.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a submodular function p:2^E→ℤ, define its dual supermodular function p# by p#(A)=p(E)-p(E∖A). Given a submodular p and a supermodular q satisfying p(A)-q(B)≥p(A∖B)-q(B∖A) for all A,B⊆E, the generalized polymatroid G(p,q) is {x∈ℝ^E:q(A)≤x(A)≤p(A) for all A⊆E}. A bounded set R⊆ℤ^E is M♮-convex if and only if R=G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E for submodular functions p,q for which G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid; equivalently, R is the coordinate projection of a bounded M-convex set on a larger ground set. If G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid, then its top layer is B(p) and its bottom layer is B(q), so G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E has top layer B(p)∩ℤ^E and bottom layer B(q)∩ℤ^E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls the standard discrete-convex characterization: coordinate projections of M-convex sets satisfy the M♮ exchange axiom, and every M♮-convex set can be obtained from an M-convex set by adding one coordinate and projecting. Polyhedrally, such projections are exactly generalized polymatroids, i.e. intersections of a submodular polyhedron B(p)+ℝ^E_{≤0} and a supermodular polyhedron B(q)+ℝ^E_{≥0}. The compatibility inequality guarantees that the top base polyhedron B(p) and bottom base polyhedron B(q) are not cut away by the intersection. Hence maximizing x(E) in G(p,q#) gives B(p), while minimizing x(E) gives B(q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.29 with Propositions 2.31 and 2.32
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p,q:2^E→ℤ be normalized integral submodular functions. Then p and q are compliant, i.e. q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for all X⊆Y⊆E, if and only if G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid. Consequently, if P=B(p)∩ℤ^E and Q=B(q)∩ℤ^E, then P ↠ Q if and only if there exists an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^E with P=R↑ and Q=R↓; in that case R is explicitly G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E. Conversely, if P and Q are the top and bottom layers of an M♮-convex set, then their associated submodular functions are compliant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The core cited result of Fujishige and Hirai identifies the generalized-polymatroid compatibility inequalities for G(p,q#) with the strong-map/compliance inequalities for p and q. Once G(p,q#) is known to be a generalized polymatroid, Theorem 2.18 implies that its lattice points form an M♮-convex set, and Lemma 2.17 identifies the top and bottom layers as B(p)∩ℤ^E and B(q)∩ℤ^E. Conversely, any M♮-convex set has a generalized-polymatroid representation G(f,g#); its top and bottom layers determine f and g uniquely by the M-convex/submodular correspondence, and the generalized-polymatroid condition gives compliance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℤ^E be bounded M-convex sets, and let p,q:2^E→ℤ be their associated normalized submodular functions. The following ten conditions are equivalent, and define Q as a quotient of P, denoted P ↠ Q. (1) For every X⊆Y⊆E, q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X). (2) For every permutation σ of E, the greedy vertices x_σ∈P and y_σ∈Q satisfy x_σ≥y_σ coordinatewise. (3) For every X⊆E, the submodular polyhedron S(q/X) is contained in S(p/X), where p/X(A)=p(A∪X)-p(X). (4) There is an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^E such that P is the top layer R↑ and Q is the bottom layer R↓; explicitly R=G(p,q#)∩ℤ^E. (5) There is an M-convex set R⊆ℤ^{E⊔X} such that P=R∖X is a deletion and Q=R/X is a contraction; the added set X can be chosen to be a singleton. (6) For all x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), there exists j∈supp−(x-y) such that x-e_i+e_j∈Q and y+e_i-e_j∈P. (7) There exists a linking set Γ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē}, meaning an M-convex set viewed from E to Ē, and an M-convex set W⊆ℤ^{Ē}, such that P is the top layer of the left projection π_E(Γ) and Q is the induction ind_Γ(W)={x∈ℤ^E: there is w∈W with (x,-w)∈Γ}. (8) There are linking sets Γ,Δ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^E such that P and Q are top layers of their left projections and Γ⪯_RΔ in Green's right preorder on the monoid of linking sets, i.e. Γ∗X=Δ for some linking set X. (9) Any compatible matroid lifts of P and Q form a matroid quotient. (10) The Minkowski sum P+Q is a flag M-convex set of type determined by the two ranks rk(Q)+ℓ and rk(P)+ℓ for some integer ℓ and some surjection of ground sets; equivalently it is an integral translate of a projection of a generalized permutohedron whose vertices lie among those of the corresponding sum of two hypersimplices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence (1)⇔(2) is Theorem 2.10, using greedy vertices along chains. For (1)⇔(3), expand the contraction formula: S(q/X)⊆S(p/X) is exactly q(A∪X)-q(X)≤p(A∪X)-p(X) for all A⊆E∖X. For (1)⇔(4), use Theorem 2.29 to identify compliance with G(p,q#) being a generalized polymatroid, then use the M♮-convex/generalized-polymatroid correspondence and top-bottom layer lemma. For (4)⇔(5), use that every M♮-convex set is a coordinate projection of an M-convex set, and deletion and contraction are the top and bottom layers of such a projection; one extra coordinate suffices. Condition (4) implies (6) by applying an M♮ exchange axiom to a top-layer point and a bottom-layer point. The implication (6)⇒(1) is proved in two steps: first Lemma 2.35 shows every single-element increment satisfies q(X)-q(X∖i)≤p(X)-p(X∖i), by repeatedly applying the asymmetric exchange property to points x∈Q maximizing q(X) and y∈P maximizing p(X∖i); then induction over |Y∖X| yields the full compliance inequality. The equivalence with (7) is proved by lifting the M♮ set in (4) to one extra coordinate and selecting its bottom layer by induction through a singleton M-convex set; conversely, applying M-convex exchange inside Γ shows the asymmetric exchange property, because an exchange into the auxiliary side would contradict top-layer maximality. The equivalence with (8) uses the product of linking sets: if Γ∗X=Δ then the left set of Δ is the induction through Γ of the left set of X, so (7) applies; conversely, realize the W in (7) as the left set of a linking set and set Δ=Γ∗X. The equivalence with (9) is obtained by constructing box lifts to matroids and using induction to show quotient preservation, while reflection follows because the coordinate-summing projection preserves top and bottom layers. The equivalence with (10) combines compatible matroid lifts with the classical theorem that a pair of matroids is a quotient exactly when the vertices of its Minkowski sum lie in the corresponding flag-matroid polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.26 and Proposition 2.28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^E be an M-convex set with submodular function p, and write E=V⊔U. For k in the interval min_{x∈P}x(V)≤k≤max_{x∈P}x(V), the basic k-th minor of P with respect to U is P^U_k={x∈ℤ^V: there exists y∈ℤ^U with (x,y)∈P and x(V)=k}=π_V(P)∩H_k. The deletion P∖U is the top such minor, and the contraction P/U is the bottom such minor. Their submodular functions are p∖U(A)=p(A) and p/U(A)=p(A∪U)-p(U) for A⊆V. The submodular function of P^U_k is p^U_k(A)=min(p(A), k+p(A∪U)-p(E∖A)) for A⊆V. If ℓ<k are both in the allowed range, then P^U_k ↠ P^U_ℓ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Project P to V. The projection π_V(P) is M♮-convex and therefore equals G(f,g#)∩ℤ^V for submodular f,g. Maximizing x(A) over the projection gives f(A)=p(A), while minimizing x(A) gives g#(A)=p(E)-p(E∖A); rewriting g from g# yields p/U(A)=p(A∪U)-p(U). Intersecting the generalized polymatroid π_V(P) with the single layer H_k is intersection with a plank, and the formula for p^U_k follows from the standard plank-intersection formula p^U_k(A)=min(f(A),k-g#(V∖A)). Finally, π_V(P)∩K(ℓ,k), where K(ℓ,k) is the plank ℓ≤x(V)≤k, is M♮-convex; its top and bottom layers are P^U_k and P^U_ℓ, so Theorem 1.2 condition (4) gives P^U_k ↠ P^U_ℓ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^E be an M-convex set with submodular function p and rank rk(P). For k>0, the k-th truncation P^tr_k is the layer (P+ℤ^E_{≤0})∩H_{rk(P)-k}. Then P^tr_k is a quotient of P. Moreover, if P ↠ Q and rk(P)-rk(Q)=k, then Q⊆P^tr_k. Thus truncation is the maximal or generic quotient of P of a fixed rank drop.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The submodular function of the k-th truncation agrees with p on all proper subsets A⊊E and has value p(E)-k on E. Compliance is immediate: for X⊆Y⊊E the differences are unchanged, and when Y=E the right side is reduced by k, still giving a quotient in the direction P ↠ P^tr_k. For any quotient P ↠ Q of rank drop k with submodular function q, compliance applied to ∅⊆A gives q(A)≤p(A)=p^tr_k(A) for A⊊E, while q(E)=p(E)-k=p^tr_k(E). Therefore every lattice point of Q satisfies the defining inequalities of P^tr_k, so Q⊆P^tr_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.38, 2.39, 2.40, and 2.41
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Quotients of M-convex sets are transitive: if P ↠ Q and Q ↠ R, then P ↠ R. A flag of M-convex sets is a sequence (P_0,...,P_k) with P_i ↠ P_{i-1} for all i; it is consecutive if rk(P_i)=rk(P_0)+i. The layers of any M♮-convex set form a consecutive flag of M-convex sets. Conversely, if (P_0,...,P_k) is a consecutive flag and p_0,p_k are the submodular functions of the endpoints, then the layers of G(p_k,p_0#)∩ℤ^E form an M♮-convex flag containing every P_i. Every nonconsecutive flag can be completed to a consecutive flag by inserting intermediate layers.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Transitivity follows by chaining the compliance inequalities r(Y)-r(X)≤q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X). For an M♮-convex set R, any two layers are the top and bottom layers of R intersected with a plank, and plank intersections preserve M♮-convexity; hence the layers form quotients. For embedding a consecutive flag in an M♮-convex flag, let p_i be the submodular function of P_i and set R=G(p_k,p_0#)∩ℤ^E. If x∈P_i, then P_k ↠ P_i gives x(A)≤p_k(A), and P_i ↠ P_0 gives p_0#(A)≤x(A); hence x∈R. Completion of a nonconsecutive flag is done quotient by quotient: for each adjacent pair P_i ↠ P_{i-1}, insert the intermediate layers of the M♮-convex set G(p_i,p_{i-1}#)∩ℤ^E.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^U be a linking set, i.e. an M-convex set viewed as a relation from V to U. If P,Q⊆ℤ^U are M-convex sets with P ↠ Q and the inductions ind_Γ(P) and ind_Γ(Q) are nonempty, then ind_Γ(P) ↠ ind_Γ(Q), where ind_Γ(P)={x∈ℤ^V: there exists y∈P with (x,-y)∈Γ}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an M♮-convex set R⊆ℤ^U with top layer P and bottom layer Q. Induction is built from Minkowski sum and restriction, so ind_Γ(R) is M♮-convex. Nonemptiness ensures the top layer of ind_Γ(R) actually comes from inducing the top layer P: the rank of a point (x,-y)∈Γ with y∈R is x(V)=rk(Γ)+y(U), so maximizing x(V) maximizes y(U), hence y∈P. Similarly the bottom layer comes from y∈Q. Thus ind_Γ(P) and ind_Γ(Q) are the top and bottom layers of ind_Γ(R), so they form a quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.14 and 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For bounded M-convex sets P,Q⊆ℤ^E, P ↠ Q if and only if there exist a linking set Γ⊆ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē} and an M-convex set W⊆ℤ^{Ē} such that P is the top layer of the left set π_E(Γ) and Q=ind_Γ(W). Here the auxiliary ground set Ē may be chosen to be a singleton in the construction from a top-bottom M♮-convex realization.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If P ↠ Q, take an M♮-convex set R with top layer P and bottom layer Q.
