Lift R to an M-convex set Γ on E plus one auxiliary coordinate recording the rank distance from the top layer, with the sign chosen according to the induction convention (x,-w)∈Γ. Taking W to be the singleton equal to the rank drop selects precisely the bottom layer, so ind_Γ(W)=Q, while the top layer of π_E(Γ) is P. Conversely, suppose P=π_E(Γ)↑ and Q=ind_Γ(W). Given x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), choose w∈W with (x,-w)∈Γ and choose an auxiliary z with (y,z)∈Γ. M-convex exchange in Γ gives a coordinate j allowing simultaneous exchange. If j were auxiliary, the E-coordinate sum of y would increase, contradicting that y lies in the top layer of π_E(Γ). Therefore j∈E and the asymmetric exchange condition of Theorem 1.2 holds, so P ↠ Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.21 and Proposition 3.24
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite set V, let M_V be the monoid of possibly unbounded linking sets Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^V, with product Γ∗Δ={(x,-z): there exists y∈ℤ^V with (x,-y)∈Γ and (y,-z)∈Δ} and identity I_V={(x,-x):x∈ℤ^V}. Green's right preorder is Γ⪯_RΔ if Γ∗X=Δ for some X∈M_V. If Γ⪯_RΔ, then the top layer of the left set of Γ is a quotient of the top layer of the left set of Δ: π_L(Γ)↑ ↠ π_L(Δ)↑. Conversely, for every quotient P ↠ Q of M-convex sets in ℤ^V, there are Γ,Δ∈M_V with P=π_L(Γ)↑, Q=π_L(Δ)↑, and Γ⪯_RΔ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ∗X=Δ, then the left set of Δ is the induction of the left set of X through Γ: π_L(Δ)=ind_Γ(π_L(X)). Taking the appropriate layer of π_L(X) whose induced rank gives the top layer of π_L(Δ), Proposition 3.14 yields π_L(Γ)↑ ↠ π_L(Δ)↑. Conversely, use the induction characterization of a quotient P ↠ Q: choose Γ and W with P=π_L(Γ)↑ and Q=ind_Γ(W). Realize W as the left set of a linking set X, enlarge ground sets if needed, and set Δ=Γ∗X. Then Γ⪯_RΔ and Q is the top layer of π_L(Δ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For |V|>2, the monoid M_V of linking sets is not regular: there exists Γ∈M_V for which no pseudoinverse Γ^{-1} satisfies Γ∗Γ^{-1}∗Γ=Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the partial product of individual pairs (x,-y)∗(z,-w), defined only when y=z. For V containing {1,2,3}, take Γ={ (e_i,-e_j): i,j∈{1,2,3}, i≠j }∪{0}. If a pseudoinverse existed, then for any (e_i,-e_j)∈Γ one would need some (e_k,-e_l) in the pseudoinverse with k≠i and l≠j so that (e_i,-e_k)∗(e_k,-e_l)∗(e_l,-e_j)=(e_i,-e_j). Choosing a∈{1,2,3}∖{k,l} would then force (e_a,-e_a)∈Γ, contradicting the definition of Γ. This structural fact may matter when trying to obtain involutions from the linking-set monoid: the monoid has Green preorders but not pervasive inverses.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Constructions 4.2, 4.4, and 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every bounded M-convex set P⊆ℤ^U contained in a box Ω can be lifted through a coordinate-summing map to an M-convex set inside a prescribed box Ψ. Given a surjection ϕ:V→U, the map π_ϕ:ℤ^V→ℤ^U is defined by (π_ϕ(y))_i=sum_{j∈ϕ^{-1}(i)} y_j. If Ω=π_ϕ(Ψ)+v for some v∈ℤ^U, the associated linking set Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ) consists of pairs (y,-x) with y∈Ψ and x=π_ϕ(y)+v∈Ω. The box lift of P is L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P)=ind_{Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ)}(P), an M-convex subset of Ψ with π_ϕ(L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P))+v=P. By choosing Ψ=[0,1]^V after splitting each coordinate interval into unit intervals, any bounded M-convex set has a matroid lift. More generally, choosing Ψ⊆[0,k]^V gives a k-polymatroid lift.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key observation is that the relation x=π_ϕ(y)+v defines an unbounded M-convex set in ℤ^V×ℤ^U, and intersecting it with a box Ψ×(-Ω) preserves M-convexity. Inducing P through this linking set selects exactly those y in Ψ whose projected and translated image lies in P. For matroid lifts, each coordinate interval [ω_i,ω_i] is decomposed as a Minkowski sum of unit intervals [0,1], producing a ground-set fiber V_i and a surjection ϕ. The resulting lift lies in {0,1}^V, hence is the set of bases of a matroid. For k-polymatroid lifts, decompose each coordinate interval into intervals [0,k] and one remainder interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.7 and 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ϕ:V→U be a surjection and v∈ℤ^U. If M,N⊆ℤ^V are M-convex sets with M ↠ N and P=π_ϕ(M)+v, Q=π_ϕ(N)+v, then P ↠ Q. Conversely, if P,Q⊆ℤ^U satisfy P ↠ Q, then any compatible matroid lifts M,N⊆ℤ^V of P and Q form a matroid quotient M ↠ N. Compatibility means that M and N are produced from the same box-lift data, i.e. the same surjection ϕ, translation v, and box Ψ, so that M=L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P) and N=L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(Q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For reflection from M,N to P,Q, choose an M♮-convex set R with top layer M and bottom layer N. The coordinate-summing projection π_ϕ and translation by v preserve the order of layers by total coordinate sum, so π_ϕ(R)+v is M♮-convex with top P and bottom Q. For preservation under compatible lifts, view the common box lift as induction through a fixed linking set Γ_{ϕ,v}(Ψ). Since P ↠ Q, Lemma 3.12 gives L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(P) ↠ L^Ψ_{ϕ,v}(Q). If Ψ=[0,1]^V, these lifts are matroids, so the quotient is a classical matroid quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P⊆ℤ^U be an M-convex set, and let P^tr_k be its k-th truncation. If M,N⊆ℤ^V are compatible matroid lifts of P and P^tr_k, respectively, then N equals the matroid-theoretic k-th truncation of M, namely N=M^tr_k∩ℤ^V_{≥0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because P ↠ P^tr_k, compatible lifts give M ↠ N, so N is contained in the M-convex truncation M^tr_k and, being a matroid, in the nonnegative orthant. Conversely, if x∈M^tr_k∩ℤ^V_{≥0}, then x≤y for some y∈M with rank lowered by k. Applying π_ϕ and the common translation v gives π_ϕ(x)+v≤π_ϕ(y)+v∈P, hence π_ϕ(x)+v lies in P^tr_k. By the definition of the common box lift, x∈N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12 and Proposition 4.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For matroids M,N on the same finite set V, viewed as M-convex subsets of {0,1}^V, the classical flag-matroid polytope criterion says that M ↠ N if and only if the vertices of M+N form a subset of the vertices of the Minkowski sum of hypersimplices Δ(rk(M),V)+Δ(rk(N),V). More generally, an M-convex set R⊆ℤ^U is a Minkowski sum P+Q of two M-convex sets with P ↠ Q if and only if R is a flag M-convex set of the corresponding two-rank type: after an integral translation, R is the image under a coordinate-summing projection π_ϕ of a generalized permutohedron whose vertices are among those of a sum of two hypersimplices with ranks equal to compatible matroid-lift ranks of P and Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use compatible matroid lifts M,N of P,Q. By Propositions 4.7 and 4.10, P ↠ Q is equivalent to M ↠ N for every compatible lift. The matroid criterion of Borovik-Gelfand-White identifies M ↠ N with the vertex condition for M+N inside the sum of two hypersimplices. Projection commutes with Minkowski sum, and the translations in the lift only translate the final M-convex set. Thus P+Q is precisely a projected flag-matroid polytope, which is the definition of a flag M-convex set of the appropriate type.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} have nonempty bounded effective domain dom(f)={x:f(x)<∞}. For u∈(ℝ^E)^*, define the minimizer set f^u={x∈ℤ^E: f(x)-⟨u,x⟩≤f(y)-⟨u,y⟩ for all y∈dom(f)}. Then f is an M-convex function if and only if every f^u is an M-convex set. Similarly, f is an M♮-convex function if and only if every f^u is an M♮-convex set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Murota. One direction is obtained by applying the exchange axiom for f to minimizers of the linear perturbation f-⟨u,·⟩; the exchange inequality forces the exchanged points to be minimizers as well. Conversely, if an exchange inequality for f failed, a separating linear functional u can be chosen so that the offending points become minimizers while all exchange candidates are more expensive, producing a minimizer set that violates the corresponding M-convex or M♮-convex exchange axiom.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f,g:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} be M-convex functions with rk(g)<rk(f). Consider four conditions. (A) There exists an M♮-convex function h:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} whose top layer is f and whose bottom layer is g. (B) There exists a linking function γ:ℤ^E×ℤ^{Ē}→ℝ∪{∞}, meaning an M-convex function on the product lattice, and an M-convex function r:ℤ^{Ē}→ℝ∪{∞}, such that f is the top layer of the left function π_E(γ) and g=ind_γ(r), where ind_γ(r)(x)=inf_y(γ(x,-y)+r(y)). (C) For every x∈dom(f), y∈dom(g), and i∈supp+(y-x), there exists j∈supp−(y-x) such that f(x)+g(y)≥f(x+e_i-e_j)+g(y-e_i+e_j). (D) For every u∈(ℝ^E)^*, the minimizer sets f^u and g^u form a quotient of M-convex sets, f^u ↠ g^u. Then (A) implies (B), (B) implies (C), and (C) implies (D). If rk(f)=rk(g)+1, all four conditions are equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (A)⇒(B), the proof uses a one-coordinate lift of h as a linking function γ and a singleton M-convex function r supported at the rank difference, with the auxiliary sign chosen so that induction selects the bottom layer; the left function of γ has top layer f. For (B)⇒(C), choose z,w with f(x)=γ(x,w) and g(y)=γ(y,-z)+r(z). Apply the M-convex exchange inequality to the two points (x,w) and (y,-z) of dom(γ). If the exchanged coordinate j lies in E, the desired inequality follows after adding r(z) and taking the definitions of f and g. If j lies in the auxiliary set, then x would move to an E-coordinate sum larger than the top layer of π_E(γ), impossible; hence j must lie in E. For (C)⇒(D), take x∈f^u and y∈g^u. The exchange inequality in (C), after subtracting the same linear terms ⟨u,x⟩ and ⟨u,y⟩ on both sides, shows that the exchanged points are also minimizers; hence f^u and g^u satisfy the asymmetric exchange criterion of Theorem 1.2. For the elementary case rk(f)=rk(g)+1, assume (D) and define h to equal f on dom(f), g on dom(g), and ∞ elsewhere. For every u, the minimizer h^u is either f^u, or g^u, or f^u∪g^u; because f^u ↠ g^u and the ranks differ by one, this union is M♮-convex. The minimizer characterization Theorem 5.2 then gives that h is M♮-convex, establishing (A).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For M-convex functions f,g,h:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} with rk(h)<rk(g)<rk(f), define f ↠_min g to mean that f^u ↠ g^u for every linear functional u. If f ↠_min g and g ↠_min h, then f ↠_min h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each u, the minimizer sets are M-convex and satisfy f^u ↠ g^u and g^u ↠ h^u. Quotients of M-convex sets are transitive by Lemma 2.38, so f^u ↠ h^u for every u. This is exactly f ↠_min h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the special case of valuated matroids, conditions (C) and (D) of Theorem 1.3 are equivalent. Here valuated matroids are M-convex functions whose domains lie in the unit hypercube {0,1}^E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Brandt-Eur-Zhang. If f^u ↠ g^u for all u, then by the flag-matroid polytope criterion the Minkowski sum f^u+g^u is the set of lattice points of a flag matroid polytope for every u. Brandt-Eur-Zhang's theorem identifies this subdivision-by-minimizers condition with the valuated quotient exchange condition f ↠_Ex g, which is condition (C). The implication (C)⇒(D) is already part of Theorem 1.3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_0,f_1,...,f_k:ℤ^E→ℝ∪{∞} be M-convex functions with rk(f_i)=rk(f_0)+i and f_i ↠_Ex f_{i-1} for each i=1,...,k, where ↠_Ex denotes the exchange condition (C) of Theorem 1.3. Assume P=⋃_{i=0}^k dom(f_i) is an M♮-convex set. Then there exist constants c_0,...,c_k∈ℝ such that h(x)=inf_i(f_i(x)-c_i) is an M♮-convex function whose layers are f_i-c_i. The paper gives an explicit recursive choice: take c_0=c_1=0, and for m≥2 choose c_m as the minimum of f_m(x)-f_{m-1}(x-e_j)+c_{m-1}-f_{l+1}(y+e_j)+c_{l+1}+f_l(y)-c_l over y∈dom(f_l), 0≤l≤m-2, x∈dom(f_m), and j∈supp−(y-x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on k. The case k=1 is the elementary equivalence in Theorem 1.3. Assume h_k=inf_{i≤k}(f_i-c_i) is already M♮-convex. Since P is M♮-convex, its first k layers are also M♮-convex after plank intersection. Define c_{k+1} using the displayed minimum, which is finite because P is bounded and the relevant exchange supports are nonempty. The M-convex exchange condition within a single layer and the already-known M♮ inequalities for h_k handle all pairs not involving the new top layer. For x in the new top layer and y in a lower layer, the choice of c_{k+1} forces h_{k+1}(x)+h_{k+1}(y)≥h_{k+1}(x-e_j)+h_{k+1}(y+e_j) for a suitable j, exactly the missing M♮ exchange inequality. Thus h_{k+1} is M♮-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f,g be sparse paving valuated matroids. This means f and g are valuated matroids and their minimizer matroids argmin(f) and argmin(g) are sparse paving matroids. If f ↠_min g, then there exists an M♮-convex function h:{0,1}^E→ℝ∪{∞} with h↑=f and h↓=g. Hence, for sparse paving valuated matroids, the conditions in Theorem 1.3 are equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let M=argmin(f), N=argmin(g), and let c_f,c_g be the minimum values of f and g. Define h to be f on the top rank, g on the bottom rank, the constant min(c_f,c_g) on all intermediate hypercube layers, and ∞ outside these layers. Each layer is M-convex. To verify the M♮ exchange inequality, suppose an inequality failed for x,y. If x is not in the top layer, then failure for two distinct exchange coordinates would force two nonbases of N whose coordinatewise minimum has rank greater than rk(N)-2, contradicting sparse paving; the bottom/top symmetric case is analogous for M. The one-coordinate case is tautological. Thus h is M♮-convex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.13, Proposition 5.15, and Theorem 5.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For M-convex functions f_0,...,f_k with finite support and any u∈(ℝ^E)^*, the minimizer of their convolution satisfies (f_0□...□f_k)^u=f_0^u+...+f_k^u, where □ denotes infimal convolution. If f,g,h have support in the nonnegative orthant and g ↠_min f and h ↠_min f, then g□h ↠_min f. More generally, if f_0,...,f_k are M-convex functions with f_{i+1} ↠_min f_i for all i, then for every u the minimizer (f_0□...□f_k)^u is a flag M-convex set of the rank type determined by rk(f_0),...,rk(f_k), up to a common lift shift and coordinate-summing projection.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The minimizer formula follows from the fact that exposed faces of a Minkowski sum are sums of exposed faces; applying this to the convex hulls of epigraphs gives minimizers of infimal convolution. For Proposition 5.15, fix u. Then (g□h)^u=g^u+h^u. Because supports lie in the nonnegative orthant, the submodular function of h^u is nondecreasing; adding such an M-convex set to g^u preserves the quotient relation to f^u by Lemma 5.14. For Theorem 5.16, apply the minimizer formula to get a sum of a flag of M-convex sets f_i^u. The finite-set compression criterion, Proposition 4.14, then identifies this sum as a flag M-convex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q⊆ℝ^E be generalized permutohedra with corresponding real-valued submodular functions p,q. The following are equivalent, giving the nonintegral analogue of an M-convex quotient. (1) q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) for all X⊆Y⊆E. (2) For every permutation σ, the greedy vertices x_σ∈P and y_σ∈Q satisfy x_σ≥y_σ. (3) For every X⊆E, S(q/X)⊆S(p/X). (4) G(p,q#) is a generalized polymatroid with top face P and bottom face Q. (5) There exists a generalized permutohedron R⊆ℝ^{E⊔X}, with X chosen as a singleton if desired, such that P=R∖X and Q=R/X. (6) For all x∈Q, y∈P, and i∈supp+(x-y), there exist j∈supp−(x-y) and ρ_0>0 such that x-ρ(e_i-e_j)∈Q and y+ρ(e_i-e_j)∈P for all ρ∈[0,ρ_0]. (7) The polyhedron (B(p)+ℝ^E_{≤0})∩(B(q)+ℝ^E_{≥0}) has only edge directions e_i-e_j and e_i. (8) There exists a generalized permutohedron Γ⊆ℝ^E×ℝ^{Ē} and a generalized permutohedron W⊆ℝ^{Ē} such that P is the top layer of the left projection of Γ and Q=ind_Γ(W). (9) There are linking permutohedra Γ,Δ⊆ℝ^E×ℝ^E with P,Q as top layers of their left sets and Γ⪯_RΔ in Green's right preorder on the monoid of linking permutohedra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states that the proofs are parallel to the integral case. The vertex criterion uses the same greedy-coordinate formula, now with real values. The contraction/submodular-polyhedron containment equivalence is algebraic. The top-bottom and deletion-contraction equivalences use the continuous generalized-polymatroid theory: generalized polymatroids are precisely projections of generalized permutohedra, and their top and bottom faces are the base polyhedra B(p) and B(q). The exchange condition is replaced by Murota's continuous exchange property for base polyhedra, allowing motion by a small real amount ρ in direction e_i-e_j. The edge-direction criterion characterizes generalized polymatroids by allowable edge directions. Induction and Green-preorder arguments repeat the linking-set proofs with generalized permutohedra and possibly unbounded linking permutohedra.

proof_techniques.name: Encode quotients as top and bottom layers
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly converts a quotient P ↠ Q into a single M♮-convex object R whose top layer is P and bottom layer is Q. Polyhedrally, R is the generalized polymatroid G(p,q#), the intersection of the submodular polyhedron above Q and below P. This packages all quotient inequalities into one convex-geometric object and lets one use layer operations, plank intersections, and projection/lifting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If elements of the relative Dressian are represented by valuated matroid quotients, a candidate involution might be studied by asking how it acts on the associated top-bottom object. For the flat subposet, this technique suggests identifying F↦F^⊥ with a layer-reversal or dual-supermodular operation on the corresponding generalized polymatroid.

proof_techniques.name: Use minimizers to test valuated quotient relations
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex functions, especially valuated matroids, quotient conditions can be checked on all linear minimizer sets. The paper proves that the exchange quotient condition implies every pair of minimizers is an M-convex quotient, and for valuated matroids it records the converse via Brandt-Eur-Zhang. Thus a valuated quotient is controlled by the matroid quotients appearing in every cell of its regular subdivision.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Tropical linear spaces are represented by valuated matroids, so an order-reversing operation on a relative Dressian could be tested cellwise on all initial matroids. This may reduce extension of F↦F^⊥ to verifying compatibility with minimizer matroid quotients under all weights.

proof_techniques.name: Greedy vertex inequalities
proof_techniques.description: For M-convex sets, the quotient inequality q(Y)-q(X)≤p(Y)-p(X) is equivalent to coordinatewise comparison x_σ≥y_σ of all greedy vertices indexed by permutations. This transforms a universal family of subset inequalities into a finite family of vertex comparisons along maximal chains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a relative Dressian element or its underlying matroid quotient can be described by rank functions, an attempted involution can be checked against chain/flag data rather than all subsets. It may also give finite obstructions to the existence of a global order-reversing map extending the flat involution.

proof_techniques.name: Induction through linking sets and linking functions
proof_techniques.description: The paper generalizes induction by bipartite graphs to M-convex sets and M-convex functions. A linking set Γ⊆ℤ^V×ℤ^U or linking function γ acts on an M-convex set/function W by selecting x for which (x,-w) lies in Γ, or by infimizing γ(x,-w)+r(w). Quotients are exactly those objects obtainable as the top left layer of a linking set together with an induction output.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to model morphisms or inclusions of tropical linear spaces as combinatorial/tropical linear maps. A putative duality on Dr(M) might be constructed by transposing linking data, while failure of such a transposition to preserve the required Green preorder could obstruct an involution.

proof_techniques.name: Green preorder of the linking-set monoid
proof_techniques.description: Linking sets from a ground set to itself form a monoid under relational product. The paper identifies quotient relations with Green's right preorder on this monoid: Γ⪯_RΔ forces the top left set of Γ to quotient the top left set of Δ, and every quotient arises in this way. This imports semigroup order tools into quotient theory.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on a relative Dressian might correspond to an anti-automorphism of an appropriate linking monoid, such as transposition turning right preorder into left preorder. The paper also notes the monoid is not regular for |V|>2, signaling that naive inverse-based constructions may not be available.

proof_techniques.name: Compatible matroid lifts
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs matroid lifts of arbitrary bounded M-convex sets by splitting each coordinate into unit intervals and using a coordinate-summing projection. Quotients are preserved under compatible box lifts, and if lifted matroids form quotients then their projections form quotients. This allows general M-convex quotient questions to be reduced to classical matroid quotients when the lifts are chosen compatibly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem starts with an ordinary matroid M but Dr(M) involves valuated/tropical objects, compatible lifts offer a route for reducing statements about generalized quotient data to classical matroid quotient and flat-lattice statements.

proof_techniques.name: Flag polytope compression
proof_techniques.description: A pair P ↠ Q can be encoded by the Minkowski sum P+Q: after compatible matroid lifting, this sum is a projection of a flag matroid polytope, equivalently a flag M-convex set. For M-convex functions, convolution has minimizers equal to sums of minimizers, so valuated quotient flags yield subdivisions whose cells are flag M-convex sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Relative Dressian elements are naturally polyhedral objects. This technique suggests looking for an order-reversing operation as a duality on subdivisions of flag polytopes, or showing nonexistence by detecting that no such subdivision duality can extend the prescribed flat duality.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical quotients from minors and truncations
proof_techniques.description: Projection of an M-convex set to a subset of coordinates is M♮-convex, and its layers are basic minors. Higher layers quotient lower layers. Truncation is the maximal quotient of a fixed rank drop: every quotient of that rank is contained in the corresponding truncation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of the opposite flat lattice into a relative Dressian via contractions resembles the paper's quotient-from-minor construction. Truncation maximality may provide canonical bounds on where an order-reversing extension could send elements.

proof_techniques.name: Layer-shifting for flags of M-convex functions
proof_techniques.description: Pairwise elementary exchange quotients f_i ↠_Ex f_{i-1} do not automatically assemble into one M♮-convex function, because relative additive constants between layers matter. Theorem 1.4 shows that if the union of supports is M♮-convex, constants can be chosen recursively so that the shifted functions become the layers of an M♮-convex function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If constructing Φ requires extending behavior from flats or elementary quotients to longer chains in Dr(M), this theorem suggests that layer constants or valuations may be adjustable, but only under a support-level M♮-convexity condition.

proof_techniques.name: Continuous generalized-permutohedron relaxation
proof_techniques.description: The final section drops integrality and proves the same quotient characterizations for generalized permutohedra, replacing discrete exchange by a local continuous exchange direction. The quotient cone becomes a deformation cone of a projected permutohedron.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the relative Dressian is studied as a fan or polyhedral moduli space, the continuous relaxation may reveal linear or cone-theoretic obstructions to an order-reversing involution before imposing integrality or valuated-matroid realizability.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly cites Haque 2012 and Brandt-Eur-Zhang 2021 for quotients of valuated matroids and tropical flag varieties; Remark 5.7 uses Brandt-Eur-Zhang's theorem equating valuated quotient exchange with flag-matroid subdivision data. Remark 5.8 notes that a deletion-contraction factorization for quotient-like morphisms is subtle beyond ordinary matroids and is not fully settled for valuated matroids; it points to Jarra-Lorscheid 2024 for matroids with coefficients. Remark 2.43 relates top-bottom quotient data to delta-matroids and jump systems, with saturated delta-matroids corresponding to generalized matroids; this may be relevant if an involution naturally acts on nonconsecutive layers. Question 4.16 asks for a more direct intrinsic description of flag M-convex polytopes, indicating that the current compression characterization depends on projections of flag matroid polytopes. Remark 6.2 observes that the space of quotients of generalized permutohedra is the deformation cone of an orthogonal projection of the regular permutohedron; M-convex quotients are its integer points and matroid quotients its 0/1 points.
why_relevant: Unifies quotients of matroids, valuated matroids, polymatroids, and M-convex functions. Since Dr(M) is naturally a quotient poset of valuated matroids, its equivalent quotient criteria are likely useful for constructing or obstructing an order-reversing involution.

title: Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces
overall_summary: The paper studies relative Dressians, especially Dr1(µ), the space of codimension-1 tropical linear subspaces of a fixed tropical linear space Trop µ, by identifying such subspaces with elementary valuated matroid quotients. Its central structural result is that Dr1(µ) is cut out by explicit three-term tropical incidence equations, and in the unvaluated case Dr1_s(M) is the order-complex fan of the elementary quotient lattice cQt1(M), equivalently the lattice of linear subclasses of M. The paper also introduces adjoints of valuated matroids, generalizing adjoints of ordinary matroids, and proves that adjoints are exactly rank-d tropical linear spaces contained in Dr1_s(µ) whose circuits are the concurrent-hyperplane incidence equations. For the original problem, the most relevant material is the quotient-poset interpretation of relative Dressians, the precise description of elementary layers Dr1(M), and the adjoint/order-reversing embedding results, since an order-reversing involution on a Dressian would have to interact strongly with these elementary quotient and adjoint structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.2 quotient-containment dictionary for tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a valuated matroid of rank d on [n], and let Trop µ be its tropical linear space in tropical projective space PT[n]. A valuated matroid θ of rank s on [n] is called a quotient of µ, denoted µ ↠ θ, when the tropical incidence-Pluecker relations between µ and θ hold. In the elementary case s = d - 1, these relations are: for every I in C([n], d - 2) and every J in C([n], d + 1), the minimum min over i in J \ I of θ(I ∪ {i}) + µ(J \ {i}) is attained at least twice. The paper uses the standard equivalence that Trop θ is contained in Trop µ if and only if µ ↠ θ. Thus Dr_k(µ), the set of codimension-k tropical linear subspaces of Trop µ, is the set of equivalence classes of rank d - k valuated matroid quotients of µ, modulo scalar addition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is reviewed as background from tropical linear space theory and tropical flag varieties. The valuated circuits of µ define tropical hyperplanes whose intersection is Trop µ. The incidence-Pluecker relations are the tropicalization of the classical incidence equations for nested linear spaces, and they are equivalent to every valuated circuit condition needed for Trop θ to lie in Trop µ. In the elementary case, the displayed relations are exactly the tropical incidence equations between rank d and rank d - 1 Pluecker coordinates; these are the relations exploited throughout Section 4 to describe Dr1(µ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 elementary quotients, linear subclasses, and modular cuts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on [n], with lattice of flats L(M), hyperplanes L1(M), and elementary quotient set Qt1(M). A quotient N of M is a matroid on [n] with L(N) contained in L(M); it is elementary if rk M - rk N = 1. A subset H of L1(M) is a linear subclass if whenever Hi and Hj in H cover Hi ∩ Hj, every hyperplane covering Hi ∩ Hj is also in H. For an elementary quotient Q of M, H = L1(Q) ∩ L1(M) is a nontrivial linear subclass of M, and conversely every nontrivial linear subclass arises from a unique elementary quotient. If cQt1(M) = Qt1(M) ∪ {M}, then Q ↦ L1(Q) ∩ L1(M) is a bijection from cQt1(M) to all linear subclasses of M. Under reverse inclusion of linear subclasses, this makes cQt1(M) into a lattice; M corresponds to the full linear subclass L1(M), and the truncation Tr M corresponds to the empty linear subclass. Equivalently, elementary quotients can also be encoded by modular cuts in L(M): the collar of a modular cut is L(M) \ L(Q), and the intersection of the modular cut with L1(M) is the associated linear subclass.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the standard matroid quotient theory recalled by the paper. If Q is an elementary quotient, then the rank d - 2 flats common to M and Q force the hyperplanes common to M and Q to be closed under the stated coline rule, giving a linear subclass. Conversely, given such a subclass H, one constructs the unique elementary quotient by modifying the rank function or lattice of flats so that precisely the hyperplanes in H remain hyperplanes from M. For modular cuts, the nontrivial modular cut F determines its collar R, the flats just below F; removing the collar from L(M) gives L(Q). The equivalence between modular cuts and linear subclasses follows because a modular cut is generated by its hyperplane elements, i.e. by joins of elements of the linear subclass.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12 and Theorem A
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let φ: Δ_n^d -> R ∪ {∞} and ψ: Δ_n^{d-1} -> R ∪ {∞} be M-convex functions. Then φ ↠ ψ is an elementary quotient of M-convex functions if and only if, for every 0 < q <= 1, the Lorentzian basis generating polynomials f_q^ψ and f_q^φ satisfy f_q^ψ ≪_L f_q^φ, meaning f_q^φ + w_{n+1} f_q^ψ is Lorentzian. Specializing to valuated matroids, if µ and θ are valuated matroids of ranks d and d - 1 on [n], respectively, then µ ↠ θ if and only if f_q^θ ≪_L f_q^µ for all 0 < q <= 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is Lemma 3.6, which says that φ ↠ ψ is an elementary quotient exactly when the function φ-hat on Δ_{n+1}^d defined by φ-hat(x,0) = φ(x), φ-hat(x,1) = ψ(x), and φ-hat = ∞ otherwise is M-convex. The basis generating polynomial of φ-hat is precisely f_q^φ + w_{n+1} f_q^ψ. Branden-Huh's characterization of M-convex functions says that a function is M-convex if and only if its q-basis generating polynomial is Lorentzian for every 0 < q <= 1. Applying this to φ-hat gives the equivalence. For valuated matroids, M-convex quotients restrict exactly to the tropical incidence-Pluecker quotient relation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.17 and Corollary 3.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a degree-d Lorentzian polynomial over the real Puiseux field K. The set L[f ≪_L]_K = {g in L_{n}^{d+1}(K) such that f ≪_L g} ∪ {0} is a convex cone. Consequently, if φ is an M-convex function, then the sets F^1(φ) = {ψ of rank d - 1 such that φ ↠ ψ} and 1F(φ) = {ρ of rank d + 1 such that ρ ↠ φ} are tropically convex. In particular, for every valuated matroid µ, Dr1(µ), the space of codimension-1 tropical linear subspaces of Trop µ, and 1Dr(µ), the space of codimension-1 tropical linear superspaces of Trop µ, are tropically convex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, over the real numbers, Proposition 1.3 proves convexity of L[f ≪_L] using the support criterion for Lorentzian proper position, ordinary stable-polynomial proper position after differentiating down to degree two, and a full-support perturbation by ℓ f. Theorem 3.17 transfers this convexity to K by Tarski's principle, because convexity is first-order over real closed fields. Tropicalization sends nonnegative K-linear combinations to tropical minima of valuations: val(c1 g1 + c2 g2) = min(val(c1) + val(g1), val(c2) + val(g2)). Proposition 3.13 identifies tropicalizations of Lorentzian proper-position pairs with elementary quotients of M-convex functions. This proves tropical convexity of 1F(φ). The lower quotient set F^1(φ) follows by the inversion x ↦ D - x on a sufficiently large discrete simplex. The valuated matroid statements are the restrictions to supports in the unit cube.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.20
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M1, ..., Mk be matroids on [n]. If they are perspective, meaning they have a common elementary quotient, or coperspective, meaning they are all elementary quotients of a common matroid, then B(M1) ∪ ... ∪ B(Mk) is the set of bases of another matroid Mk+1. Moreover, M1, ..., Mk, Mk+1 are again perspective, respectively coperspective.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Corollary 3.18 to the trivial valuations of the matroids. Tropical sum of trivial valuations is the pointwise minimum, and the support of min(µ1, µ2) is the union of the supports. Since the appropriate quotient set is tropically convex, the pointwise minimum remains a valuated matroid quotient in the same incidence relation. Inducting over k gives a matroid whose bases are the union of the original bases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a rank-d valuated matroid on [n], and let θ: C([n], d - 1) -> R ∪ {∞} be any function satisfying the incidence relations defining Dr1(µ). Then supp(θ), the collection of (d - 1)-subsets on which θ is finite, is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof verifies the basis exchange axiom for supp(θ). Take A and B in supp(θ). Both are independent in the underlying matroid M of µ. If cl_M(A) = cl_M(B), then ordinary basis exchange inside the hyperplane cl_M(A), together with the parallel-hyperplane incidence relations, shows that for every i in A \ B there is j in B \ A with A - i + j still in supp(θ). If cl_M(A) is different from cl_M(B), choose j in B \ A such that A ∪ {j} is a basis of M, and write A = D ∪ {i}. If there exists k in B \ A with D ∪ {j,k} a basis of M, then the incidence relation indexed by (D, D ∪ {i,j,k}) forces at least one of A - i + j or A - i + k to lie in supp(θ). If no such k exists, then all relevant k lie in cl_M(D ∪ {j}), so cl_M(B) = cl_M(D ∪ {j}); the same-hyperplane case then gives D ∪ {j} in supp(θ). Thus the exchange axiom holds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a valuated matroid of rank d on [n]. The tropical incidence relations between a function θ on C([n], d - 1) and µ suffice to cut out Dr1(µ). In other words, if θ satisfies all elementary incidence relations with µ, then θ is automatically a valuated matroid of rank d - 1 and µ ↠ θ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 4.1, the support of θ is the basis set of an ordinary matroid. A theorem of Murota then reduces checking that θ is a valuated matroid to checking the three-term tropical Pluecker relations. For each S of size d - 3 and distinct i,j,k,l outside S, one must show tropical vanishing of min{θ(Sij)+θ(Skl), θ(Sik)+θ(Sjl), θ(Sil)+θ(Sjk)}. If rk_M(Sijkl) = d - 1, choose m extending Sijkl to rank d and use four incidence relations involving m to rewrite the three θ-sums in terms of a Pluecker relation for µ; subtracting a finite common term gives the desired vanishing. If rk_M(Sijkl) = d, analyze how many of µ(Sijk), µ(Sijl), µ(Sikl), µ(Sjkl) are finite. When all are finite this is the known uniform-support argument; when exactly one is infinite, an affine re-normalization makes the finite µ-values zero and the incidence relations force the θ-Pluecker relation; when two are infinite, one θ-term is infinite and the remaining incidence relations give the result directly.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a valuated matroid of rank d on [n], with underlying matroid M. Then Dr1(µ) is cut out by the following three-term incidence relations. First, if A in C([n], d - 1) is dependent in M, then θ(A) = ∞. Second, if A and B are independent (d - 1)-sets with |A \ B| = 1 and cl_M(A) = cl_M(B), then for any k such that A ∪ {k} and B ∪ {k} are bases of M, θ(A) + µ(B ∪ {k}) = θ(B) + µ(A ∪ {k}); the difference is independent of k. Third, if D has size d - 2 and i,j,k are distinct elements outside D such that D ∪ {i}, D ∪ {j}, and D ∪ {k} are independent, then min{θ(Di)+µ(Djk), θ(Dj)+µ(Dik), θ(Dk)+µ(Dij)} is attained at least twice. These relations imply all elementary incidence relations and hence, by Theorem 4.2, define exactly Dr1(µ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The displayed three classes are immediate consequences of the full incidence relations: dependent hyperplanes are forced to have θ-value ∞ by using a circuit inside A and a basis extending it; parallel hyperplanes give two-term incidence equations; concurrent hyperplanes are exactly the three-term incidence equations indexed by (D, Dijk). For the converse, take an arbitrary incidence relation indexed by A in C([n], d - 2) and D in C([n], d + 1), and write its finite terms as min{θ(A1)+µ(D-1), ..., θ(Am)+µ(D-m)}. Suppose this minimum has a unique minimizer, say the term with 1. Pick k1 in D - 1 so that A1k1 is a basis of M. The Pluecker relation for µ indexed by (Ak1, D) yields k2 with µ(A1k1)-µ(Ak1k2) >= µ(D-k2)-µ(D-1). The assumed unique minimum then gives a strict inequality after adding θ(Ak2)-θ(A1). The three-term relation for (A, A1k1k2) forces θ(Ak1)+µ(A1k2) to be finite and strictly smaller in the corresponding way. Repeating this construction gives a sequence k1,k2,... in the finite set D; once an index repeats, summing the resulting strict inequalities around the cycle gives a strict inequality between two equal finite sums, a contradiction. Thus every incidence relation tropically vanishes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.2 simplified relative Dressian Dr1_s(µ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a rank-d valuated matroid with underlying matroid M. Choose, for each hyperplane H in L1(M), a basis D_H in C([n], d - 1) of H. The degenerate and parallel relations from Theorem 4.3 imply that a point θ in Dr1(µ) is uniquely determined by the values θ(D_H), one for each H in L1(M). The resulting space Dr1_s(µ) is a tropical prevariety in PT^{L1(M)} isomorphic, as a polyhedral complex, to Dr1(µ). It is cut out by tropical equations indexed by concurrent triples of hyperplanes, where a concurrent triple is a set of three distinct hyperplanes meeting in a common corank-2 flat.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The degenerate relations remove all dependent (d - 1)-sets. If A and B are bases of the same hyperplane H, a chain of basis exchanges inside H and the parallel-hyperplane relations determine θ(A) - θ(B) purely from µ, so specifying θ on one chosen basis D_H determines θ on every basis spanning H. Substituting these determined values into the concurrent three-term relations gives equations in the coordinates indexed by H in L1(M). Because the reconstruction from Dr1_s(µ) to Dr1(µ) is affine on coordinates and inverse to the restriction map, the two spaces are isomorphic as polyhedral complexes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank d, and let Dr1_s(M)^o = Dr1_s(M) ∩ T^{L1(M)} be the dense torus part of the simplified codimension-1 relative Dressian of the Bergman fan Trop M. For every chain of linear subclasses ∅ ⊊ H1 ⊊ ... ⊊ Hk ⊊ L1(M), form the cone cone(e_{H1}, ..., e_{Hk}) + R1 in R^{L1(M)}, where e_H is the indicator vector of the subset H of hyperplanes. The collection of these cones is a simplicial fan, and its support is Dr1_s(M)^o. Equivalently, Dr1_s(M) is the order-complex compactification associated with the lattice cQt1(M) of elementary quotients, via the bijection between elementary quotients and linear subclasses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If w = a1 e_{H1} + ... + ak e_{Hk} with ai >= 0 along a chain of linear subclasses, then every concurrent triple of hyperplanes has one of the membership patterns forced by the linear-subclass axiom: none of the triple appears yet, one appears before the other two, or all three enter at the same stage after possibly one earlier element. In each case the minimum in the tropical equation for that concurrent triple is achieved at least twice, so w lies in Dr1_s(M)^o. Conversely, take w in Dr1_s(M)^o, translate so all coordinates are nonnegative and at least one is zero or positive as needed, and write w uniquely as a positive linear combination of indicator vectors of its strict upper level sets G1 ⊊ ... ⊊ Gk. The tropical equation for each concurrent triple implies that if two hyperplanes of the triple lie in Gi, then the third also lies in Gi. Hence every Gi is a linear subclass. The relative interiors of these cones are disjoint by uniqueness of the level-set decomposition, so the cones form a simplicial fan with the stated support.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If M has rank d, then Dr1_s(M) has cones of dimension d - 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof embeds L(M) into cQt1(M) by sending a flat F to the principal truncation of M by F. In terms of linear subclasses, this is the set of hyperplanes containing F; in terms of modular cuts, it is the upper interval of F. If F covers G in L(M), then no modular cut strictly between the upper interval of F and the upper interval of G exists: any extra hyperplane H containing G but not F satisfies F ∧ H = G and the modularity equality needed to force G into the cut. Hence maximal chains in L(M) map to maximal chains in cQt1(M). Since maximal chains in L(M) have length d, the corresponding cones in the projectivized fan have dimension d - 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Identify Qt1(M) with the subset of Dr1_s(M) consisting of trivial valuations on elementary quotients of M. Then Dr1_s(M) is the tropical convex hull, or tropical polytope, generated by the join-irreducible elements of the lattice Qt1(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A point θ in Dr1_s(M)^o lies in a cone from Theorem 4.7, so after adding a scalar it has the form θ = sum_i a_i e_{H_i} for a chain ∅ ⊊ H1 ⊊ ... ⊊ Hk of linear subclasses and positive ai. This can be rewritten as a tropical minimum of shifted trivial valuations δ_{H_i^c}: θ = min{sum_i a_i + δ_{∅^c}, sum_{i>=2} a_i + δ_{H1^c}, ..., δ_{Hk^c}}. Thus θ lies in the tropical span of all elementary quotient points. In the elementary quotient lattice, tropical sum of trivial valuations agrees with the join operation. Since every lattice element is a join of join-irreducible elements, the tropical span of the join-irreducibles already contains every elementary quotient point, and hence the closure Dr1_s(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and W be simple matroids of the same rank d. The following are equivalent. (1) The ground set of W is L1(M), and every concurrent triple of hyperplanes of M is a circuit of W. (2) There is an order-reversing embedding L(W) -> cQt1(M) that maps the atoms L1(W) onto L1(M). (3) The ground set of W is L1(M), and Trop W is contained in Dr1_s(M). (4) W is an adjoint of M, i.e. a rank-d matroid on L1(M) whose lattice of flats realizes the classical adjoint order-dual incidence of L(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between (2) and (4) is cited from Bachem-Kern and Cheung's embedding theorem for adjoints. To prove (1) implies (2), define for a flat F of W the subset of hyperplanes H in L1(M) with H <=_W F; because concurrent triples are circuits of W, this subset is already a linear subclass of M. Sending F to this linear subclass gives an order-reversing injective map into cQt1(M), and atoms go to the corresponding hyperplanes. The implication (2) implies (3) follows from Theorem 4.7: Trop W is the order complex of L(W), and the order-reversing embedding sends its cones into the order-complex fan Dr1_s(M). Finally, if Trop W is contained in Dr1_s(M), every concurrent triple equation of Dr1_s(M) makes that triple dependent in W; since W is simple, a dependent triple is a circuit, proving (1).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be an adjoint of a rank-d matroid M. Then: (1) if B is a basis of M, the d hyperplanes cl_M(B \ {i}) for i in B form a basis of W; (2) for every flat F of W, the set of flats of M lying below F under the adjoint incidence embedding is a modular cut of M; (3) if M is modular, then W is unique and there are lattice isomorphisms L(M) ≅ cQt1(M) ≅ L(W)^op, and in particular Dr1_s(M) ≅ Trop M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records these as known properties of adjoints, citing the adjoint literature. Part (1) is the combinatorial analogue of the fact that the coordinate hyperplanes opposite a basis of a projective space form a basis in the dual geometry. Part (2) is Cheung's modular-cut interpretation of the adjoint embedding: lower sets in L(W), pulled back to flats of M, satisfy the modular-cut closure condition. For (3), modularity of L(M) forces all relevant modular cuts or linear subclasses to be principal, so the elementary quotient lattice is exactly the order dual of L(M); uniqueness of W and the identification of the order-complex fan with Trop M follow.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5.1 and Propositions 5.2-5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a rank-d valuated matroid on [n] with underlying matroid M. A simplified adjoint of µ is a rank-d valuated matroid Σ_s on ground set L1(M), with simple underlying matroid, such that Trop Σ_s is contained in Dr1_s(µ). Equivalently, for a valuated matroid Σ_s on L1(M) with simple underlying matroid W, the following are equivalent: (1) Σ_s is an adjoint of µ; (2) Trop Σ_s ⊂ Dr1_s(µ) and W is an adjoint of M; (3) Σ_s has rank d and every defining equation of Dr1_s(µ) is a valuated circuit of Σ_s. There is also an unsimplified version: a rank-d valuated matroid Σ on the ground set C([n], d - 1) is an adjoint of µ if Trop Σ ⊂ Dr1(µ) and its simplification to L1(M) has simple underlying matroid. Equivalently, its underlying simplification is an adjoint of M, or, equivalently, every three-term incidence relation defining Dr1(µ) is a valuated circuit of Σ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the simplified version, rec(Trop Σ_s) = Trop W. Since Trop Σ_s ⊂ Dr1_s(µ), taking recession spaces gives Trop W ⊂ rec Dr1_s(µ) ⊂ Dr1_s(M). Proposition 4.12 then forces W to be an adjoint of M. Conversely, if W is an adjoint and Trop Σ_s lies in Dr1_s(µ), then the concurrent triples of M are circuits of W; the defining equations of Dr1_s(µ) must therefore be valuated circuits of Σ_s. The unsimplified statement is obtained by using the parallel classes of bases of hyperplanes: dependent (d - 1)-sets become loops, bases of the same hyperplane become parallel, and simplifying Σ recovers Σ_s.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4 cofactor formulas for valuated adjoints
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a rank-d valuated matroid on [n], and let Σ be an unsimplified adjoint of µ, a rank-d valuated matroid on C([n], d - 1). If D is a basis of µ, write calD = {D \ {i}: i in D}, which is a basis of Σ. Then Σ satisfies the following formulas. (1) If D1 and D2 are bases of µ sharing an element i, and B is an independent (d - 1)-set such that calD1 - {D1 \ {i}} + {B} is a basis of Σ, then calD2 - {D2 \ {i}} + {B} is a basis and Σ(calD2 - {D2 \ {i}} + {B}) - Σ(calD1 - {D1 \ {i}} + {B}) = (d - 2)(µ(D2) - µ(D1)). (2) For any bases D1 and D2 of µ, Σ(calD2) - Σ(calD1) = (d - 1)(µ(D2) - µ(D1)). (3) If calD is induced by a basis D of µ, i in D, B is an independent (d - 1)-set, and D' = B ∪ {i}, then the exchange value satisfies Σ(calD - {D \ {i}} + {B}) - Σ(calD) = µ(D') - µ(D), with the degenerate nonbasis case interpreted by both sides being forced infinite in the corresponding coordinates.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove (1) when D1 = A ∪ {a} and D2 = A ∪ {b} differ in one element and i is in A. For each k in A \ {i}, the hyperplanes cl(D1 \ {k}), cl(D2 \ {k}), and cl(A) are concurrent. Since Σ is an adjoint, the corresponding three-term incidence equation is a valuated circuit of Σ. Use elementary basis exchange in Σ to replace D1 \ {k} by D2 \ {k} one k at a time, keeping B fixed. The change in the Σ-basis valuation at each exchange is the difference of the corresponding valuated circuit entries, equal to µ(D2) - µ(D1). Summing over d - 2 such exchanges gives (1) in the adjacent case; arbitrary D1,D2 with common i follow by a sequence of basis exchanges. For (2), reduce to adjacent D1,D2 and perform one additional elementary exchange replacing D1 \ {i} by D2 \ {i}; this contributes another µ(D2) - µ(D1), so together with (1) the coefficient becomes d - 1. Formula (3) is obtained by applying (1) and (2) to D and D' = B ∪ {i}; if i lies in cl_M(B), then both B ∪ {i} and the exchanged set are nonbases, as also follows from the adjoint dependence criterion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If the underlying matroid M of a valuated matroid µ is a direct sum of finite projective spaces, then µ has a unique simplified adjoint Σ_s. Moreover, Σ_s is isomorphic to µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Direct sums of finite projective spaces are rigid as valuated matroids, so after translation µ may be assumed to be the trivial valuation on M. Finite projective spaces are modular, and direct sums preserve the modular behavior needed here. By Proposition 4.14(3), Dr1_s(M) is isomorphic to Trop M and the ordinary adjoint is unique. Therefore the only rank-d tropical linear space inside Dr1_s(M) with the required underlying adjoint structure is the corresponding copy of Trop M itself, giving existence, uniqueness, and Σ_s ≅ µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a d-dimensional linear subspace of K^n over the Puiseux field, and let µ be the valuated matroid such that Trop P V = Trop µ. Then the tropicalization of Gr(d - 1, V), viewed inside Gr(d - 1, n) and then in the Pluecker torus, is a rank-d valuated matroid adjoint of µ. If V is a generic hyperplane in K^n, then Dr1(µ) is a translation of Trop M_{K_n}, where M_{K_n} is the graphic matroid of the complete graph K_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first statement, the classical linear space Gr(d - 1, V) parametrizes hyperplanes in P V and lies as a projective linear subspace in the Pluecker embedding of Gr(d - 1, n). Its tropicalization is contained in Dr1(µ), because every hyperplane of V tropicalizes to a codimension-1 tropical subspace of Trop V. Jell-Markwig-Rincon-Schroeter identify the underlying matroid of this Pluecker-linear space as the matroid of lines of the hyperplane arrangement associated to V; by Proposition 4.13 this matroid is an adjoint of the underlying matroid of µ. Proposition 5.3 then gives the valuated adjoint. For a generic hyperplane, Trop V is a translate of Trop U_{n-1,n}. Identifying hyperplanes [n] \ {i,j} of U_{n-1,n} with edges ij of K_n, the concurrent-triple equations are exactly the 3-cycle circuit equations of M_{K_n}. These circuits form a tropical basis for Trop M_{K_n}, giving Dr1(U_{n-1,n}) = Trop M_{K_n}; translation gives the stated result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition A.4 and Proposition A.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional vector space, and consider the plethystic map Γ: Gr(d, E) -> Gr(d, ∧^{d-1} E), V ↦ ∧^{d-1} V. After choosing an ordered basis of E and a total order on the basis elements indexed by C([n], d - 1), there is an embedding Φ into P(∧^d(∧^{d-1}E)) such that the diagram Gr(d,E) --Γ--> Gr(d,∧^{d-1}E), with the two Pluecker embeddings downward and Φ across, commutes. In coordinates, if x(I) are the Pluecker coordinates of V and J = {J1,...,Jd} is a d-subset of C([n], d - 1) with J1 = {i1,...,i_{d-1}}, then the J-coordinate of Φ([x]) is the determinant of the (d - 1) by (d - 1) matrix with entries ± x(J_k ∪ {i_l}) for 1 <= l <= d - 1 and 2 <= k <= d, with signs determined by the chosen order. Proposition A.2 is the corresponding generalized cofactor identity for a d by n matrix A: the d by d minors of the generalized cofactor matrix of A are determinants of matrices built from the d by d minors of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a d by n matrix A representing V, form the generalized cofactor matrix B whose columns are indexed by (d - 1)-subsets J of [n] and whose entries are the (d - 1) by (d - 1) minors of A obtained by deleting one row and using columns J. This matrix represents ∧^{d-1}V. Proposition A.2 computes an arbitrary d by d minor B_J. By continuity one may assume all relevant d-minors of A are nonzero and the J_k are pairwise disjoint after adding repeated columns. Row operations reduce the submatrix on J1 to an identity block, where the determinant formula is checked directly. Thus the Pluecker coordinates of ∧^{d-1}V are polynomial determinant expressions in the Pluecker coordinates of V. Proposition A.4 packages these expressions as Φ and proves the Pluecker diagram commutes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank-d matroid. Define the discrete interpolation property to mean that for every d - 1 rank-1 quotients Q1,...,Q_{d-1} of M, there is an elementary quotient N of M such that all Qi are quotients of N. If M has an adjoint, then M has the discrete interpolation property. If M has the discrete interpolation property, then M has the Levi intersection property, meaning every d - 1 hyperplanes of M are contained in a nontrivial linear subclass.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume W is an adjoint of M. A rank-1 quotient corresponds to a proper flat of M, so take d - 1 proper flats A. Inside L(W), recursively build an order ideal: start with the lower interval generated by the atoms in A, then whenever a flat G in A not yet included covers an element in the current ideal, enlarge to the lower interval of the join with G. Each enlargement raises rank by at most one, so after at most d - 1 steps the ideal is generated by a proper flat of W. By Proposition 4.14(2), its pullback to L(M) is a nontrivial modular cut. The chosen flats avoid the collar of this cut, so the associated elementary quotient contains all corresponding rank-1 quotients. Conversely, if H1,...,H_{d-1} are hyperplanes and the discrete interpolation property holds, then an elementary quotient containing the rank-1 quotients associated to these hyperplanes yields a nontrivial linear subclass L1(N) ∩ L1(M) containing H1,...,H_{d-1}; this is the Levi property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem D and Lemma 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a rank-d valuated matroid. If µ has an adjoint Σ, then Trop µ has the linear interpolation property: every set of d - 1 points of Trop µ is contained in a codimension-1 tropical linear subspace Trop θ ⊂ Trop µ. The key Lemma 6.8 says: if [w] lies in Trop µ ∩ T[n] and [V] = [w^{∧(d-1)}] is the point of T^{C([n],d-1)} with V(B) = sum_{i in B} w(i), then the stable intersection H_V ∩_st Trop Σ, where H_V is the tropical hyperplane centered at [V], is contained in Dr1(µ; w), the set of codimension-1 quotients θ of µ with [w] in Trop θ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 6.8, take θ in H_V ∩_st Trop Σ. To prove w in Trop θ, one checks the incidence relations min_{i in D}{θ(D-i)+w(i)} for every d-set D. If D is a basis of µ, then the valuated circuit of the truncation associated with D has coordinates CD(B) = min_{B'}{Σ(calD - {B} + {B'}) - V(B')}. Lemma 5.4(3) rewrites CD(B) + V(B) as a quantity independent of B, using the fact that w in Trop µ implies the minimizers of min_D{µ(D)-w·e_D} cover [n]. Thus the circuit relation from H_V ∩ Trop Σ is exactly the incidence relation for w in Trop θ. If D is not a basis but spans a hyperplane, the parallel-hyperplane relations reduce the check to the incidence relation for w in Trop µ. For Theorem D, given d - 1 torus points w1,...,w_{d-1}, set V_i = w_i^{∧(d-1)}. Since Σ has rank d, the stable intersection H_{V1} ∩_st ... ∩_st H_{V_{d-1}} ∩_st Trop Σ is nonempty. Lemma 6.8 puts every point of this stable intersection in Dr1(µ; w1,...,w_{d-1}). Boundary points of Trop µ are handled by approximating by torus points and using compactness and closedness of incidence relations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem E
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank-d matroid. If M does not have the Levi intersection property, then there exist d - 1 points in Trop M ∩ T[n] that are not contained in any codimension-1 tropical linear subspace of Trop M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose hyperplanes H1,...,H_{d-1} that are not contained in any nontrivial linear subclass. For a fixed c > 0, define points w_i in R^n by w_i(k) = c if k is in H_i and w_i(k) = 0 otherwise. Each [w_i] lies in Trop M because it is a tropical convex combination of the cocircuit for H_i and the all-c coordinates. Suppose Trop θ ⊂ Trop M contains all w_i, with θ normalized so min θ(B) = 0. The initial matroid M^{w_i} is the direct sum of M restricted to H_i and a loopless rank-1 matroid on the complement. Since θ^{w_i} is loopless, θ has a basis crossing the complement, and the containment forces the coordinate θ_{H_i} associated to the hyperplane H_i to be at least c. The defining equations of Dr1_s(M) then propagate the inequality θ_H >= c along every concurrent-triple closure. Because the smallest linear subclass containing the H_i is all of L1(M), all θ_H are at least c, contradicting min θ = 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.13 and Theorem F
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ0 ↠ µ_{d-1} be a quotient of valuated matroids of corank d - 1, where µ_{d-1} has rank 1. Then: (1) there exist valuated matroids µ1,...,µ_{d-2} such that µ0 ↠ µ1 ↠ ... ↠ µ_{d-2} ↠ µ_{d-1}, and the underlying ordinary matroid flag is the Higgs factorization of the underlying quotient M0 ↠ M_{d-1}; (2) there is a valuated matroid µ on [n] ∪ I, with |I| = d - 1, such that µ \ I = µ0 and µ / I = µ_{d-1}. Consequently, for any point [w] in Trop µ0, the partial flag {[w]} ⊂ Trop µ0 can be completed to a full flag of tropical linear spaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After translation, write the rank-1 endpoint as the trivial valuation on U_{0,F} ⊕ U_{1,[n]\F}. If rk_{M0}(F) = 0, then F is empty and repeated ordinary truncations by the uniform corank-1 valuated matroid produce a chain down to U_{1,n}; the minimizer-cover criterion ensures the endpoint quotient persists. If rk_{M0}(F) = d - 1, repeated principal truncations by F are used; the incidence relation for the endpoint is verified by comparing minimizers before and after truncation. For intermediate rank, first use uniform truncations until F becomes a hyperplane, then use principal truncations by F. The resulting ordinary flag is the Higgs factorization. For the extension statement, each principal truncation arises as contraction of a principal extension; compatibility of these extensions lets one add elements of I successively, reducing the length of the flag one step at a time until a single valuated matroid on [n] ∪ I realizes deletion and contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on E and N a matroid on E'. There is a canonical lattice isomorphism κ: cQt1(M) × cQt1(N) -> cQt1(M ⊕ N). In quotient notation, κ(Q1,Q2) = Q is characterized by κ^{-1}(Q) = (Q / E', Q / E). In terms of linear subclasses, if H is a linear subclass of M and G is a linear subclass of N, then κ(H,G) = H × {E'} ∪ {E} × G, a linear subclass of M ⊕ N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Hyperplanes of M ⊕ N are H ⊔ E' with H in L1(M), or E ⊔ G with G in L1(N). If two hyperplanes in H × {E'} ∪ {E} × G meet in a corank-2 flat, either they both come from one factor, where the linear-subclass axiom in that factor applies, or one comes from each factor, in which case they are the only two hyperplanes covering their intersection. Thus the displayed set is a linear subclass. Injectivity is clear. For surjectivity, any linear subclass L of M ⊕ N has the displayed form for some subsets H and G; applying the concurrent-triple axiom to triples wholly in the M-factor or N-factor shows H and G are linear subclasses. The contraction formula follows directly from how hyperplanes and flats of direct sums contract.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 6.14 and Proposition 6.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists a rank-4 matroid V8^- on [8], with 4-element circuits 1234, 3456, 1256, and 3478, that has two elementary quotients Q1 and Q2 with no common elementary quotient. Specifically, Q1 and Q2 are obtained from modular cuts generated respectively by {127, 568, 3478} and by {12, 34, 56}; the common flats of Q1 and Q2 are {∅, 7, 8, 127, 568, 3478, [8]}, which do not contain the lattice of flats of any rank-2 matroid on [8]. Proposition 6.16 further states that if M1 and M2 are matroids of the same positive rank on E and N is any matroid on E', then M1 and M2 have a common elementary quotient if and only if M1 ⊕ N and M2 ⊕ N have a common elementary quotient. Together these show that the quotient poset Qt(U_{n,n}) is not upper semimodular for every n >= 8.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The example is checked by explicitly listing the modular cuts and comparing the resulting lattices of flats: the intersection L(Q1) ∩ L(Q2) is too small to contain a rank-2 quotient lattice, so no common elementary quotient exists. Proposition 6.16 follows immediately from the product decomposition of elementary quotient lattices in Proposition 6.15: cQt1(M1 ⊕ N) ∩ cQt1(M2 ⊕ N) = (cQt1(M1) ∩ cQt1(M2)) × cQt1(N). Hence the intersection is nonempty in one setting exactly when it is nonempty in the other. Applying this to direct sums with free or auxiliary factors propagates the rank-4 counterexample to all n >= 8.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.20
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let µ be a loopless valuated matroid on [n]. Let P = Trop µ, let P_infty = Trop(µ / 1), and let π1: PT[n] \ {[(0,∞,...,∞)]} -> PT[n-1] be the projection forgetting the first coordinate by setting it to ∞. For [w] in T[n-1], if [w] lies in π1(P), then there exists [w'] in T[n] ∩ P with π1([w']) = [w]. Moreover, the following are equivalent: (1) [w] lies in P_infty; (2) there exists [w'] over [w] such that [w' + x e1] lies in P for all x >= 0 and 1 is a coloop of the initial matroid µ_{w' + x e1}; (3) π1^{-1}([w]) ∩ P contains more than one point; (4) for some [w'] over [w] in P, 1 is a coloop of the initial matroid µ_{w'}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Normalize w = (∞,0,...,0). If w is in π1(P), then minimizers of min_{1 notin B} µ(B) cover [n] \ {1}. Varying the first coordinate x makes min_{1 in B}(µ(B)-x) move continuously through all real values; choose x where it equals the previous minimum. Then the minimizers cover all of [n], so x e1 lies in P. If w lies in Trop(µ/1), then minimizers among bases containing 1 cover [n]; for all sufficiently large x, these are precisely the minimizers of min_B(µ(B)-x e1·e_B), proving the ray and coloop condition. The implications from a ray to multiple preimages are immediate. If there are two preimages x1 e1 and x2 e1 with x1 > x2, then for all x > x2 the minimizers are exactly the minimizers at x2 that contain 1; since x1 e1 lies in P, these cover [n], giving the coloop condition. The same argument recovers containment in P_infty.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem C
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Trop µ be a tropical plane, i.e. µ has rank 3. Then any two tropical lines contained in Trop µ intersect. Equivalently, two coperspective tropical lines are perspective.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n using deletion-contraction projection. If one coordinate is a loop for one line, reduce by contraction. Otherwise take two loopless rank-2 quotients θ1 and θ2 of µ, set P = Trop µ, L1 = Trop θ1, L2 = Trop θ2, and project by π1. The contractions θ1/1 and θ2/1 give points a_infty and b_infty on P_infty = Trop(µ/1). In the special case where 1 is a coloop of µ, sweep P by tropical hyperplanes H_t defined by x(1) = x(2) + t. The stable sections P_t are all copies of the tropical line P_infty. Track the stable intersections a_t = L1 ∩_st H_t and b_t = L2 ∩_st H_t, as well as set-theoretic intersections. Tropical convexity of line segments and the balancing condition for degree-1 tropical curves force either a_t = b_t for some t or one line to contain the relevant segment to the other, giving an intersection. In the general case, induction gives a point in π1(L1) ∩ π1(L2). If this point is not in P_infty, Lemma 6.20 makes π1 injective there, so L1 and L2 intersect. If it lies in P_infty, Lemma 6.20 shows that along the interior of the relevant lifted region, the initial matroid has 1 as a coloop, so the local sweeping argument applies. Boundary cases are handled by analyzing valuated cocircuits of P and, if necessary, moving slightly along a tropical segment to return to the torus case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.18 and Corollary 6.19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Two rank-2 matroids M1 and M2 on the same ground set are perspective if and only if they are coperspective. Consequently, if M is any matroid of rank at most 3, then Qt(M), the poset of all matroid quotients of M, is a modular poset, meaning both it and its order dual are upper semimodular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For rank-2 matroids, the lattice of flats has a simple form: a loop set A, the ground set, and a partition of the complement into parallel classes. If M1 and M2 are coperspective, i.e. elementary quotients of a common rank-3 matroid, then if one is a principal truncation Cheung's compatible extension theorem gives a common elementary quotient; otherwise both are simple and share U_{1,n}. Conversely, assume M1 and M2 have a common rank-1 quotient, equivalently a common proper flat. The proof constructs a rank-3 matroid M whose lattice of flats contains L(M1) ∪ L(M2). It does so by a case analysis on whether the common flat is the loop set, a rank-1 flat, or one loop set contained in the other, and then applies Lemma 6.17, which gives a rank-3 geometric lattice from set families satisfying disjointness and intersection conditions. The corollary uses that all rank-3 matroids have adjoints, together with Theorem 6.4 and the rank-2 perspective-coperspective equivalence, to obtain the required semimodularity properties for quotient intervals of rank at most 3.

proof_techniques.name: Translate inclusion of tropical linear spaces into valuated matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper consistently replaces a containment Trop θ ⊂ Trop µ by the quotient relation µ ↠ θ, expressed through tropical incidence-Pluecker equations. This turns geometric incidence questions into questions about the quotient poset of valuated matroids and, in the unvaluated case, the quotient poset of ordinary matroids. The elementary layer Dr1(µ) is then a tropical prevariety in Pluecker coordinates, and its unvaluated skeleton is governed by elementary quotient lattices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on Dr(M) can be studied as an anti-automorphism of the poset of valuated matroid quotients of the trivial valuation on M. The natural embedded copy of L(M)^op consists of special quotients, so checking compatibility with quotient relations is the first reduction.

proof_techniques.name: Analyze the codimension-one layer before the whole relative Dressian
proof_techniques.description: The strongest structural results in the paper concern Dr1(µ), the codimension-1 subspaces. Theorem 4.3 gives explicit three-term equations, and Theorem 4.7 identifies Dr1_s(M) with the order-complex fan of cQt1(M). Thus the first nontrivial stratum of the relative Dressian has a purely combinatorial model in terms of linear subclasses of M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any global order-reversing involution on Dr(M) should send high-rank subspaces to low-rank subspaces; its behavior on hyperplanes or points is constrained by Dr1(M) and by rank-1 quotients. Obstructions or constructions can often be detected already on this elementary layer.

proof_techniques.name: Use linear subclasses as coordinates for elementary quotients
proof_techniques.description: For ordinary matroids, elementary quotients of M are encoded by linear subclasses H of the hyperplane set L1(M). The lattice operation on cQt1(M) is reverse inclusion of these subclasses, and in Dr1_s(M) the cones are indicator cones of chains of such subclasses. This replaces potentially complicated quotient matroids by subsets of hyperplanes satisfying a local closure rule over corank-2 flats.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the given order-reversing flat involution permutes hyperplanes and colines, one can test whether it induces operations on linear subclasses. A candidate extension to Dr1(M) would need to transform these chain cones and linear-subclass closures compatibly.

proof_techniques.name: Simplify Dr1(µ) by choosing one basis for each hyperplane
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces Dr1_s(µ), where coordinates are indexed by hyperplanes of the underlying matroid M rather than by all (d - 1)-subsets. Degenerate and parallel incidence equations show that all bases of the same hyperplane carry affinely determined values, so no information is lost. The remaining equations are exactly concurrent-triple equations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a matroid with a polarity or order-reversing involution, hyperplanes and points are paired. Dr1_s(M) is the natural coordinate system in which to compare the polarity with elementary tropical subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Adjoints as tropical linear spaces inside Dr1_s(µ)
proof_techniques.description: The paper's definition of a valuated adjoint is geometric: it is a rank-d tropical linear space contained in Dr1_s(µ). In the ordinary case, Proposition 4.12 identifies adjoints of M with simple matroids W on L1(M) whose circuits include the concurrent triples, equivalently with order-reversing embeddings L(W) into cQt1(M). This gives a precise tropical generalization of classical dual projective geometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis is an order-reversing involution on L(M), which resembles a polarity/self-adjoint structure. The adjoint framework suggests trying to realize the polarity as, or through, a matroid W with Trop W ⊂ Dr1_s(M), then extending from elementary quotients to larger subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Cofactor formulas for valuated adjoints
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.4 gives explicit identities relating the basis valuation of µ to the basis valuation of its adjoint Σ. These identities are proved by elementary basis exchange in Σ along valuated circuits corresponding to concurrent triples. They tropicalize the classical cofactor determinant identities for the map V ↦ ∧^{d-1}V.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a putative involution on Dr(M) is modeled on classical polarity or orthogonal complement, the cofactor formulas indicate what Pluecker-coordinate transformations should look like on the elementary hyperplane-parametrizing layer.

proof_techniques.name: Use recession spaces to pass from valuated to ordinary constraints
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses rec(Trop µ) = Trop M, where M is the underlying matroid, and rec of an intersection lies inside the intersection of recessions. For example, if Trop Σ_s ⊂ Dr1_s(µ), then Trop W = rec(Trop Σ_s) lies in Dr1_s(M), forcing W to be an ordinary adjoint by Proposition 4.12.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A map on valuated subspaces of Trop M should have a well-defined effect on underlying matroids or recession fans. This can provide necessary combinatorial conditions for an involution before handling valuations.

proof_techniques.name: Dimension and rigidity tests for possible incidence constructions
proof_techniques.description: For rigid matroids, all valuations with a fixed underlying matroid are translations, so relative Dressian pieces can have small dimension. The paper uses this to show that generic tropical lines may not lie in tropical planes with prescribed rigid underlying matroid, and that finite projective-space cases have unique adjoints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When searching for or disproving an involution, rigid self-dual matroids are good test cases: the valuation freedom is minimal, so any extension of a flat polarity to all of Dr(M) is highly constrained.

proof_techniques.name: Detect poset obstructions via semimodularity and common quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper studies whether quotient posets behave like geometric lattices. It proves positive rank-3 results but constructs rank-4 and higher failures of upper semimodularity using two elementary quotients without a common elementary quotient. These failures are transported by direct sums through the product formula for cQt1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution often transfers upper-incidence properties to lower-incidence properties. Nonsemimodularity or missing common quotients in Dr(M) or Qt(M) may obstruct an anti-automorphism extending a lattice polarity, especially in higher rank.

proof_techniques.name: Direct-sum factorization of elementary quotient lattices
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 6.15 gives a clean product formula cQt1(M ⊕ N) ≅ cQt1(M) × cQt1(N). It is proved directly in linear-subclass coordinates by classifying hyperplanes of a direct sum and checking the concurrent-triple closure rule factorwise.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This allows construction of larger examples from smaller ones and suggests that any involution on Dr(M ⊕ N) should decompose or interact predictably with componentwise quotient data.

proof_techniques.name: Stable intersections with an adjoint produce interpolating hyperplanes
proof_techniques.description: Theorem D uses an adjoint Σ to find codimension-1 subspaces through prescribed points of Trop µ: for a point w, intersect Trop Σ stably with a hyperplane centered at w^{∧(d-1)}, and Lemma 6.8 shows the result lies in Dr1(µ; w). Intersecting d - 1 such hyperplanes with the rank-d tropical linear space Trop Σ gives a nonempty set of codimension-1 quotients containing all points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the flat involution supplies or implies an adjoint, this method may produce canonical incidence correspondences between points and hyperplanes, a possible ingredient for defining a polarity-like map on tropical subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Projection-contraction induction for tropical incidence
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem C analyzes tropical lines in a tropical plane by projecting away one coordinate. Lemma 6.20 controls fibers of the projection in terms of contraction and coloop initial matroids. A sweeping tropical hyperplane argument plus balancing proves intersections in the coloop case, and induction reduces the general case to it.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to reduce questions about subspaces of Trop M to deletion and contraction minors, which may be useful if an involution on Dr(M) must be compatible with minors or with the flat-complement operation.

proof_techniques.name: Classical plethysm as a template for tropical polarity
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A explains the classical map Gr(d,E) -> Gr(d,∧^{d-1}E), V ↦ ∧^{d-1}V, and expresses its Pluecker coordinates by generalized cofactor determinants. This is the classical incidence geometry behind adjoints of valuated matroids.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For realizable matroids with a classical polarity, one can compare the desired involution on relative Dressians with tropicalizations of classical orthogonal-complement or cofactor maps. The paper supplies the coordinate identities that such a tropicalization would need to satisfy.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that Dr1(µ) is much simpler than the full Dressian Dr(d,n): it is tropically convex and, in the unvaluated case, has an order-complex fan structure controlled by elementary quotients. It also notes that all rank-3 matroids have adjoints, while the Vamous matroid lacks the Levi intersection property; thus adjoint-like hypotheses are genuinely restrictive in higher rank. The incidence properties expected from classical projective geometry partly fail tropically: common intersections of codimension-1 subspaces can fail in dimension at least 4, and quotient posets of matroids are not upper semimodular for n >= 8. Conversely, no counterexample is known in the paper to the general problem of completing arbitrary partial flags of tropical linear spaces, though completion with a prescribed underlying matroid flag can fail. Relevant cited sources for further background include Cheung on adjoints and compatible extensions, Dress-Wenzel on valuated matroids and Tutte groups, Fink-Moci on relative Dressians over valuation rings, and Jell-Markwig-Rincon-Schroeter on moduli of codimension-one subspaces and matroids of lines.
why_relevant: This is the most directly relevant paper: it introduces relative Dressians Dr(M), identifies tropical subspaces of Trop(M) with valuated matroid quotients, studies their poset/incidence geometry, and introduces adjoints for tropical linear spaces.

title: Matroid Adjoints and the Minimum Rank of Zero-Nonzero Matrix Patterns
overall_summary: The paper studies zero-nonzero matrix patterns X through the intersection lattice L(X) generated by row zero sets and the class R(X) of matroids whose flats contain those row zero sets. Its core combinatorial mechanism is a zero-set Galois connection between rows and columns: transposing a pattern dualizes the associated intersection lattice, giving L(X)^* ≅ L(X^T).
