For the fundamental pattern of a matroid M, namely the complement of the hyperplane-point incidence pattern, L(X) is exactly the flat lattice L(M), and adjoints of M are characterized as the rank r(M) matroids in R(X^T). The main results relate the existence or nonexistence of matroid adjoints to whether the matroid minimum rank of a pattern equals its triangle number, with a sharp equivalence for transposed fundamental patterns. For the original problem, the useful content is not tropical but lattice-theoretic: it gives a precise framework in which order-reversing flat-lattice maps are adjoint/self-adjoint data, and it quotes related work implying that having the dual flat lattice itself be a matroid flat lattice is highly restrictive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid, and let X be its fundamental pattern, meaning the zero-nonzero pattern with one row for each hyperplane of M and one column for each point or ground element of M, with entry 0 exactly when the hyperplane contains the point. For any zero-nonzero pattern X, let L(X) be the intersection lattice generated by the zero sets of the rows of X, ordered by inclusion. Then L(X) equals L(M), the lattice of flats of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The zero sets of the rows of the fundamental pattern are precisely the hyperplanes of M. Every hyperplane is a flat, and intersections of flats are flats, so every element of L(X), being an intersection of hyperplanes, is a flat of M. Conversely, the paper invokes the standard matroid fact that every flat is an intersection of hyperplanes. Hence the subsets appearing in L(X) are exactly the flats of M, with the same inclusion order.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid. A simple matroid M' is called an adjoint of M if r(M') = r(M) and there is a map φ from L(M), the lattice of flats of M, to L(M'), the lattice of flats of M', such that: (i) φ is injective; (ii) whenever F1 and F2 are flats of M with F1 ⊆ F2, one has φ(F2) ⊆ φ(F1); and (iii) the restriction of φ to the hyperplanes of M is a bijection from the hyperplanes of M to the points, i.e. rank-1 flats, of M'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition rather than a proved result. The key structural point used later is that an adjoint supplies an inclusion-reversing copy of the flat lattice of M inside the flat lattice of a same-rank simple matroid M', with hyperplanes of M becoming points of M'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a zero-nonzero pattern with row-label set R and column-label set C. For a set A of row labels or a set A of column labels, let Z(A) denote the intersection of the zero sets of all labels in A, so Z(A) lies on the opposite side of the bipartition. Define the neighborhood N(A) to be the set of labels on the same side as A whose zero sets contain Z(A): if A ⊆ C, then N(A) = {t ∈ C : Z(A) ⊆ Z(t)}, and similarly for A ⊆ R. Then: (i) Z(N(A)) = Z(A); (ii) Z(Z(A)) = N(A); and, when A ⊆ C, (iii) N(A) is the least element of L(X) containing A; consequently, (iv) if A ∈ L(X), then Z(Z(A)) = A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), every element of A belongs to N(A), and by definition every element of N(A) has zero set containing Z(A), so intersecting the zero sets over N(A) gives exactly Z(A). For (ii), assume A ⊆ C; then a column c lies in N(A) exactly when every row that is zero on all columns of A is also zero on c, which is precisely the condition c ∈ Z(Z(A)); the row case is symmetric. For (iii), the least element of L(X) containing A is the intersection of all row zero sets that contain A. The rows whose zero sets contain A are exactly the rows in Z(A), so this least element is Z(Z(A)), which equals N(A) by (ii). For (iv), if A already lies in L(X), then it is the least element of L(X) containing itself, so N(A) = A, and (ii) gives Z(Z(A)) = A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be the fundamental pattern of a matroid M, and let C be the set of column labels of X, identified with the ground set or point set used in the pattern. If A ⊆ C, then the neighborhood N(A) defined from X equals the matroid closure cl_M(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 4.3(iii), N(A) is the least element of L(X) that contains A. By Lemma 2.10, L(X) = L(M). In a matroid, cl_M(A) is exactly the smallest flat containing A. Therefore N(A) = cl_M(A).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every zero-nonzero pattern X, the dual lattice L(X)^*, obtained from the intersection lattice L(X) by reversing the order, is isomorphic to L(X^T), the intersection lattice of the transpose pattern. An explicit isomorphism φ: L(X)^* → L(X^T) is given by φ(A) = Z(A), where A is viewed as a set of columns of X, equivalently a set of rows of X^T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First show that φ is well defined. If A ∈ L(X), then A is a set of columns of X; in X^T it is a set of rows, and Z(A) is an intersection of row zero sets of X^T, hence an element of L(X^T). For surjectivity, take T ∈ L(X^T), so T is a set of columns of X^T, equivalently a set of rows of X. Put A = Z(T), computed in X; then A ∈ L(X), and Lemma 4.3(iv), applied to X^T, gives Z(A) = Z(Z(T)) = T. For injectivity, if A,B ∈ L(X) and Z(A) = Z(B), then applying Z again and using Lemma 4.3(iv) gives A = Z(Z(A)) = Z(Z(B)) = B. Finally, the zero-set operation reverses inclusion: A ⊆ B implies Z(B) ⊆ Z(A). Conversely, for A,B ∈ L(X), if Z(B) ⊆ Z(A), then applying Z and using double-zero closure gives A = Z(Z(A)) ⊆ Z(Z(B)) = B. Thus the map is order-preserving from the reversed lattice L(X)^* to L(X^T).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be the fundamental pattern of a matroid M. Let R(Y), for a pattern Y, denote the class of matroids on the column labels of Y such that the zero set of each row of Y is a flat. Then a matroid M' is an adjoint of M if and only if M' ∈ R(X^T) and r(M') = r(M), with the column labels of X^T corresponding to the hyperplanes of M and hence, in the adjoint situation, to the points of M'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, suppose M' is an adjoint of M with inclusion-reversing map φ: L(M) → L(M'). Let p1,...,pn be the points of M and H1,...,Hm the hyperplanes of M. The adjoint condition identifies the points of M' with φ(H1),...,φ(Hm). The ith row of X^T corresponds to the point pi of M. Its jth entry is 0 exactly when the (j,i)-entry of X is 0, exactly when pi ⊆ Hj in M. Since φ reverses inclusion, this is equivalent to φ(Hj) ⊆ φ(pi). Therefore the zero set of the ith row of X^T is precisely the set of points of M' lying in the flat φ(pi), so it is a flat of M'. This proves M' ∈ R(X^T), and equality of ranks is part of the adjoint definition. For the converse, assume M' ∈ R(X^T) and r(M') = r(M). Then L(X^T) embeds into L(M') because every generator row zero set of X^T is a flat of M'. By Theorem 4.5, L(X^T) is isomorphic to L(X)^*, and by Lemma 2.10, L(X) = L(M). Composing these maps gives an injective inclusion-reversing map L(M) → L(M'). In this construction, a hyperplane Hj of M is sent to the singleton column corresponding to Hj in X^T, so hyperplanes of M map bijectively to points of M'. The paper also notes that every row of the fundamental pattern X is nonzero and distinct from every other row; since M' ∈ R(X^T), this rules out loops and parallel pairs in M', so M' is simple. Hence M' satisfies the definition of an adjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a zero-nonzero pattern, and let Y be obtained from X by appending one new column e whose zero set is the intersection of the zero sets of some subset A of the old columns of X. Suppose M ∈ R(X). Let M' be obtained from M by freely adding a point e to the closure of A in M. Then, with the new point e corresponding to the appended column, M' ∈ R(Y).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The single-element extension M' is described via the modular cut consisting of flats of M that contain A: M' = M +_M e, where e is freely added to cl_M(A). Consider a row r of Y, and let T be its zero set in the old pattern X. Since M ∈ R(X), T is a flat of M. The flat T belongs to the modular cut exactly when A ⊆ T. This is equivalent to saying that row r has zero entries in all columns of A, which by the construction of the appended column is equivalent to e also being zero in row r. Thus the zero set of r in Y is T ∪ {e} exactly when T is in the modular cut, and is T otherwise. The standard description of flats in such a single-element extension says that if T is not in the modular cut then T remains a flat of M', while if T is in the modular cut then T ∪ {e} is a flat of M'. Therefore every row zero set of Y is a flat of M'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a zero-nonzero pattern, and let X' be obtained by appending one new column whose zero set is the intersection of the zero sets of some subset of the old columns of X. Let mr R(Y) denote the minimum rank among matroids in R(Y). Then mr R(X') = mr R(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For mr R(X') ≤ mr R(X), take a minimum-rank matroid M ∈ R(X) and freely add a point to the closure of the subset A of columns used to define the new column. Lemma 4.7 shows that the resulting matroid lies in R(X'), and free extension into a flat does not change the rank. For the reverse inequality, take a minimum-rank matroid M' ∈ R(X') and delete the element e corresponding to the appended column. If a row zero set of X' is a flat of M', then its restriction to the old ground set is a flat of M' \ e by the standard deletion property for flats. Hence M' \ e lies in R(X), and its rank is at most r(M'). This gives mr R(X) ≤ mr R(X'), completing equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Triangle-number lower bound, Section 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a zero-nonzero pattern X, let tri(X) be the maximum size of a square subpattern that can be independently row- and column-permuted to become lower triangular with nonzero diagonal. The paper uses the result from Deaett 2020 that tri(X) equals the height of L(X). Since every matroid M ∈ R(X) has all row zero sets of X as flats, L(X) embeds as a subposet of L(M), and therefore tri(X) ≤ r(M) for every M ∈ R(X). Consequently tri(X) ≤ mr R(X). Also, because every matrix with pattern X represents a matroid in R(X), one has tri(X) ≤ mr R(X) ≤ mr Q_F(X) over every field F, where mr Q_F(X) is the usual matrix minimum rank over F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality tri(X) = height L(X) is cited from prior work. If M ∈ R(X), the generators of L(X), namely row zero sets, are flats of M; since flats of a matroid are closed under intersection, every element of L(X) is a flat of M. Thus any chain in L(X) is a chain in L(M), so height L(X) ≤ height L(M) = r(M). Taking the minimum over M ∈ R(X) gives tri(X) ≤ mr R(X). The matrix inequality uses the earlier theorem that each matrix with pattern X represents a matroid in R(X), so minimizing over the larger matroid class cannot give a larger rank.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a zero-nonzero pattern. Suppose M is a matroid of rank k with M ∈ R(X). If M has an adjoint, then mr R(X^T) ≤ k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let M' be an adjoint of M, so r(M') = k. Choose a pattern Y such that Y^T is the fundamental pattern of M. Since Y^T is fundamental, M ∈ R(Y^T), and Theorem 4.6 gives M' ∈ R(Y). Now enlarge Y to a pattern Ŷ by appending every column, not already present, whose zero set is an intersection of zero sets of columns of Y. Repeated application of Theorem 4.8 gives mr R(Ŷ) = mr R(Y) ≤ k. The columns added to Y correspond to intersections of hyperplanes of M, hence to all flats of M; equivalently, the rows of Ŷ^T include rows whose zero sets are exactly the flats of M. Since M ∈ R(X), every row zero set of X is a flat of M, so after matching labels X appears as a row-deleted subpattern of Ŷ^T. Therefore X^T appears as a column-deleted subpattern of Ŷ. Taking a matroid in R(Ŷ) of rank at most k and deleting the elements corresponding to the extra columns produces a matroid in R(X^T) of rank at most k. Hence mr R(X^T) ≤ k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a zero-nonzero pattern and let k = tri(X). If mr R(X) > k, then every matroid M ∈ R(X^T) of rank k fails to have an adjoint.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by contrapositive. Suppose there exists a rank-k matroid M ∈ R(X^T) that has an adjoint. Applying Lemma 4.9 to the pattern X^T gives mr R((X^T)^T) = mr R(X) ≤ k. Since the triangle-number lower bound always gives k = tri(X) ≤ mr R(X), this would force mr R(X) = k, contradicting mr R(X) > k. Thus no rank-k matroid in R(X^T) can have an adjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be the fundamental pattern of a matroid M. Then mr R(X) = tri(X) = r(M), and M is the unique matroid of rank r(M) in R(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.10, L(X) = L(M). The height of L(M) is r(M), and the cited result tri(X) = height L(X) gives tri(X) = r(M). Since the row zero sets of X are the hyperplanes of M, they are flats of M, so M ∈ R(X), which gives mr R(X) ≤ r(M). The triangle-number lower bound gives mr R(X) ≥ tri(X) = r(M), hence equality. For uniqueness, let M' ∈ R(X) have r(M') = r(M). Every hyperplane of M is a row zero set of X and hence a flat of M'. Every flat of M is an intersection of hyperplanes of M, and flats of M' are closed under intersection, so every flat of M is a flat of M'. The paper invokes the standard quotient criterion that this inclusion of flat sets implies M is a quotient of M'. Since the two ranks are equal, the standard equal-rank quotient corollary gives M = M'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let X be a zero-nonzero pattern such that X^T is the fundamental pattern of M. Then M has no adjoint if and only if mr R(X) > tri(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First suppose M has an adjoint M'. By Theorem 5.2 applied to X^T, mr R(X^T) = tri(X^T) = r(M). The triangle number is unchanged by transposition, so tri(X) = r(M). Since r(M') = r(M), and Theorem 4.6 applied to the fundamental pattern X^T gives M' ∈ R(X), there is a matroid in R(X) of rank tri(X). The lower bound tri(X) ≤ mr R(X) then forces mr R(X) = tri(X). Conversely, suppose M has no adjoint. By Theorem 4.6, again applied to the fundamental pattern X^T, no matroid in R(X) can have rank r(M), because any such matroid would be an adjoint of M. Theorem 5.2 gives r(M) = tri(X^T) = tri(X). Therefore all matroids in R(X) have rank strictly greater than tri(X), i.e. mr R(X) > tri(X).

proof_techniques.name: Zero-set Galois connection between rows and columns
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats the zero-set operation Z as an antitone correspondence between subsets of columns and subsets of rows. Applying Z twice gives a closure operator: Z(Z(A)) = N(A), and the closed column sets are exactly the elements of the intersection lattice L(X). This turns transposition into lattice duality, because a closed set of columns in X maps by Z to a closed set of columns in X^T with inclusions reversed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks for an order-reversing extension from flats to a larger poset. This technique suggests looking for an antitone Galois-type operation on the larger objects whose double application is a closure and whose restriction to flats is the given polarity.

proof_techniques.name: Encode a matroid flat lattice by the complement of hyperplane-point incidence
proof_techniques.description: For a matroid M, the fundamental pattern records non-incidence between hyperplanes and points. Because every flat is an intersection of hyperplanes, the intersection lattice of the row zero sets recovers L(M) exactly. This reduces questions about the flat lattice to questions about the row-zero-set lattice of a pattern, where transposition and zero-set duality are available.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural embedded copy of L(M)^op in the relative Dressian is controlled by contractions along flats. Encoding the flat lattice by hyperplane incidence may help isolate the purely lattice-theoretic part of any proposed involution before addressing the tropical or valuated structure.

proof_techniques.name: Adjoints as realizations of the transposed fundamental pattern
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.6 converts the abstract definition of a matroid adjoint into a pattern-realization statement: for the fundamental pattern X of M, adjoints of M are exactly same-rank matroids in R(X^T). The proof identifies the zero set of a row of X^T with the set of adjoint-points lying in the flat corresponding to a point of M, using the inclusion-reversing map on flat lattices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The hypothesis of an order-reversing flat-lattice involution resembles an adjoint map with M' = M, at least after checking simplicity and labeling of points by hyperplanes. This gives a concrete combinatorial test for whether the flat-level involution should be viewed as self-adjointness data.

proof_techniques.name: Completion by intersection columns without changing minimum rank
proof_techniques.description: When a new column is appended to a pattern with zero set equal to an intersection of existing column zero sets, the paper realizes the new column matroidally by freely adding a point to the closure of the corresponding old elements. This operation preserves rank and membership in the pattern class. Repeating it allows one to enlarge a pattern so that all intersection-derived columns are present, without changing matroid minimum rank.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one attempts to extend an involution from flats to more general objects, it may be useful to first close the combinatorial data under the operations forced by flats or quotients. The free-extension argument is a model for enlarging the ambient combinatorial object while preserving a rank or dimension invariant.

proof_techniques.name: Detect adjoint obstruction via transpose asymmetry
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.10 proves a contrapositive principle: if a rank-k matroid in R(X^T) has an adjoint, then the matroid minimum rank of X cannot exceed k = tri(X). Thus a gap between triangle number and matroid minimum rank for X forces every rank-k matroid in the transposed pattern class to have no adjoint. Theorem 5.7 makes this an equivalence when X^T is a fundamental pattern.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, this suggests searching for obstructions to extending a flat polarity by checking whether the relevant combinatorial types would require adjoints or self-adjoints that are known not to exist. Conversely, in highly self-dual or modular cases, such obstructions may disappear.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness from hyperplane flat constraints via matroid quotients
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 5.2, if a rank-r matroid M' has every hyperplane of a rank-r matroid M as a flat, then every flat of M is a flat of M'. The paper invokes a quotient criterion: this inclusion of flat lattices makes M a quotient of M'. Equal ranks then force equality. This proves that the fundamental pattern has a unique minimum-rank matroid, namely M itself.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The embedded flat part of the relative Dressian may be rigid if all hyperplane or contraction data are fixed. This quotient-rigidity argument is a possible template for proving that any extension of a flat-level involution is forced on certain boundary or matroidal strata.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that not every matroid has an adjoint; Cheung proved that the Vámos matroid has none. It also quotes Fu-Tang-Wang 2024: the dual lattice L(M)^* is itself the lattice of flats of some matroid exactly when M is modular, and in that case the adjoint is unique. Since an order-reversing involution of L(M) is an isomorphism L(M)^* ≅ L(M), this cited criterion may be highly relevant to the original hypothesis, subject to checking the exact conventions on simplification, points, and modular matroids. Section 6 asks whether a converse to Theorem 4.10 holds under the nonvacuity hypothesis that R(X^T) contains rank tri(X) matroids, and asks for minimal collections of hyperplanes that determine a matroid of fixed rank. The paper contains no results on Bergman fans, tropical linear spaces, tropical projective space, valuated matroids, or relative Dressians; its usefulness is through flat-lattice duality and adjoint combinatorics.
why_relevant: Reformulates matroid adjoints via transpose patterns and dual lattices. This gives a modern combinatorial way to analyze adjoints/polarities that may interact well with quotient-poset constructions.

title: The tropical Grassmannian
overall_summary: Speyer and Sturmfels develop the tropicalization of polynomial ideals through initial ideals and Gröbner fans, then apply this framework to the Plücker ideal to define and study the tropical Grassmannian G_{d,n}. The paper proves that the tropical Grassmannian parametrizes realizable tropical linear spaces, gives an explicit tropical duality via complementation of Plücker coordinates, identifies G_{2,n} with the space of phylogenetic trees, and computes G_{3,6} in detail. Its core techniques are the monomial-free initial ideal criterion for tropicalization, universal Gröbner bases, tree-metric four-point conditions, and explicit cone-by-cone Gröbner computations. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the uniform/realisable model for an order-reversing duality on tropical linear spaces, especially the construction L ↦ L* from Plücker complementing and the containment criterion for tropical hyperplanes. The examples G_{2,n}, G_{3,6}, and G_{3,7} also provide useful test cases and warnings about relying only on combinatorial type, rank-two behavior, or realizability over a fixed characteristic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (fundamental theorem for tropical varieties)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an algebraically closed field with a valuation deg: K* → R whose residue field k is algebraically closed, and let I be an ideal in K[x_1,...,x_n]. For f=∑_{a∈A} c_a x^a, define trop(f)(w)=min_a(deg(c_a)+a·w), and let T(trop(f)) be the set of w∈R^n where this minimum is attained at least twice. For w∈R^n let in_w(I)⊂k[x_1,...,x_n] be the ideal generated by the w-initial forms of all polynomials in I. Then the following three subsets of R^n coincide: the closure of {deg(u_1),...,deg(u_n) : u∈(K*)^n and f(u)=0 for all f∈I}; the intersection over all f∈I of the tropical hypersurfaces T(trop(f)); and the set of all w∈R^n such that in_w(I) contains no monomial. This common set is denoted T(I), the tropical variety of I.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a point u∈V(I) in the algebraic torus, if f(u)=0 then the lowest valuation terms of f(u) cannot consist of a single term; otherwise that term could not cancel. Thus deg(u) lies in every T(trop(f)), and equivalently every in_w(f) is non-monomial. This gives the inclusions from algebraic valuations to tropical hypersurface intersection and then to the monomial-free initial ideal condition. For the converse, take w satisfying the monomial-free condition. By density of the value group and closedness it is enough to treat w=deg(v) for some v∈(K*)^n, and after rescaling variables by v one reduces to w=0. Since in_0(I) contains no monomial, the Nullstellensatz gives a torus point ū∈V(in_0(I))⊂(k*)^n. Let m̄ be its maximal ideal and let S be the multiplicative set of polynomials over the valuation ring R_K whose reductions are not in m̄. Localize R_K[x]/(I∩R_K[x]) at S and choose a minimal prime P. The proof shows P contracts trivially to R_K: if a nonzero c∈R_K contracted into P, then c becomes a zero-divisor after localization, so some f∈S would satisfy cf∈I; since c is invertible over K this would imply f∈I, contradicting the choice of S. After tensoring with K, extend P to a maximal ideal of K[x]/I, giving a torus zero u∈V(I). Writing the equations x_i-u_i with coefficients in R_K and reducing modulo the maximal ideal, the proof checks that u_i∈R_K and u_i reduces to ū_i; otherwise either in_0(I) would contain a unit or an element of S would become a unit in the localization. Hence deg(u_i)=0 for all i, and undoing the rescaling gives a point with valuation w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.2 (lifting zeros of initial ideals)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any ideal I⊂K[x_1,...,x_n] and any weight vector w∈R^n, every torus zero over the residue field k of the initial ideal in_w(I) lifts to a torus zero over K of I with the prescribed initial residue, after the standard rescaling corresponding to w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is isolated as the key point in the proof of Theorem 2.1. After translating w to 0 by rescaling variables, a zero ū of in_0(I) determines a maximal ideal m̄ in k[x]. Localizing at the multiplicative set of elements whose reductions avoid m̄ and choosing a minimal prime gives a prime over the valuation ring contracting trivially. Extending to K gives a K-rational torus zero u of I, and the same coefficient-clearing argument forces u to reduce to ū.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.3 (finite tropical basis from a universal Gröbner basis)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let UGB(I) be a finite universal Gröbner basis of an ideal I⊂K[x_1,...,x_n], meaning that UGB(I) contains a Gröbner basis of I for every weight vector w∈R^n. Then T(I) equals the finite intersection of tropical hypersurfaces ∩_{f∈UGB(I)} T(trop(f)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 2.1, membership in T(I) is equivalent to in_w(I) containing no monomial. For a fixed w this condition can be checked on any Gröbner basis for the w-order. Since UGB(I) contains such a basis for every w, it suffices to impose the tropical hypersurface condition for the finitely many polynomials in UGB(I).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4 and Corollary 2.5 (purity and fan structure)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If I⊂K[x_1,...,x_n] is prime and K[x]/I has Krull dimension r, then T(I) is a pure polyhedral complex of dimension r. If, additionally, I has constant coefficients over the residue field, then T(I) is a fan in R^n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The purity statement is cited as the Bieri-Groves theorem. The fan statement follows because, with constant coefficients, all coefficient valuations in trop(f) are zero and the initial ideals depend only on cones in the ordinary Gröbner fan; scaling a weight vector by a positive scalar does not change which terms are minimal. Thus the tropical variety is a subfan of the Gröbner fan.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1 and dimension reductions for the tropical Grassmannian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let I_{d,n} be the Plücker ideal in the polynomial ring with variables p_I indexed by d-subsets I⊂[n]. The tropical Grassmannian G_{d,n}=T(I_{d,n}) is the set of w∈R^{choose(n,d)} such that in_w(I_{d,n}) contains no monomial. It is a pure polyhedral fan in R^{choose(n,d)}, and every maximal cone has dimension (n-d)d+1. If φ:R^n→R^{choose(n,d)} sends a=(a_1,...,a_n) to the vector with I-coordinate ∑_{i∈I} a_i, then image(φ) is the common intersection of all cones of G_{d,n}, and (1,...,1) lies in image(φ). The quotient G'_{d,n}=G_{d,n}/R(1,...,1) has dimension d(n-d), the quotient G''_{d,n}=G_{d,n}/image(φ) has dimension (d-1)(n-d-1), and intersecting G''_{d,n} with the unit sphere gives a polyhedral complex G'''_{d,n} whose maximal faces have dimension nd-n-d^2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Plücker ideal is prime, has constant coefficients, and its coordinate ring has Krull dimension (n-d)d+1, so Theorems 2.4 and 2.5 give a pure fan of that dimension. The lineality image(φ) comes from rescaling the n columns of a representative matrix: adding ∑_{i∈I}a_i to the valuation of p_I does not change the underlying initial degeneration in the relevant projective torus direction. Quotienting by the global scalar line or by the full image of φ subtracts the stated dimensions, and taking a spherical link lowers dimension by one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 3.4 and 4.2 (G_{2,n} is the space of phylogenetic trees)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T_n be the simplicial complex whose vertices are unordered splits {A,B} of [n] with |A|,|B|≥2, and whose faces are collections of pairwise compatible splits, equivalently the flag complex on the graph where two splits are adjacent if one side of one split is contained in one side of the other. Faces correspond to semi-labeled trees with leaves [n], and facets correspond to trivalent trees. The complex T_n has 2^{n-1}-n-1 vertices, (2n-5)!! facets, and the homotopy type of a bouquet of (n-2)! spheres of dimension n-4. For each face σ, define B_σ⊂R^{choose(n,2)} by requiring -w_{ij} to be the distance between leaves i and j in a realization of the tree σ, up to addition of image(φ); let C_σ be its image in R^{choose(n,2)}/image(φ). Then the closures of the C_σ are simplicial cones generated by the split vectors E_{A,B}=∑_{i∈A,j∈B} e_{ij}, they form a simplicial fan isometric to the Billera-Holmes-Vogtmann space of trees, and this fan is the quotient tropical Grassmannian G''_{2,n}; equivalently G'''_{2,n} is the simplicial complex T_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Tree metrics are characterized by the four-point condition: for every four leaves i,j,k,l there is a relabeling such that w_{ij}+w_{kl}=w_{ik}+w_{jl}≤w_{il}+w_{jk}, with w the negative distance vector in the paper's convention. For a fixed tree σ these equalities and inequalities describe the closure of B_σ, and Buneman's additive linkage algorithm reconstructs σ from such a vector. All B_σ have common lineality image(φ), and the paper gives the explicit decomposition B_σ=image(φ)+R_{≥0}{E_{A,B}:{A,B}∈σ}. The intersection formula B_σ∩B_τ=B_{σ∩τ} then gives a fan whose face poset is T_n. On the Grassmannian side, I_{2,n} is generated by quadrics p_{ij}p_{kl}-p_{ik}p_{jl}+p_{il}p_{jk}; tropicalizing these quadrics gives exactly the three possible four-point conditions for each quadruple. Hence every point of G_{2,n} lies in a tree cone. Conversely, for a trivalent tree σ and w in the relative interior of C_σ, the corresponding initial ideal contains the binomial ideal J_σ generated by p_{ij}p_{kl}-p_{ik}p_{jl} for the four-leaf subtrees of σ. After choosing a planar circular labeling of σ, Kempe's circular straightening law gives a term order whose initial ideal of I_{2,n} is the squarefree monomial ideal generated by crossing monomials p_{ik}p_{jl}. This term order refines w, so in_prec(in_w(I_{2,n})) equals that monomial ideal and is contained in in_prec(J_σ). Since J_σ⊂in_w(I_{2,n}), equality follows, proving that every tree cone is a Gröbner cone of the tropical Grassmannian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 3.5, 4.4, and 4.5 (rank-two consequences)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n, the simplicial complex G'''_{2,n} is a flag complex. Moreover, every initial binomial ideal of the Plücker ideal I_{2,n} is prime, and the tropical Grassmannian G_{2,n} is independent of the characteristic of the ground field.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The flag property is built into the split-complex description of T_n. For primality, if in_w(I_{2,n}) is binomial then w satisfies strict four-point inequalities and in_w(I_{2,n})=J_σ for a trivalent tree σ. Introduce one variable y_e for each edge e of σ and define a homomorphism ψ:Z[p_{ij}]→Z[y_e] by sending p_{ij} to the product of y_e over the path from leaf i to leaf j. The ideal J_σ is contained in ker(ψ), and both have the same dimension and degree because the circular straightening initial ideal is a radical equidimensional squarefree monomial ideal whose facets are triangulations of an n-gon. Hence J_σ=ker(ψ), a toric prime ideal. Characteristic-independence follows because the relevant initial binomial ideals have generators with coefficients ±1, so reduction modulo any characteristic does not create monomials.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.8 (the tropical Grassmannian parametrizes tropical planes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In this paper, a tropical d-plane in n-space is the closure of the coordinatewise valuation image deg(X) of a d-dimensional K-linear subspace X⊂K^n, considered in R^n or modulo the line R(1,...,1). The classical bijection between the Grassmannian G_{d,n} and d-planes in K^n induces a unique bijection w↦L_w between the projective tropical Grassmannian G'_{d,n}=G_{d,n}/R(1,...,1) and the set of tropical d-planes in n-space. Given w∈G'_{d,n}, L_w is the intersection, over all (d+1)-subsets J⊂[n], of the tropical hypersurfaces of the tropical linear forms F_J(x)=min_{j∈J}(w_{J\{j}}+x_j), with signs irrelevant tropically.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a Plücker vector ξ with deg(ξ)=w representing a d-plane X⊂K^n. By Cramer's rule, X is cut out by linear forms f_J(x)=∑_{j∈J} ± ξ_{J\{j}}x_j for all |J|=d+1. Their tropicalizations are precisely the F_J. The paper uses the known fact that the f_J form a universal Gröbner basis for the ideal they generate. Corollary 2.3 then implies deg(X)=∩_J T(F_J), so the displayed construction produces the tropicalization of X and is compatible with the classical Grassmannian. To prove injectivity, suppose L_w is known as a subset of R^n. For any (d-1)-subset I⊂[n] and distinct j,k∉I, choose a very large positive rational M and take a point x∈L_w in the affine slice x_i=M for i∈I with all x_l for l∉I much smaller than M; the existence of such points is obtained by solving the corresponding equations on a d-plane tropicalizing to L_w. For J=I∪{j,k}, the terms indexed by I in F_J are too large to attain the minimum, so the tropical hypersurface condition forces w_{I∪{j}}-w_{I∪{k}}=x_j-x_k. These differences for all I,j,k reconstruct all Plücker valuations up to one global additive constant, which is exactly the quotient defining G'_{d,n}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Duality observation after Theorem 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Plücker ideals I_{d,n} and I_{n-d,n} are identified by signed complementation of Plücker coordinates. Consequently G_{d,n} and G_{n-d,n} are isomorphic. If L=L_w is a tropical d-plane, define its dual tropical (n-d)-plane L*=L_{w*}, where the coordinate of w* indexed by [n]\I is w_I for every d-subset I⊂[n]. The paper states that a tropical hyperplane with coefficient vector a=(a_1,...,a_n) contains L* if and only if a∈L_w, and that (L*)*=L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Classically, replacing Plücker coordinates by signed complementary coordinates is the passage from a d-plane to its annihilator or orthogonal (n-d)-plane. Since tropicalization ignores the signs in the tropical linear forms, this gives the coordinate formula w*_{[n]\I}=w_I. The containment criterion is the tropicalized form of the classical statement that a linear functional vanishes on the annihilator precisely when its coefficient vector lies in the original subspace. Applying complementary coordinates twice returns the original Plücker vector, giving (L*)*=L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.1 (rank-two tropical planes recover the tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let w∈G_{2,n} lie in the cone C_σ corresponding to a semi-labeled tree σ. Then the image of the tropical 2-plane L_w in R^n/R(1,...,1) is a tree of combinatorial type σ. If r and s are vertices of this tree connected by an edge e, then r=s+c∑_{i∈S} e_i for some c>0, where S⊂[n] is the set of leaves on the s side of e; assigning length c to e gives the tree metric -2w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For d=2 the tropical Cramer equations are the tropical linear forms F_{ijk}=min(w_{ij}+x_k,w_{ik}+x_j,w_{jk}+x_i). Solving the conjunction of their hypersurface conditions is equivalent to the same four-point data used in the tree reconstruction algorithm. The directions of edges in R^n/R(1,...,1) are the split directions ∑_{i∈S}e_i, and lattice length recovers the corresponding pairwise distance data with the paper's normalization -2w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.3 (many dual tropical planes are not complete intersections)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L_w be a tropical 2-plane in R^n whose associated tree is not combinatorially isomorphic to the caterpillar tree with internal splits { {1,...,i}, {i+1,...,n} } for i=2,...,n-2. Then the dual tropical (n-2)-plane L_w* is not a complete intersection of two tropical hyperplanes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that L_w*=T(a)∩T(b) for two tropical hyperplanes. By the duality observation, the coefficient vectors a and b lie on the tree L_w. Let γ be the path in L_w from a to b. Since the tree is not a caterpillar, γ passes through fewer than n-1 edges; after deleting those edges, fewer than n connected components remain, so two leaves j,k are not separated by any edge of γ. Along every edge of γ, the coordinates j and k change together or not at all, hence a_j-a_k=b_j-b_k. It follows that T(a)∩T(b) contains all points with x_j+a_j=x_k+a_k and all other x_i-x_j sufficiently positive, a codimension-one subset of R^n/R(1,...,1). This contradicts the fact that L_w* is pure of codimension two.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition and Remark 6.4 (type of a tropical plane)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a tropical d-plane L_w and a maximal d-dimensional cell F of L_w, the affine span of F is cut out by equations of the form x_k-x_j=w_{J\{j}}-w_{J\{k}}. These equations define a d-partition {A_1,...,A_d} of [n]: indices j and k lie in the same block exactly when x_k-x_j is constant on F. The type of L_w, denoted type(L_w), is the set of d-partitions arising from all maximal cells of L_w. A maximal face F is uniquely specified by its d-partition, and F is bounded if and only if every block A_i has size at least two; in particular a tropical d-plane in R^n has no bounded d-faces when n≤2d-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equations defining the affine span impose an equivalence relation on coordinates by constant differences. Since F has dimension d, there are exactly d equivalence classes, giving a d-partition. The paper notes directly that this partition determines the maximal face. If a block is a singleton, the corresponding coordinate direction gives an unbounded ray of the face; conversely, if all blocks have size at least two, the tropical linear constraints bound the face in the quotient directions. For d=2 this recovers the Buneman split description of tree edges, together with the singleton splits representing unbounded leaf edges.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.1-5.3 and Theorem 5.4 (explicit structure of G_{3,6})
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For d=3,n=6, write e_{ijk} for the standard basis vectors of R^{20} indexed by triples. For each 4-subset {i,j,k,l}, set f_{ijkl}=e_{ijk}+e_{ijl}+e_{ikl}+e_{jkl}; for each tripartition {{i_1,i_2},{i_3,i_4},{i_5,i_6}}, define g_{i_1i_2i_3i_4i_5i_6}=f_{i_1i_2i_3i_4}+e_{i_3i_4i_5}+e_{i_3i_4i_6}, together with the cyclically related vector g_{i_1i_2i_5i_6i_3i_4}. Viewed in R^{20}/image(φ), the vertex set of G'''_{3,6} is E∪F∪G, with |E|=20, |F|=15, |G|=30. Its 1-skeleton has 550 edges in the following S_6-orbits: EE edges 90 like {e_{123},e_{145}} and 10 like {e_{123},e_{456}}; FF edges 45 like {f_{1234},f_{1256}}; GG edges 15 like {g_{123456},g_{125634}}; EF edges 60 like {e_{123},f_{1234}} and 60 like {e_{123},f_{1456}}; EG edges 180 like {e_{123},g_{123456}}; and FG edges 90 like {f_{1234},g_{123456}}. Let Δ be the flag complex on this graph. Then Δ has 1410 triangles, 1065 tetrahedra, 15 four-dimensional simplices, and no higher faces. The actual tropical Grassmannian G'''_{3,6} is the 3-dimensional simplicial complex Δ' obtained from Δ by removing the 15 FFF triangles {f_{1234},f_{1256},f_{3456}}, the 30 FFFG tetrahedra containing them, and the 15 FFFGG four-simplices. Thus G'''_{3,6} has 65 vertices, 550 edges, 1395 triangles, and 1035 tetrahedra; it is not flag, and its integral homology is concentrated in top dimension with H_3(G'''_{3,6},Z)=Z^{126}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result was obtained and verified by explicit computation. First, the listed seven symmetry types of 3-dimensional cones were checked to be Gröbner cones of I_{3,6} with monomial-free initial ideals. The relation g_{i_1i_2i_3i_4i_5i_6}+g_{i_1i_2i_5i_6i_3i_4}=f_{i_1i_2i_3i_4}+f_{i_1i_2i_5i_6}+f_{i_3i_4i_5i_6} shows that each FFFGG four-simplex of the abstract flag complex is geometrically a bipyramid with three F-vertices as the base and two G-vertices as cone points, so the tropical Grassmannian uses a triangulation by three tetrahedra around the GG edge rather than a 4-simplex. Completeness of the list is checked using the codimension-one connectedness theorem for tropical varieties from Einsiedler-Kapranov-Lind-Ward: for each known triangle orbit, one computes the zero-dimensional tropical variety of the corresponding initial ideal in_w(I_{3,6}), i.e. the link of that triangle, and verifies that all adjacent 3-cells are already in the list. The FGG triangles are special: their links have two points and they lie in the interior of the bipyramids, incident only to two of the three FFGG tetrahedra in that bipyramid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5 (non-prime initial binomial ideals in G_{3,6})
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Not every initial binomial ideal of I_{3,6} is prime. More precisely, if w lies in the relative interior of an FFGG cone of G'''_{3,6}, for example w=f_{1256}+f_{3456}+g_{123456}+g_{125634}, then in_w(I_{3,6}) is the intersection of two distinct codimension-10 prime ideals in Z[p]. In the displayed example these primes are P=in_w(I_{3,6})+⟨p_{125}p_{346}-p_{126}p_{345}⟩ and Q=in_w(I_{3,6})+⟨p_{135},p_{136},p_{145},p_{146},p_{235},p_{236},p_{245},p_{246}⟩; their degrees are 38 and 4, while degree(I_{3,6})=42.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a direct Gröbner computation. For the chosen w, the paper lists explicit binomial generators of in_w(I_{3,6}). Applying the computational criterion cited as Sturmfels, Gröbner Bases and Convex Polytopes, Corollary 1.9, the authors decompose this ideal as P∩Q with P and Q as above. Both components have codimension 10 and their degrees add to 42, the degree of the Grassmannian, confirming that the intersection is the whole initial ideal and that the initial binomial ideal is reducible and non-prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1 (characteristic dependence and higher-degree generators)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper states, using the rank-two result and the computation of G_{3,6}, that in the small cases d=2 and G_{3,6}, every monomial-free initial ideal of I_{d,n} is generated by quadrics and the tropical Grassmannian is characteristic-free. Both properties fail for all d≥3 and n≥7. In particular, for d=3,n=7 there are weight vectors whose membership in G_{3,7} depends on the characteristic of the residue field, and in every characteristic there is a monomial-free initial ideal of I_{3,7} requiring a minimal generator of degree three.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to construct examples in G_{3,7}; the paper says an easy lifting argument extends them to all d≥3,n≥7. Let w=e_{124}+e_{235}+e_{346}+e_{457}+e_{156}+e_{267}+e_{137}, whose support is the set of lines in the Fano plane. Over characteristic zero, a reduced Gröbner basis of I_{3,7} with respect to the reverse-lexicographically refined weight order from -w has 196 elements: 140 quadrics, 52 cubics, and 4 quartics. Among the cubics is f=2p_{123}p_{467}p_{567}-p_{367}p_{567}p_{124}-p_{167}p_{467}p_{235}-p_{127}p_{567}p_{346}-p_{126}p_{367}p_{457}-p_{237}p_{467}p_{156}+p_{134}p_{567}p_{267}+p_{246}p_{567}p_{137}+p_{136}p_{267}p_{457}. In characteristic not 2, in_w(f)=p_{123}p_{467}p_{567} is a monomial, so w∉G_{3,7}. In characteristic 2 the leading term with coefficient 2 disappears, the displayed initial form has seven terms, and the computation shows none of the leading forms of the 196 Gröbner basis elements is a monomial; hence w∈G_{3,7}. For the modified vector w'=w-e_{124}, the initial form in_{w'}(f)=2p_{123}p_{467}p_{567}-p_{367}p_{567}p_{124} is non-monomial in characteristic zero but becomes monomial in characteristic 2, and a recomputation shows w' lies in G_{3,7} in characteristic zero but not in characteristic 2. Thus neither characteristic-zero nor characteristic-two tropical Grassmannian contains the other. Finally, in any characteristic either the w or w' example supplies a monomial-free initial ideal with a necessary cubic generator, proving that quadrics do not suffice.

proof_techniques.name: Tropicalization via monomial-free initial ideals
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces a tropical membership question by an algebraic initial-ideal question: a weight vector w lies in T(I) exactly when in_w(I) contains no monomial. This moves tropical geometry into the Gröbner fan, where finite universal Gröbner bases, initial ideals, and cone computations can be used.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a Dressian or relative Dressian, this suggests testing candidate involutions cone-wise through initial degenerations or valuated-matroid subdivisions. It is especially useful for checking whether a proposed order-reversing map preserves the tropical linear subspace condition and the containment order.

proof_techniques.name: Plücker-coordinate complementation as tropical duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the classical identification of G_{d,n} and G_{n-d,n} by signed complementation of Plücker coordinates and tropicalizes it. For a tropical d-plane L_w, the dual L* is defined by complementary Plücker valuations w*_{[n]\I}=w_I, and tropical hyperplanes containing L* are exactly those whose coefficient vectors lie in L_w.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the closest result in the paper to the desired order-reversing involution: it gives a proven involutive duality on realizable tropical linear spaces in the uniform ambient case. A solver can try to imitate this construction for relative Dressians by identifying the appropriate analogue of Plücker complementation or matroid duality inside Trop(M).

proof_techniques.name: Recovering Plücker valuations from the tropical linear space
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 3.8, injectivity of w↦L_w is proved by recovering all differences w_{I∪{j}}-w_{I∪{k}} from carefully chosen points of L_w. One fixes a large M on coordinates in a (d-1)-subset I and chooses a point of L_w with remaining coordinates much smaller; the tropical Cramer equation for J=I∪{j,k} then forces the desired valuation difference.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an involution on a Dressian is defined geometrically on tropical subspaces rather than on valuated matroids, this technique suggests how to prove it is well-defined or unique by reconstructing coordinates from the subspace. It may also help test whether the flat involution forces a unique extension on dense or realizable strata.

proof_techniques.name: Four-point condition and split-fan models
proof_techniques.description: For G_{2,n}, the tropical Plücker relations are exactly the four-point conditions for tree metrics. Compatible splits give a flag simplicial complex, and each cone is generated by split vectors E_{A,B}. The proof uses Buneman tree reconstruction to pass between inequalities, trees, and tropical Grassmannian cones.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rank-two or corank-two relative situations may reduce to split systems or tree-like fans. This provides a concrete model where order reversal can be studied combinatorially on splits before attempting higher-rank Dressians.

proof_techniques.name: Toric degeneration of rank-two tree cones
proof_techniques.description: For a trivalent tree σ, the initial ideal J_σ of I_{2,n} is identified with a toric kernel by mapping p_{ij} to the product of edge variables along the path from i to j. A circular term order gives a squarefree monomial initial ideal, and degree plus dimension comparisons prove equality with the toric kernel.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When flats or boundary strata produce tree-like degenerations, toric models can make primality and characteristic-independence transparent. Such models may simplify proving that a proposed involution respects strata indexed by contractions or restrictions.

proof_techniques.name: Explicit cone enumeration using symmetry and links
proof_techniques.description: The computation of G_{3,6} lists vertex and cone orbits under S_6, verifies each candidate cone by checking its initial ideal is monomial-free, and proves completeness by computing links of codimension-one cones. Codimension-one connectedness of tropical varieties is used to ensure that walking through links finds all maximal cones.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a small matroid M, the same strategy can produce a finite model of Dr(M) on which one can test whether any order-reversing involution extending the flat involution exists. The G_{3,6} bipyramids also warn that the geometry of cones, not just the 1-skeleton, may matter.

proof_techniques.name: Characteristic-sensitive perturbations of matroid configurations
proof_techniques.description: The G_{3,7} example perturbs the incidence vector of the Fano plane and uses a cubic Gröbner basis element whose leading coefficient is 2. This makes tropical Grassmannian membership depend on whether the residue characteristic is 2, reflecting realizability of the Fano matroid.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The relative Dressian is usually meant to include non-realizable valuated matroids, while the tropical Grassmannian is realizable. This technique highlights where realizable duality arguments may fail to capture all Dressian points, especially for matroids with characteristic-dependent realizability.

proof_techniques.name: Combinatorial encoding by d-partitions of maximal cells
proof_techniques.description: The paper associates to each maximal cell of a tropical d-plane a d-partition of [n], grouping coordinates whose differences are constant on that cell. The resulting type generalizes the split encoding of trees, with bounded maximal cells corresponding to partitions whose blocks all have size at least two.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For tropical subspaces inside Trop(M), analogous cell types or matroidal subdivisions may give a combinatorial handle on the inclusion order. However, Example 6.5 shows that this type can be coarser than the Gröbner cone, so it should be supplemented by initial-ideal or valuated-matroid data.

other_useful_info: Scope note: throughout this paper a tropical d-plane means a realizable tropicalization deg(X) of a K-linear d-plane; the full Dressian of all valuated-matroid tropical linear spaces and relative Dressians are not defined in the paper. The directly portable duality result is therefore the uniform, realizable Plücker-complement duality, not a theorem about arbitrary matroids M. Example 6.5 shows that type(L_w) does not determine the Gröbner cone in G_{3,6}: the three tetrahedra in a bipyramid have the same listed type, so maximal-cell partition data can be strictly coarser than initial-ideal data. There is an apparent typo in the printed statement of Theorem 7.1: it says d=3 and n=3, while the surrounding text and computations refer to the small computed case G_{3,6}. Cited tools that may be worth consulting separately are Buneman's four-point tree reconstruction, Billera-Holmes-Vogtmann tree space, and the codimension-one connectedness theorem for tropical varieties from Einsiedler-Kapranov-Lind-Ward.
why_relevant: Introduces the tropical Grassmannian and its relation to tropicalized linear spaces and Plucker relations. It is basic background for comparing realizable tropical subspaces with all valuated-matroid subspaces in a Dressian.

title: The Bergman Complex of a Matroid and Phylogenetic Trees
overall_summary: Ardila and Klivans give a purely combinatorial description of the Bergman fan and Bergman complex of a matroid by studying minimum-weight bases. Their main result is that the fine subdivision of the Bergman complex is exactly a geometric realization of the order complex of the proper part of the lattice of flats: a weight class is valid precisely when its associated flag is a flag of flats. A key technical input is that the matroid of minimum-weight bases for a flag decomposes as a direct sum of minors along the flag, and its flat lattice decomposes as a product of intervals in the flat lattice. They also describe how the coarser subdivision by equality of the minimum-weight matroid is obtained from the fine subdivision, with adjacent maximal flat flags identified exactly across diamond intervals. For the original problem, the paper is useful because it explains exactly how flat-lattice data controls the Bergman/tropical linear space itself, and it supplies order-complex, interval, and minor-decomposition tools for testing whether an order-reversing flat-lattice involution can be extended beyond the flat-lattice stratum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Minimum-weight bases form a matroid, Section 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r on ground set [n]. For a weight vector ω∈R^n and a basis B of M, define the weight ω_B=∑_{b∈B}ω_b. Let M_ω be the collection of bases of M whose weight is minimum among all bases of M. Then M_ω is itself the collection of bases of a matroid on [n]. Equivalently, if P_M is the matroid polytope conv{∑_{b∈B}e_b : B is a basis of M}, then P_{M_ω} is the face of P_M on which the linear functional ω is minimized.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vertices of the matroid polytope P_M are the basis indicator vectors. The bases minimizing ω are exactly the vertices on the face of P_M where the linear functional ω is minimized. The paper invokes the Gelfand-Serganova characterization: a collection S of r-subsets of [n] is the set of bases of a matroid iff every edge of the polytope P_S spanned by their indicator vectors is parallel to e_i−e_j for some i,j. Since P_{M_ω} is a face of P_M, every edge of P_{M_ω} is an edge of P_M, hence has the required direction. Therefore the minimizers are the bases of a matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1, minimum bases from the weight flag
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on [n] with rank function r. Given ω∈R^n, let F(ω) be the unique flag of subsets ∅=:F_0⊂F_1⊂...⊂F_k⊂F_{k+1}:=[n] such that ω is constant on each block F_i−F_{i−1} and the constant values strictly increase from F_i−F_{i−1} to F_{i+1}−F_i. If F=F(ω), then a basis B of M is in M_ω iff, for every i=1,...,k+1, the set B contains exactly r(F_i)−r(F_{i−1}) elements of the block F_i−F_{i−1}. Consequently M_ω depends only on the flag F, not on the actual numerical values of ω; the paper denotes this matroid by M_F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the greedy algorithm characterization of minimum-weight bases in a matroid. The greedy algorithm first chooses elements of smallest weight, all lying in F_1, and it can choose exactly r(F_1) independent elements there before no further element of F_1 can be added. It then moves to the next weight block and chooses exactly r(F_2)−r(F_1) elements from F_2−F_1, so that after the second block it has a basis of F_2. Continuing block by block, after processing F_i−F_{i−1} it has chosen r(F_i) elements in F_i, and by the end it has chosen r(M) elements forming a basis. Thus every greedy output has the stated block counts. Conversely, any basis with these block counts can be obtained by resolving ties in the greedy algorithm so as to choose its elements within each equal-weight block, because its intersection with F_i has size r(F_i) and is independent, hence a basis of F_i. Since the possible greedy outputs are exactly the minimum-weight bases, the result follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2, direct sum of minors along a flag
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on [n], and let F be a flag of subsets ∅=:F_0⊂F_1⊂...⊂F_k⊂F_{k+1}:=[n]. Let M_F be the minimum-weight matroid associated to any weight vector whose equality classes are the blocks F_i−F_{i−1} in increasing order. Then M_F decomposes as the direct sum M_F = ⊕_{i=1}^{k+1} ((M|F_i)/F_{i−1}), where M|F_i is the restriction of M to F_i and /F_{i−1} denotes contraction of F_{i−1}; the i-th summand has ground set F_i−F_{i−1}. In the flat-flag case used later in the paper, the lattice of flats of M_F is naturally isomorphic to the product ∏_{i=1}^{k+1}[F_{i−1},F_i], where [F_{i−1},F_i] is the interval of flats G of M satisfying F_{i−1}≤G≤F_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Run the greedy algorithm block by block. After the first i−1 blocks, the algorithm has chosen a basis of F_{i−1}. During the i-th block it must choose elements from F_i−F_{i−1} which, after adjoining the already chosen basis of F_{i−1}, extend to a basis of F_i. These choices are precisely the bases of the minor (M|F_i)/F_{i−1}. Since the choices in different blocks occur on disjoint ground sets and independently subject to these minor conditions, the whole minimum-weight matroid is the direct sum of these minors. For the lattice statement, use the standard fact that the lattice of flats of a direct sum is the product of the lattices of flats of its summands. When F_{i−1} and F_i are flats, the flats of (M|F_i)/F_{i−1} identify with the interval [F_{i−1},F_i] in the lattice of flats of M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, fine Bergman subdivision is the flat-lattice order complex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on [n]. Define the Bergman fan ~B(M)={ω∈R^n : M_ω has no loops}. Define the Bergman complex B(M) as the intersection of ~B(M) with the sphere {ω∈R^n : ∑_iω_i=0 and ∑_iω_i^2=1}. A weight class, equivalently a flag F=∅=:F_0⊂F_1⊂...⊂F_k⊂F_{k+1}:=[n], is called valid if M_F has no loops. Then the weight class of F is valid for M iff every F_i in the flag is a flat of M. Therefore the fine subdivision of B(M) into valid weight classes is a geometric realization of Δ(L_M−{hat0,hat1}), the order complex of the proper part of the lattice of flats L_M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First suppose some F_i is not a flat. Then there exists e∉F_i lying in the closure of F_i. By Proposition 1, every basis in M_F contains r(F_i) elements from F_i, i.e. contains a basis of F_i. Since e is dependent on that basis of F_i, no such minimum-weight basis can contain e. Hence e is a loop in M_F, so the class is not valid. Conversely suppose every F_i is a flat. Fix any element e∈[n], and choose i such that e∈F_i−F_{i−1}. After the greedy algorithm has processed the earlier blocks, it has produced a basis of F_{i−1}. Since F_{i−1} is a flat and e∉F_{i−1}, e is not in the closure of that basis, so e can be chosen at the next step of the greedy algorithm. Resolving ties to choose e and then completing greedily gives a minimum-weight basis containing e. Since every element e is contained in some minimum-weight basis, M_F has no loops. The final identification follows because valid weight classes are exactly chains of proper flats, and the normalized cones corresponding to such chains give the simplices of the order complex of L_M−{hat0,hat1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary to Theorem 1, topology of the Bergman complex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid of rank r, and let μ_{L_M} be the Möbius function of its lattice of flats. Put hatμ(L_M)=(-1)^r μ_{L_M}(hat0,hat1). Then the Bergman complex B(M) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of hatμ(L_M) spheres of dimension r−2. Moreover, the subdivision of B(M) into weight classes is a pure shellable simplicial complex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 1, the fine subdivision of B(M) is the order complex of the proper part of the lattice of flats of M. The lattice of flats of a matroid is a geometric lattice. The paper invokes the standard theorem, cited from Björner, that the order complex of the proper part of a geometric lattice of rank r is pure, shellable, and homotopy equivalent to a wedge of |μ(hat0,hat1)| spheres of dimension r−2. Translating |μ| as hatμ gives the stated count.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Coarse subdivision observation, Section 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The coarse decomposition of B(M) into cells consisting of all weights ω inducing the same minimum-weight matroid M_ω is also a pure polyhedral complex. It is a subcomplex of the spherical polar to the matroid polytope P_M, and it is coarser than the fine subdivision by weight flags.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The face of P_M minimized by ω has vertex set corresponding to M_ω. Thus equality of M_ω is the same as lying in the same normal cone, or equivalently in the same cell of the polar decomposition determined by P_M. Intersecting this normal-fan or polar decomposition with the Bergman sphere and retaining the loopless face matroids gives the coarse subdivision. Since each weight flag fixes M_ω but several adjacent flags can yield the same M_ω, the flag subdivision refines this coarse polyhedral subdivision.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, adjacent fine cells merged in the coarse subdivision
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F and F' be adjacent maximal flags of flats of a matroid M, meaning that each has one flat in each rank, they agree except in rank i, and F−F_i=F'−F'_i. Write the common neighboring flats as F_{i−1} and F_{i+1}. Let M_F and M_{F'} be the minimum-weight matroids associated to the two flags, and let M_{F−F_i} be the minimum-weight matroid for the nonmaximal flag obtained by deleting F_i. Let diamond denote the rank-2 poset with a minimum element, a maximum element, and exactly two rank-1 elements. Then the following conditions are equivalent: (i) M_F=M_{F'}; (ii) M_F=M_{F−F_i}; (iii) F_i∪F'_i=F_{i+1}; (iv) the interval [F_{i−1},F_{i+1}] in L_M is a diamond poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the direct-sum description of Proposition 2. For j in the flag set M_j=(M|F_j)/F_{j−1}; define M'_j analogously for F'. Let N_i=(M|F_{i+1})/F_{i−1}, and let N be the direct sum of all common factors outside ranks i and i+1. Then M_F=N⊕M_i⊕M_{i+1}, M_{F'}=N⊕M'_i⊕M'_{i+1}, and M_{F−F_i}=N⊕N_i. The rank of M_i, M_{i+1}, M'_i, and M'_{i+1} is 1, while N_i has rank 2. The lattice of flats of M_i⊕M_{i+1} is the four-element diamond {∅, F_i−F_{i−1}, F_{i+1}−F_i, F_{i+1}−F_{i−1}}, the analogous lattice for M'_i⊕M'_{i+1} is obtained by replacing F_i by F'_i, and the lattice of flats of N_i is {F−F_{i−1} : F∈[F_{i−1},F_{i+1}]}. If the interval [F_{i−1},F_{i+1}] is not a diamond, then the rank-2 minor N_i has a different flat lattice from the rank-1 direct-sum lattice, so M_F≠M_{F−F_i}; also F_i∪F'_i is not all of F_{i+1}, and the two rank-1 decompositions have different flat lattices, so M_F≠M_{F'}. If the interval is a diamond, then F_i and F'_i are the only rank-i flats between F_{i−1} and F_{i+1}. Since N_i has no loops, every element of F_{i+1}−F_{i−1} lies in one of the two atoms, forcing F_i∪F'_i=F_{i+1}. The three relevant flat lattices then coincide, and since a matroid is determined by its flats, the three matroids agree on the varying block; adjoining the common factor N gives all equivalences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3, Bergman fan of K_n and ultrametrics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let δ:[n]×[n]→R be a dissimilarity map, meaning δ(i,i)=0 and δ(i,j)=δ(j,i). Regard δ as a weight vector on the edges of the complete graph K_n. Then δ is an ultrametric, i.e. for every triple i,j,k two of δ(i,j), δ(i,k), δ(j,k) are equal and not less than the third, iff the corresponding edge-weight vector lies in the Bergman fan ~B(K_n) of the graphical matroid of K_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof identifies three equivalent conditions on an edge-weighting of K_n: (1) in every triangle the largest edge weight is achieved at least twice; (2) in every cycle the largest edge weight is achieved at least twice; (3) every edge belongs to some spanning tree of minimum total weight. Condition (1) is exactly the ultrametric condition. To show (1) implies (2), suppose a cycle v_1v_2...v_k has a unique largest edge, say v_1v_2. Applying (1) to triangle v_1v_2v_3 forces ω(v_1v_3)=ω(v_1v_2); applying it to v_1v_3v_4 forces ω(v_1v_4) to have the same value, and continuing reaches another edge of the original cycle with the same largest value, a contradiction. Condition (2) implies (3): given an edge f and a minimum spanning tree T, if f∉T then T∪f has a unique cycle; by (2) there is an edge e on that cycle with ω(e)≥ω(f), so T−e+f has no larger weight and hence is also a minimum spanning tree containing f. Finally, if (3) holds but some triangle has a unique largest edge e with other edges f,g, choose a minimum spanning tree T containing e. If T contains f, then it cannot contain g and replacing e by g gives a smaller spanning tree; if T contains neither f nor g, then at least one of f or g can replace e unless both form cycles in T−e, and those two cycles together with e would create a cycle already in T, impossible. Thus (3) forces (1). In a graphical matroid, M_ω has no loops exactly when every edge lies in a minimum-weight spanning tree, so (3) is precisely membership in ~B(K_n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 proposition, Bergman fan of K_n and tree space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T_n be the space of equidistant n-trees, decomposed into cells by combinatorial rooted tree type and internal edge lengths. Let f:T_n→~B(K_n) send an equidistant tree to its vector of pairwise leaf distances, regarded as weights on the edges of K_n. Then f is a piecewise-linear homeomorphism. Moreover, it identifies the decomposition of T_n by combinatorial tree type with the coarse decomposition of the Bergman fan ~B(K_n) by equality of the minimum-weight matroid M_ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: On a fixed combinatorial type of rooted tree, the distances between leaves are linear functions of the internal edge lengths; when an internal edge length is set to zero, the same linear formulas agree with the neighboring degenerate tree type, so f is piecewise linear. The cited ultrametric/tree theorem says equidistant trees are exactly ultrametrics, and Theorem 3 identifies ultrametrics with ~B(K_n), giving bijectivity and hence the homeomorphism in this polyhedral setting. For the compatibility with coarse cells, first fix a tree type. The branching pattern of every triple of leaves determines which edges in the corresponding triangle of K_n have maximum weight. This determines the maximum-weight edges of every cycle, because an edge is maximum in a cycle iff it is maximum in each triangle formed with a vertex of that cycle. Using the cycle-breaking version of the greedy algorithm for minimum spanning trees, start with K_n and repeatedly remove a maximum edge from a cycle; the possible resulting trees are exactly the minimum-weight spanning trees. Therefore a fixed tree type determines M_ω. Conversely, if M_ω is known and has no loops, then for any triangle with edges e,f,g one can choose a minimum spanning tree T containing e. At least one of T−e+f or T−e+g is a spanning tree. If such a replacement remains minimum, the replaced edge has the same weight as e and e is a maximum edge of the triangle; otherwise the replacement edge is heavier and e is not maximum. Applying this to all triangle edges recovers the triple branching data, from which the combinatorial tree type is reconstructed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary, partition lattice order complex subdivides tree space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the graphical matroid of the complete graph K_n, the order complex of the proper part of the partition lattice Π_n is a subdivision of T_n, the link of the origin in the space of phylogenetic trees. Consequently these complexes have the same homotopy type, namely a wedge of (n−1)! spheres of dimension n−3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The flats of the graphical matroid of K_n are in bijection with partitions of [n], and the lattice of flats is the partition lattice Π_n. By Theorem 1, the fine Bergman subdivision is the order complex of the proper part of Π_n. By the Section 4 proposition, the coarse subdivision of the Bergman fan is the tree-type decomposition of T_n. Since the fine subdivision refines the coarse subdivision, the partition-lattice order complex subdivides T_n. The homotopy type follows from the general Möbius-number formula for Π_n, where hatμ(Π_n)=(n−1)!.

proof_techniques.name: Stratify weights by flags of equal coordinates
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces arbitrary weight vectors by their ordered equality classes. A vector ω determines a flag ∅=F_0⊂F_1⊂...⊂F_{k+1}=[n], where the blocks F_i−F_{i−1} are exactly the sets of equal coordinates of ω, ordered by increasing weight. The greedy algorithm then shows that the minimum-weight bases depend only on this flag and are characterized by the rank increments r(F_i)−r(F_{i−1}).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete way to model the flat-lattice part of a tropical linear space: chains of flats become the cells of the fine Bergman subdivision. An order-reversing involution of the flat lattice can be tested first on these chains by reversing their order and applying F↦F^⊥.

proof_techniques.name: Detect looplessness by flatness of the flag
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 1 proves validity of a weight class by a two-sided loop test. If a set F_i in the flag is not a flat, an element in cl(F_i)−F_i can never appear in any minimum-weight basis and is therefore a loop of M_F. Conversely, if all F_i are flats, then for any element e in a block F_i−F_{i−1}, the greedy algorithm can be forced to choose e immediately after building a basis of F_{i−1}, because e is not in the closure of the flat F_{i−1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural inclusion of flats into the relative Dressian uses contractions with loops on a flat. This technique suggests reducing compatibility questions to whether elements become loops after passing to a weight class, contraction, or direct-sum minor, a condition expressible purely in the flat lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Factor minimum-weight matroids as direct sums of minors
proof_techniques.description: For a flag F, the minimum-weight matroid M_F splits as ⊕_i((M|F_i)/F_{i−1}). This isolates the contribution of each interval of the flag: the i-th block only sees the minor between F_{i−1} and F_i. In the flat-flag case, the lattice of flats of M_F is the product of the corresponding intervals [F_{i−1},F_i].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing anti-automorphism of L_M sends an interval [A,B] to an oppositely ordered interval [B^⊥,A^⊥]. This factorization is a natural place to look for a candidate extension on objects built from chains, minors, contractions, or direct sums.

proof_techniques.name: Use matroid polytopes and normal cones for coarse structure
proof_techniques.description: The paper views M_ω as the face of the matroid polytope P_M minimized by ω. Thus the coarser equivalence relation ω∼ω' iff M_ω=M_{ω'} is governed by the normal fan or spherical polar of P_M. The fine flag subdivision refines this polyhedral structure, while the coarse cells identify adjacent flag cones that produce the same face matroid.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the relative Dressian is studied as a poset or polyhedral object, this technique suggests looking for an involution at the level of face data, normal cones, or matroid subdivisions rather than only on individual flags.

proof_techniques.name: Analyze descent from fine flags to coarse cells using rank-two intervals
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2 gives a local criterion for when adjacent maximal flat flags define the same minimum-weight matroid: the only issue is the rank-two interval between the neighboring common flats, and equality occurs exactly when that interval is a diamond. This turns a geometric identification of cells into a purely order-theoretic condition in the lattice of flats.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any construction first defined on flat chains must respect identifications among chains if it is to descend to coarser tropical or Dressian data. Diamond intervals are invariant under lattice anti-automorphisms, so this criterion is useful for checking whether a flat-lattice involution is compatible with such quotients.

proof_techniques.name: Use complete-graph Bergman fans as explicit test models
proof_techniques.description: For the graphical matroid of K_n, the Bergman fan is identified with ultrametrics, equivalently with equidistant phylogenetic trees. The fine subdivision is the order complex of the partition lattice, while the coarse subdivision records only combinatorial tree type. The K_4 example is especially concrete: the fine subdivision is almost the Petersen graph, and the coarse subdivision removes the extra vertices corresponding to height coincidences not seen by tree type.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When searching for or testing an extension of a flat-lattice involution, these graphical examples provide explicit finite models where the fine/coarse distinction and the effect of flat-lattice symmetries can be computed by hand.

other_useful_info: Examples in the paper: for the graphical matroid M(K_n), flats are vertex-disjoint unions of complete subgraphs and are naturally identified with partitions of [n], so L_{M(K_n)}=Π_n ordered by refinement. For M(K_4), the fine subdivision of B(K_4) is a wedge of six 1-spheres; it is almost the Petersen graph, with three extra vertices labelled 13, 24, and 56 in the paper's edge labeling. In the coarse subdivision these extra vertices disappear because neighboring weight classes yield the same M_ω; Theorem 2 explains such identifications via diamond intervals. The paper cites Sturmfels [15] for Bergman complexes/tropical varieties of linear ideals, Björner [4] for homology and shellability of geometric-lattice order complexes, Speyer-Sturmfels [13] for tropical Grassmannians, and Billera-Holmes-Vogtmann [3] plus Semple-Steel [12] for the phylogenetic tree and ultrametric interpretations.
why_relevant: Shows how the Bergman complex is controlled by the order complex of the lattice of flats. This is useful for understanding the flat-lattice part of Dr(M) that the desired involution must extend.

title: Tropical Linear Spaces
overall_summary: Speyer defines tropical linear spaces via tropical Plücker vectors, equivalently valuated matroids, and identifies tropical Plücker vectors with regular matroidal subdivisions of hypersimplices. The paper develops the local matroidal structure of a tropical linear space: a point lies in the space exactly when its initial matroid is loop-free, links are controlled by lattices of flats, bounded faces correspond to loop-free and coloop-free initial matroids, and the tropical linear space determines its Plücker vector up to adding a scalar. The most directly relevant operation for an order-reversing map is ambient dualization: p is sent to p⊥ by complementing Plücker indices, the induced subdivision is sent by x_i ↦ 1 − x_i and matroid cells are replaced by dual matroid cells, and L ↦ L⊥ is stated to be an inclusion-reversing involution on the class of all tropical linear spaces in R^n. The paper also defines stable intersection by a tropical Plücker formula, proves that it is a tropical linear space, proves it lies in set-theoretic intersection and equals it under transversality, and realizes it as a limit of generic translates. These results give a precise comparison point for any attempted relative duality on tropical subspaces: one can test compatibility with ambient Plücker duality, matroid duality on initial cells, lattices of flats in links, and stable intersection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 and Remark 2.1: tropical Plücker vectors are exactly regular matroidal hypersimplex subdivisions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [n] = {1,...,n}, let Δ(d,n) be the hypersimplex conv(e_I : I ⊂ [n], |I| = d), where e_I = sum_{i in I} e_i, and let p = (p_I) be a real-valued function on the d-subsets of [n]. Let D_p be the regular subdivision of Δ(d,n) obtained by taking the lower faces of conv((e_I,p_I)) in Δ(d,n) × R and projecting to Δ(d,n). Then the following are equivalent. (1) p is a tropical Plücker vector: for every S ⊂ [n] of size d−2 and every four distinct i,j,k,l in [n] \ S, the minimum of p_{Sij}+p_{Skl}, p_{Sik}+p_{Sjl}, and p_{Sil}+p_{Sjk} is attained at least twice. (2) The one-skeleton of D_p is the same as the one-skeleton of Δ(d,n), equivalently every edge of every cell of D_p is an edge of Δ(d,n). (3) Every cell of D_p is matroidal, meaning it is P_M = conv(e_B : B is a basis of M) for some rank d matroid M on [n]. Remark 2.1 identifies this definition with valuated matroids over the tropical semiring (R,+,min).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), Speyer measures the length of a segment between vertices e_I and e_J by |I \ J|. He proves by induction on this length that no edge of D_p can have length at least 2. In the base case length 2, the two vertices are opposite vertices e_{Sij} and e_{Skl} of the octahedron on the six vertices e_{Sij}, e_{Sik}, e_{Sil}, e_{Sjk}, e_{Sjl}, e_{Skl}. The tropical Plücker relation exactly says that the induced subdivision of this octahedron is either the whole octahedron or one of the subdivisions into two square pyramids, and in none of these cases is the opposite diagonal an edge. For the induction step, if an edge e connects e_{ST} and e_{ST'} with |T| = |T'| = ℓ > 2, consider the face of the hypersimplex with vertices e_I satisfying S ⊂ I ⊂ S ∪ T ∪ T'. The edge e lies in a two-dimensional cell G; the other boundary path γ of G from e_{ST} to e_{ST'} cannot contain an edge of length ℓ, since then convexity would force the same midpoint as e. By induction all edges of γ have length 1. Their directions span a two-dimensional space generated by vectors of the form e_i − e_j, forcing the original edge direction to have length at most 2, contradicting ℓ > 2. For (2) implies (3), the paper invokes and reproves the Gelfand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova criterion: a subpolytope of a hypersimplex whose edges are hypersimplex edges is a matroid base polytope. The proof verifies the basis exchange axiom by taking two vertices e_I and e_J and an element j in J \ I, using the linear functional φ(x) = sum_{i in I} x_i + d x_j. The set P ∩ {φ ≥ d} is connected, so a path from e_I to e_J must start by exchanging in j. For (3) implies (1), if a tropical Plücker relation failed, the induced subdivision would contain a one-dimensional cell joining opposite octahedron vertices e_{Sij} and e_{Skl}, which is not a matroidal polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition and Proposition 2.3: loop-free initial matroid criterion for membership in L(p)
