key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Given a tropical Plücker vector p on d-subsets of [n], define the associated tropical d-plane L(p) in R^n by the condition that w = (w_1,...,w_n) lies in L(p) if and only if, for every J ⊂ [n] with |J| = d+1, the minimum of p_{J\{j}} + w_j over j in J is not unique. For w in R^n, let M_w be the matroid whose bases are the d-subsets I minimizing p_I − sum_{i in I} w_i; this is a matroid because it is the cell of D_p exposed by w. Then w belongs to L(p) if and only if M_w is loop-free, i.e. every element of [n] belongs to at least one basis of M_w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By replacing p_I by p_I − sum_{i in I} w_i and then subtracting a common constant, it suffices to prove the result for w = 0 and min p_I = 0. Then M_0 has bases precisely those I with p_I = 0. If M_0 has a loop j, choose a basis I = {i_1,...,i_d} of M_0. Then j is not in I and every d-subset obtained by replacing one i_r by j has positive p-value. For J = I ∪ {j}, the tropical linear relation has a unique minimum at the term indexed by I, so 0 is not in L(p). Conversely assume M_0 is loop-free. Fix any J of size d+1. The goal is to show that among the d-subsets of J at least two have p-value 0. The proof varies the weight vector along the line spanned by e_J = sum_{j in J} e_j, in the paper's notation M_s = M_{−s e_J}, and takes the extreme direction for which minimizing bases are contained in J. As s crosses finitely many breakpoints, M_s changes by restriction/contraction: between consecutive breakpoints one has M_s = M_{s_i}|J ⊕ M_{s_i}/J = M_{s_{i+1}}|([n]\J) ⊕ M_{s_{i+1}}/([n]\J), with the displayed formula as in the paper. This shows that if an element j in J is a loop at some stage, then it was already a loop at all earlier stages. Since no element is a loop in M_0, no j in J is a loop in the limiting matroid supported on J. A rank d matroid on the d+1 element set J with no loops has at least two bases, exactly giving non-uniqueness of the minimum for the J-th tropical linear form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4: local links are governed by lattices of flats
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L(p) be a tropical linear space, let P_M be a cell of the associated matroidal subdivision D_p, and let M∨ be the dual cell in the polyhedral subdivision of R^n by initial matroids. If v = M∨ / R(1,...,1) is a vertex of L(p) / R(1,...,1), then the link of L(p) / R(1,...,1) at v is homeomorphic to the order complex of the lattice of flats of M. More generally, if N∨ is a face of L(p) containing v, then N decomposes as a direct sum N = ⊕_{a=0}^{r−1} F_{a+1}/F_a for some flag of flats ∅ = F_0 ⊂ F_1 ⊂ ... ⊂ F_r = [n] in M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted from Ardila and Klivans. The normal fan of a matroid base polytope P_M is described by flags of flats of M; faces corresponding to loop-free initial matroids give the Bergman complex. After passing to the quotient by the line R(1,...,1), the loop-free part of the normal fan has a subdivision identified with the order complex of the proper part of the lattice of flats. A cone corresponding to a flag ∅ = F_0 ⊂ ... ⊂ F_r = [n] has initial matroid equal to the direct sum of the minors F_{a+1}/F_a, which gives the stated description of faces containing the vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5: matroid polytopes of direct sums are products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let M = ⊕_i M_i be its decomposition into connected components, with the M_i on disjoint ground sets. Then the matroid base polytope P_M is the direct product of the polytopes P_{M_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A basis of a direct sum M = ⊕_i M_i is exactly a union of bases, one chosen from each component M_i. Therefore the vertices e_B of P_M are precisely the Cartesian products of the vertices of the P_{M_i} in the corresponding coordinate subspaces, giving P_M = product_i P_{M_i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6: normal fans of faces of tropical linear spaces are controlled by braid fans
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M∨ be an r-dimensional face of a tropical linear space L(p). Then the normal fan to M∨ is a subfan of a coarsening of the r-dimensional braid fan Br. If M∨ is bounded modulo R(1,...,1), then M∨ is a Minkowski summand of the r-th permutahedron.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The normal fan to M∨ is the same as the link of the dual cell P_M inside the subdivision D_p. If P_M is contained in a larger matroidal cell P_{~M}, then the local cone of P_{~M} at P_M is the negative of the normal cone of P_{~M} dual to P_M. The normal fan of a matroid polytope is a coarsening of the braid fan, and the cones dual to codimension i faces are unions of cones of Br. Thus the normal fan to M∨ is a subfan of such a coarsening. If M∨ is bounded, its normal fan is complete. Since Br is the normal fan of the permutahedron, any polytope whose complete normal fan is coarsened by Br is a Minkowski summand of a sufficiently large multiple of the permutahedron.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8: a tropical linear space determines its tropical Plücker vector up to scalar
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = L(p) be a d-dimensional tropical linear space in R^n with tropical Plücker coordinates p_I. Then the polyhedral set L determines all p_I up to addition of a common scalar.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a (d−1)-subset S of [n]. The face F of Δ(d,n) spanned by the vertices e_{S∪{i}} for i not in S is an (n−d)-simplex, so it is not subdivided by D_p. It corresponds to a loop-free matroid Φ, hence to a face Φ∨ of L. This face has the form (R_{≥0}^S + R(1,...,1)) + q, where q_i = p_{S∪{i}} for i not in S, up to adding a multiple of (1,...,1). Speyer observes that no other face of L has this same recession cone, so L identifies q modulo R(1,...,1). Hence L determines all differences p_{S∪{i}} − p_{S∪{j}} for fixed S and i,j outside S. Since the graph of d-subsets connected by single exchanges is connected, these differences determine all p_I up to adding one global constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.9: tropical linear spaces are pure and contractible
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every d-dimensional tropical linear space L(p) in R^n is a pure d-dimensional polyhedral complex and is contractible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Purity is checked after quotienting by R(1,...,1). The link of any vertex v of L(p)/R(1,...,1) is, by the Ardila-Klivans result quoted in Proposition 2.4, subdivided as the order complex of the lattice of flats of a rank d matroid. This lattice is graded of length d, so its order complex is pure of dimension d−2; adding back the lineality gives pure dimension d. Contractibility follows because every tropical hyperplane is tropically convex in the sense of Develin-Sturmfels, and L(p) is an intersection of tropical hyperplanes. Tropical convex sets are contractible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.10: bounded faces are exactly loop-free and coloop-free initial matroids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a point w in a tropical linear space L(p), let M_w be its initial matroid and P_{M_w} the corresponding cell of the hypersimplex subdivision D_p. The following are equivalent. (1) w lies in a bounded face of L(p), modulo the line R(1,...,1). (2) P_{M_w} is an interior face of the subdivision D_p of Δ(d,n), i.e. it is contained in no facet of Δ(d,n). (3) M_w is both loop-free and coloop-free.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A matroid base polytope P_M lies in the hypersimplex facet x_i = 0 exactly when i is a loop of M, and lies in the facet x_i = 1 exactly when i is a coloop of M. Hence P_M is interior exactly when M has neither loops nor coloops. Duality between the regular subdivision D_p and the polyhedral subdivision of R^n says that an interior cell of D_p has a bounded dual cell, while a boundary cell has an unbounded dual cell. Proposition 2.3 already ensures that points of L(p) correspond to loop-free initial matroids, so the additional condition for boundedness is absence of coloops.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.12: ambient dualization of tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be a tropical Plücker vector on d-subsets of [n]. Define p⊥ on (n−d)-subsets by p⊥_I = p_{[n]\I}. Then p⊥ is again a tropical Plücker vector. The regular subdivision D_{p⊥} of Δ(n−d,n) is the image of D_p under the affine map (x_1,...,x_n) ↦ (1−x_1,...,1−x_n), and this map sends each matroid cell P_M to the base polytope of the dual matroid M*. If L⊥ denotes L(p⊥), then L ↦ L⊥ is an inclusion-reversing bijection from the set of tropical linear spaces in R^n to itself. Moreover, the bounded part of L⊥ is the negative of the bounded part of L, and if dim L = d then the bounded part of L has dimension at most min(d,n−d).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The complement map on vertices sends e_I in Δ(d,n) to e_{[n]\I} in Δ(n−d,n), which is the same as x ↦ 1−x on the affine hyperplane sum x_i = d. Since p⊥ assigns to the complementary vertex the same height as p, the lower hull subdivision is carried to D_{p⊥}. A set of d-subsets is the set of bases of M exactly when the complementary (n−d)-subsets are bases of M*, so matroidal cells go to dual matroidal cells; Proposition 2.2 then gives that p⊥ is tropical Plücker. The assignment is involutive. The paper states that one easily checks it is inclusion-reversing on tropical linear spaces. For the bounded-part claim, compare initial matroids at w for p and at −w for p⊥: minimizing p_{[n]\J} − sum_{j in J}(−w_j) is equivalent, up to adding the constant sum_i w_i, to minimizing p_I − sum_{i in I} w_i with I = [n]\J. Thus the initial matroids are dual. Being loop-free and coloop-free is self-dual, so by Proposition 2.10 the bounded cells correspond under w ↦ −w. Since L has dimension d and L⊥ has dimension n−d, their common bounded complex has dimension at most min(d,n−d).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 and Corollary 3.2: stable intersection is a tropical linear space and its cells are matroid intersections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = L(p) and L' = L(p') be tropical linear spaces in R^n of dimensions d and d', with d+d' ≥ n. For each J ⊂ [n] of size d+d'−n, define q_J = min(p_I + p'_{I'}), where the minimum is over d-subsets I and d'-subsets I' satisfying I ∩ I' = J; equivalently these conditions force I ∪ I' = [n]. Then q is a tropical Plücker vector, and Speyer denotes L(q) by L ∩_stable L'. Moreover, every cell of the hypersimplex subdivision associated to q is of the form P_{M ∧ M'} for cells P_M of D_p and P_{M'} of D_{p'}, where M ∧ M' is the matroid intersection operation when it has the required rank; otherwise the corresponding intersection is empty.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let P and P' be the polyhedra above the lower convex hulls of the lifted points (p_I,e_I) and (p'_{I'},e_{I'}). Embed Δ(d+d'−n,n) into the Minkowski sum Δ(d,n)+Δ(d',n) by ι(e_J) = e_J + (1,...,1). Consider Q = (P+P') ∩ (R × ι(Δ(d+d'−n,n))). The lower faces of Q project to the regular subdivision induced by q, since the height at J is exactly the minimum of p_I+p'_{I'} over decompositions with I ∩ I' = J. A face of this subdivision has the form R = ι(Δ(d+d'−n,n)) ∩ (P_M + P_{M'}). Its vertices correspond to pairs of bases I of M and I' of M' with I ∪ I' = [n], and the projected vertex is I ∩ I'. The matroid intersection M ∧ M' is defined so that its spanning sets are U ∩ U', where U and U' span M and M'. If M ∧ M' has rank d+d'−n, R is exactly P_{M ∧ M'}; if its rank is larger, no such basis pairs exist and R is empty. Thus all cells are matroidal, so q is tropical Plücker by Proposition 2.2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: transverse matroid intersections are loop-free and built by parallel connection
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and M' be loop-free matroids on [n] of ranks d and d'. Write their connected decompositions as M = ⊕_{i in I} M_i and M' = ⊕_{i' in I'} M'_{i'}, with ground sets S_i and S'_{i'}. Form a bipartite graph Γ(M,M') with vertex set I ⊔ I' and one edge labelled by each element e of [n], joining the component of M containing e to the component of M' containing e. Suppose M and M' are transverse in the sense that Γ(M,M') is a forest with no multiple edges. Then M ∧ M' is a loop-free matroid of rank d+d'−n. Its connected components are in bijection with the connected components of Γ(M,M'), and it is obtained by repeatedly taking parallel connections of the component matroids M_i and M'_{i'} along the elements labelling the edges of Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Speyer first gives an explicit construction for any forest F whose vertices v carry connected loop-free matroids M(v) on subsets S(v), with adjacent vertices sharing exactly the edge label. Repeatedly contract an edge e = (v,w) of F and replace M(v), M(w) by their parallel connection along e. The resulting matroid is independent of the order of contractions. To prove independence and identify its bases, he characterizes a basis U by choosing bases B(v) of all M(v) such that for each ground element e exactly one of four possibilities holds: if e is an edge (v,w) and e is in U, then e lies in both B(v) and B(w); if e is not a forest edge and e is in U, then e lies in the unique B(v); if e is an edge and not in U, it lies in exactly one of B(v),B(w); and if e is not an edge and not in U, it lies in no B(v). This condition is unchanged by contracting an edge and taking a parallel connection. Applying this to Γ(M,M'), the resulting bases are exactly B ∩ B' where B and B' are bases of M and M' with B ∪ B' = [n], which are the bases of M ∧ M'. Parallel connection preserves loop-freeness, and the cardinality of B ∩ B' is d+d'−n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4: stable intersection lies in ordinary intersection and equals it under transversality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L and L' be tropical linear spaces in R^n of dimensions d and d', with d+d' ≥ n. Then L ∩_stable L' is contained in the set-theoretic intersection L ∩ L'. If L and L' meet transversely, then L ∩_stable L' = L ∩ L'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix w in R^n. Let M∨, (M')∨, and N∨ be the cells of the dual subdivisions containing w for L, L', and L ∩_stable L', respectively; the dual primal cells are P_M, P_{M'}, and P_N. By the stable-intersection construction, P_N = (P_M + P_{M'}) ∩ ι(Δ(d+d'−n,n)), and N = M ∧ M' when nonempty. If M has a loop e, then every point of P_M has e-coordinate 0, and the corresponding coordinate restrictions force e to be a loop of N. The same holds if M' has a loop. Therefore, if N is loop-free, then M and M' are loop-free; by Proposition 2.3, w in L ∩_stable L' implies w in L and w in L'. Conversely assume L and L' meet transversely and w belongs to L ∩ L'. Then M and M' are loop-free. Writing them as direct sums of connected components, the affine spans of M∨ and (M')∨ impose equalities x_i − x_j = constant inside components. Transversality forces the graph Γ(M,M') of Lemma 3.3 to be a forest without multiple edges. Lemma 3.3 then gives that N = M ∧ M' is loop-free, so w lies in the stable intersection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6: stable intersection is the limit of generic translated intersections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L and L' be tropical linear spaces in R^n. For a generic vector v in R^n, L and L' + v meet transversely. Moreover, L ∩_stable L' is the limit, as v tends to 0 through generic vectors, of the ordinary intersections L ∩ (L' + v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each pair of faces F of L and F' of L', if their affine spans do not together span R^n, then the set of translations v for which F meets F' + v lies in a proper affine subspace of R^n. There are finitely many pairs of faces, so a generic v avoids all non-transverse incidences; hence all intersections are transverse. The Plücker coordinates of L' + v are p'_I + sum_{i in I} v_i, so the stable-intersection Plücker coordinates vary continuously with v. For generic v, Proposition 3.4 gives L ∩ (L' + v) = L ∩_stable (L' + v). Taking v → 0 and using the continuity of the tropical Plücker-coordinate formula gives the stated limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1: initial-ideal criterion for tropicalization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let κ be an algebraically closed field, let K be an algebraic closure of κ((t)) with its valuation v, and let I be an ideal in K[x_1,...,x_n]. For w in R^n, define In_w I in κ[x_1,...,x_n] by substituting x_i ↦ t^{w_i}x_i in each f in I and taking the lowest t-order part, then taking the ideal generated by all such initial forms. Then Trop(I), the closure of the valuations of points of the variety of I in (K*)^n, is exactly the set of w such that In_w I contains no monomial.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Theorem 2.1 of Speyer-Sturmfels, Tropical Grassmannian. It is used as the bridge between combinatorial tropical linear spaces and tropicalizations of linear spaces over the valued field K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2: tropicalization of an ordinary linear space equals the combinatorial L(p)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a d-dimensional linear subspace of K^n in the finite-Plücker-coordinate situation considered in the paper, with Plücker coordinates P_I and p_I = v(P_I). Then the tropicalization Trop(L) is equal to the tropical linear space L(p) defined by these tropical Plücker coordinates.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every point X in L satisfies the ordinary linear Plücker equations sum_r (−1)^r P_{j_1...hat{j_r}...j_{d+1}} X_{j_r} = 0. Taking valuations shows that v(X) satisfies the corresponding tropical linear equations, so Trop(L) is contained in L(p). Conversely, take w in L(p). After rescaling coordinates, reduce to w = 0 and min p_I = 0. Let P_I(0) be the residue of P_I when p_I = 0; these residues satisfy the ordinary Plücker relations and define an initial linear space L(0) over κ, equal to In_w L. The d-subsets with p_I = 0 form the bases of the initial matroid M_w. Since w is in L(p), Proposition 2.3 says M_w is loop-free, so L(0) is not contained in any coordinate hyperplane and its ideal contains no monomial. The initial-ideal criterion of Theorem 4.1 then implies w lies in Trop(L).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3: stable intersection matches realizable intersection after generic diagonal rescaling
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L and L' be ordinary linear subspaces of K^n. Then there exists a linear space ~L' obtained from L' by a diagonal scaling with constants in κ* such that Trop(~L') = Trop(L') and Trop(L) ∩_stable Trop(L') = Trop(L) ∩_stable Trop(~L') = Trop(L ∩ ~L'). If Trop(L) and Trop(L') meet transversely, then already Trop(L) ∩_stable Trop(L') = Trop(L ∩ L') without changing L'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let P_I, P'_I, and Q_J be Plücker coordinates of L, L', and L ∩ L'. Classically Q_J = sum_{I∩I'=J} ± P_I P'_{I'}. The tropical stable-intersection coordinate is the minimum of v(P_I P'_{I'}) over the same terms. Equality v(Q_J) = that minimum can fail only by cancellation among lowest-order terms. If ~L' = diag(u_1,...,u_n)L' with u_i in κ*, then ~P'_I = P'_I product_{i in I} u_i. For generic u_i, no such leading cancellations occur in any Q_J, so the tropicalization of the classical intersection has the stable-intersection Plücker vector. In the transverse case, Speyer proves that cancellation cannot occur because each relevant sum has a unique lowest-valuation term. The uniqueness argument chooses a facet P_N of the stable subdivision containing J, takes w in N∨, and lets M,M' be the initial matroids for L,L'. Transversality makes Γ(M,M') a forest. If two pairs of bases (B_1,B'_1) and (B_2,B'_2) contributed to the same minimum with B_s ∩ B'_s = J and B_s ∪ B'_s = [n], the difference of their incidence functions defines a 1-cocycle on Γ. Since Γ is acyclic, this cocycle is zero, forcing B_1 = B_2 and B'_1 = B'_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4: any matroid occurs as a face of a tropical Plücker subdivision
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank d matroid on [n], and let ρ(S) be the rank in M of the flat spanned by S, equivalently the matroid rank of S. Define p_I = −ρ(I) for every d-subset I of [n]. Then p satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. The original matroid M appears as a cell of the induced matroidal subdivision of Δ(d,n), namely the cell where p_I is minimal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a tropical Plücker relation indexed by S of size d−2 and i,j,k,l outside S, write ρ(Sij) = ρ_{M/S}(ij) + ρ(S). Thus the check reduces to the case d = 2 and n = 4 for the contracted matroid M/S, where it is a direct verification of rank possibilities. Since p_I = −ρ(I), the minimal value is −d and is attained exactly on the d-subsets I that are bases of M. Therefore the minimizing face is P_M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.6: constructible tropical linear spaces are closed under coordinate deletion/contraction minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For disjoint subsets S,T of [n], let Δ(d,n)\S/T denote the face of Δ(d,n) cut out by x_i = 0 for i in S and x_i = 1 for i in T. If p is a tropical Plücker vector, define p\S/T on (d−|T|)-subsets I of [n] \ (S ∪ T) by (p\S/T)_I = p_{I∪T}; this gives a tropical linear space denoted L\S/T. A tropical linear space is called constructible if it can be built from tropical hyperplanes, equivalently points, by successive dualization and transverse intersection. If L is constructible, then L\S/T is constructible for all disjoint S,T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a hyperplane, every minor L\S/T is either a hyperplane or the whole ambient space, hence constructible. If the claim holds for L, then it holds for L⊥ because (L⊥)\S/T = (L\T/S)⊥. If the claim holds for L and L', then it holds for their stable transverse intersection because Speyer verifies the identity (L ∩_stable L')\S/T = (L\∅/(S∪T)) ∩_stable (L'\S/T). These three observations match the recursive definition of constructibility.

proof_techniques.name: Valuated matroid and hypersimplex subdivision dictionary
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces tropical Plücker equations by the geometry of regular subdivisions of the hypersimplex. A vector p of tropical Plücker coordinates is studied through the lower-hull subdivision D_p of Δ(d,n), and the tropical Plücker relations are equivalent to every cell of D_p being a matroid base polytope. This converts algebraic-looking tropical equations into matroidal combinatorics of cells, edges, faces, and dual complexes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Elements of a Dressian or relative Dressian can often be treated as valuated matroids or matroidal subdivisions. Any proposed order-reversing involution can be tested on the induced subdivision: does it send cells to the expected dual or flat-complement cells, and does it preserve the condition of lying inside the fixed ambient tropical space?

proof_techniques.name: Initial matroid loop and coloop tests
proof_techniques.description: For a point w, Speyer considers the cell exposed by the linear functional p_I − sum_{i in I} w_i and calls its matroid M_w. The criterion w in L(p) iff M_w is loop-free gives a local matroidal membership test for the tropical linear space. Boundedness is similarly local: a face is bounded modulo the diagonal iff M_w is both loop-free and coloop-free.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a relative tropical subspace contained in Trop(M), containment can plausibly be checked by initial matroids. A candidate duality extending F ↦ F⊥ should preserve the appropriate loop-free conditions under the flat-lattice involution or under matroid duality of initial cells.

proof_techniques.name: Ambient Plücker dualization by complementing bases
proof_techniques.description: The paper's dualization sends p_I to p⊥_J = p_{[n]\J}. On subdivisions this is the affine map x ↦ 1−x between Δ(d,n) and Δ(n−d,n), and on cells it sends a matroid base polytope P_M to P_{M*}. On tropical linear spaces it is an involutive inclusion-reversing bijection L ↦ L⊥, and the bounded complex of L⊥ is the negative of the bounded complex of L.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the natural first comparison for the desired order-reversing involution on a relative Dressian. If the given flat involution on L(M) is induced or mirrored by matroid duality, one can try to restrict, conjugate, or modify Speyer's ambient duality so that it preserves tropical subspaces of Trop(M).

proof_techniques.name: Links as order complexes of flat lattices
proof_techniques.description: At a vertex dual to a matroid cell P_M, the link of the tropical linear space is homeomorphic to the order complex of the lattice of flats of M. Faces containing the vertex correspond to flags of flats, with initial matroid decomposing as the direct sum of successive minors F_{a+1}/F_a. Thus flat-lattice combinatorics appears literally as the local combinatorics of tropical linear spaces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem starts with an order-reversing involution on the lattice of flats, this local model suggests checking whether the involution gives an anti-automorphism of the relevant links or Bergman complexes, and whether such local anti-automorphisms glue across the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Stable intersection through Minkowski sums and matroid intersection
proof_techniques.description: Speyer defines stable intersection by the tropical Plücker formula q_J = min(p_I+p'_{I'}) over I ∩ I' = J. The proof that q is tropical Plücker uses the Minkowski sum of the lower hulls for p and p' and identifies cells with matroid intersections M ∧ M'. Under transversality, these matroid intersections are loop-free exactly when the original initial matroids are loop-free, so stable intersection equals ordinary set-theoretic intersection.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If relative tropical subspaces are described as intersections inside a fixed ambient Trop(M), stable intersection may provide a controlled operation for constructing dual or complementary subspaces. The matroid-intersection cell description gives a way to verify whether such operations remain inside the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Generic translation to reduce to transverse intersections
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.6 shows that for generic v, L and L' + v meet transversely, and L ∩_stable L' is the limit of L ∩ (L' + v) as v tends to 0. The proof uses the fact that non-transverse incidences for finitely many pairs of faces occur only on a finite union of proper affine subspaces of translation parameters, while Plücker coordinates vary continuously under translation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a desired involution or complement is hard to define on non-transverse configurations, one can try defining it first generically and then taking a stable limit. This is potentially useful for relative Dressians, where containment relations may degenerate on walls.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering Plücker coordinates from the tropical linear space
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.8 proves that L(p) determines p up to adding a global scalar by examining special recession cones corresponding to faces of the hypersimplex containing a fixed (d−1)-subset. Thus the geometric polyhedral set carries the same information as the projective tropical Plücker vector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing map on tropical linear spaces can be studied equivalently as a map on projective valuated matroids, provided one is in Speyer's finite-coordinate setting. This can make extension from flats more rigid: the image of enough distinguished subspaces may force the image Plücker vector.

proof_techniques.name: Realizable lifting and generic diagonal rescaling
proof_techniques.description: For linear spaces over a valued field, Speyer proves the combinatorial tropical linear space L(p) is the actual tropicalization. For intersections, generic diagonal rescaling by residue-field units removes leading-term cancellations, so stable intersection matches tropicalization of classical intersection; under transverse tropical intersection, no rescaling is needed because there is a unique minimal term.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In realizable cases, one could try to construct the desired order-reversing involution using ordinary linear algebra, such as orthogonal complement under a bilinear form, and then tropicalize. The paper supplies precise results for when tropicalization respects dual Plücker coordinates and intersections.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that L(p) is essentially Dress's tight span of the valuated matroid p, up to sign conventions and quotienting by the diagonal. It cites Dress-Wenzel for valuated matroids, Ardila-Klivans for Bergman complexes and flat-lattice links, and Speyer-Sturmfels for the tropical Grassmannian and the initial-ideal criterion. Section 4 gives several citable nonrealizability phenomena: matroidal subdivisions can contain nonrealizable matroid cells, can fail global realizability even if all cells are realizable, can be nonregular, and can be induced by both realizable and nonrealizable height vectors. Proposition 4.4 is especially broad: every matroid can occur as a cell in some tropical Plücker subdivision, so local matroidal behavior in tropical linear spaces is highly general. The paper does not formulate relative Dressians, but its ambient dualization and stable intersection are the main operations directly comparable to a relative order-reversing involution.
why_relevant: Foundational source on tropical linear spaces, tropical Plucker vectors, dualization, stable intersection, and examples. The dualization operation here is a natural candidate or comparison point for any order-reversing operation on Dr(M).

title: Matroid Polytopes, Nested Sets and Bergman Fans
overall_summary: The paper gives a polyhedral and combinatorial construction of the Bergman fan of a matroid as a subfan of the normal fan of the matroid polytope. Its central result is that, for every building set in the lattice of flats, the associated nested set fan is a unimodular triangulation of the Bergman fan; locally at a basis this is proved by identifying the local Bergman fan with the normal fan of a Minkowski sum of simplices. The paper also identifies the face of the matroid polytope associated to a flat as a product/direct sum of a restriction and a contraction, and characterizes facet-defining flats by connectedness of these two minors. For the original problem, the most useful extracted tools are the explicit control of Trop(M) via flats, contractions, interval minors, and nested-set decompositions, especially Theorem 4.4, which decomposes initial matroids on nested cones as direct sums of matroids attached to intervals in the lattice of flats.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank r matroid on ground set [n] = {1,...,n}, with set of bases also denoted M. For a basis σ write e_σ = sum_{i in σ} e_i in R^n, and let P_M = conv{e_σ : σ is a basis of M}. Let Δ = {x in R^n : x_i ≥ 0 for all i and x_1+...+x_n = r}. If rank(F) denotes the matroid rank of a flat F ⊆ [n], then P_M is exactly the set of all x in Δ satisfying sum_{i in F} x_i ≤ rank(F) for every flat F of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every facet inequality of P_M may be written as sum a_i x_i ≤ b. Since every edge of a matroid polytope is parallel to some e_i − e_j, the normal vector of a facet can only impose equalities a_i = a_j along edge directions lying in that facet. After adding a multiple of the affine equation sum x_i = r and scaling, the normal vector can be assumed to be a 0/1 vector, hence the inequality has the form sum_{i in G} x_i ≤ b_G for some subset G of [n]. The optimal right side over basis vectors is max{|σ ∩ G| : σ basis of M}, which equals rank(G), because every independent subset of G can be extended to a basis of M. If F is the flat spanned by G, then G ⊆ F and rank(G) = rank(F), so the flat inequality for F implies the inequality for G. Therefore the inequalities indexed by flats, together with the simplex equations and nonnegativity, cut out exactly P_M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid M on [n], define two elements i,j in [n] to be equivalent if there is a circuit C of M with {i,j} ⊆ C. The equivalence classes are the connected components of M, and c(M) denotes their number. Then the dimension of the matroid polytope P_M is n − c(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The affine span of P_M is generated by differences e_σ − e_τ between basis indicator vectors. Such differences are spanned by elementary basis-exchange directions e_i − e_j, where i and j can be exchanged between two bases. The elements i and j are exchange-connected in this sense exactly when they lie in a common circuit, i.e. when they are in the same connected component of M. Thus the linear space parallel to aff(P_M) is the direct sum, over connected components, of the hyperplanes in the coordinate subspaces of those components whose coordinate sums are zero. Its dimension is the sum over components C of |C|−1, namely n−c(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank r matroid on [n]. For w in R^n, let M_w be the matroid whose bases are the bases σ of M maximizing the w-weight sum_{i in σ} w_i, and let P_M^w be the face of P_M on which the linear functional x ↦ sum_i w_i x_i is maximized. The following are equivalent: (1) w lies in the Bergman fan B̃(M), meaning that for every circuit C of M the minimum of {w_i : i in C} is attained at least twice; (2) M_w has no loops; (3) every element i in [n] lies in some basis of M_w; (4) P_M^w has nonempty intersection with the relative interior of the simplex Δ = {x_i ≥ 0, sum x_i = r}; (5) the functional sum_i w_i x_i attains its maximum over P_M at a point of ∂P_M \ ∂Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Conditions (2) and (3) are equivalent by the definition of a loop in a matroid: an element is a loop precisely when it belongs to no basis. Conditions (3) and (4) are equivalent because P_M^w is the convex hull of the indicator vectors of the bases of M_w; this convex hull contains a point with all coordinates positive exactly when the union of those bases is all of [n]. Conditions (4) and (5) are the same geometric assertion, since P_M^w is the maximum face and points in the interior of Δ avoid the boundary of the ambient simplex. For the equivalence with (1), if the Bergman circuit condition fails, then there is a circuit C and an element i in C with w_i strictly smaller than all w_j for j in C\{i}. Whenever a basis contains i, circuit exchange along C replaces i by some j of larger w-weight, so no maximum-weight basis contains i; hence i is a loop of M_w. Conversely, if i is a loop of M_w, the paper uses the standard matroid exchange/circuit characterization to produce a circuit C containing i such that w_i is strictly smaller than the other weights on C, so the Bergman condition fails.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is a connected matroid on [n]. Let F be a flat of rank s. Define the restriction M[∅,F] to be the rank s matroid with bases σ ∩ F, where σ runs over bases of M with |σ ∩ F| = s. Define the contraction M[F,[n]] to be the rank r−s matroid with bases σ\F, where σ runs over bases σ of M. Let P_M^F be the face of P_M on which the flat inequality sum_{i in F} x_i ≤ rank(F) is tight. Then the matroid associated to this face is M_F = M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]] = {σ basis of M : |σ ∩ F| = s}. The flat F is a flacet, meaning that P_M^F is a facet of P_M, if and only if both M[∅,F] and M[F,[n]] are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality sum_{i in F} x_i = s selects precisely those basis vectors e_σ for which σ contains a basis of F, equivalently |σ ∩ F| = s. Such a basis decomposes uniquely into a basis of the restriction to F and a basis of the contraction by F, giving the direct-sum decomposition M_F = M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]]. Consequently P_M^F is the direct product P_{M[∅,F]} × P_{M[F,[n]]}. By Proposition 2.4 its dimension is |F| − c(M[∅,F]) plus n−|F| − c(M[F,[n]]). Since M is connected, P_M has dimension n−1, so P_M^F is a facet exactly when this sum is n−2. Both component counts are positive integers, so this happens exactly when both minors are connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let P_M^* be the polar dual of its matroid polytope P_M, after quotienting by the line R(1,...,1) or equivalently intersecting the normal fan with the sphere S = {w in R^n : sum_i w_i = 0 and sum_i w_i^2 = 1}. For each face Γ of P_M^*, let M_Γ be the initial matroid M_w for any w in the corresponding relatively open cone of the normal fan of P_M. Then the Bergman complex B(M) is the subcomplex of ∂P_M^* consisting of all faces Γ such that M_Γ has no loop. If M is connected, the vertices of B(M) are indexed by the flacets of M. Moreover B(M) is homotopy equivalent to ∂P_M \ ∂Δ, where Δ is the ambient hypersimplex/simplex containing P_M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion is a direct translation of Proposition 2.5: a normal cone belongs to the Bergman fan exactly when its associated initial matroid is loopless. Vertices of the dual polytope correspond to facets of P_M, and among the non-simplex boundary facets the relevant facet-defining flats are precisely the flacets, giving the vertex statement for connected M. The homotopy equivalence is the standard Alexander-duality-type relation between a subcomplex of the boundary of a polytope and the complementary co-complex in the boundary of the dual polytope; barycentric subdivision gives an explicit proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.1, 3.2, 3.5 and Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a finite lattice with bottom element 0 and top element 1. A subset G of L_{>0} is a building set if, for every X in L_{>0}, writing max G_{≤X} = {G_1,...,G_k}, there is a poset isomorphism product_j [0,G_j] ≅ [0,X] whose j-th factor embeds by the natural inclusion. If G contains 1, a subset S of G is nested if, whenever X_1,...,X_t are pairwise incomparable elements of S with t ≥ 2, their join X_1∨...∨X_t does not belong to G. The nested sets form an abstract simplicial complex Ñ(L,G), a cone with apex 1; its link is denoted N(L,G). If L is atomic with atoms A_1,...,A_n, each G in G has a characteristic vector v_G in R^n recording which atoms lie below G, and the cones R_{≥0}{v_G : G in S}, for nested sets S, form a simplicial fan Σ̃(L,G). Proposition 3.6 states: (1) for any atomic L and building set G, Σ̃(L,G) is unimodular; (2) if H ⊆ G are building sets in L, then Σ̃(L,G) is obtained from Σ̃(L,H) by a sequence of stellar subdivisions, and all such fans have the same support; (3) for any atomic L and building set G, N(L,G) is homeomorphic to the order complex Δ(L) of the proper part of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes these as known results from Feichtner-Yuzvinsky and Feichtner-Müller rather than reproving them. The construction uses the factorization property of a building set to ensure that nested sets give linearly independent characteristic vectors and that cones meet along common faces. Enlarging a building set corresponds combinatorially to inserting additional building-set elements and geometrically to stellar subdivision at the corresponding ray. Since stellar subdivision preserves support and homeomorphism type, all nested set complexes for a fixed atomic lattice have the same support and topology as the order complex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a family of subsets of [r]. Then F is a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] if and only if F contains every singleton {i}, i in [r], and satisfies the closure condition: whenever F,F′ in F have F ∩ F′ nonempty, their union F ∪ F′ also lies in F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For X ⊆ [r], the set F_{≤X} consists of members of F contained in X. The product decomposition required in the definition of a building set says exactly that the maximal elements of F_{≤X} are pairwise disjoint and have union X. Having all singletons ensures the union condition for every X. The pairwise-disjointness of maximal elements is equivalent to saying that two overlapping members of F cannot both remain separate below their union; by repeatedly applying the condition F∩F′ nonempty implies F∪F′ in F, overlapping pieces merge into a larger member of F. This is precisely the Boolean building-set condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any family F of subsets of [r], there is a unique minimal enlargement bF of F that is a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r]. The paper calls bF the building closure of F. Explicitly, bF consists of all singletons together with all subsets X of [r] for which the simplicial complex with facets max F_{≤X} is connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a building set contains F, Lemma 3.9 forces it to contain every union of overlapping members of F, iterated as necessary, and also every singleton. For a fixed X, such forced unions are exactly detected by connectedness of the simplicial complex whose facets are the maximal F-members contained in X. The family bF defined by these connected subsets satisfies the Boolean building-set overlap condition of Lemma 3.9, hence is itself a building set. Minimality and uniqueness follow because every building set containing F must contain all the subsets just described.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12 and Corollary 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a family F of subsets of [r], let Δ_F = {x in R^r : x_i ≥ 0, sum_i x_i = 1, and x_i = 0 for i notin F} be the face of the standard simplex supported on F, and let Δ_Family = sum_{F in F} Δ_F be their Minkowski sum. Then Δ_Family consists of all nonnegative x in R^r with sum_i x_i = |F| and, for every G ⊆ [r], satisfying sum_{i in G} x_i ≥ |{F in F : F ⊆ G}|. It is enough to impose these inequalities for G in the building closure bF. If [r] belongs to F, then Δ_Family has dimension r−1 and the irredundant facets are indexed by bF.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each summand Δ_F is a face of the standard simplex, so every edge of the Minkowski sum is parallel to some e_i−e_j; thus the polytope is a Minkowski summand of the permutohedron and is cut out by inequalities involving coordinate sums over subsets G. The minimum of sum_{i in G} x_i on the summand Δ_F is 1 exactly when F ⊆ G and is 0 otherwise. Summing over all F gives the stated lower bound. The face where the inequality for G is tight decomposes as the Minkowski sum of the simplices Δ_F for F ⊆ G plus the simplices Δ_{F\G} for F not contained in G. For such a face to be a facet, the subfamily inside G must be connected in the sense of the building closure. If [r] is itself a summand, the full polytope is (r−1)-dimensional, and the same face-dimension calculation shows that every G in bF gives an actual facet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] and assume [r] belongs to F. Let Δ_Family = sum_{F in F} Δ_F be the Minkowski sum of the corresponding faces of the standard simplex. Then Δ_Family is an (r−1)-dimensional simple polytope. Its normal fan is a unimodular simplicial fan, and this fan is combinatorially isomorphic to the nested set complex N(2^[r],F).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F is already its own building closure and contains [r], Corollary 3.13 gives an (r−1)-dimensional polytope whose facets are indexed by the proper members of F. Pick a generic linear functional w and relabel so w_1 < w_2 < ... < w_r. The vertex v minimizing w on Δ_Family has coordinate v_i equal to the number of sets F in F whose smallest element is i. For a building-set member G, the facet inequality indexed by G is tight at v exactly when every F whose w-minimal element lies in G is actually contained in G. This happens precisely for the sets G_i = union{F in F : min(F)=i}. The building-set property ensures each G_i lies in F. Since G_1 = [r], the proper tight facets at v are G_2,...,G_r, exactly r−1 of them, so v is simple; by relabeling, every vertex is simple. These tight-facet collections are exactly the maximal nested sets, obtained recursively by splitting into maximal proper nested pieces. Finally, the edge directions are among the roots e_i−e_j, a unimodular configuration, so the normal fan is unimodular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any family F of subsets of [r] with building closure bF, the normal fan of Δ_bF triangulates the normal fan of Δ_F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F ⊆ bF, the Minkowski sum Δ_F is a Minkowski summand of Δ_bF. For polytopes, taking a larger Minkowski sum refines the normal fan. Theorem 3.14 says that the normal fan of Δ_bF is simplicial when bF is a building set, so this refinement is a triangulation of the possibly nonsimplicial normal fan of Δ_F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] with [r] in F. An F-tree is a rooted tree whose nodes ν are labelled by nonempty, pairwise disjoint subsets T_ν of [r], such that for each non-root node ν the union T_{≤ν} of all labels in the subtree below ν belongs to F. For a tree T, let sets(T) = {T_{≤ν} : ν is a non-root node}. Then for every nested set S of F there is a unique F-tree T such that sets(T)=S, and the nested set complex is exactly {sets(T) : T is an F-tree}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given an F-tree, the nonempty differences T_ν = T_{≤ν} \ T_{<ν} force the associated subsets to satisfy the nestedness condition: incomparable subtree unions cannot have a join lying in F without contradicting the tree decomposition. Conversely, starting from a nested set S, include [r] for the coned version and let G_2,...,G_k be the maximal proper elements of S. Their union cannot be all of [r] unless it violates nestedness, so the root receives the nonempty label [r]\(G_2∪...∪G_k). The restrictions of F and S to each G_i again form a Boolean building-set/nested-set pair, so the construction recurses inside each G_i. This gives existence, and the maximal proper elements at each recursive step are forced, giving uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be any matroid and let G be a building set in its lattice of flats L_M, with the nested-set conventions of Section 3. Then the nested set fan Σ̃(L_M,G) refines the Bergman fan B̃(M). Equivalently, after quotienting by the line R(1,...,1) and intersecting with the unit sphere, the geometric realization Σ(L_M,G) of the nested set complex N(L_M,G) is a triangulation of the Bergman complex B(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to basis charts. For a basis σ of M, consider the local Bergman complex B_σ(M), the intersection of B(M) with the facet of the dual matroid polytope corresponding to σ. Theorem 4.2 identifies B_σ(M) with the normal fan of a Minkowski sum of simplices determined by the fundamental circuits of elements outside σ, and identifies the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ) with a simplicial refinement of that normal fan. Now take any point w on the sphere and any basis σ of the initial matroid M_w. Then w lies in the support of the global nested set complex if and only if it lies in the support of the localized nested set complex for σ; by Theorem 4.2 this is equivalent to lying in the local Bergman complex for σ; and this is equivalent to w lying in B(M). Gluing these local triangulations over all bases gives the global refinement.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is a connected rank r matroid and σ is a basis of M. Let L_M(σ) be the sublattice of flats of M that are spanned by subsets of σ; it is a Boolean lattice of rank r. For a building set G in L_M, let G_σ be the flats in G that are spanned by subsets of σ. Let B_σ(M) be the local Bergman complex obtained by intersecting B(M) with the facet of P_M^* dual to the vertex e_σ of P_M. Then the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ) triangulates B_σ(M). Both complexes are homeomorphic to an (r−2)-sphere, and each is naturally realized as the boundary complex of an (r−1)-dimensional polytope.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Relabel so σ = {1,...,r}. For each i outside σ, let F_i ⊆ σ be the subset such that F_i ∪ {i} is the fundamental circuit of i with respect to σ. If w lies in the local Bergman fan where σ is a maximum-weight basis of M_w, then basis exchange gives w_i ≤ w_j for all j in F_i, and the Bergman circuit condition on F_i∪{i} forces w_i = min{w_j : j in F_i}. Thus the whole local Bergman fan is parametrized by the piecewise-linear map ψ : R^r → R^n sending (w_1,...,w_r) to (w_1,...,w_r,w_{r+1},...,w_n), with w_i = min_{j in F_i} w_j for i>r. The domains of linearity of the coordinate min function for F_i are the cones of the normal fan of the simplex Δ_{F_i}; the common refinement is the normal fan of the Minkowski sum Δ_F = sum_{i>r} Δ_{F_i}. Therefore B_σ(M) is combinatorially the normal fan of Δ_F. Under the identification L_M(σ) ≅ 2^σ, the localized building set G_σ contains the building closure of the family {F_i}. By Theorem 3.14 and Corollary 3.15, the normal fan associated with G_σ is a unimodular simplicial refinement of the normal fan of Δ_F. This is the desired triangulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any matroid M of rank r, the Bergman complex B(M) is homeomorphic to the order complex Δ(L_M) of the proper part of the lattice of flats L_M. Consequently B(M) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension r−2, and the number of spheres is the absolute value of the Möbius function of L_M from bottom to top.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 4.1 with the maximal building set G = L_M. For this building set, nested sets are exactly chains, so the nested set complex is the order complex of L_M. The homotopy type of the order complex of a geometric lattice is the classical one: a wedge of top-dimensional spheres, with the number of spheres given by the absolute value of the Möbius invariant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid, G a building set in L_M, and S a nested set. Let M_S = M_w for any vector w in the relative interior of the cone/simplex corresponding to S in the nested set fan. Choose a basis σ such that S lies in the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ), represent S by the corresponding labelled G_σ-tree T, and for each node ν define flats F_{≤ν} = span(T_{≤ν}) and F_{<ν} = span(T_{<ν}) in L_M. Replace the label of ν by the interval [F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] of L_M; the resulting tree T_S is independent of the choice of σ. If M[F,G] denotes the interval matroid/minor associated to the interval [F,G] of flats, then M_S = direct sum over nodes ν of T_S of M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A basis σ of M is a basis of the initial matroid M_S precisely when the cone for S lies in the local normal cone at σ, equivalently when S belongs to N(L_M(σ),G_σ). For such a σ, the tree T representing S has node labels T_ν that partition σ in a way compatible with the nested flats. For each node ν, T_ν is a basis of the interval matroid M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}], so σ is a basis of the direct sum of all these interval matroids. Conversely, if σ is a basis of that direct sum, then for each ν the set (σ ∩ F_{≤ν})\F_{<ν} is a basis of M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}]. Relabelling the same tree by these sets gives a G_σ-tree representing S, so S lies in the σ-local nested complex and σ is a basis of M_S. Since the two matroids have exactly the same bases, they are equal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the conventions of the nested set fan, facets of the nested set complex N(L_M,G) are represented by partitions of the ground set [n] into r nonempty parts, where r is the rank of M. More explicitly, for a maximal nested cone, the tree decomposition in Theorem 4.4 has r rank-one interval matroids, and the corresponding subsets F_{≤ν}\F_{<ν} form a partition of [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a maximal nested set, the associated tree has r relevant nodes, and each interval matroid M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] has rank one. Since the direct sum in Theorem 4.4 is loopless and has total ground set [n], the ground sets F_{≤ν}\F_{<ν} of these rank-one summands are nonempty and disjoint and cover [n]. Thus each maximal cone/facet determines such an unordered r-block partition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.1 and Corollary 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a connected matroid M, let N(M) denote the nested set complex for the minimal building set G_min of L_M, whose elements are the connected flats. Every flacet is a connected flat, but a connected flat need not be a flacet. The Bergman complex B(M) is triangulated by N(M) without adding vertices if and only if every connected flat of M is a flacet.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vertices of B(M), by Theorem 2.7, are indexed by flacets. The vertices of the minimal nested set complex are indexed by connected flats, by the definition of G_min. Proposition 2.6 implies a flacet must be connected, but examples in the paper show the converse can fail. Therefore the triangulation from Theorem 4.1 introduces no new vertices exactly when the two indexing sets coincide, i.e. when every connected flat is a flacet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a connected matroid, and let N(M) be the nested set complex for the minimal building set of connected flats in L_M. Then N(M) equals the Bergman complex B(M), rather than merely triangulating it, if and only if for every pair of flats F ⊂ G with G connected, the interval matroid M[F,G] is connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each simplex S of N(M), let Γ_S be the smallest face of B(M) containing it. Equality N(M)=B(M) is equivalent to dim S = dim Γ_S for every S. The simplex has dimension |S|−1. The Bergman face Γ_S corresponds to the initial matroid M_S, and its dimension is c(M_S)−1, where c denotes the number of connected components. By Theorem 4.4, M_S is a direct sum over the interval matroids M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] appearing as labels in the tree T_S. Thus c(M_S)=|S| exactly when every one of these interval matroids is connected. In such labels, F_{≤ν} is always a connected flat and F_{<ν} is a subflat, proving the forward implication from the stated interval-connectedness condition. Conversely, given any F⊂G with G connected, take the nested set consisting of G together with the connected components of F. In the associated tree, the root interval is [F,G]. If N(M)=B(M), the preceding dimension comparison forces M[F,G] to be connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Algorithm 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper gives an algorithm for computing the maximal faces of the Bergman complex B(M) and the nested set triangulation N(M) of a rank r matroid M on [n], when M is given by its bases. The output represents maximal faces of B(M) by unordered partitions {B_1,...,B_r} of [n] into r nonempty blocks, as in Corollary 4.7, and for each such face records the maximal nested sets that subdivide it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First precompute all connected flats of M. For each basis σ, compute for every i outside σ the fundamental-circuit subset F_i ⊆ σ such that F_i∪{i} is a circuit. For each ordering of σ, assign each outside element i to the first element of F_i in that ordering; together with the basis elements themselves this produces an r-block partition. Taking the union over all bases and all orderings gives the set Ω of partitions representing facets of B(M), because locally the Bergman fan is the normal fan of the Minkowski sum of the simplices Δ_{F_i}, and its vertices are obtained from generic orderings as in Theorem 3.14. For each partition ω in Ω, a connected flat F of rank s gives a nested-set vertex lying on the corresponding Bergman face exactly when F is the union of s blocks of ω. The collection Π of such flats is then used to list the maximal nested sets subdividing that face. If |Π|=r, the Bergman face is already an unsubdivided simplex; otherwise one computes the maximal nested subsets of Π.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 6.4 and Lemma 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is realized by an r-dimensional linear subspace V of C^n, or by X in P^{n−1}, and let X° = X ∩ (C*)^{n−1} be the hyperplane arrangement complement. Let I_X be the linear ideal of X. The map φ : X° → T_M, t ↦ t·I_X embeds X° into the projective toric variety T_M associated with the matroid polytope P_M, and the tropical compactification X_trop is the closure of φ(X°) in T_M. For a cone C in the normal fan of P_M and w in its relative interior, the following are equivalent: C is a cone of the Bergman fan B̃(M); the initial ideal in_w(I_X) contains no monomial; the initial ideal in_w(I_X) contains no variable. The paper then uses the standard tropical compactification orbit criterion that the cones whose torus orbits meet X_trop are precisely the cones satisfying this no-monomial condition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The torus orbit decomposition of T_M is the normal fan decomposition of P_M, and two weights w,w′ lie in the same cone exactly when they give the same initial ideal of I_X. Since I_X is generated by linear forms, an initial ideal contains a monomial if and only if it contains some variable. The matroid associated to the linear ideal in_w(I_X) is exactly the initial matroid M_w. Thus the condition that no variable lies in in_w(I_X) is the condition that M_w has no loop, which is equivalent to w being in the Bergman fan by Proposition 2.5. The final orbit-intersection statement is the general Hilbert-scheme/tropical compactification criterion: an initial degeneration is nonempty in the dense torus exactly when the corresponding initial ideal has no monomial. Note: in the supplied text, item (4) of Lemma 6.5 is printed with the words 'does not intersect', but the subsequent proof of Theorem 6.1 uses the standard 'intersects' formulation stated here.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X° be the complement of a hyperplane arrangement associated with a realizable connected matroid M. Let X_wond be the De Concini-Procesi wonderful compactification built from the minimal building set of connected flats, and let X_trop be the tropical compactification of X° in the toric variety associated with the matroid polytope P_M. Then there is a canonical morphism X_wond → X_trop. This morphism is an isomorphism whenever the following combinatorial condition holds: if a flat G in L_M corresponds to a divisor in X_wond, then G also corresponds to a divisor in the wonderful compactification of X ∩ F for every intersection of hyperplanes F containing G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each connected flat G, the projection to the coordinate projective space P_G gives the De Concini-Procesi map defining X_wond. Equivalently, X_wond is the closure of X° in the projective toric variety T_G associated with the Minkowski sum of simplices Δ_G over connected flats G. The matroid polytope P_M is a Minkowski summand of this Minkowski-sum polytope; this follows by comparing the flat inequality description of P_M from Proposition 2.3 with the inequality description of sums of simplices from Proposition 3.12. A Minkowski-summand relation gives a projective toric morphism T_G → T_M. The embedding of X° into T_M defining X_trop is the composition X° ⊂ T_G → T_M, hence taking closures gives a projective morphism X_wond → X_trop. The cones of the normal fan of the connected-flat Minkowski sum whose orbits meet X_wond form the minimal nested set fan N(M), while the cones of the normal fan of P_M whose orbits meet X_trop form the Bergman fan B(M). If the stated combinatorial condition holds, it translates to the interval-connectedness criterion of Theorem 5.3, so N(M)=B(M); in that case the toric morphism restricts to an isomorphism on the compactifications.

proof_techniques.name: Normal-fan interpretation of the Bergman condition
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces the circuit definition of the Bergman fan by a statement about normal cones of the matroid polytope. A weight vector w determines an initial matroid M_w by selecting maximum-weight bases, and w lies in the Bergman fan exactly when M_w is loopless. Geometrically, this is equivalent to the exposed face P_M^w meeting the interior of the ambient simplex. This converts tropical linear conditions into polyhedral face and matroid-minor conditions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For questions about tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M), this gives a concrete way to test containment or boundary behavior by initial matroids and looplessness. It may help analyze any proposed order-reversing map on Dr(M) by checking how it acts on the initial matroids or normal cones associated with flats.

proof_techniques.name: Flat faces factor as restriction times contraction
proof_techniques.description: For a flat F, the face of P_M cut out by sum_{i in F} x_i = rank(F) has associated matroid M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]], the direct sum of the restriction to F and the contraction by F. The dimension calculation for this product proves that F defines a facet precisely when both minors are connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem's embedded copy of L(M)^op in Dr(M) is defined using contractions M/F together with loops on F. This paper's face-factorization is a precise polyhedral place where contractions and restrictions appear together, and it suggests studying a flat anti-involution through its effect on the two factors of such flat faces.

proof_techniques.name: Triangulating Bergman fans by nested sets of flats
proof_techniques.description: The main construction takes a building set G in the lattice of flats and forms a nested set fan from characteristic vectors of flats. Theorem 4.1 shows that this fan is a unimodular triangulation of the Bergman fan. Different building sets give different subdivisions, related by stellar subdivisions, with the same support.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on the lattice of flats can be tested first on the combinatorial nested set models of Trop(M). If it sends nested configurations to nested configurations, or sends one building-set fan to another, it may provide a finite combinatorial scaffold for constructing or obstructing an involution on the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Local basis charts via fundamental circuits and Minkowski sums of simplices
proof_techniques.description: At a basis σ, the local Bergman fan is parametrized by the weights on σ; each outside element i has weight equal to the minimum over the fundamental-circuit subset F_i ⊆ σ. The domains of linearity are exactly the normal fan of the Minkowski sum of simplices sum_i Δ_{F_i}. The localized nested set fan is the normal fan of a larger Minkowski sum associated with a building closure, hence triangulates the local Bergman fan.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a local Boolean-lattice model for Trop(M). If the flat involution interacts well with bases or with fundamental circuits, one can try to define or disprove a global Φ by checking compatibility on these local Minkowski-sum fans.

proof_techniques.name: Tree representation of nested sets and interval-minor decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Nested sets in a Boolean localization are represented uniquely by rooted labelled trees. For a nested cone in the Bergman fan, Theorem 4.4 labels the nodes by intervals [F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] in the lattice of flats and decomposes the corresponding initial matroid as the direct sum of interval matroids M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution F ↦ F^⊥ on L(M) naturally reverses intervals, sending [F,G] to something like [G^⊥,F^⊥]. The interval-minor decomposition suggests a concrete way to see whether a candidate involution on tropical subspaces could be defined by applying this reversal node-by-node to nested decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Dimension comparison through connected components
proof_techniques.description: To decide when the minimal nested set complex equals the Bergman complex, the paper compares the dimension |S|−1 of a nested simplex with the dimension c(M_S)−1 of the Bergman face containing it. Theorem 4.4 then reduces equality to connectedness of all interval minors M[F,G] with G connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful obstruction-detection pattern: if an involution on a poset of tropical subspaces is expected to respect cells or dimensions, failures of connectedness in interval minors may create nonsimplicial cells or subdivisions that any extension of a flat-level involution must handle.

proof_techniques.name: Minkowski-summand toric morphisms
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 6.1 compares the matroid polytope P_M with a Minkowski sum of simplices indexed by connected flats. Because P_M is a Minkowski summand of that larger polytope, the corresponding normal fans refine and there is a projective toric morphism from the wonderful-model toric ambient space to the matroid-polytope toric ambient space. The nested set fan maps to the Bergman fan under this morphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If Dr(M) or a subposet of it can be realized through polyhedral or toric moduli data, an order-reversing involution might be studied via dual or opposite morphisms between polytopal normal fans. The summand/refinement method gives a way to convert combinatorial refinements into actual maps.

proof_techniques.name: Orbit-intersection criterion for tropical compactifications
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable matroid, the Bergman fan is identified with the set of cones in the normal fan of P_M whose initial ideals contain no monomial, equivalently whose torus orbits meet the tropical compactification. This bridges matroidal Bergman fan combinatorics and algebraic tropical compactification strata.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If tropical linear subspaces in Trop(M) are approached through realizations or initial degenerations, the no-monomial/no-loop criterion can be used to verify whether a candidate transformed object remains inside Trop(M).

other_useful_info: The paper does not discuss the relative Dressian Dr(M) or order-reversing involutions on it directly; its relevance is through the detailed structure of Trop(M) as a Bergman fan and through the role of flats, contractions, and interval minors. Example 2.8 gives a rank 4 matroid on 6 elements whose Bergman complex has square two-faces, showing that Bergman complexes need not be simplicial; the minimal nested set complex subdivides those squares. Examples 1.2, 3.4, and Remark 5.1 show that connected flats need not be flacets: for the graphic matroid of K4 with one edge removed, the Bergman complex is K_{3,3} while the minimal nested set complex subdivides one edge. Remark 5.4 notes that for the graphic matroid of the complete graph K_n, the nested set complex equals the Bergman complex and is the space of phylogenetic trees; the paper also cites Ardila-Reiner-Williams for equality of Bergman and nested set complexes for finite reflection arrangements. Example 5.7 treats the self-dual regular matroid R10 and computes its connected flats, nested tetrahedra, and Bergman facets; this may be a useful nontrivial test case for any proposed flat-duality extension. The paper cites Ardila-Klivans for the order-complex triangulation of Bergman complexes, Speyer for tropical linear spaces, and Tevelev for tropical compactifications.
why_relevant: Gives a detailed construction of Bergman fans from matroid polytopes and the lattice of flats. This is directly relevant to the embedding of flats into Dr(M) and to how contractions/localizations appear inside Trop(M).

title: Representing Tropical Linear Spaces by Circuits
overall_summary: The paper studies tropical linear spaces through their circuit hyperplane descriptions, with special emphasis on the constant-coefficient case, where tropical linear spaces are Bergman fans of matroids. Its main combinatorial tools are support-based circuit conditions, reduction of tropical-basis equality to 0/1 points and flats, and generation of circuit conditions by pasting circuits. It also recalls Speyer's matroidal-subdivision description of tropical linear spaces and proves a cocircuit parametrization theorem for realizable tropical linear spaces. For the original problem, the most useful content is that containment/equality of Bergman-type tropical spaces can often be tested by circuits and flats, while realizable tropical linear spaces also admit a dual cocircuit/tropical-span presentation that may interact with order-reversing dualities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Circuit description of tropical linear spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the tropical semiring R∪{∞} with tropical addition given by minimum and tropical multiplication given by ordinary addition, and let TP^{n-1} be tropical projective space. A point p=(p_B) indexed by d-subsets B of [n] is a tropical Plücker vector if, for every (d−2)-subset S of [n] and every four distinct i,j,k,l outside S, the minimum of p_{Sij}+p_{Skl}, p_{Sik}+p_{Sjl}, and p_{Sil}+p_{Sjk} is attained at least twice. For each (d+1)-subset I of [n], define the associated circuit tropical linear form h_I(x)=min_{i in I}(p_{I minus {i}}+x_i). The tropical linear space associated to p is the set of x in TP^{n-1} such that, for every I, the minimum in h_I(x) is attained at least twice. In the constant-coefficient case, where all coefficients are 0 or ∞, these tropical hyperplanes depend only on their finite supports; those supports are the circuits of a matroid M, and the tropical linear space is the Bergman fan or tropical variety T(C)=intersection over circuits C of M of the sets of x such that min{x_i : i in C} is attained at least twice.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the setup recalled in the introduction and Section 2. The only point emphasized by the paper is that when coefficients are only 0 and ∞, adding the finite coefficients does not change any comparisons except by deleting ∞ entries, so each tropical hyperplane condition depends solely on the support of the corresponding form. Thus the constant-coefficient theory can be expressed purely in terms of the circuit supports of a matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the direct sum M1⊕M2 of matroids on disjoint ground sets, and let C, C1, and C2 be their respective circuit sets. A subset B of C is a tropical basis for M, meaning that the intersection of tropical hyperplanes T(B) equals the full Bergman fan T(C), if and only if B is the union B1∪B2 of tropical bases B1 for M1 and B2 for M2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The circuits of a direct sum of matroids are exactly the circuits of the summands, viewed in the disjoint union of ground sets. Therefore imposing all circuit hyperplane conditions for M is the same as imposing all circuit conditions for M1 on the first set of coordinates and all circuit conditions for M2 on the second set. If B1 and B2 are tropical bases, their union clearly cuts out the same intersection as all circuits of M. Conversely, if B is a tropical basis for M and its restriction to, say, C1 failed to be a tropical basis for M1, an extra point satisfying the B1-conditions but not all C1-conditions could be extended in the coordinates of M2 to give an extra point for M, contradicting that B is a tropical basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid with circuit set C, and for B⊂C let T(B) be the intersection of the tropical hyperplanes determined by the circuits in B. If T(B)∩{0,1}^n equals T(C)∩{0,1}^n, then T(B)=T(C). Equivalently, in the constant-coefficient case, to verify that a collection B of circuits is a tropical basis it is enough to check equality on 0/1 vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since B⊂C, every full circuit condition contains at least as many hyperplanes as the B-conditions, so T(C)⊂T(B). Suppose T(B) has an extra point x not in T(C). Then some circuit C0 in C∖B excludes x, meaning that m=min{x_i : i in C0} is attained at a unique coordinate of C0. Define a 0/1 vector v by v_i=1 if x_i>m and v_i=0 if x_i≤m. On C0, the minimum of v is still attained uniquely, so v is not in T(C). On the other hand, if a circuit C' in B has its minimum on x attained at least twice, then after thresholding at m the minimum on v over C' is also attained at least twice: either two entries of C' have x-value at most m, or all entries have x-value greater than m, or the original two minimum entries remain tied. Thus v lies in T(B). This produces an extra 0/1 point, proving the contrapositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Flat criterion for 0/1 points, quoted from Ardila-Klivans
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid M with circuit set C, a 0/1 vector v lies in the Bergman fan T(C) if and only if its support supp(v)={i : v_i=1} is a flat of M. Consequently, a collection B of circuits is a tropical basis if and only if the circuit hyperplanes from B exclude every 0/1 vector whose support is not a flat.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Ardila and Klivans for this fact. In circuit language, a 0/1 vector with support F violates the circuit condition for a circuit C exactly when C has precisely one element outside F, because that unique outside element has coordinate 0 and all other elements of C have coordinate 1. The standard circuit characterization of flats says that F is a flat exactly when no circuit has exactly one element outside F. This matches the tropical condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a collection of circuits of a matroid M. Suppose every circuit of M not in S can be obtained by successively pasting circuits in S, where pasting two circuits A and B with a unique common element e means replacing them by their symmetric difference A△B=(A∖B)∪(B∖A), assumed at each step to be a circuit. Then S is a tropical basis for M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to show that if a weight vector x satisfies the tropical hyperplane conditions for two circuits A and B sharing only e, then it also satisfies the condition for C=A△B. The paper checks three cases. First, if A attains its minimum at two elements a,a' different from e and B attains its minimum at two elements b,b' different from e, then C attains its minimum at least twice among whichever of {a,a'} or {b,b'} has the smaller weight. Second, if A has minimum at a and e while B has minimum at b and b' not equal to e, then b and b' have weight no larger than e, while a has the same weight as e, so C has a repeated minimum at b,b' or possibly at more elements. Third, if both A and B attain their minima using e, say at a,e and b,e, then x_a=x_e=x_b, and C has a repeated minimum at a and b. Iterating this argument along the prescribed sequence of pastings shows that all circuit conditions follow from S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the uniform matroid U_{d,n}, whose circuits are all (d+1)-subsets of [n], fix an element i in [n]. Then B={C⊂[n] : |C|=d+1 and i∈C} is an inclusion-minimal tropical basis for U_{d,n}. The paper notes that such bases need not be cardinality-minimal and are not unique in general.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take i=1. By Lemma 3 it is enough to exclude all 0/1 points excluded by some circuit. If a 0/1 point x is excluded by a circuit Cx, then Cx has exactly one coordinate k with x_k=0. If 1 lies in Cx, then Cx is already in B. If 1 does not lie in Cx and x_1=1, replace any element of Cx other than k by 1; the resulting (d+1)-set is a circuit containing 1 and still has k as the unique zero, so it excludes x. If 1 does not lie in Cx and x_1=0, replace k by 1; the resulting circuit contains 1 and now has 1 as its unique zero, so it excludes x. Thus B is a tropical basis. For inclusion-minimality, fix C* in B and define x by x_j=1 for j in C*∖{1} and x_j=0 otherwise. The circuit C* excludes x, while every other circuit in B has at least two zero coordinates on x, so removing C* creates an extra point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the graphic matroid of a graph G with no loops and no parallel edges, with ground set E(G) and circuits equal to the cycles of G. The unique minimal tropical basis for M is the set of induced cycles of G, meaning cycles that are induced subgraphs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For sufficiency, take any non-induced cycle C. The induced subgraph on the vertices of C has a chord, which splits C into two smaller cycles. Repeating this chord decomposition expresses C as a successive pasting of induced cycles. Lemma 4 then implies that the induced cycles impose all cycle conditions. For necessity, let C be an induced cycle and assign weight 1 to all but one edge of C, weight 0 to the remaining edge of C, and weight 0 to every edge not in C. Then C has a unique minimum and is excluded. Any other cycle either avoids the problem or, if it uses edges of C but is not C, must use at least two edges outside C; otherwise C would have a chord and would not be induced. Those outside edges have weight 0, so the other cycle has a repeated minimum. Thus each induced cycle is excluded by a witness point not excluded by any other cycle condition, forcing it to appear in every tropical basis. Since the induced cycles themselves are sufficient, they form the unique minimal tropical basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected 3-edge-connected graph, and let M be its cographic matroid, whose circuits are the inclusion-minimal edge cuts of G. The unique minimal tropical basis for M consists of exactly those edge cuts C such that removing C splits G into two connected subgraphs each of which is 2-edge-connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For sufficiency, if a cut C splits G into connected subgraphs U and V, define the index of C to be the total number of bridges in U and V. Index 0 cuts are precisely those splitting G into two 2-edge-connected subgraphs. The paper proves by induction on the index that any positive-index cut is obtained by pasting lower-index cuts. If e is a bridge in U, splitting U into A and B, let br_A, br_B, and br_V be the numbers of bridges in A, B, and V. Then index(C)=1+br_A+br_B+br_V. Let C' be the cut separating A from B∪V, and let C'' be the cut separating B from A∪V. Because G is 3-edge-connected, there are at least two edges between B and V; otherwise e together with a unique such edge would disconnect G.
