Hence any bridge in B∪V is already a bridge in B or V, so index(C')≤br_A+br_B+br_V, and similarly index(C'')≤br_A+br_B+br_V. The original cut C is obtained by pasting C' and C'' along e, so induction plus Lemma 4 proves sufficiency. For necessity, suppose C splits G into 2-edge-connected subgraphs U and V. Put weight 0 on all edges inside U and inside V, put weight 0 on one chosen edge of C, and put weight 1 on all other edges of C. Then C is excluded by having a unique minimum. Any other minimal cut C' must disconnect U or V; if it disconnects U into A and B, then because U is 2-edge-connected there are at least two weight-0 edges between A and B in C'. Thus C' has a repeated minimum. Hence every index 0 cut is forced, and the basis is unique minimal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R10 be the regular matroid whose ground set is the edge set of K5 and whose circuits are the fifteen 4-cycles of K5 together with the complements of those 4-cycles. The unique minimal tropical basis of R10 consists of the fifteen 4-cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Necessity of each 4-cycle is shown by a 0/1 witness. For a 4-cycle C={a1,a2,a3,a4}, set x_{a1}=x_{a2}=x_{a3}=1 and set all other coordinates to 0. Then C has a unique minimum, while no other 4-cycle shares three elements with C and every 4-cycle-complement contains enough elements outside C to have a repeated minimum. Thus every 4-cycle must occur in any tropical basis. For sufficiency, Lemma 3 reduces to 0/1 points. If a 0/1 point is excluded by a 4-cycle-complement C={a1,...,a6}, relabel so a1 is the unique zero on C and a2,...,a6 are ones. The paper uses the K5 picture: no 4-cycle-complement contains a 4-cycle, and for any five-element subset of the six edges of such a complement there exists a 4-cycle meeting that subset in exactly three edges. This gives a 4-cycle that also excludes the point. Therefore the complement circuits add no conditions beyond the 4-cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Matroidal subdivision description, recalled from Speyer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ_{d,n} be the hypersimplex, the convex hull in R^n of the 0/1 vectors with coordinate sum d. A vector p indexed by d-subsets of [n] is a tropical Plücker vector if and only if the regular subdivision of Δ_{d,n} induced by p is matroidal, meaning every face is a matroid polytope. The corresponding tropical linear space is the polyhedral subcomplex of the dual subdivision consisting of cells dual to matroid polytopes of loop-free matroids. A coordinate p_B equal to ∞ is equivalent to deleting the corresponding vertex of Δ_{d,n}. In the constant-coefficient case, with all coordinates 0 or ∞, the subdivision contains a single matroid polytope, and the circuits of the associated matroid are the supports of the circuit forms. Because Δ_{d,n} lies in the affine hyperplane with coordinate sum d, the dual complexes and tropical linear spaces have lineality space containing R(1,...,1). Since every cell of a matroidal subdivision is a matroid polytope, the star of any face in a tropical linear space is isomorphic to a constant-coefficient tropical linear space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence between tropical Plücker vectors and matroidal subdivisions, and the dual-subcomplex description of the tropical linear space, are recalled from Speyer. The paper then explains the consequences used later: infinite Plücker coordinates remove vertices; 0/∞ heights give the constant-coefficient single-matroid-polytope case; lineality comes from the affine span of the hypersimplex; and local stars are constant-coefficient because locally a dual cell sees the normal fan of a matroid polytope.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Realizable tropical linear spaces and their circuit forms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the field of Puiseux series with complex coefficients, and let deg:K→T send a nonzero series to its lowest exponent and send 0 to ∞. If L is a d-dimensional linear subspace of K^n with Plücker vector P, then deg(P) is a tropical Plücker vector. When a tropical Plücker vector arises this way, the corresponding tropical linear space coincides with deg(L)⊂T^n and is called realizable. For a linear form f=a_1X_1+...+a_nX_n over K, its tropicalization is min_i(deg(a_i)+x_i). In this realizable setting, the circuit forms defining the tropical linear space are precisely the tropicalizations of linear forms with minimal support in the orthogonal complement L⊥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is recalled in Section 4 as standard background. The Plücker vector of L tropicalizes to a tropical Plücker vector, and the tropical linear space defined by the resulting circuit forms agrees with the coordinatewise degree image of L. Minimal-support linear forms in L⊥ give the classical circuits; tropicalizing them yields exactly the circuit hyperplanes used in the tropical definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be an (n−k)-dimensional linear subspace of K^n, where K is the Puiseux series field, and assume all Plücker coordinates of L are nonzero. Let M be an m by n matrix over K with m≥k whose rows are nonzero elements of L⊥. If the rows of M form a tropical basis for L, meaning that their tropicalizations cut out deg(L), then every choice of k columns of deg(M) has tropical rank k. Here the tropical determinant of a square matrix is the minimum over permutation sums, and tropical rank k for an m by k matrix means that it contains a k by k tropically nonsingular submatrix, i.e. one whose tropical determinant has a unique minimizing permutation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume some m by k submatrix A of deg(M) has tropical rank less than k. By Develin-Santos-Sturmfels, cited as [4, Theorem 5.5], A has Kapranov rank less than k: there exists an m by k matrix A' over K with deg(A')=A and ordinary rank less than k. Choose a nonzero vector v in the kernel of A'. Then deg(v) satisfies all tropical hyperplane conditions determined by the rows of A, because each ordinary zero dot product forces the corresponding tropical minimum to be attained at least twice. Extend deg(v) to a vector w in T^n by putting ∞ in all coordinates outside the chosen k columns. This w lies in the tropical prevariety cut out by the rows of deg(M). Its finite support has size at most k. But since all Plücker coordinates of the (n−k)-dimensional L are nonzero, every point of deg(L) has support size at least k+1. Therefore w is not in deg(L), contradicting that the rows form a tropical basis. Hence each k-column submatrix has tropical rank k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the notation of Section 5, let L be a linear subspace over the Puiseux field K, and call a cocircuit of L a nonzero vector of L whose support is inclusion-minimal among nonzero vectors of L. Every nonzero v in L can be written as a finite sum v=v_1+...+v_d of cocircuits v_i in L, as stated in the paper, such that deg(v)=deg(v_1)⊕...⊕deg(v_d), i.e. coordinatewise deg(v)_j=min_i deg(v_i)_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v is already a cocircuit, there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, by definition of not being support-minimal, choose a cocircuit u with supp(u) strictly contained in supp(v). Multiply u by a scalar c of sufficiently large degree so that deg(cu)≥deg(v) coordinatewise. Choose c so that cu and v actually coincide in at least one coordinate, forcing supp(v−cu) to be strictly smaller than supp(v). Put v_1=cu. Since deg(v_1)≥deg(v), the nonarchimedean degree relation gives deg(v)=deg(v_1)⊕deg(v−v_1). If v−v_1 is not a cocircuit, repeat the same construction. The support strictly decreases at each step, so the process terminates with cocircuit summands satisfying the required tropical minimum identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be an n by d matrix over K whose column space is a linear subspace L⊂K^n. Let deg(A):T^d→T^n be the tropical linear map given by tropical matrix multiplication, so its image is the tropical span of the degree vectors of the columns of A. Then deg(L)=im(deg(A)) if and only if every cocircuit of L is represented in A, where a cocircuit is represented if its one-dimensional cocircuit class, equivalently its minimal support vector up to nonzero scalar, occurs among the columns of A. For any A one always has deg(L)⊇im(deg(A)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The general containment holds because every column of deg(A) is the degree vector of an element of L, and tropical linear spaces are closed under tropical linear combinations. For the if direction, take any nonzero v in L. By Lemma 15, v is a sum of cocircuits v_i with deg(v)=⊕_i deg(v_i). If every cocircuit is represented among the columns of A up to scalar, each deg(v_i) is a tropical scalar multiple of a column of deg(A). Hence deg(v) is in the tropical image of deg(A), giving deg(L)⊂im(deg(A)); together with the general containment this is equality. For the only-if direction, suppose a cocircuit c of L is not represented by A. Then deg(c) lies in deg(L). But any tropical linear combination of columns of deg(A) that is finite on all coordinates of supp(c) must use columns with finite entries there. Since no column has support contained in supp(c) unless it represents c, such columns also have finite coordinates outside supp(c); tropical minima cannot cancel those finite outside coordinates. Thus no vector in im(deg(A)) has exactly the finite support of deg(c), so deg(c) is not in im(deg(A)). This contradicts equality. The paper also notes that two cocircuits with the same support are scalar multiples, since otherwise a linear combination could cancel a coordinate and produce a smaller support.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit-hyperplane encoding of tropical linear spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper consistently replaces a tropical linear space by the intersection of tropical hyperplanes attached to its circuits. In the constant-coefficient case this becomes purely combinatorial: for each matroid circuit C, one imposes that the minimum of the coordinates indexed by C is attained at least twice. A smaller set of circuits is a tropical basis exactly when it excludes the same points as all circuits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Inclusion among tropical linear spaces in a relative Dressian can be attacked by comparing which circuit minimum conditions are imposed or implied. For subspaces such as Trop(M/F⊕U_{0,F}), one can try to write explicit circuit conditions and test how an order-reversing candidate would transform them.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction to 0/1 witnesses and flats
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3 turns any extra tropical point satisfying a proposed circuit basis into a 0/1 extra point by thresholding at the unique minimum of a violated circuit. Combined with the Ardila-Klivans flat criterion, this identifies the 0/1 points of a Bergman fan exactly with flats of the matroid. Thus tropical-basis equality can be checked by whether all nonflat supports are excluded.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Because the original problem starts with an involution on the lattice of flats, this technique gives a bridge from tropical containment questions back to flat combinatorics. It suggests testing any proposed involution on Dr(M) first on 0/1 strata or flat-supported points.

proof_techniques.name: Circuit pasting to generate tropical conditions
proof_techniques.description: If two circuits share a unique element and their symmetric difference is again a circuit, then the tropical condition for the symmetric difference follows from the two original conditions. The proof is a direct case analysis on whether the shared element participates in the repeated minima. Iterating this gives a way to prove that a small circuit set is a tropical basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a matroid with a flat-lattice duality, one can look for circuit or cocircuit generating sets stable under the duality and use pasting to reduce verification of containment or equality to those generators.

proof_techniques.name: Unique 0/1 witnesses for necessity of basis elements
proof_techniques.description: In the graphic, cographic, uniform, Fano, and R10 examples, the paper proves that certain circuits are unavoidable by constructing a 0/1 weight vector excluded by that circuit and by no other relevant circuit. This gives uniqueness or minimality of tropical bases in special classes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Such witness constructions can reveal rigid elements of Dr(M): if a tropical subspace or constraint is uniquely characterized by a 0/1 witness, any order-reversing involution extending a flat involution may be forced to send it to a correspondingly rigid dual object.

proof_techniques.name: Local reduction via matroidal subdivisions
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls that tropical Plücker vectors are equivalent to matroidal subdivisions of the hypersimplex and that the tropical linear space is the dual subcomplex supported on loop-free matroid polytopes. Since each cell is a matroid polytope, the star of any face of a tropical linear space is a constant-coefficient tropical linear space.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to localize questions about arbitrary tropical linear spaces in Dr(M) to Bergman fans of matroids. A proposed global order-reversing map could be tested on local stars, where flat lattices, circuits, and cocircuits are available.

proof_techniques.name: Cocircuit tropical parametrization
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable linear space L over the Puiseux field, the paper studies when deg(L) is the tropical image of a matrix A whose columns span L. The answer is exactly that all cocircuits of L must be represented among the columns. The proof rests on decomposing every vector of L into cocircuits in a way compatible with coordinatewise tropical minima.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a dual presentation of realizable tropical linear spaces: circuits cut them out as intersections, while cocircuits can generate them as tropical spans. Since the original problem asks for an order-reversing extension of a flat duality, this circuit/cocircuit exchange is a natural mechanism to investigate.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical rank obstruction for tropical bases
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 12 gives a necessary condition for a set of linear forms to be a tropical basis of a realizable tropical linear space with all Plücker coordinates finite: every choice of k columns in the degree matrix of the defining forms must have tropical rank k, where k is the codimension. The proof constructs a low-support point in the tropical prevariety from a Kapranov-rank defect.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When testing candidate images of realizable tropical subspaces under a proposed involution, this rank condition can rule out proposed circuit descriptions that cannot define the intended tropical linear space.

proof_techniques.name: Direct-sum decomposition of tropical bases
proof_techniques.description: The direct sum of matroids has circuit set equal to the union of the circuit sets of the summands, and tropical bases decompose accordingly. This lets one analyze tropical basis questions componentwise.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The natural embedding of flats in the original problem uses matroids of the form M/F⊕U_{0,F}. The direct-sum lemma suggests decomposing their circuit and Bergman-fan descriptions into quotient and loop components.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Ardila-Klivans for the fact that the intersection of a Bergman fan with a sphere is a geometric realization of the order complex of the lattice of flats; it also notes that deleting loops and replacing each parallel class by one element does not change the lattice of flats. The paper raises Problem 7: what happens to tropical bases under minors, deletion and contraction; this is directly relevant to constructions involving M/F. Conjecture 13 states that the converse of Theorem 12 should hold when all Plücker coordinates are nonzero. Example 14 shows that the finite-Plücker-coordinate hypothesis in Theorem 12 is essential: for the 2-dimensional subspace of K^4 that is the kernel of [[1,0,1,1],[0,1,1,1],[1,-1,0,0]], the degree matrix [[0,∞,0,0],[∞,0,0,0],[0,0,∞,∞]] has rows forming a tropical basis even though its last two columns have tropical rank only 1; moreover the first two rows have the same tropical ranks on every subset of columns as the whole matrix but do not form a tropical basis, so tropical ranks alone do not determine tropical bases when some Plücker coordinates are ∞. The paper records further examples: for partition matroids, a minimal tropical basis is given by enough pairs in each block to form a spanning tree on that block; for the Fano matroid, the unique minimal tropical basis consists of the seven 3-element line circuits, with the 4-element circuits obtained by pasting; and it asks whether all regular matroids have unique minimal tropical bases. The cocircuit parametrization theorem is noted to specialize in the constant-coefficient case to Develin-Santos-Sturmfels [4, Proposition 7.5].
why_relevant: Gives circuit-based descriptions of tropical linear spaces. Circuit and cocircuit descriptions are often the most effective way to test containment, duality, and quotient conditions.

title: On Adjoints and Dual Matroids
overall_summary: The paper studies lattice-theoretic adjoints of geometric lattices associated to matroids and compares this notion with ordinary set-theoretic matroid duality. Its main result is a counterexample to a question of Mason: the rank-3 non-Desargues matroid has an adjoint, but its ordinary dual matroid does not. The core obstruction is Cheung's embedding theorem, which says that the existence of an adjoint forces an order-preserving embedding of the adjoint lattice into the extension lattice of modular filters; this implies a Levi-type intersection property for copoints. The counterexample proves failure of this Levi property by exhibiting six hyperplanes in the dual non-Desargues matroid whose generated modular filter is already the top filter, i.e. any point extension constrained to lie on all six hyperplanes makes the new point a loop. For the original problem, the most useful import is the strategy of deriving a necessary intersection/generation property from an order-reversing lattice duality and then disproving extension by a finite incidence obstruction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition: adjoint of a geometric lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice. A geometric lattice L^A is called an adjoint of L if rank(L^A) = rank(L) and there exists an order-reversing injective map φ: L -> L^A such that points, i.e. rank-one elements, of L are mapped into copoints, i.e. corank-one elements, of L^A, and copoints of L are mapped onto the points of L^A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition introduced in the paper following Cheung's terminology. The paper emphasizes that this is a lattice-theoretic notion of duality and is distinct from ordinary matroid duality, where bases are sent to set-theoretic complements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 observations: modular, linear, and rank-three geometric lattices have adjoints
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every modular geometric lattice L has an adjoint, namely the order-dual lattice obtained by reversing the order relation. More generally, if L is a linear geometric lattice, meaning that its elements are the linear subspaces spanned by a finite set of points in a projective space P, then L has an adjoint obtained by embedding L into the modular lattice P, applying the anti-isomorphism P -> P^op, and then applying an appropriate retraction. Also, every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint; the paper notes that one may first construct a possibly infinite modular extension by successively intersecting lines, and then use the same embedding/anti-isomorphism/retraction construction.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a modular geometric lattice, reversal of the order relation already gives a geometric lattice of the same rank with the required order-reversing correspondence. For a linear lattice L sitting inside a projective-space lattice P, the projective-space lattice is modular and has the usual anti-isomorphism to its order dual; composing the inclusion L -> P, this anti-isomorphism, and a retraction back to a suitable lattice gives the desired order-reversing injective map sending points to copoints and copoints to points. For rank 3, the paper asserts that one can build a modular extension by repeatedly adding intersections of lines; after embedding the original rank-3 lattice into this modular extension, the same construction yields an adjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Point-extension/modular-filter correspondence and the extension lattice E(L)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice with top element 1_L. A modular filter in L is a subset F of L satisfying: if x is in F and y >= x, then y is in F; and if x,y are in F and x,y form a modular pair, then x ∧ y is in F. The paper recalls the standard one-to-one correspondence between single-element point extensions L ∪ p of L and modular filters F of L: the filter F consists of those elements of L whose image in the extension contains the new point p. The set E(L) of all modular filters of L, ordered by inclusion, is a lattice called the extension lattice. It has unique minimum element {1_L}, corresponding to adding a point in general position, and unique maximum element L, corresponding to a loop extension. The atoms, or minimal nonzero elements, of E(L) are exactly the filters {H, 1_L} where H is a copoint of L; these atoms are called the points of E(L), so points of E(L) are in bijection with copoints of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as the standard correspondence between point extensions and modular filters, referring to earlier sources. It does not prove the correspondence in detail, but it explains the interpretation: specifying which original flats contain the new point p gives an upward-closed set, and the modular-pair closure condition is precisely what makes this specification compatible with the lattice axioms for a single-element extension. Inclusion of filters orders extensions by the containment constraints imposed on p. The minimum filter {1_L} imposes only that p lies in the top flat, while the maximum filter L forces p to lie in every flat and hence makes p a loop.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1: Cheung's embedding theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L and L^A be geometric lattices of the same rank. If L^A is an adjoint of L, then there exists an injective order-preserving map ε: L^A -> E(L) into the extension lattice of L such that ε maps the points of L^A onto the points of E(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses this result as its main tool and cites it to Cheung. No proof is given in the paper. The key content for later use is that an order-reversing adjoint map L -> L^A can be converted into an order-preserving embedding of L^A into the modular-filter lattice E(L), and the copoint-to-point part of the adjoint definition becomes the assertion that the points of L^A exactly cover the point filters {H,1_L} of E(L).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.2: existence of an adjoint implies Levi's property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice of rank r. The paper says that L has Levi's property if the maximum element 1_{E(L)} of the extension lattice E(L), equivalently the modular filter L, is not the join of fewer than r points of E(L). Equivalently, the filter L is not generated by fewer than r atom filters {H,1_L}; geometrically, for every set of r-1 copoints H_1,...,H_{r-1} of L, one can add a new point p in a point extension L ∪ p such that p lies on all H_i without being forced to be a loop. Corollary 2.2 states: if L has an adjoint, then L has Levi's property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let L^A be an adjoint of L and let ε: L^A -> E(L) be Cheung's order-preserving injective map from Proposition 2.1, sending points of L^A onto points of E(L). Take any k < r points p_1,...,p_k of E(L). By surjectivity on points, choose points q_1,...,q_k of L^A with ε(q_i)=p_i. Since L^A has rank r, the join x = q_1 ∨ ... ∨ q_k is strictly below the top element 1_{L^A}. Because ε is order-preserving, each p_i = ε(q_i) is at most ε(x), hence p_1 ∨ ... ∨ p_k <= ε(x) in E(L). Since x < 1_{L^A} and ε is injective and order-preserving, ε(x) is a proper element of E(L), so the join of p_1,...,p_k is not the top element of E(L). Thus no fewer than r point filters generate the maximum modular filter L, which is exactly Levi's property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1: six hyperplanes in the dual non-Desargues matroid force a loop extension
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the non-Desargues rank-3 matroid on the 10-element ground set S labelled as in the paper's Figure 1, with p the center of perspective and {x,y,z} the broken 3-point line. Let H_1,H_2,H_3 be the three 3-point lines of the triangle abc, and let H_4,H_5,H_6 be the three 3-point lines paa', pbb', pcc'. For each i, let H_i^* = S \ H_i, which is a hyperplane of the ordinary dual matroid M^*. Let F^* be the modular filter of the flat lattice of M^* generated by H_1^*,...,H_6^*. Let N^* := M^* ∪ q be the point extension of M^* corresponding to F^*. Then q is a loop in N^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let N be the ordinary dual of N^*. Since N^* is a point extension of M^*, deleting q from N^* gives M^*, and therefore contracting q in N gives M, i.e. N/q = M. The proof uses the standard dual rank identity: for a dual pair on ground set T, r_N(T \ A) = |T \ A| - r_{N^*}(T) + r_{N^*}(A). In N^*, the new point q lies on each hyperplane H_i^*, so r_{N^*}(H_i^* ∪ q)=6 for i=1,...,6, while r_{N^*}(S ∪ q)=7. Applying the rank identity with T=S∪q and A=H_i^*∪q gives r_N(H_i)=|H_i|-7+6=3-7+6=2. Hence all six chosen H_i remain 3-point lines in N. From the lines paa' and pbb', together with the side line of triangle abc containing a,b,x, it follows that {p,a,a',b,b',x} lies in a plane E of N. Since N/q=M and {x,a',b'} is a line of M, the set {x,a',b',q} spans a plane E' of N. The intersection E ∩ E' contains {x,a',b'}, and the paper concludes that {x,a',b'} is a line in N. The same argument gives that {y,b',c'} and {z,a',c'} are lines in N. Thus all 3-point lines of M remain 3-point lines in N. Considering the three side lines of triangle abc shows that {a,b,c,x,y,z} is coplanar in N; similarly, the corresponding primed-side lines show that {a',b',c',x,y,z} is coplanar. Since {x,y,z} spans a plane in M, and hence in N, these two coplanar sets lie in the same plane, so S \ {p} is coplanar. Finally, because paa' is a line and a,a' are already in this plane, p also lies in it, so all of S is a plane in N. Since N has rank 4 and S has rank 3, this means q is a coloop in N, equivalently q is a loop in N^*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main counterexample: ordinary duality need not preserve existence of adjoints
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the rank-3 non-Desargues matroid from Figure 1 of the paper. Then M has an adjoint, but its ordinary dual matroid M^* does not have an adjoint. Consequently, the answer to Mason's question, whether M^* must have an adjoint whenever M has one, is negative.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The matroid M has rank 3, and Section 1 explains that every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint. The dual M^* has rank 7 because M has rank 3 on a 10-element ground set. By Proposition 3.1, the modular filter generated by the six hyperplanes H_1^*,...,H_6^* of M^* is the whole flat lattice of M^*: the corresponding point extension forces the new point to be a loop. In the extension lattice E(M^*), this says that the top element is the join of the six point filters associated with these six hyperplanes. Since six is fewer than rank(M^*)=7, M^* fails Levi's property. By Corollary 2.2, any geometric lattice with an adjoint must have Levi's property, so M^* cannot have an adjoint.

proof_techniques.name: Encode point extensions by modular filters
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces single-element point extensions by modular filters in the flat lattice. In this encoding, requiring a new point p to lie on a copoint H corresponds to including the atom filter {H,1_L} in the generated modular filter. The join of such atoms in the extension lattice E(L) represents the strongest constraints forced by requiring p to lie on the chosen copoints; if that join is the top filter L, the only possible extension makes p a loop.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset such as the relative Dressian, this suggests looking for an analogous lattice/poset of extensions or subobjects where flat-induced elements generate a maximal or minimal object. A hypothetical order-reversing involution extending a flat polarity may force certain generated joins or meets not to collapse; finding a collapse would obstruct the extension.

proof_techniques.name: Use an embedding theorem to convert anti-duality into a positive-order obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Cheung's theorem turns the existence of an order-reversing adjoint L -> L^A into an order-preserving injection L^A -> E(L) that is surjective on points. This is powerful because rank constraints in L^A then impose join constraints in E(L): fewer than r points in a rank-r lattice cannot join to the top, and their images therefore cannot generate the top modular filter.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar strategy for the original question would be to show that an order-reversing involution of Dr(M) extending the flat-lattice involution induces an order-preserving embedding of some known rank-controlled subposet into an extension or interval poset. Rank or atom-count constraints in that subposet could then become testable join/meet constraints in Dr(M).

proof_techniques.name: Levi-property contrapositive as a nonexistence certificate
proof_techniques.description: Rather than trying to classify all possible adjoints, the paper proves nonexistence by violating a necessary property. Corollary 2.2 says that an adjoint forces Levi's property: any r-1 copoints must be simultaneously intersectable by a nonloop point extension. Therefore, to disprove existence of an adjoint for a rank-r lattice, it suffices to exhibit r-1 copoints whose associated atom filters generate the top of E(L).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Dr(M), one could try to formulate a necessary intersection property forced by an order-reversing involution and then search for a small family of flat-induced tropical linear spaces whose meet/join behavior violates it. This is especially plausible because the original problem already contains a natural flat subposet L(M)^op inside Dr(M).

proof_techniques.name: Dualize a point-extension constraint into a primal coextension rank chase
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 3.1, the paper studies a point extension N^* of M^* by passing to the ordinary dual N. The condition that q lies on specified hyperplanes H_i^* of M^* becomes, via the dual rank formula, the condition that the complementary 3-point sets H_i remain rank-2 lines in N. This converts an abstract modular-filter generation problem into an incidence-geometric problem inside a rank-4 coextension N with N/q=M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem is attacked through valuated matroids or tropical linear spaces associated with minors, this technique suggests translating inclusion constraints under duality/contraction into concrete rank or incidence constraints on complementary flats. It may help turn a hypothetical involution into verifiable combinatorial conditions on M.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence propagation from a small enforced configuration
proof_techniques.description: The non-Desargues proof starts from six enforced lines in the coextension N and propagates them through plane intersections to force additional 3-point lines to remain lines. Once enough lines are forced, two large coplanar sets sharing the spanning set {x,y,z} must coincide, so the entire original ground set S becomes a plane. This forces the new element q to be a coloop in the dual coextension N, equivalently a loop in the original point extension N^*.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For candidate matroids with a flat-lattice polarity, a finite incidence subconfiguration may be enough to force collapse of an attempted extension of the polarity. Non-Desargues-type configurations are natural test cases for finding obstructions because a small number of imposed incidences can force global degeneracy.

other_useful_info: The paper stresses that lattice-theoretic adjoints and ordinary matroid duals are distinct notions and that their compatibility is not automatic; the main counterexample shows actual incompatibility. It notes that matroids with adjoints properly generalize linear matroids: every linear geometric lattice has an adjoint, and every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint, but some matroids have no adjoint. It also notes that the converse to Cheung's embedding theorem was proved in Bachem and Kern, Extension equivalence of oriented matroids, European J. Combin. 7 (1986), 193-197. Other cited pointers relevant to adjoints and extensions include Cheung, Adjoints of a geometry, Canadian Math. Bull. 17 (1974), 363-365; Bachem and Wanka, Matroids without adjoints; Crapo and Rota on combinatorial geometries; Las Vergnas on point extensions of oriented geometries; and Cordovil on polarity and point extensions in oriented matroids.
why_relevant: Studies the relationship between ordinary matroid duality and lattice-theoretic adjoints, including negative examples. Useful for anticipating obstructions to extending a flat-lattice polarity to a larger quotient poset.

title: Adjoints of a Geometry
overall_summary: Cheung formalizes an adjoint of a geometric lattice G as a geometric lattice G^A of the same rank into which the inverted lattice G' embeds, with the points of G'—equivalently the copoints of G—mapped onto the points of G^A. The note relates any such adjoint to the lattice E(G) of G-extensions, represented by linear subclasses of copoints or equivalently by modular filters: every flat of G^A determines a linear subclass of G, giving a meet-preserving embedding G^A → E(G). The main obstruction technique is to compute joins of principal linear subclasses inside E(G) via modular filters, and then use semimodularity of the hypothetical geometric adjoint to derive a rank contradiction. The paper applies this to an explicit 8-point geometry and proves that it has no adjoint. For the original tropical problem, the most relevant content is the classical mechanism: an attempted extension of order reversal on flats can be constrained by embedding into an extension lattice and by the behavior of principal extensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition and stated basic properties of an adjoint
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice, let G' denote the inverted lattice of G, and let copoints mean coatoms of G. An adjoint of G is a geometric lattice G^A of the same rank as G together with a one-to-one order-preserving map e: G' → G^A that maps the points of G' onto the points of G^A. Since the points of G' are the copoints of G, the paper thereafter identifies copoints of G with points of G^A. The paper states that these defining assumptions imply three additional properties of e: e is cover-preserving, e is rank-preserving, and e is meet-preserving, i.e. e(u ∧_{G'} v) = e(u) ∧_{G^A} e(v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The note records these properties immediately after the definition and does not supply a proof. The later proof uses them in a precise way: if two flats of G have a join that covers each of them, then in the inverted lattice G' the corresponding elements cover their meet; cover preservation and meet preservation transfer this to G^A. Rank preservation is then used to know the ranks of the corresponding elements in G^A, so that the top element cannot cover them when the rank gap is larger than one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: correspondence between linear subclasses and modular filters
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice. A linear subclass C of G is a set of copoints of G such that for any copoints x, y, z of G, if x, y, and z all cover x ∧ y ∧ z, then x, y ∈ C implies z ∈ C. A modular filter M of G is a subset M ⊆ G satisfying: first, if x ∈ M and y ≥ x, then y ∈ M; second, if x, y ∈ M form a modular pair, then x ∧ y ∈ M. The proposition quoted in the paper says: if C is a linear subclass of G, then the set M_C of all elements x of G such that every copoint z ≥ x belongs to C is a modular filter of G; conversely, if M is a modular filter of G, then the set of copoints belonging to M is a linear subclass of G. The paper uses this as the correspondence between G-extensions and linear subclasses or modular filters. A G-extension is called principal if the corresponding modular filter has a least element. The G-extensions, equivalently the linear subclasses ordered by inclusion, form a lattice denoted E(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted from Crapo–Rota and no proof is given in the note. The forward construction packages a linear subclass C as the filter of flats whose containing copoints are all in C. The converse extracts from a modular filter M its copoints; the modular-pair meet axiom is exactly what enforces the linear-subclass closure rule for triples of copoints covering their common meet. Cheung uses the proposition operationally as follows: to show that a generated linear subclass is all copoints, pass to its modular filter M and repeatedly apply the rule that if M contains two modular flats, it contains their meet; once the bottom element is forced into M, upward closure gives M = G, so the corresponding linear subclass is the top element of E(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: any adjoint embeds meet-preservingly in the extension lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice and let G^A be an adjoint of G. For each flat a of G^A, define C(a) to be the set of copoints H of G such that the corresponding point of G^A lies below a. Then C(a) is a linear subclass of G. Consequently the assignment a ↦ C(a) gives an embedding of G^A into E(G), the lattice of linear subclasses of G ordered by inclusion. This embedding preserves meets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The points of G^A are identified with the copoints of G. In any geometric lattice, the set of points below a flat is linearly closed: if two points p and q are below a flat a, then the whole line determined by p and q is below a. Under the adjoint identification, line-closure of a set of points of G^A is the same as the linear-subclass condition for copoints of G: whenever copoints x, y, z cover x ∧ y ∧ z, the corresponding points are collinear, so containing two forces containing the third. Thus C(a) is a linear subclass. The map a ↦ C(a) is injective because a flat of a geometric lattice is determined by the set of points below it, and it is order-preserving by construction. Finally, both flats of G^A and linear subclasses in E(G) are closure systems on the same Boolean algebra of copoints of G, with the image of G^A contained in E(G); meets in these closure systems are intersections. Therefore C(a ∧ b) = C(a) ∩ C(b), so the embedding is meet-preserving.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example computation: two principal subclasses generate the top extension in the 8-point geometry
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the 8-point geometry G with points A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H in which the only 4-point planes are ABCD, ABEF, ABGH, CDEF, and CDGH. Let (AB) and (CD) denote the principal linear subclasses associated to the lines AB and CD, and let L be the linear subclass generated by (AB) and (CD). Let M be the modular filter corresponding to L. Then L is the top element 1 of E(G), i.e. L consists of all copoints of G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because L contains the principal subclasses associated to AB and CD, the corresponding modular filter M contains the planes ABEF and ABGH, which contain AB, and also CDEF and CDGH, which contain CD. The planes ABEF and CDEF meet in the line EF, and EF is covered by both planes; in this geometry they form a modular pair, so the modular-filter axiom gives EF ∈ M. Similarly, ABGH and CDGH meet in GH and form a modular pair, so GH ∈ M. The lines EF and GH form a modular pair whose meet is the bottom element 0 of the lattice, so 0 = EF ∧ GH belongs to M. Since M is upward closed, containing 0 forces M = G. Therefore every copoint of G belongs to the linear subclass corresponding to M, so L is the top element of E(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem: the 8-point geometry has no adjoint
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be the 8-point geometry with points A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H whose only 4-point planes are ABCD, ABEF, ABGH, CDEF, and CDGH. Then G has no adjoint in the sense of the paper: there is no geometric lattice G^A of the same rank into which the inverted lattice G' embeds with the points of G' mapped onto the points of G^A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that G has an adjoint G^A. Let x and y be the flats of G^A corresponding, under the embedding of G', to the lines AB and CD of G. In G, the join AB ∨ CD is the plane ABCD, and this plane covers both AB and CD. Therefore, in the inverted lattice G', the elements AB and CD cover ABCD. By the stated cover-preserving and meet-preserving properties of an adjoint embedding, x and y cover x ∧ y in G^A. Now view G^A inside E(G) via the preceding proposition. The example computation shows that the linear subclasses generated by the principal subclasses associated to AB and CD have join 1 in E(G); hence, in the paper's notation, x ∨ y = 1 in E(G). Since the image of G^A is a closure subsystem inside E(G), if the E(G)-join of x and y is already top, then no proper flat in G^A can contain both x and y; hence x ∨ y = 1 also in G^A. But G^A is a geometric lattice and therefore semimodular. In a semimodular lattice, if two elements cover their meet, then their join covers each of them. Thus 1 should cover both x and y. This is impossible: by rank preservation, x and y have the ranks corresponding to the lines AB and CD in the inverted rank-4 lattice, so they have rank 2 in G^A, while the top element has rank 4 and cannot cover rank-2 elements. This contradiction proves that no adjoint exists.

proof_techniques.name: Represent adjoint flats as linear subclasses of copoints
proof_techniques.description: The paper exploits the defining feature of an adjoint: points of G^A are exactly copoints of G. A flat of G^A can therefore be regarded as a subset of the copoints of G, namely the copoints whose corresponding points lie below the flat. Because flats in a geometric lattice are linearly closed sets of points, this subset satisfies the linear-subclass axiom for copoints of G. This gives a canonical embedding of the hypothetical adjoint into E(G), the extension lattice of G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the tropical question, the natural inclusion of flats into the relative Dressian resembles the inclusion of principal extensions into an extension lattice. A useful strategy is to identify whether tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M) carry an analogous closure-system or extension-lattice structure, so that any proposed order-reversing extension is forced to interact with principal objects in a rigid way.

proof_techniques.name: Use modular filters to compute joins of generated linear subclasses
proof_techniques.description: Instead of directly manipulating linear subclasses, Cheung passes to the corresponding modular filters. Joins generated by principal subclasses can be detected by showing that the corresponding modular filter must contain more and more lower-rank flats. The key rule is: if a modular filter contains a modular pair x, y, then it contains x ∧ y. In the 8-point example this rule forces ABEF and CDEF to give EF, ABGH and CDGH to give GH, and then EF and GH to give the bottom element, which makes the filter all of G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A tropical analogue would be to compute the subspace generated by principal tropical objects through a dual or auxiliary structure, rather than by direct containment. If relative Dressians admit a valuated-matroid or extension-theoretic analogue of modular filters, this technique suggests a concrete way to force a generated object to become maximal.

proof_techniques.name: Semimodularity contradiction from a forced top join
proof_techniques.description: The no-adjoint proof combines two independent facts about a pair of elements. First, incidence in G implies that their images x and y in any adjoint must cover x ∧ y. Second, extension-lattice computation implies that their join must be the top element. In a geometric lattice, semimodularity says that if x and y cover their meet, then x ∨ y covers both x and y. If rank preservation makes x and y too low-rank for the top element to cover them, this gives a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a template for obstruction arguments: find flats F and G whose reversed images should have a local covering relation, but whose associated principal objects in the larger Dressian-like poset generate the whole ambient object. Any additional geometric, ranked, or semimodular structure forced by a proposed extension could then become inconsistent.

proof_techniques.name: Top-forcing transfers even without join preservation
proof_techniques.description: Cheung explicitly notes that the embedding G^A → E(G) need not preserve joins in general. Nevertheless, the proof only needs a special case: if two elements of the sub-closure-system G^A have join equal to the top element in the larger closure system E(G), then their join inside G^A is also top. Indeed, a proper G^A-flat containing both would also be a proper E(G)-linear subclass containing both, contradicting that their E(G)-join is top.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful for the relative Dressian because one may not have well-behaved global join preservation. It may suffice to prove only top-forcing statements for carefully chosen principal tropical subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Finite incidence configuration as an obstruction certificate
proof_techniques.description: The obstruction is encoded in a small, explicit incidence list: eight points and five specified 4-point planes. The listed planes create two modular intersections yielding lines EF and GH, and those lines then form a modular pair meeting in the bottom element. This finite configuration is enough to certify that two principal extensions generate the top of E(G), which drives the contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests searching among small matroids with prescribed flat-lattice symmetries for analogous finite certificates in the relative Dressian: a small set of flats whose principal tropical subspaces force maximal containment behavior incompatible with an extended duality.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that the embedding G^A → E(G) obtained from an adjoint is not necessarily cover-preserving and not necessarily join-preserving; it says the rank-three geometry of six points in general position exhibits this phenomenon. The cited background sources are Crapo, Orthogonal Representations of Combinatorial Geometries, for the broader polarity/orthogonality motivation; Crapo–Rota, On the Foundations of Combinatorial Theory: Combinatorial Geometries, for descriptions of G-extensions via linear subclasses, modular filters, elementary quotients, and elementary strong maps; and Higgs, A lattice order for the set of all matroids on a set. By the definition in this paper, if a geometric lattice G already has an order-reversing self-isomorphism taking copoints to points, then taking G^A = G gives an adjoint; consequently Cheung's 8-point no-adjoint geometry cannot itself have such a polarity. The paper does not discuss tropical linear spaces or Dressians, but it supplies the classical lattice-extension obstruction pattern that the original problem is tropicalizing.
why_relevant: Introduces adjoints of geometric lattices and relates them to extensions. This is the classical source for the lattice-theoretic polarity problem that the question tropicalizes.

title: Polarity and Inner Products in Oriented Matroids
why_relevant: Develops polarity and inner-product analogues in oriented matroids and shows failures of classical polar-duality properties. Provides useful analogies and cautionary examples for tropical polarity.

title: Valuated matroids
why_relevant: The foundational paper defining valuated matroids and their projective equivalence. Since tropical linear spaces and Dr(M) are encoded by valuated matroids and quotients, this is essential background.

title: On pseudomodular matroids and adjoints
why_relevant: Compares set-theoretic and lattice-theoretic duality and constructs higher-rank matroids with adjoints. Its Levi/intersection-property viewpoint is relevant to incidence obstructions in Dr(M).

title: Polarity and point extensions in oriented matroids
why_relevant: Relates polarities of oriented matroids to extension posets and incidence properties. This is close in spirit to extending a flat-lattice order reversal to a poset of subobjects.
